Anyways there was this one night after I returned back to work and my supervisor found me resting in my office, so she gave me this impossible task of organizing the folders in her office. She screamed at me because I could not figure out a certain detail. That night she was very rude and hostile and she made me question the whole field of psychology, she verbally abused me and was slamming filing cabinet drawers because of her anger. Needless to say, she left a very negative impression on me about how even individuals educated in psychology and tasked with helping others can be rude, unprofessional, and conceited and should have their license stripped from them due to their poor attitude and not meeting the ethical standards of the profession. 
 
 Eventually I met a mentor, most of the psychologists on staff were very nice and friend, but this one elderly female who was a PhD in Psychology and had Masters in Social Work, she would always praised me for my hard work and enthusiasm. She was so friendly and she always motivated me and she was so uplifting and had an very positive attitude. Anyways, there was this one night she gave me a task of performing a neuropsychiatric evaluation on one of her patients. After I completed the task, she took me out to dinner after work and gave me great career advise. She praised my work ethic and intellectual competency and recommended that I pursue a career in psychology as a therapist. Because of her guidance and inspiration, she wrote me a letter of recommendation, which allowed me to become accepted at the most prestigious doctoral institution for psychological treatment. I am now a licensed therapist and I work with adults with developmental disabilities or who have had criminal justice issues. The warmth I received made me optimistic about the future. Mentor are vital to career success. I look forward to continuing my career in Neuropsychology.
Overall_Why: I find my work meaningful because it allows me to help individuals with brain injuries overcome their personal limitations that they may have experienced from an accident, disease, or genetic disorder. Helping people with their cognitive deficits allows me to become a better clinician. Anyways, there was this one night before I completed my degree where I was working with a female neuropsychologist who gave me a task of helping a disabled man walk up and down a short flight of stairs. After I completed that rehabilitation task, she took me out to dinner after treatment was over and gave me great advise about how important it is for the recovery process to love what you do and to find it meaningful, because she said finding meaningful work helps to improve the immune system, reduces inflammation, and reduces psychological stress. That was the day I decided I was going to love being a licensed clinician. Having a close encounter with the field of neuropsychology makes you quickly prioritize your life goals. The lead psychologist praised my work ethic and intellectual competency and recommended that I pursue a career in psychology as a therapist after I graduate, because I know how hard the struggle is with recovering from a serious accident, I believe that I could help others achieve their goals. The warmth and praise I received while working at the hospital made me feel confident, motivated, and optimistic about the future. Having a mentor and therapist is vital to the career process but I have now learned that a degree is equally as important for developing a career in neuropsychology.

Work_Story: I work at a boutique Independent Living for Seniors in the heart of the city. It is a large building that is central to everything so the convenience for them is perfect. I am the Recreation Coordinator Manager at my work so I spend a majority of my time with the residents doing activities. The other part of my job is to prepare the schedule and the calendar for their monthly activities. I am also in charge of all the independent contractors that come in for activities such as yoga, meditation, circuit training, etc. The people I hire must pass a background check and have experience working with seniors. In this field, patience and confidentiality is the key to a successful career path. Patience is definitely something I had learned in this field. It is also one of the most rewarding jobs I have ever worked in my entire life. Seeing the same faces everyday, the kindest seniors I've ever known. It has also showed me that Seniors are one of the most neglected people. There are many instances where I see residents without any family visiting and or occasions where they just come in to visit them for money. It pains to see how many people are so cruel to the people that gave them life. Other things I may do at my work is to facilitate in the events that happen. On top of planning and hiring, I have to be present during these events to insure that it goes smoothly. We also go on day trips to grocery stores as well as outings to museums and basically anywhere that is accessible to seniors. One part of this job that I never realized was so complicated is always making sure that the places we go to are wheelchair accessible due to many seniors with mobility issues. There are also instances where I have to carry a resident who is wheel chair bound onto the bus and off the bus. Because we are an Independent Living, seniors require to be mostly independent, we only have a couple residents that are wheel chair bound and they have caregivers to give them assistance. One rule of our facility is that all caregivers as well as homeaids must be in street clothing. There is a no scrub rule here because we want to differ from all the other seniors center where it often feels like a hospital. Our place has large bay windows with lots of sunshine shining through as well as marbled flooring for the ultimate brightness. The staff here are also what make this place so special because everyone genuinely cares for the job they are doing. Everybody gets attached to all the seniors we see every day and thus we care and we go above and beyond for our job. I have done many jobs in my life in many different fields and this is the first time where I have genuinely felt appreciation and love for my career. There is also equal opportunity for everybody in my work. Many times we have helped with foreign visas for immigrant workers to get an opportunity to experience our great country and what it has to offer. It truly is a spectacular line of work where you can meet great people and get to know a piece of history from the people who lived it.
Overall_Why: I find it extremely meaningful because I get to work first hand with real people, the same people, everyday so I really get to know them. It has taught me virtues that I never had before and shown me a happiness I never experienced before in any other work environment. Seniors often get neglected and forgotten and being one of the people that assist them everyday and seeing their smile in appreciation lights my heart up. There is nothing that can be compared to the feeling when you help them with even the smallest and easiest things and they show the world of gratitude. Seniors are the direct path to the history we never experienced. With their own experiences they can pass down their own wisdom and share their stories of the past; this makes them extremely important and eye opening. Caring for other people and helping other people really does bring the light to a job. When you are doing something you love for the people you love, this is no longer considered a job but a passion. This is what we strive to find every single day in every aspects of our life and I am so lucky to be in that passion and fire. I hope to myself everyday that the wisdom and kindness they show me, I can learn and pass down to our future generation.

Work_Story: For my regular full time job, I work as a Sales Account Manager for a large national wine and alcohol distribution and sales company in the state of {state}. I work on a team of five other employees under a team leader/boss. While we all communicate daily via email, text messaging and voice phone calls, I tend to work most of the day by myself. I am for the most part able to make my own schedule. I work from Monday to Friday and decide when I start work and when I finish my work day. I am on salary so I work until my job is done. Doing extra hours gets me nowhere since I am not able to get over time. I tend to start my work day at 8 am and finish up around 4 pm. My job is to sell wine, champagne and liquors to my accounts and to make sure they have everything that they need and are happy. If they succeed then my company in turn succeeds. I have a set amount of accounts that I work with. It is mostly convenience stores and grocery stores such as Walgreens, CVS, Publix, Walmart and Target. I have a set route that I tend to follow every week to make sure that I am able to visit each store at least one time per week. The larger accounts call for more than one visit per week. When I go into each store I first go to their alcohol and wine section and take stock of everything that they have and how many bottles they have of each brand and type. I have to see what brands and types of wine they are out of and make a list of what needs to be ordered. I then make sure all of the wine and liquor tags are correct and updated and print out any new tags that are needed for the shelves, including weekly special and sales wine price tags. I dust off all of their wine bottles and tidy up the shelves. I then go to the back of the store and take out any back stock of wine that they have and add that to the shelves. After it is all put out, I meet with the store manager and have them sign off on my work. I go over every type of thing I need and want them to order as well as let them pick things they want ordered and once it is all agreed upon, I order what they want and give them the invoice. That is pretty much what I do all day for each and every one of my wine accounts. There is always a set amount of things that the stores automatically order but I can also get bonuses and extra commissions if I can convince the stores to order cases of expensive wines and champagne. Once a month my team has a team meeting where we will get together on the first Monday of the month in the morning and talk about how we are doing, what we need to accomplish to meet the goals for the month and discuss any issues or concerns that we may have. It is a bit of a boring job since it's the same thing every day at every single store but it makes it easier for me so I can just go through my day without having to think too much. The routine of it all makes me better at focusing on the customer service aspect of the job since the physical part of the job is the same thing at almost every store.
Overall_Why: For the most part I do not find my job to be all that meaningful. Some would say it's sad but the only reason that I do my job is because it provides me with a pretty decent income, gives me freedom of schedule so that I can spend a good amount of time with my husband, kids and friends and have somewhat of a social life. In one way I guess you could say that my job is a little bit meaningful. It is meaningful for the reason that most adults like to drink wine and other alcohols to celebrate special occasions and to just unwind after a long day at work. Without my job and my company, the grocery and convenience stores would not have these products that adults want and need. My job helps to provide a good and smooth running service to these stores. That makes it so they make money and when they make money, my company and all of the employees at my company also make money to be able to support their families. Other than that I can not say that my work is meaningful. We don't do anything that helps out needy people or makes a difference in the world. The products that we sell are a "want" and most definitely not a "need". People can live without alcohol and some people would honestly be much better off if they didn't have access to alcohol for their mental and physical health as well as for their relationships with their family. I would only say my work was meaningful if I was actually helping children, sick people, homeless people or making some sort of a difference in the world. My job makes no difference in the world. It is just a business for people to make money off of.

Work_Story: I am a team leader at a rug factory. My department is the last department in the production line, after us the rugs go down to shipping. My job is to keep the employees in line, make sure that they come back from their breaks on time (people like to slack a lot), keep them off of their phones while they're working, lead team meetings in the mornings and keep everyone motivated to work harder and push our numbers as far as humanly possible. Also, I work with quality control so that I can walk the lines and make sure that I can identify and correct quality problems. I also bridge the gap between our department and our upper management, as well as our department manager. I am all about improving quality of life for all employees as well as making all the big guys happy. I am part ambassador, part manager. I really do wear a lot of hats. I also watch over the lines and figure out which employees will be working on which line. On top of that, I also am there to find ways to improve production times. So that's something I spend a lot of time doing. I have to be creative, original, but also intelligent. I need to bring them something they can latch on to. If I can improve run time for the machines, I can keep things running smoothly and quickly and we won't lose as much money. On top of that, I also have to keep track of shipments of 'hot rugs', which are top priority shipments that need to get down to the truck as quickly as possible. Sometimes these rugs are hot because the previous departments took too long to run them through latex, or through the weave room, and they've absolutely got to get where they need to go or people are going to be really angry. So that's an absolute priority. Hot rugs have to be labeled and rushed through the lines before everything else. There are hundreds of carts of rugs on the production floor at any given time, so these need to be organized. Also there needs to be someone to minimize yarn color changes as much as possible because they have to stop when doing changeovers. Basically when the rugs come to our department they're just one giant woven rug with gaps in between them made of junk material, and they have no edging on them. Our job is to run them through the main boxes to put on the edging on the sides. Once they come down from the main boxes they're taken to production lines, where they're cut into individual rugs and the edging gets woven on the parts that were connected. After that the corners are heated down with a hot gun so there aren't loose ends, and the rugs get send down to the wrapping machines where they're wrapped in plastic PVC. After that they're sent down to shipping on forklifts. As well as all these responsibilities I also need to handle any kind of problem that is brought down to use from upper management. Sometimes there are emergencies that need to be taken care of, or I need to break out people to send them to other departments if things go slow. So in a nutshell those are my priorities and that's what we do. I'm committed to my job and I spend a lot of time outside work thinking about ways that I can make improvements for us. I absolutely love my job and I want to continue to move up the ladder and make something more of myself.
Overall_Why: I find it meaningful because I love my coworkers. I'm committed to my coworkers, friends, and the responsibility I have to them to make things better. I have room to grow and become more professional, stronger, faster, better, and overall just a better person. I need to feel like there is room to advance and get promotions, etc. So I have that. I'm satisfied with the pay although I do want more. I know that if I just apply myself I will certainly get there. So that's where I'm at right now with my job and everything. I definitely don't ever think about leaving or going somewhere else. I feel challenged at my job, like it's not too difficult but it's not too easy either which is right where you want to be. I like a good challenge, and I absolutely love the feeling of accomplishment that you get when you finally overcome those challenges to achieve something. I don't seek glory but in a way I do seek power. I seek to be useful. I seek to become something greater than what I am right now. And to me that's really all I look for. I have strong connections with all of my coworkers and I feel confident in my ability to be a good leader. I think those are really the skills that I want to get better at. I am never going to be satisfied or complacent. So I have something I can really apply myself and I like that a lot.

Work_Story: I am a home health nurse. I work as a freelancer for 3 different home health companies. I am a licensed RN and I used to do work at a hospital. Now my role at these companies is to perform visits for elderly people to check on them, check things like their blood pressure, make sure they're healthy, and be friendly with them. After I make the visits I have to check in on the company app so that the visit has been registered on their system. I do a lot of driving back and forth to perform these visits, so almost 90 percent of my job is literally just driving. Unfortunately the companies do not reimburst me for my mileage but the pay is pretty good. Now on top of this, another role of my job is to look over patient charts and sign off on them to make sure that everything is kosher. This can only be done by a trained medical professional, it can't be done by an aide or anyone like that. So honestly I really don't mind driving all over the place. A lot of these elderly people are by themselves with no one to watch over them, their kids make visits but usually don't live with them. sometimes the conditions they live in are absolutely horrible. It makes me so sad to see it. I've seen some really awful things. Sometimes the kids are taking advantage of them or even abuse them in some cases. They usually have medical conditions that need to be watched over, or they have dementia and are on their way towards getting moved to hospice. So this is the kind of stuff that I do. It can be heartbreaking sometimes but I make a lot of friends in these patients, and I do a lot of good. I'm very proud of my ability as a nurse. You just have to be careful because you don't know what you're walking into when you go inside these houses. You could come across literally anything. Hostile patients, needles under the couches, some patients have been in drug dens and I didn't know if I'd get out safely. Usually I use a gps to get around but sometimes these houses are all the way out in the middle of nowhere and I lose my signal so it can also be a process to figure out exactly where I need to be. I always have to carry written directions with me just in case. There are a lot of lonely, sad elderly people that are going through a loss of control in their lives and they don't know how to handle it. I hope that doesn't become me someday. Anyways, the pay fluctuates because it depends on how many customers we have through these companies. Sometimes it's less but then other times I can make a whole lot of money very quickly and that's something that I find very attractive about the job. The only real drawback is having to file self employed taxes and the fact that there is no direct deposit, but other than that I'm honestly really happy with my job. I would never want to do anything else. I always want to be helping people until the day I die. As much joy as I can spread, as much good as I can do. I hope to be able to retire happy and know that I've made an impact on the world. What else can a person do? You know?
Overall_Why: I find it meaningful yes because i feel like there are people out there that really need me. these people are scared, they are sick, they are helpless, and they need love and care. they need to know that everything is going to be okay. And I can be that person. I can make sure that they have what they need. and while I can't perform any medical procedures or anything, I can give my advice as a nurse and as a professional, and I can make sure that if something goes really wrong with them they get the help and care they need from the hospital. So yes this job is very meaningful and I'm right where I want to be in life, in my career. I hope to continue doing this for many many years so that I can continue to be a positive influence on others and really make the world a better place. The elderly hold a soft spot in my heart because it reminds me of my grandma. so there's a personal connection there that you really can't fake. I feel like when I'm with some of these people, she's there with me. And that comforts me. There is no greater calling or purpose in life than to care for others and I very firmly believe that that's true. I find meaning in love, in kindness, I find purpose in my existence and I go to sleep at night knowing that I've done something that someone will hopefully remember. I just want to be remembered fondly.

Work_Story: I work for the {state} Comptroller of Public Accounts, which is the state's tax collector and accountant. My particular section acts as a go between for the Audit and IT divisions. I'm the manager of this small section of 8 people. Right now we're preparing for a new equipment refresh where all our field staff (around 750 people) will be getting new laptops, MiFi hotspots, and portable printers. In preparation for this I had to write up our division specific laptop requirements (from screen size to RAM to hard drive space to having a 10-key) and justify these specifications to IT. We just got the demo machines based off of those specifications this past week so our next steps will be installing all of our division specific software and testing the functionality of both the applications and the demo machines. We knew up front some of our division specific applications wouldn't work because the new machines will have Windows 10 which doesn't support older versions of SQL Server Express that we currently use. So IT has been working on updating those applications and we've already begun that testing on different Windows 10 test machines. However, now that we have the Audit specific demos myself and several other members of the team will be testing all of our applications to ensure nothing breaks that we weren't expecting and also testing the in-progress applications from IT as they have new builds ready. In addition to this we also have a large ongoing project with IT related to a mainframe system that 3 of my team members are assigned to. When they have differences, they bring the subject to me and I make a final determination of how we're going to move forward. Since this project involves updating a mainframe system that hasn't really been modernized in 20+ years, there are a LOT of questions that have to be answered. This is an important project for both IT and Audit as well as some executive management above them, so on a weekly basis I have to prepare a progress report that documents how much testing was completed, how many defects are outstanding, if there are any critical items that are preventing testing, and overall how the project is progressing. There seems to be a lot of disagreement on how the project progress should be measured because of all the upper management involvement, so a lot of my work on this one is soothing ruffled feathers and providing reassurance to both IT and Audit upper management that things are under control. On top of this large project, we have two projects related to new offices that I have to provide regular status reports on and several projects related to legislation that were passed in this most recent {location} Legislation cycle. In addition to working directly on the equipment deployment, testing our division specific apps, and coordinating projects, I also act as Tier 2 support for several of our division specific applications. One of my group members provides user level support directly and if he can't resolve the problem with one of our applications, then it goes to a different member of our group that acts as Tier 1 support who has database level access to all our applications. If he is unable to resolve the problem, then it comes to me and I either troubleshoot and provide a resolution or determine it's a bug in the application and send it on to the IT developers. The vast majority of the time (9 times out of 10) it's just a data replication issue and I'm able to resolve it. My group also handles our division specific internal webpage and I'm the backup for our web developer. When she's unavailable for more than a day or two, or something is needed urgently, I will make the modifications myself. I also act as our division's data security liaison and work with both the internal audit and information security divisions to ensure appropriate policies are put in place and enforced in Audit division. When someone violates a security policy or procedure, I coordinate with their direct management to determine the details of the incident and what actions need to be taken. Finally, as a manager twice a year I have to write up evaluations on my employees. The one at the half a year mark is the interim evaluation and it's generally only 1-2 pages, but the one at the end of the year is a full evaluation and it's 3-4 pages in length per person.
Overall_Why: I don't really feel like my work is very meaningful to me. I do feel like I help a lot of people which is something I do appreciate, but I'm not really making a difference in the world or even my local community. So much of what I do is paperwork and administrative stuff and dealing with personnel issues that I frequently feel like a combination of a secretary and a babysitter, which is not gratifying at all. I know my division's purpose is important to our state's economy and for keeping businesses on an equal playing field, but it's also hard to feel good about being a tax collector. I also have to give a lot of bad news to people when they violate our data security policies and it makes more work and stress for everyone. Unfortunately we are limited in how long we can take to respond to an incident, so even if it's 4 days before Christmas, I may have to tell someone they're getting written up and it will go in their HR file. That's a really terrible feeling because those disciplinary actions impact their interims, evaluations, and chances of getting a promotion/raise. So I would say overall, more of my job is bad or negative than good, and the little impact I do have helping people with their computer or software problems, is mostly outweighed by all of that. However, it's still a job and it pays decently so I'm grateful for that, but I try to find meaning other places (like volunteering at the local animal shelter), instead of finding meaning in my work. For me it's something that pays the bills and not my life's purpose.

Work_Story:
I am a public safety dispatcher. I answer 911 calls and dispatch officers to assist people. The job is not for everyone, 50 % of hires here do not make it through the first year of training. After the first year break-in, we lose further people to agencies with higher pay, or just other jobs where nights and weekends are not mandatory. Schedules are chosen by seniority, and it can take a decade on the job to get a piece of the weekend off again. Some people leave for more traditional 9-5 jobs, others leave to have families, because tis very difficult to work 12 hour shifts with young children. I have been here 8 years now and barely get a part of a weekend off. 
 
 95 percent of the calls we receive are not actual emergencies. Of the other 5 percent, 4.9 percent are "routine" emergencies, traffic collisions, requests for medical assistance, etc. The remaining .1 percent are the ones that can leave you with PTSD; We don??t see the tragedies, but we hear them. You can??t be the same person once you??ve been on the phone with someone who pulls the trigger. When you answer the phone, you never know what's coming in on the other end. It could be a parking complaint, it could be someone reporting their family murdered. You just don't know. There is a lot of stress in that. Most people can not handle it on a day to day basis. In between the priority incidents there is always too much to do, We have tons of data entry tasks, putting people into warrant databases, booking arrestees into jails, storing cars, recording stolen property. We answer the radio calls from officers with one ear and 911 calls with the other, sometimes simultaneously. At any one time I have 30 officers in my line up, all handling their respective incidents, and myself on a 911 call. I have to make sure everyone is where they need to be, make sure they have tow trucks, medical started, backup if needed, and a myriad of other resources. I have to run their warrant checks, license plate checks and stolen property inquiries, all while still paying attention to whomever is on the 911 line. It is exhausting and often thankless work. Most of our dispatcher go out on injury at some point, with carpal tunnel, neck injuries, etc from lack of ergonomic equipment; 12 hours a day in a chair with a phone glued to both ears while typing across 5 screens is quite a strain. We have decades old desks, broken cords abound and duct tape on the carpet at our communication center, yet I work for one of the largest law enforcement agencies in the world. Dispatchers are the "red-headed stepchildren" of the law enforcement world.
 
 There are good things about my job also. I get to help people everyday. Not everyone can say that. I know that my work makes a difference in the world. I am paid a decent, not exorbitant, but livable salary. I have medical benefits, vacation days, retirement. I am thankful for these things that keep me secure when there is so much uncertainty in our world these days. I am thankful for our 12 hour shifts that allow me to spend 3 or 4 days home a week with my small children.
Overall_Why: Of course I find my work meaningful, or the negatives definitely would have outweighed the job by now. We have a dispatcher appreciation week in April, and the officers always spoil us. Our chief becomes our barista for the week and someone delivers lunch almost everyday, each office in our division takes turns spoiling us for a day. While that is great, the everyday things are the important ones. I know for a fact that my efficiency contributes to the response times for public emergencies. I get to help people every minute of every day. I have heard babies first cries on the phone in my work, I have talked suicidal subjects into giving life another chance, I assist mothers crying over their sick babies. I have assisted the elderly, kids, disabled and every other vulnerable population you could think of. I regularly get thanked by random strangers who call me at work. Not every caller will stop to thank the dispatcher that answers the phone but I know I helped them through one of the most trying times in their lives regardless.

Work_Story: I work as a behavioral technician in an autism school. I have been employed in my position for 3 years. I love my job. We have eighteen students enrolled currently and three different classrooms. We are a small school but a lot goes on and no two days are alike. I primarily work in the autistic support classroom with our older students (middle to high school aged). Our kids come to us because they can't function in a public school setting, even in specialized classrooms in their home districts. My day is spent working one on one with students providing them with assistance and educational help. I support the classroom teacher by keeping students on task, assisting students with their work and managing behaviors. Most of our students are at our school because they are severely low functioning but some are higher functioning but have behaviors that are unmanageable at their prior school placements. I have 4 students that display very aggressive behaviors. One student, in particular, has such aggressive behaviors that he has been hospitalized on three occasions in the last 2 years. The behaviors that I deal with include hitting, punching, spitting, kicking, hair-pulling, self-injury, screaming, cursing, throwing items, breaking items, property damage, elopements, etc. The students have no self-control and will attack sometimes without provocation and with no clear antecedent. A big part of my job is maintaining the safety of all. This includes the other students in the classroom with the aggressor, the staff and the aggressive student themselves. I have had extensive training in safe crisis management and have a great deal of experience in managing situations such as this. That being said, managing behaviors is not the only thing I do, despite my job title... A big part of my job is data collection and analysis. Educational data is taken every day on our students to ensure they are getting the best possible outcomes and to make sure we are setting measurable and actionable goals for them. All of our students have the ultimate goal of returning to a public school setting and functioning at their highest possible potential. Our data can show us how the small little goals are working toward larger goals and ultimately may result in their return to a mainstream educational setting. Behavioral data is also taken daily to demonstrate the effectiveness of the student's positive behavior support plan. Every student has a positive behavior support plan in place and it details the consequence of behaviors and what staff members should do at certain levels of aggressiveness in students. Behavior data entails documenting an antecedent, the behavior itself and a consequence. An example would be: the teacher made a demand, the student threw a book, the teacher instructed the student to complete the demand and made the student retrieve his book. This data is imperative because it allows us to track what things can trigger behaviors in students and we can work with the students to learn ways to manage those triggers in healthy ways. The last part of my job that I will discuss is documentation related as well. Each student in our school requires a progress note be written every day. A lengthy document that captures the highlights of that student's day is written and submitted to the student's home school district daily and that is how my school gets paid to provide the services for the student. A less detailed note is also sent home to the parents daily.
Overall_Why: I find my work to be incredibly meaningful. My students are a joy most of the time and even when they aren't, they are simply acting in ways that they have learned work to get them what they want in the fastest manner possible. I love starting my day by greeting each student by name as they get off their vans and enter the school. So many of them are just happy to see a smiling face. I dedicate my entire working day to makes sure these kids have the best possible experience educationally, emotionally and socially. A lot of my students struggle because they had been kicked out of a lot of other educational settings and have serious trust issues regarding educational or institutional settings because of prior experiences. Providing them with a place that is safe and friendly is of the utmost importance. Another reason my job is incredibly meaningful is that the students are doing things that the people closest to them have never imagined them doing. I have a student currently who was non-verbal when he started at my school and was at the level of an 18-month-old child, even though he was 14 years old. In the last 2 years he has learned over 100 words, speaks the words with accuracy, has learned colors, can count to 50, can identify all the letters in the alphabet, lowercase and capital, can write most of the letters, can write his full name, can tell you his phone number and address and is now identifying currency, as well as the values of the currency and safety sign identification. It isn't hard to see why I do what I do when I can look at the progress that student made and know that I made a huge difference in his life.

Work_Story:
I work as an applications engineer in a company that provides professional, scientific and technological services in the electronics / computer engineering industry. My employer is a large sized publicly listed company that often collaborates with other suppliers or knowledge partners in engineering design and development. I have a total of eight years of work experience and the longest position that I have held is my current position, where I started out in a junior role six years ago and then worked my way up. I am responsible for leading a team of highly skilled professionals like myself in software and technical product development. These activities are often cross-functional and sometimes involve teams from our client organizations working alongside us in the design and development process. 
 
 In my own organization, I have held three different positions since I started. I was first a junior engineer in the technical support department for two years, then I was an associate engineer in software development for two more years and now I lead a team of eight people in the field of software development. Over the past two years, I have developed this team of my own and supervise them regularly. I am in charge of overseeing regular technical product creation, troubleshooting issues and creating progress reports to keep track of build and release of such software. I also consult as a subject matter expert on various teams such as sales or technical support. My job responsibilities have increased as I consult with or support other teams and departments more often because of my experience in my current workplace. I am allowed to take ownership of products /projects entrusted to me and all their expenses. 
 
 I report to a manager of my own from upper/senior management. Twice a year, I am provided performance reviews and feedback on my work, I am also provided a choice in the projects available so I can choose my own targets to work towards. This helps me to not only pick my own goals to work at my pace, but also be accountable for them in every way. I am in turn responsible for conducting the performance reviews on the team members I oversee. I get to define project goals, set tasks or schedules and generally manage all financial and budgetary concerns for my team. I believe in having an open door policy with my co-workers as well as my subordinates because I feel that such a process of communication encourages exchange of important information and fosters co-operation. I also strongly believe that this reduces instances of conflict in the workplace and encourages positive teamwork and healthy competition. I like to encourage my team to not only work towards their expected professional goals, accomplish their mandated tasks or perform above the required standards, but also take the time and initiative for self-development.
 
 As a hard working employee, I believe that relaxation is a well earned reward. I get about five weeks of vacation time in a year and a great comprehensive package of employee benefits to take care of my needs outside my professional life. The management of my organization also encourages and provides me with professional development opportunities at least once a year, so I feel valued and appreciated as an employee.
Overall_Why: I think that the work I do serves a useful purpose and while it utilizes my own skills and learning to a large extent, I also get to constantly learn and improve from the process. My work involves a certain amount of creative freedom and this provides a sense of exploration, offering me the chance to be unconventional and imaginative. To me, there is a lot of meaning in such a process because I feel that I am really contributing something of value through my work and efforts. Being able to come up with entirely new and innovative solutions gives me a sense of achievement. At the end of every such creative process, I am able to see the new benefits / possibilities afforded by my efforts and the end product of every such effort provides me fulfillment. When my team works together on a project and comes up with a truly impressive product, I feel not only personal satisfaction but also a small pride in this collaborative outcome. When each of my team members or subordinates achieves a victory, I feel that it is my small personal victory too. When one works in a team, problems get divided up and profits/ achievements get multiplied through sharing. After every such success, I feel that I am making small but definite strides towards achieving a meaningful life and career. I believe that I could become the best possible version of myself professionally as well as personally and all of this happiness currently comes to me through my work. Therefore, I find my work extremely meaningful.

Work_Story: I am a Material Planner for a local construction company. What that means is that I am responsible for determining the quantity and type of material required to construct any given project. The way that I do this is after being given a set of construction drawings and specifications, I am responsible for reading through them to determine exactly what it is that the client wants, how they want it built, as well as how long they expect the project to take. Not to mention what they are and are not willing to spend on materials. Once I've read through the specifications and have noted exactly what specific grades of steel and specific types of materials are required for certain parts of the project being built, I then begin going through each drawing. I have to first study any legend or key that can sometimes be provided to help out in deciphering the drawings and once that's done I am looking at the design of the structure that is going to be built. I'm looking for each piece of beam, plate, pipe, etc... and using the dimensions given on the drawing to determine how much of each is required and I have to mark each piece on each drawing with the size and quantity. Once I've done this for all the drawings that require it, I then have to make a list of each piece in a spreadsheet. This is the most tedious part of the job as it sometimes can take half a day to work on one drawing, depending on how much information is included on that drawing. Not to mention that a lot of drawings will have you reference other drawings so there's a lot of back and forth and it can be very time consuming. I then have to double check everything, or have someone else within my department check my information to make sure there are no mistakes. Once I've done all that and I have all the information on the spreadsheet, I then have to manipulate this spreadsheet by including filters and sorting the information so that everything can be displayed by each part, then material grade or type of each part, then size of each part. Once I've done this I can then use this information to begin inputting into our AS400 Material Take off/Material Order system. Once all this information is inputted into the system I am able to run various types of reports. Other departments within our company rely on the reports that I can run with this system. I have to run recap reports for the Estimating Department, Weight Reports for Estimating as well as our Design team. But the most important report that my department runs is called the Material Recap report. This report is the main one in which we send to the Purchasing department. It is this report that tells Purchasing exactly what to order. It is crucial that the information that gets inputted into our system is correct because these can be really expensive material orders. It's not unusual for these to be million dollar orders minimum so when that much money is on the line, there can be very little room for error so at the last minute before sending this information to Purchasing we usually do another last minute, "triple check" as we like to call it, before sending it off because once Purchasing orders the material, there's usually no going back.
Overall_Why: I do because when it comes to my work, I feel that I am a part of something special. In a way it's an extension of the creative process. The creative process being those who came up with the project and designed it from the ground up, and my work being the one responsible for translating that information into something the project team can use to make the project come to life. I am like a middle man between the project team and the client. They rely on me for any questions they may have with regards to interpretation of the drawings to what material grade certain pieces need to be. I am the one who always has his hands dirty with that information so I feel a sense of pride and self worth when people come to me with questions or concerns about certain aspects of the project. In a sense once design is done, the whole process starts with me and that's something I don't take lightly. The project can only be built with the right materials and quantities of said materials. When I have the project manager counting on me to get my job done and done right, that's when I find the work that I do the most meaningful. Once the project is all said and done I can look at it with satisfaction knowing that each and every piece was fabricated using material that I was responsible for having the company bring in.

Work_Story: I work as a Marketing Database Analyst for the largest credit union in the state of {state}. I've only been in my role for about three months now. I worked previously for a regional bank as a Marketing Analyst. Being that I'm about 90 days into my new job, my description of what I do probably won't capture it all. I'm still learning and therefore haven't even been introduced to many of the tasks I will be doing on a routine basis. But here is a good overview of what I do. I work in the Data and Analytics Department for the credit union which is a part of our IT Department. There are about 10 of us in the department and I am the only one with the role of Marketing Database Analyst. I primarily was hired to liaise with the credit union's Marketing Department. We have 3 marketing employees in my city and another 4 in Indianapolis, but I primarily work with the 3 women here locally. We have weekly meetings to go over what's going on as far as current and upcoming projects are concerned. Many of these projects require data in the form of customer lists or reports. That's where I come in. For example we're working on an upcoming promotion to celebrate reaching the 300K member threshold. I have been offering advice as far as what, we in the IT Department, can track and measure as part of the contest. (People can get contest entries for, say, setting up direct deposit or using their debit card). I take that information back to my team and we will set up the logic using SQL server and set up some sort of tracking mechanism to determine who got a contest entry in the entry period. So this is really just a taste of what I do, but it is really much larger than this. I spend a lot of time learning SQL server (I've never used it before so I've received a ton of on-the-job training) to fulfill research and list requests. Just the other day I fulfilled a list request for our Marketing team of members with Health Savings Accounts. Apart from working on list requests, I also do a lot of work with our member surveys. I've been cross training on the process of what we do to send out our weekly net promoter score surveys. I have also been working to revamp the look of this survey as well as our semi-annual member satisfaction survey. Another thing I've been working on is familiarizing myself with Qlik, which is a data visualization software program, similar to Tableau. We can take build queries in SQL, bring those queries into Qlik, create connections to the data warehouse and build these awesome apps that tell a story about our credit union's members. We can build charts, tables, etc. that really paint a picture about how well our products are performing and how we are growing as an organization. Another thing I am involved in is learning our mapping software. It's not something that's really used a lot but could be of use if we decide to, say, often a new branch location. So what I am hoping to use it for is to measure drive times to some of our locations. This will give us an idea of how much traffic a branch has seen over time. It's also a good way to visualize a certain geographic area to see where our branch locations compared to where our members live.
Overall_Why: By and large, I find my job extremely meaningful because I am actually learning something. And that's really important because at my last job I wasn't learning a thing. I had been at my last job for over 15 years. It had got to the point where I was just doing the same mundane tasks everyday and I felt tremendously bored. I started to hate my job. Of course it didn't help that a new manager came in and we all hated him. I think that served as a wake up call for me that it was time to move on. I did a lot of soul searching and thought about the pros and cons of my job. It occurred to me that I had really plateaued in my role and there was not no room for me to grow both professionally and financially. There were some skills I wanted to learn but other people were called in to manage that aspect of the business. So when I found my new job, all of this changed. That one skill I really wanted to acquire the most was learning SQL server and I'm accomplishing that in my job. My new boss at my new job has made it clear that if there's anything I want to do or learn, they will help me with that. I love that I can come into work everyday and learn something new. It's never boring and I enjoy being there. My work is meaningful because not only am I helping further the mission of the business, I am achieving goals for myself. It's very satisfying.

Work_Story: I currently work as an E-Rate Consultant. I have been in the field for a little over 10 years now. It is not a very common field, but one that I feel provides a tremendous service. I get asked almost weekly about my job, people are not very knowledgeable about the program. Basically, what I do is make it possible for school districts to obtain funding they need for all of their technology expenses. I meet with district staff and determine their technology needs on an annual basis. I am often able to save them between 80-90% on these expenses. There is a lot of paperwork required, which is often audited. I handle all of these forms. I calculate their eligibility based upon the income level of families within the particular district. I then go through every bill they have for services such as telephone, cell phone, internet access, web hosting, etc and determine what their expenses currently are. With this information on hand I am able to provide them with the exact amount I am able to get covered for them. Where do the funds come from? This is the question I am asked most often. The answer is we all, as Americans, contribute to the pot that the funds come from. Everyone has a Federal Universal Service charge on their phone bills, such funds are what provide educational entities with E-Rate funding. Another component of the program is Internal Connections. Those school districts who are in greatest need of funds are eligible to obtain new cabling, servers, and components for their technology which are one time rather than recurring expenses. These projects often cost millions of dollars, so for districts to be able to implement these components at only 10-20 percent of the cost is an amazing opportunity. As mentioned before, many applications are audited by the E-Rate program. Since school's do not possess the knowledge to complete such, I spend a great deal of time responding to audits on their behalf. There are semi-annual on site visits for most of the clients, though it varies. Much of my job entails obtaining the wealth of information needed from them via phone or email instead. Once funds are approved, which is the case 99% of the time, my role switches to being an intermediary between the districts and the providers who actually provide services. Ensuring they bill correctly, that the requested services are implemented and there are no other issues. The whole cycle takes a year, and at the present time I handle this process for over 50 school districts. It is an incredibly demanding job, but one that is greatly rewarding. With each client, my work enables hundreds to thousands of children to gain access to the technology that is so important in this century. I build some great relationships with my clients and continually find ways for them to save money and remain up to date with their schools technology. I too have developed great relationships with the federal program employees who are very helpful and engaging. It is not something I ever thought about doing, but I fell into the field and absolutely love it.
Overall_Why: I find my work to be extremely meaningful. Though it sometimes feels a bit too routine, when I think about what I am accomplishing, the satisfaction is great. My goal in life has always been to help others. I have always been drawn to helping those that I felt were less fortunate than myself. With this job, I don't exactly get to do this, but in another way it's exactly what I do. Through my work, there are thousands of children who would otherwise not be able to have access to technology that are now able to do just that. They are able to learn a great deal and therefore remain competitive in what continues to be a technologically advancing environment. I am able to give staff and students a lifeline, contact with those outside the classroom or school. I am able to create an environment which not only teaches children, but gets them engaged in an exciting and hands on manner. In any other field I may be able to help a few people each day, but as an E-Rate Consultant I reach the lives of hundreds every single day. Mostly the lives of children who I will never meet. I remind myself of this often, especially when things get overwhelming as they so often do in this field. It, I feel is an incredibly humbling blessing to be able to serve so many others through my work. To give to society and aid in the development of productive and successful adults.

Work_Story:
I am a data processing specialist for a small niche market in a small manufacturing plant. I am in a supervisory role and oversee about 7 employees. My day to day activities consist of answering the phones from customers inquiring about technical issues with their products, concerns they may have or general customer service questions and shipping inquiries. I also compile and create spreadsheets to better map out data configuration, shipping and routing analysis, and financial projections. I also have to take on the shipping manager/coordinators role and contact the various shipping, freight, and freight brokers to decide and confirm we are using the best form of freight possible. I have meetings every Tuesday with the VP of the company, and every Thursday with my team.
 
 When I speak with customers it is mostly elevated conversations where they are upset about the product, the customer service, the shipping logistics, or technical issues they have encountered. Regular calls to the customer service line are not routed directly to me. These people's issues can range from many different things. It is my job to appease the customer and make sure that they are satisfied to my best ability whether that is to offer them refunds (I have a cap of $600.00, we sell high end merchandise), offer them better shipping options at no charge to them(we have several, one of which costs $250.00 more for in house assembly), exchanging their products for another one, or something similar, etc.
 
 My compiling and creating spreadsheets is consistent with pretty much every manufacturing and shipping companies. That tends to be tedious as I must sort through a lot of data, export/import through different methods and different programs and then give direct to the VP of the company for consideration, thoughts, and approval.
 
 As the shipping manager, and shipping coordinator I must make sure that we are using the most cost efficient freight companies, and freight broker companies that we have access to. I must also compile a substantial amount of data through these companies to decipher which are the best to use, and which we should forego using in the future. I track down all shipment issues with said companies and I am the direct contact for when larger shipping manifests run into issues. These issues mostly consist of the barge losing our freight (it happens often, unfortunately). I then have to find out where our freight is, how to get it back, and how to get it to the customer is the most timely and costly efficient manner. It's a lot of logistics, a lot of long phone conversations, a lot of approvals, and takes a great chunk of my time during the day, and sometimes into the night.
 
 Regarding my meetings every week, as stated I have to meet with the VP of the company every Tuesday to discuss the previous weeks progress, financial earnings, financial drops and overall how my team is doing, what they are working on, what we are lacking, what we may need to better do our jobs, etc. I meet every Thursday with my team to then discuss what the VP and I discussed on Tuesday and get their feedback on how I could make their jobs easier. These meetings tend to last at least an hour, sometimes two. These are very time consuming as well.
 
 Other than that I do your basic filing and office assistant/office managerial duties that include working with technology and customer/client interaction.
Overall_Why:
I think that I do find it mostly meaningful. I deal in very high end therapeutic furniture (such as massage chairs, bath tubs, couches, etc). The type of furniture that really helps people with their true quality of life. Although I am just a cog in the overall machine, I really help people to better understand what they've purchased, how it will affect them short term and long term, as well as how it will improve their lives. I have assisted with people suffering from crippling back injuries that can't even get out of bed most days to using our products and feeling better the very DAY after just a 30 minute use. I find that truly meaningful because I am making the world a better place through therapeutics, something I truly believe in.
 
 I also find it meaningful because by introducing people to therapeutic remedies that we have available through our various items of furniture, they in turn will possibly, and most likely pass on the word to their loved ones, their friends, and their family.. who in turn will pass it on to others, etc. It is much like a "pay it forward" movement for me. If I can help one person, what if they can help another and so on.
 On the flip side, I feel that we could make use of more rigid standards when manufacturing the products and I don't believe we should be outsourcing the production to Asian countries as there are plenty of Americans here that need good sustaining jobs.

Work_Story:
I work inside of a local restaurant. My job entails a lot of different things to do. The primary thing I do, is cook food to order. We are an American restaurant, so I mostly focus on things like Burgers, French Fries, Hot Dogs, all that type of thing. But, we also have Italian entrees a lot too, we get a lot of requests for Alfredo, Lasagna, Chicken Cutlet and the like. The most popular dish I seem to prepare most often is Macaroni and Cheese though. 
 
 I am expected to keep my station, and the stations around me completely clean. We are a professional restaurant, even if we aren't on the higher scale of pay. We have sous chefs, we have people who work on garnish, and we have just the plain chefs. The three chefs that work there (of which I am one) we are responsible for making sure the kitchen is running smoothly. The biggest part of that is definitely keeping everything clean. One thing that happens in a professional kitchen fairly often is things get dirty, fast. There are all types of health codes and things like that that need to be followed to the letter. If we do not have a clean station, we pretty much are not allowed to prepare food. So I must be sure that everything around me is kept up to code, including my own station.
 
 Prep work is also another very important thing when it comes to cooking. You can save yourself a ridiculous amount of time in the kitchen by having things prepared. For example, we typically have a tomato sauce cooked and ready to go for main entrees. We have soups sitting there just lightly heated so they can be spooned into a plate on demand. So, on top of actually preparing the soup and sauce, we have to get them ready. By this I mean, cutting up shallots, cutting up tomatoes, peeling and mincing garlic, and other vegetables. You don't want to cut up vegetables too much before you use them because some of them will brown, and it's considered unsanitary to leave out vegetables a long time. So, after doing this, you use them to prepare the various dishes, and keep them heated to a certain temperature depending on the dish, and the various health codes applied to it.
 
 The final, most important thing at least in my opinion, I do is called "floating." This means that no matter what station needs help, I'm experienced enough to hop in. Someone is unsure exactly what medium rare is in a steak? I step in and help. I find that is the most common thing I need to help with, steak cooking is very difficult unless you are experienced. I view my role primarily as a teacher, despite all the other things I do. Menus are rotating consistently, and not everyone has been working there as long as I have, so it's kind of hard for all the cooks to keep up.
 
 So in short, my job is to keep everything running smoothly first and foremost, keep everything clean and up to code, and to cook. There is a lot going into a kitchen that people don't know about, but it is true that it's a demanding, hard job. The greatest thing about my job, is that it builds teams...there will never be another job I work where my coworkers and I feel like we've survived something dramatic outside of warfare!
Overall_Why:
I think meaningful is not the best word to describe it. I think rewarding is a little bit better. I look at meaningful work as something that benefits mankind as a whole. People who work in charities, people who invent things, the military, the peace corps, those type of things are meaningful work to me. My job is stressful, and it does serve the community in the fact that we give some back, but that obviously isn't the primary purpose of the restaurant I work at.
 
 I think when it comes down to friendships I've made, the money I bring home to support my family, getting me out of the house and being a giving member of society, it's meaningful in that way. Anything that gets you to be better than you were before you started, could be considered meaningful. 
 
 I don't feel as if it's EXTREMELY meaningful by any definition though. There are a ton of things that I could be doing that would benefit others a lot more than cooking. But, teaching people, making sure the homeless in our community get some food, and the like - these things make the job meaningful to me. The fact that I would be at home doing nothing if I lost this job, that makes it meaningful to me. So, overall as a human it's probably not meaningful, but to me...it is. It is a means to take care of people, my family primarily.

Work_Story: I am a social worker for the state I live in. I work for the department of health through the office of aging. I work with the elderly and disabled adult population. I assist these individuals in transitioning from nursing facilities back into the community. The participants either return to a previous home or they will be assisted in finding a rental property. The program they receive if they are able to return into the community is a Medicaid Waiver program. The program allows for a personal care attendant to come into the participants home daily and assist them with their basic activities of daily living including bathing, dressing, grooming, toileting, transferring, bed mobility, cleaning, cooking, getting to their doctors appointments and other basic daily needs. The program is only eligible to those participants that will qualify for Medicaid services. I am the Nursing facility transition coordinator for my area in the state. I go visit the potential participants in the nursing facility that have been placed on a list for the services. I make the participant an offer for services. From that point someone else will come out and do an assessment with the participant in which a serious of questions will be asked and behaviors observed. From that assessment it will tell if the participant qualifies for the waiver program or not. If they do qualify i will then along with a team of supports assist in the transition process from the nursing facility back into the community. This will include things such as, housing needs, rental assistance, setting up personal care attendant services, setting up home health services, assist with getting medical equipment ordered for the participant to use at home, assessment of the home environment to assure safety. Some participants will not be able to transition home with the waiver program if their needs are too great and they do not have natural supports to assist them in the times that the personal care attendants are not in the home. Once a participant has sucessfully transitioned into the community, i will track the participant for 2 years after coming out of the nursing facility to ensure they are receiving all the supports that are needed to maintain them in the community. I work from a home office and make my schedule to go into nursing facilities on some days of the week. My travel usually consists of about 3 days per week. Other duties include conference calls with state agencies, nursing facilities, and support coordination agencies that over see the participants also. Monthly training is also required with the state agency. The job that i have is a very rewarding job because i am able to see people that are institutionalized be able to go back into the community and regain their independence to a certain degree. In some instances returning to the community is not the best scenerio for the individual and they will choose to remain in the facility. Many individuals benefit form this program and it has a very high success rate.
Overall_Why: I do find my work meaningful because i feel like i am making a difference in peoples lives. Some people that are not appropriate to stay in nursing facilities would remain there for the rest of their lives if they were not given the opportunity to qualify for the program i work for. Because i work with the elderly, i find it very meaningful because a lot of that population do not have others that will stand up for them or offer them hep. Many people are put into nursing homes by family members and their families do not have a goal of taking them out or even visiting them. The program i work for offers hope for many elderly and disabled adults. There has been may success stories over the years that i have worked at this job. It is very rewarding to see an individual so excited to get to go home. Most people just want to be home, wherever that may be to them. Others have to find a new place and home to stay and it is also rewarding to see those individuals start a new beginning in a place of their own. It is very meaningful when you see an elderly persons eyes light up when you visit them and they are happy instead of being sad stuck in a facility with no hope of getting out. It can also be heartbreaking to see other elderly or disabled individuals have to stay in a nursing facility because their health is too bad to return to the community.

Work_Story:
I work as a full time meat cutter in a grocery store in the southeastern United States. I have been employed by my company for almost 7 years and have filled several different roles in my tenure there. I also do other jobs as needed, not always directly related to the meat department. I have learned much about my line of work, not only from my managers and co-workers, but also from customers that come in that may have their own experience in a meat department. Much of what I have learned to do in my job is based on current company guidelines, which have tended to change over time. The way I may process a certain cut of meat might differ from the way I was trained to do it when I first started with the company, and the cuts themselves also have evolved in appearance over the time I've been with the company.
 I help many customers in my daily experience at my job. Not only do I have to fill the case up for the general traffic of customers that we may have each day, I also respond to customers' requests for special cuts of meat. I gather and put equipment together everyday to help me produce the varieties of meat products needed to satisfy our vast customer base, not only diverse in age, but also in ethnicity, religion, nationality, and tradition.These pieces of equipment include vertical bandsaws (used to cut meat products that contain bones), large knives (used to cut steaks, roasts, and chops), small boning knives (used to trim away fat and for finesse and detailed cuts), plastic scrapers (used to remove bone debris from the cuts of meat after using the vertical bandsaw), steel mesh gloves (used to protect the cutter from personal injury due to accidents while cutting meat), sharpening or honing steels (used to maintain a sharp edge on the blade of knives, resulting in higher productivity and less waste), power tenderizer (used for cuts such as beef and pork cubed steak). a deli slicer (used to cut meat into thin slices with greater consistency than if cut by hand), a grinder (to create ground beef of various varieties, and ground pork), and wrapping scales (to package, weigh and price meat). 
 There are many different cuts of meat I produce on a daily basis, generally either beef or pork. In beef, I produce cuts from what we refer to as primals. Primals are larger "chunks" of meat that we cut our specific products from. Regularly cut primals include top round, bottom round,eye of round, top sirloin, sirloin tip, short loin, ribeye (bone in and boneless), chuck, and shank (leg). In pork, the only primals I currently cut from are the bone-in and boneless loins, the butt (shoulder) and the legs (portion hams, shank, picnic butts). 
 I also help customers make selections in our seafood department everyday. I stock a display of different kinds of fish fillets, shellfish, fish steaks, and other seafood products. I answer questions and give advice about their product selection, and occasionally will cook their food for them in a high-pressure steamer in the department.
 Occasionally, I have to work late and close down the departments, which involves removing, packaging, and storage of food items, disassembling the equipment and cleaning the department and equipment.
Overall_Why: I do find my work extremely meaningful. On a typical day, I help people decide what food they are going to purchase for their family to enjoy. I feel as if my reliable and knowledgeable advice could potientially play a huge role in whether or not the customer has an enjoyable experience or if they are dissatisfied. I feel that I do my job well, and that many people depend on me to do my job to my full capacity, not only the customers, but also my co-workers and managers. I enjoy learning as much as I can everyday from my managers and my customers, because I believe that gaining as much knowledge as possible ensures that I will be able to take care of the next customer even better. Even beyond the ideas of just how I do my job, I find my job extremely meaningful, because I meet new people everyday, and I've gained friends from my job that I will have for the rest of my life. If I have a positive interaction with a customer or a co-worker, it makes that person have a better day, and I also have a better day because of it. My interactions with customers could potentially create new family traditions for them, if they greatly enjoy preparing and eating the products that I either cut or recommended to them. I think that my job impacts so many more people than myself or the customer I'm specifically helping at any given moment. Lasting memories are created when people are happy, and I'm glad to be even just a tiny part of that.

Work_Story: I work for an Information Technology services company and have been with the same company for just over three years now. My official title is Network Administrator, but that is only a small part of what I do. My company has hundreds of clients within the general area and we service three states with an office or two in each state. We supply services and equipment to other businesses and don't deal with any residential clients, only commercial clients. Typically a contract will be for infrastructure and server support, but we also do many projects like new server installations or workstation roll outs and upgrades. I have three primary duties at my job. My first duty is server support. This is where the majority of my day to day effort is spent. I am one of five people on my team who supports all customer servers in the state. I have roughly forty clients that I am primarily responsible for out of two hundred in the state. All of my servers report to a single monitoring desktop that I have sitting at my desk. If there are any problems with one of my machines, then i'll see it there first. Any help desk calls from one of my clients will be routed straight to me first and if I'm not there it will go to one of my teammates. If I'm not busy with anything else than I will spend my time reviewing server logs making sure that all of my servers are operating at their best. My second duty is customer projects. Usually once a year or so. each client will have some kind of upgrade project. Projects can range from adding a new workstation all the way up to upgrading hundreds of workstations with a new software package. I will act as the project manager on all of my clients' projects and can request additional resources from my manager depending on the size and scope of the project. A simple project can take a single afternoon while a large project can take a team several days or even weeks. While a project is going on, I may be out of the office on site with the client for several days making it extremely important that the rest of the team be able to fill in for me on my other duties. If the project is large enough, we will sometimes even hire outside consultants to help out. My third duty is support of the local data center at my office location. We maintain a small data center of about thirty servers and the associated network equipment to run the infrastructure of our own facility. Usually when building out a new server or workstation for a client, it will be put into a server rack in our own data center first where it will be configured and tested. Once tested, it will be disconnected after a few days of smooth operation and moved to the client site. Also, about once a week I will go through the logs of our own server infrastructure to make sure everything is running smoothly. Besides my three main duties, I am also involved in the standard operation of the team. I attend staff meetings usually once a week and sometimes back up other members of the team when they are busy on projects or service calls. We all try to at least have a basic familiarity with all of our customers in case we need to work on a service call.
Overall_Why: I have spent my entire career working in the Information Technology industry. For me, it's what I've always wanted to do with my life. From the time when I was a little boy and saw the original Star Wars in the movie theater, I always knew that I wanted to work in technology. There are many parts of my jobs that can be a total pain, but the technology itself is something that I have always loved and found meaningful to me. I started out working for a small services company after finishing school and from there worked in technology in the financial industry for the bulk of my career. No matter the line of business I was working for, I always enjoyed learning about and supporting new technologies. In the late 2000's when the recession hit, I was laid off and needed to decide if technology was still right for me. At that time I came to the decision that there wasn't anything else that gave my life that level of meaning that I got from being around computers and technology all day long. I did decide back then to scale back a little bit. Instead of going for the maximum salary and biggest title I could get, I would work closer to home and find something so that I didn't need to put in the insane hours that I had been working anymore. This small change has added to my level of happiness and I am very satisfied with where I am and what I'm doing right now.
