Work_Story:
I am an elementary education teacher. I am currently in my third year of teaching fourth grade. I work in a high poverty school with students from varying backgrounds.
 
  My job is to teach my students the Common Core State Standards that apply to our grade level. I spend hours planning with my team of three other teachers, as well as on my own. I develop lesson plans and assessments to determine if students have met the required standards. My goal is to send students to fifth grade with the knowledge that they need to be successful.
 
 A key part of student success is communication with parents. I work with parents all year to ensure students are where they need to be. Parent conferences are held twice a year, which is when I share student data and test scores. I also send home report cards twice a year to let parents know whether or not students are meeting standard. I have developed several communication models to keep in contact with parents regarding student behavior, and I try to send positive notes home once a week. 
 
 At the end of each school year, students take part in state testing to determine whether or not they achieved the desired goals. It is a stressful task for both the students and myself. The data from these tests is shared with the state and our local school district to help drive instruction and make improvements in the following year. 
 
 As a teacher, I try to be involved with as many school activities as possible. I lead a service group that consists of about 30 students who are in third and fourth grade. The purpose of this group is to teach children how to become leaders, both within their school and their community. We meet twice a month and take on about eight projects per year. They do things like ring the Salvation Army bell at Christmas, help with the Special Olympics, and do things around the school like pick up garbage and take care of recycling. 
 
 Another program I run is an after school science program. Students learn about engineering and create things as a group. We meet once a week after school. I enjoy watching the students learn to work collaboratively and tackle challenges. 
 
 I am also on our schools leadership team and discipline team. I work with other teachers to review data and determine what kind of extra support our students need. We develop plans and share them with the rest of the school. It is our job to get teachers to buy in so that our programs can be successful. We meet twice a month to discuss program and implementation ideas. Currently, we are working on developing a program that would allow students to act as safety helpers after school. Once it gets going, I will personally be responsible for training students. 
 
 I feel that the most important part of my job aside from teaching them information is loving them. I have students that come to school every day and look forward to being their because it is a safe place. I strive to create an environment where all children can be successful and feel loved. I teach students to believe in themselves and to persevere through tough times.
 
 My classroom is full of students with different levels of skill and it is my job to prepare lessons that will reach all of them. I am currently working on a masters degree in special education so that I have the skills to help every student be successful. I take my work home with me every night and spend hours grading papers and planning long after school hours, but I absolutely love my job and wouldn't trade it for the world.
Overall_Why:
Overall, I find the work that I do extremely meaningful. While a large part of my job is to teach information and facts, I am also responsible to teach values, respect, self-esteem, and much more. Some students come to school to learn, and other students come to school to be loved. I think that the skilled teacher determines what each child needs the most and finds a way to balance so that they receive both knowledge and love. 
 
 For some students I am teaching them the skills that they will need to successful in future grades, but I am also teaching them skills that they will need to be successful in life. For other students, I am showing them that no matter what, there is always a way to achieve success and they always have an adult that believes in them. Some of the stories from my job that I would consider the most meaningful aren't tied to a grade. 
 
 My job is also meaningful because I am held to a high moral standard and am required by law to report any concerns I may have about a child's well being. This means that I must always be aware of what my students are dealing with or going through at home. I could potentially be the one person who notices something is wrong and reports it. I might be the only adult a child has to protect them. 
 
 I don't know where we would be without teachers. If you ask just about anyone, they can think back to a teacher who changed their life. There are several meaningful professions in the world, but I think that being a teacher is right up there at the top of the list.

Work_Story: I am a pediatric physical therapist. I work with children who have physical and mental disabilities. Some common injuries include spinal cord injury, strokes, and cerebral palsy. In my job, I am responsible for evaluating, treating, and discharging patients when they are better. I work closely with doctors and nurses in my line of work. I help children with physical limitations learn to walk and help patients with mental challenges such as autism learn how to be functional with their play skills (ex: learn how to catch a ball, run, ride a bike). Sometimes, I am required to make orthotics to help support children's limbs during an injury. I will make adjustments to orthotics as necessary. I enjoy my job because it is very versatile and I meet new people and learn new skills. My job can be challenging because it is fast paced. It can also be challenging seeing children hurt. There have been times in my career where patient's have passed away due to terminal illness, which is very hard to cope with in my job. Another aspect of my job where I have to work closely with nurses is conducting wound care treatments. On a daily basis, I work roughly twelve to fourteen hours a day. I work Monday through Friday and sometimes can be on call on the weekends. I see between fifteen to twenty patients per day and have to complete daily documentation, which involves writing about what treatment I did with the patient for that day and the plan of how I will continue to work with the patient to get them rehabilitated. Documentation is required as part of my job duties in order to bill insurance companies for the services I render. Another part of my job requires working with parents and caregiver's of the children I serve. i have to educate the families on how to help their child further improve their skills (ex: walking) when they leave the hospital setting. I may have to teach families how to set up specific equipment such as using a special bath chair or how to help their child walk effectively using crutches. I am also responsible for teaching children how to use their mobility devices such as a stander, which is a device that helps children gain strength in their legs. I may teach children how to use a gait trainer, which is a device that helps children learn to walk on their own. In my job, there is a lot of lifting as I have to transfer children from a variety of surfaces (ex: from the bed to their wheel chair). The common ages of children I work with are between the ages of 3-15, although I have seen some children younger and older than those ages. Overall, I enjoy my job because there is a good balance between being challenged and being rewarded knowing that I helped someone get better. I love that I can be creative with my treatment plans and can learn from my wonderful patients and colleagues. I would highly encourage others who are thinking about a different or new career consider physical therapy. It is a rewarding experience knowing that you've helped someone have a better quality of life.
Overall_Why: Yes, I find my job extremely meaningful because I am able to help children have a better quality of life after an injury. My job is rewarding because I get to work with talented professionals in the field of physical therapy as well as the medical field in general. I love making the children I work with smile and realize that they can walk or ride their bike just like their peers. I also enjoy seeing the hope in the parents knowing that their child has been rehabilitated. I enjoy the creative nature of my job, which also makes my job extremely meaningful. I love to think outside the box and my job allows me to do so. I think having the flexibility to be creative makes my job meaningful and makes my treatment plans fun for children. My job is also meaningful because I am not only able to learn from my colleagues, but I am able to be a teacher in my profession. I think I could see myself teaching in a university as I feel that I have a good amount of clinical experiences I could share with those entering into the physical therapy program. When I go home every night and wake up in the mornings, I always feel that I am lucky to be able to be apart of my profession as a physical therapist. Granted, there are aspects of my job that can be extremely stressful, but I fell the reward is much greater than the challenges presented.

Work_Story:
I am an Account Sales Manager for a company that distributes moulding, stairparts and columns. My main customers are a big box retailer, window and door manufactures and many local lumber yards. I also sell product to a few interior design places. I am an outside sales rep and I have a large territory. I actually have 3 subcontractors that work underneath me to help with the basic aspects of the job. I set the subcontractors schedule and I tell them exactly what to do and how I would like for them to speak to. I always have to be prepared to take a phone call from them. The subs give me the opportunity to take care of my larger accounts and cold call on new accounts. I like to write orders for my larger accounts because it is critical they never run out of product. Inventory management is by far the most important thing I do on a daily basis. My bonus is based on my EBIDTA so it is important that I maximize each line of every order I write. 
 I stated earlier my territory is big and I am always looking to expand it. I have a very unique situation in that I am the farthest west rep so I can look to expand without worrying if I am on someone else's turf. The only thing I have to worry about is if the delivery is profitable. 
 
 My job also allows for me to be creative. One of my favorite thing to do is stairpart take-offs. That is when I go to a assisted living facility of expensive house that is being built. I design the staircases and baluster systems. I suggest style and wood. I write everything up and give to the general contractor for approval. My first write up is often rejected and we work on something together to fit within the budget and guidelines of the facility. This part of my job gives me a chance to network with many people and the profit margin is phenomenal. I am often able to also sell them moulding units and columns at a completive price based on the staircase package. 
 
 I also cull product that is unsellable and work close with managers to make sure my product is marketed correctly. We have a great indoor marketing department that can get me basically anything my clients need from in store signage to pamphlets and information for customers to take home. This part of the job always me to use my create side. The culling process is simply removing and writing credits for any product that is deemed unsellable. I have accounts that get full credit and others that get only partial credit. It is based on their tier pricing. 
 
 Another thing I do is product knowledge. Several times a year I will set up in a store and give demonstrations for customers and employees alike. It gives me a chance to talk to the people that are selling my product and the people buying them. This allows me to keep up with current trends, if their is a whole in the market we can fill and I give things away, which people love. At a diy clinic about 10 years ago someone asked me about exterior vinyl moulding. After looking around I had a hard time finding anything in my market. We cut a deal with a company to distribute their product and we now sell this line company wide with a great margin, a quick refill rate and a top seller in several categories.
Overall_Why:
I love my job. It always me to be independent, resourceful and smart. their are defiantly jobs that are more important, such saving lives, but they are just not for me. I went to school to be a nurse but quickly changed my major. I don't feel I am making a huge difference in the world, other than I am able to provide for my family and community. The company I work puts a lot of emphasis on being environmentally friendly and helping out and giving to the community. A large part of our product is wood, but everything we used is from a plantation forest. All of our third party vendors also produce their product from plantations and last year we actually decided to no longer purchase from any mill in Chile based on their questionable practices. We use a gmo ponderosa pine tree that grows 12 foot a year and is used strictly for the milling process. 
 Because we deal in the building industry we also give to habit for humanities. Every employee is encouraged to donate time as well as resources to local projects. I have also personally donated time and materials to help build baseball fields and parks in urban areas. Once a year we are invited to the company headquarters where we work as a team to do something for the community. Our CEO sets up the project and it has included building a day care and rebuilding a church that was damaged by fire.

Work_Story:
I am currently employed as an Engagement Manager in {state}. The company I work for has a contract with a provider of residential fiberoptic services - internet, television and landlines. The Engagement Management teams work as outside sales people for the provider.
  
 I have an assigned territory that covers the southern half of {state} state. Within that territory, I have a portfolio of communities including apartments for rental, condos and town homes. I am responsible for driving sales from those properties along. The role is really broken down into relationship building, event hosting and phone sales with some customer service activities.
 
 In terms of relationship building, I am responsible for visiting my communities on a regular basis. I talk with the leasing and property managers about the services we are offering and any current promotions. It's up to me to get them to promote our services to their residents, existing and new ones. The better the relationships I can build, the more likely they are to refer business to me. I encourage them to share information about people moving into new apartments as well as those who are moving out. Many of them are willing to place my promotional materials in their leasing office and to include it with the move-in packs that they hand out to new residents.
 
 Some communities allow me to flyer their properties or will email blast for me. From time to time, I host events where residents can come and learn more about our services. I've been in this role for about three years so there are some properties where the residents know me - if not by name often by sight.
 
 A lot of my time is spent following up on leads provided by the properties. I work from home where I will call and email people to let them know I'm their rep. I put quotes together for them and, if accepted, complete the sale with them. I then follow through if the have questions regarding their installation or need general support just after they have received service. 
 
 About a year about service has been installed, I reach out to my customers to see how everything is going and if they are moving (many of them only have annual leases). I am paid partly through a fixed salary but also by commission on what I sell.
 
 Overall, there is a lot of freedom in the job. The company owner encourages us to think of this as our own business (to the extent we can do). There are ten of us in our team, scatted across the state, and we all work from home. We have the flexibility of setting our own schedule and coming up with our own plans for lead generation, hosting events and flyering. There are targets set but if we are hitting those, we really can do anything we want (within reason). 
 
 I enjoy not having someone watching me every second of the day or telling me what I have to do. It's definitely a role for someone who is self-motivated and can use their own initiative. There are some admin tasks that need to be completed on a regular basis. These are much less enjoyable but they're not too onerous and usually can be finished pretty quickly. As a team we don't get together too much but are more reliant on phone calls or group texting.
Overall_Why:
Overall, I do not. There are days that I really enjoy. When I'm talking to a property manager and they start pouring out things about their lives and I'm there to offer a supporting shoulder, it feels fairly nice to know that they trust me enough to talk to me that way. Unfortunately, it's not usually happy things.
 
 From time to time customers will also compliment me or phone me up to tell me how professional the technicians were and how much they appreciate the service we provided. I like that but again, it's more likely I hear from customers when their technician didn't show up or something has gone wrong. So I do feel like I'm making a little bit of an impact on people's lives, albeit a fairly superficial one. 
 
 For me, I get more enjoyment out of thinking about things and coming up with new products and services or implementing programs that can drive business efficiency or profitability. I spent many years working in product marketing and did not realize how much that environment drove my work satisfaction. 
 
 I have been here for about three years. After the initial training period and getting to know my territory, I now feel that I am doing much of the same activities day after day. There is always something new to learn but overall, there's a lot of sameness and I feel like my brain is starting to get bored. I need constant change to keep me inspired and motivated. Hitting a sales goal is demanding but not in the mental way that I really love.

Work_Story:
I work as a legal assistant at a small law firm. I enjoy my work as the tasks I perform are well suited for my personality and skill set. The firm where I work is a great place to be and that is equally important to me at work. I assist one main attorney and there are several other attorneys at our office who also have legal assistants. Everyone gets along well and we really do good work for our clients. 
 My favorite thing to do at work is to talk to people who wonder if they have a claim or recourse regarding issues with their employment. My team at the law firm is the employment team and I manage all of the incoming callers and contacts. We also have a computer based system for possible clients to submit information. I enjoy all interactions with these people but it is particularly enjoyable to chat with someone directly and be able to help them. People who have concerns regarding their employment are often very upset and they have needed to discuss issues for some time in many cases. Some people have an urgent issue and they need to be guided through something. It is always nice to help people and know you did something for others, even though it is your paid job. 
 I also enjoy working on our accepted cases. There are a lot of fun tasks I get to perform. I enjoy the process of initial research that goes into deciding to accept a case. Typically after that is done I set up physical and electronic files then I assist the attorney in moving forward. This usually involves research and communications such as letters and demands. Eventually lawsuits are filed and these documents must be perfectly drafted and executed. We have to do a lot of things by strict deadlines and we also have to monitor systems of follow up on actions we take. A lot of calendar maintenance is involved as well as one on one interaction and reminders to attorneys. 
 A fun part of litigation is that of discovery. Documents must be managed and reviewed and shared between parties. Reviewing and cataloging documents is very interesting and rewarding. These exchanges must be done carefully to protect privileged information and review of incoming information from other parties involves careful analysis and strategy for the case. 
 Hearings and trials happen from time to time and attendance at court can be hectic but also enjoyable in moderation. I enjoy being a legal assistant but I have never wanted to be an attorney because the responsibility and skill needed to appear at court is not something that suits me well. It has been fun to assist attorneys in court from time to time but I am glad that is not part of what I do every day.
 In addition to the enjoyable tasks I have at work we also have a very nice work environment. We have an office manager who takes care of the employees and making sure everything is working together nicely. We have good benefits and even small perks like a nice kitchen with good coffee and a lot of food and treats are provided to us. I know this is not something most offices have or do so I really value it and enjoy most everything about my work.
Overall_Why:
I find my work meaningful. I interact with people who are in need of assistance with very important issues. A range of situations result in employment law claims and many require careful and prompt guidance. I appreciate that I work with good people who help me facilitate responses when potential or current clients contact me with a concern. I like to know that I have helped someone by assisting with information or action and sometimes just making notes for the attorney which means I have spent time listening to a concern. I think this helps people so that mean a lot to me. 
 I certainly prefer the work I do to work like being in sales or collections or something where you are contacting people that do not want to be hearing from you or interacting with you. I think that would be a bad feeling. 
 Of course my work is also meaningful as it provides me income. I feel that the income I earn and benefits I receive are very valuable and in that context they are meaningful. Earning a living is meaningful really not only to me but also to my family. 
 I find the relationships I have with my coworkers meaningful as well. I consider the people at work to be my friends and I really think we all feel that way. We take time out to have gatherings and meetings and lunches and that is important and something I know not all places do. I look forward to these things.

Work_Story:
My full time job is working as management in an SEO/internet marketing company. We've been in business since 2007. Since then, I've worked on everything from writing website content to installing Wordpress to setting up full e-commerce stores. 
 
 As of right now, my daily responsibilities are many and varied. I'm very much the lead tech when it comes to anything that has to do with any of our websites (we have probably 30 main sites right now). When issues are identified either with the website's mobile responsiveness or other layout issues, I'm the one in charge of either fixing it or having it fixed if I'm not capable of doing it. I'm capable of a lot, but some things (like Google AMP implementation) I'm just not an expert in. 
 
 I manage hosting accounts and getting rid of any detected instances of malware or viruses that may be present. Sometimes these are easier to find than others. 
 
 I manage our domain registrar accounts, which domains point to where, domain renewals, WhoIs privacy settings, DNS, and nameservers. 
 
 I also manage setting up e-mail addresses and social accounts for any of the sites we have. Sometimes I have to set up the e-mail addresses in the Mozilla Thunderbird or Microsoft Outlook installations of other people that work with us, because they don't know how to do it. 
 
 I often end up handling some of the communications with clients over technical issues, such as login information for their website that we're working on, hosting login information, etc. 
 
 I still do written content from time to time and it often ends up being long, heavily researched content. It's typically in the realm of 1000-5000 words or so. It has to be well written with cited sources. 
 
 I do a lot of research into Google's website technical requirements, as well, and work on getting those implemented on all of our sites, plus our clients' websites. These things have to be implemented correctly or the websites' rankings in Google can suffer or may never increase. 
 
 When we have e-commerce websites to work on, I make sure all of the products & their variations are loaded correctly. The shipping settings are often tedious to set up, as well as the settings for charging for taxes. 
 
 I'm also responsible for working on conversion optimization on e-commerce and affiliate websites that we work on. I use programs like Visual Website Optimizer and ClickMagick to track conversions and make the sites convert better. 
 
 I'm responsible for outsourcing our web design needs, whether it's small changes to the site I'm working on or a complete revamp. Sometimes I end up doing that kind of stuff myself just to save time, but it's not that often that I have to do it myself anymore. I'm typically allowed to outsource most things now. It wasn't that way just a few years ago, though. 
 
 Being perfectly honest about it, I'm the most versatile person in the company. I can do so many things that no other single person that works with us can do, I'm pretty much indispensable at this point.
Overall_Why:
I think that the work that I do for my company is very meaningful, if for no other reason than most people simply aren't capable of doing everything that I do. We help clients take their websites and make the most out of them, whether they're wanting to make money with affiliate marketing, e-commerce, or use it for some other purpose, like spreading information. 
 
 For our client to win, we have to win and so do their customers. Everyone has to benefit from what we do on all ends or it just doesn't work. The results don't last and the client is never happy long term. 
 
 The written work that we do is also valuable to the website's visitors. It's our job to be as informative and truthful with any content that we write as possible. It's also important to lay that content out in a fashion that makes it easy to digest for the reader. This often involves making images or producing videos that help the reader more easily consume the content without getting confused or becoming otherwise dissatisfied and leaving the website. 
 
 The end goal is either a happy customer or a happy website visitor in general. Making people happy and informing them about what they're looking for to the best of our ability is what we do in general, I guess. Spreading positive vibes and truthful information is probably at the core of what we do, as well as honest business tactics and practices. I'm pretty happy with what we do overall.

Work_Story: I have been a full-time community college math teacher at {workplace} in {city}, {state} for the last ten and a half years. Before that, I worked as an adjunct math instructor at two different community colleges in {state}. Teaching is my second career, one that I chose because I wanted to make a difference in people's lives. I can't tell you how many times I have heard people say that they hate math. I've always enjoyed math and been good at it. When my first career didn't really inspire me, I decided that I wanted to help people better understand and appreciate math so that it doesn't prevent them from achieving their dreams. I began by teaching primarily developmental math-- basic arithmetic, algebra, and intermediate algebra-- because that's where I knew I could make a real difference. I could help students who really needed it. Over the years, I have branched out to teach many different math classes such as applied math for business, calculus, statistics, college math, and math for elementary school teachers. Because I have an undergraduate degree in engineering, I have also taught some introductory engineering classes as well. In all of my classes, I help my students master the math skills required for the class and I also make sure they understand how the math they learn applies to the real world and will help them in their lives and future careers. Interacting with and inspiring students is my favorite part of my job definitely not all of it. While I do spend about half of my time in class and working with students outside of class, I spend the other half preparing for classes, grading student work, completing professional growth opportunities, in meetings, and working on campus and district initiatives. Of these, my favorite is spending time learning. I love that my job allows me to continually learn new things. I then use what I learn to continuously improve my class experience for my students. Over the years, I have added many components to my classes to help my students be successful in life as well as in the math or engineering class they are taking with me. I feel my goal is to prepare my students to be productive citizens of the world. My least favorite part of my job is attending meetings and working on campus and district initiatives. Meetings always seems like such a waste of time. It's rare that we have productive meetings. I really wish we would take the time to really decide what meetings are necessary and get rid of the rest of them. And while the goal of many of the campus and district initiatives is to improve student success, I feel like many of them are misguided, not well thought out, or not well implemented in part because we are a very large district (10 colleges) and each college is different from the others. Sometimes I really think our students would be better served if our college was on our own rather than a part of the district. It is also a challenge being in a state that seems to care so little about education and wants to do everything possible to cut funding toward education.
Overall_Why: I do find my work extremely meaningful. I help students every day to pursue their dreams. For those who aren't sure what that dream is, I help them find ways to discover their passion. Some days I get bogged down in the day to day operations of teaching and with the negative students who complain, but there are just as many times that I hear from previous students how much I helped them and how instrumental I was in helping them succeed. I always love hearing from past students no matter what stage of their career or education they are in. One student I had in my college math class came back a year after being in my class to ask me to read a personal statement he wrote for a scholarship. Reading his personal statement made me cry because in it, I found out that I was his inspiration for choosing engineering as a career. He had been bored in his classes during his first semester, the same semester he was in my College Math class. But my sharing my experiences about my engineering background inspired him to look into engineering. While I always believed it to be important to share my background with my students and for them to know me, I never really understood before that how big an impact doing so could have on students. It made me realize that making a personal connection with each of my students is just as important if not more important than teaching them math. Though I may not get through to every student I have, I do my best to make a lasting positive impact on each and every one. And knowing that I do reach some of them makes me really feel like I make a difference and that makes my work meaningful to me.

Work_Story:
I currently work full-time as a stock room employee for an abrasives manufacturer. Sometimes I help in the shipping department as well, but primarily I work in the stock room. My main responsibility is gathering orders and getting them together to be ready for shipping. There are many different parts and keeping track of them all is nearly impossible. We have a large stock room where many orders can be completed by simply going to the shelves, but when parts are not in stock they have to be made. That is my second primary responsibilty, getting the correct parts for orders made. If we do have the part ordered in stock, I have to create an order in another department to either mold or finish the part ordered. 
  Creating orders is a very time consuming part of my job. I have to locate if the part is available in any department either waiting to be mounted or finished. If I locate the part, I bring them to the stockroom and sort out enough for the order. Then I return the rest with the paperwork and they get the parts to shipping when ready. If I do not locate the part It will need to be created in molding. That is a short process of creating paperwork and making copies. 
  Another major part of my day is spent on the phone with the main office doing stock checks and giving order status. When the office calls looking for a part, it could anywhere and stock checks are always done manually. Our customers need accurate estimates and accurate stock checks are important. Some customers have priority and when we have the part they are requesting we will set it aside or expedite it for them. Sometimes we have orders from the Dept. of Defense and they have very time consuming paperwork and labelling process. We also have some other very picky customers and if something is returned, I get most of the blame for being the last in line before shippers. 
  My job also involves quality control and working close together with quality control to make sure the part ordered is correct. I am person gathering items before they go to the shipper and the shippers do not have much part knowledge. Everything needs to be the correct amount and type and have all the correct labels. When we get caught up on orders I also help with packing and shipping orders. We use scales to count and specific bags and labels for each order. Box everything up with bubble wrap, label it and print the shipping sticker. It is a very repetitive job, but the day goes fast. Most of the employees at my job are piecework and get paid by production, not hourly or salary. I am paid hourly and only the best piecework employees and managers make more money. The owner is a female and it is a very small local company. I was lucky to get the job here, I saw a sign driving by and applied The interview process went fast and I was hired and working withing two weeks. The manager who originally hired me no longer works here.
  Keeping the stockroom organised and in correct order is another major responsibility that I have. There are thousands of different parts and keeping them in order is very tough.
Overall_Why:
I find that my work is extremely meaningful to the company as a whole. I am the person ensuring that the customers get the right amount and type of part they ordered. In a company with thousands and thousands of different parts that is rough. Some of these customers will send everything back if one little mistake is made. Returns are very costly and often the parts can not be resold. I believe it is very important I do my job correctly. We almost never have problems lately and everyone is in a much better mood. All the other departments in the building rely on accurate numbers and part types from me in order to do their job and get paid. Orders need to be completed fast so the piecework people have time to make their money for the day. Often they can not start work unless an order has been made and they will not get paid if their is nothing pulled. Also if I do not have enough orders ready to ship some people in the shipping department might get sent home early do to lack of work. 
  So, I would say with the company my work is extremely meaningful and I do not believe other people within the company realize how meaningful the stock person is. It is a really hard job and I am not paid very well at all. I like the company and owner and management is alright. There really is not much room to advance and I will probably never receive much of a raise.

Work_Story:
Right now I am a home care provider for 2 people and I also am there representative payee. My day to day work consists of many things. My first client is also my mother in law. She is disabled and is agoraphobic. She also has schizophrenia. In the morning I wake up and get dressed and head over to her house which isn't that far from me. When I get there I wash her dishes and make her coffee. I cook her breakfast and help her with what ever she needs me to do for the day. It usually consists of me cleaning her house and making sure she takes her medications. I also spend a lot of time just talking to her with my wife by my side. My wife helps her with the shower stuff because I don't feel comfortable doing that. I also take her to her doctor appoints that is a job in itself. She has full blown panic attacks when ever she has to leave her house so it takes a lot of talking and just calming her down.For her I get 4 hours a day. Before I leave I make sure I make her something to eat and a snack for later. Some days are better than others with her because someday she gets triggered by the easiest things. The other day she was getting really hysterical about earthquakes. She was watching YouTube and a video scared her and got her feeling that a earthquake was going strike and kill her. That was a very interesting day to say the least.
  My next client is my mother. She has lupus and rheumatoid arthritis. She isnt very mobile and needs a lot more help getting around. I go to her house after I am done with my mother in law. When I get there my mom is already awake and has already eaten. With her it a little more militant. She likes things really clean. So I clean and wipe down her bathroom, sweep the floor. She doesn't like vacuums so I sweep. I hand wash the dishes and make her bed. It all has to be done in a certain way. So growing up with her is definitely a plus because I know the routine very well. I sort her medication and make sure they are put in the pill compartment. She take a large amount of medications. I make her lunch and take her where she wants to go. She is the type that wants to do things by herself and is too proud to ask for help. So go with her to her appointments and and take her to the stores. I walk her dogs and feed her guinea pig. Sometimes she cant even get out of bed and just starts crying because she says that her body is breaking down.Those days are the worst because my mother is very strong woman, but this sickness is just consuming her. This part of the job is always hardest for me to deal with. This are the days that I have to help her go to the bathroom, I have to help bat her, and have to help feed her. She lashes out and throws things, says hurtful things, and threatens to kill herself. I fully understand that my mother is just scared, and there isnt much she can do alleviate the problem.I closely monitor her medication when she says things like this. Its hard on some days.
 So after I finish with what I have to do with her I take any laundry that needs to be washed and bring it home so I can do them and take them back in the next day. While this job can be frustrating and trying at times, I enjoy spending my day with both of them.
Overall_Why: Yes I find this job extremely meaningful. I'm doing something that not only makes me feel good but is also making a impact on my clients/parents. My mother in law doent really have a connection with people other than her family. She is afraid to go anywhere and when my wife and I show up you can see that she is excited and loves that interaction with us. My mother is really all I ever had growing up so being able to help her when she absolutely needs it, is not only meaningful but I feel like I can now pay back all the love and support that she has giving me through out the years.I dont think anybody else would do a good job helping with my mother. I can see how she would frustrate other people that dont know her so this why I wouldn't want anybody else taking care of her, I like waking up knowing that I am going to make a difference in somebodies day regardless if they are having a bad day or not. This is a thankless job that I dont think everybody is suited for. I get yelled at, called nasty names and sometimes get stuff thrown at me, but Im not gonna take it to heart. One person has a mental illness and the other is scared because her body is failing her. I would be so scared in any of their situations. I truly like what I do and who I do it for.

Work_Story: I am a Land Surveyor. My job responsibilities include surveying land, acquiring right-of-way, obtaining easements and subdividing land. Most of our customers are residential customers who wish to know they're property boundaries so that they can construct a fence or other structure. A large percentage of our business comes from utility companies and electrical engineering companies. They hire us to obtain easements, mark existing easements and locate easements using county records. A smaller percentage of our work comes directly from fence companies who hire us to make sure the fence they are installing is on their customers property so they are not liable to move it. A large portion of my day entails researching records online through county websites. I look for county records pertaining to ownership, legal descriptions, gis data and recorded plats and subdivisions. Once this information is obtained and we have a clear understanding of what it is that we are surveying, we begin the field work. The field work consists of finding existing or known points. These points are usually 1/2" iron rebar placed in the ground at the property corners but can also be railroad ties, iron pipes, aluminum markers, stones, old fence posts and cut crosses or notches. Once we find one corner, we can measure to find other corners and once we find enough corners and verify that they are in the right spot using angular measurements and distances, or at least accept them as such, we can begin our work by using platted or calculated measurements and angles. We use a theodolite (transit) and prism most of the time. A theodolite is basically a telescope on a tripod that we set up over a known point and shoot a distance and angle to another known point. The theodolite is precisely placed vertically over the existing point and is leveled with footscrews and tubular spirit bubbles. Once level, the theodolite is set at a 00 angle and it is focused on a 3 mirror prism which is on a pole on a 2nd known point. The theodolite sends a signal to the prism and measures angle and distance. A graphing calculator is used to input existing points and these points are numbered. Working together with angles and distances, the user can shoot existing points, tell the calculator where he is sitting and what he is looking at and the calculator will calculate the distance and angle to set or recover new points. The calculations are given in right angles and distances and the prism is moved slightly each time until a certain tolerance is reached and if no corner is found, a new one will be set with the surveyors registration number on a plastic cap set on top of the rebar. A wooden lathe is usally driven next to the rebar as a witness point so it can be found later as it is usually driven flush with the ground. Once all the corners are either recovered or set, a drawing is prepared showing all of the field conditions, all improvements and the angular values and distances. This document can be recorded with the county and will supersede any past documents.
Overall_Why: My work is very meaningful to me. It is something that I enjoy doing and something that I am good at. I actually was interested in becoming a surveyor when I was young after witnessing some surveyors working at a local racetrack. They were working on the elevations of the track and the inclines in the corners that would keep the racecars on the track as they banked around the sharp turns. I questioned my dad and grandfather about it and received a quick lesson in surveying and always had it in the back of my mind that i would become a surveyor. I find it especially meaningful that I am working outside and working with numbers, which I love. Every job is different and the pay is good. I believe it does make a difference in the world as it is necessary for construction, roads, improvements and settles property disputes. The history of surveying is actually very interesting as well and the new technology that is being developed keeps me on the cutting edge of computers and new gps technology. All in all, I find the work meaningful and interesting and although I can imagine myself doing pretty much anything outdoors (except manual labor), the tie-in with math and working with computers seals the deal for me. I have worked for the same company for 17 years and my hours and schedule are ideal for my family life and have allowed me to be financially successful.

Work_Story:
I coach rowing at a community-based rowing club. Throughout the year I coach athletes as young as twelve on the middle school team, and as old as 85 on the adult team. There are four main seasons that dictate what my job is like. The winter, spring, summer and fall seasons all have their own goals and challenges. 
 Right now, I am primarily coaching adult recreational rowers. Since it is winter, we spend a lot of our time now indoors doing physical conditioning. Practices last for an hour and a half and we do a combination of cardio and lifting exercises. Planning workouts for this group can be challenging as I have athletes who train everyday and are extremely fit along side athletes who are retired and participating in the sport for fitness and fun. 
 Once the winter season is over the main competition season starts. During this time I will continue to train the adult rowers in the morning, then coach the middle school program in the afternoon. We have a team of around 20 middle schoolers of all genders and backgrounds. I have to drastically alter the workouts, my language, and the goals of each practice to suit a team so young. I am working hard to safely develop their muscles to be able to handle the sport at the varsity level in high school and college. Spring season revolves around races that are two kilometers long. These are called sprint races in rowing and the teams will travel to nearby states to find good competition.
 Over the summer I will run a lot of camps, both to teach new rowers and to develop those in the club already. This will be an opportunity to work on the warm flat water and develop skills that will help come fall racing season. This is when I teach people how to row in the smaller, single racing shells as they can be more prone to flip than the larger boats. The adult rowers will continue to train, and some will compete over the summer months.
 In the fall I will resume with the middle school team again, progressing those who have been on the team for a full season. Fall rowing is based on long-distance racing, so much of the training revolves around building and maintaining a solid cardio base. Hopefully, we have worked hard enough all year to be able to race at full speed for over 20 minutes. 
 When I am not at practice working on physical or technical rowing changes, I complete a lot of administrative tasks. There are always e-mails to send out, travel arrangements to be made, or fundraisers to plan. Our association has a long history of fundraisers, so many of these are already pre-planned from the years before, but still take a considerable amount of time. I also have to makes sure all of our records are kept for insurance and safety purposes.
 The other main aspect of my job is taking care of the equipment. I often have to do maintenance on the boats, or the motor of the boat that I drive when I coach next to them. I love to work with the boats, so I really enjoy this part of my job. It is great to really understand the equipment that you work with. I also do a lot of teaching with my athletes so they can understand how to care for the boats.
Overall_Why:
I do find my work extremely meaningful. For each person who walks into the boathouse, they have started on a journey. It is different for everyone and many do not fully understand what they are about to get themselves into. I strive hard to ensure that each rower is empowered to challenge themselves to become a better self. For some that means they will learn something that will help them persevere in life, and others they will find a gateway to health they may never have known before. 
 
 I work with all ages of athletes, and though my language is often different based on the crew, they all need the same things to be successful. They need the time to learn and experiment without judgement or fear of failure. They need to be pushed to leave their comfort zone when they are ready, and taught how to celebrate the small victories on the way to the big goals. 
 
 What makes my job the most rewarding is how much I learn from my crews, and the community that exists at the boathouse. There are a few hundred people that cycle through the boathouse, and many of them feel like family. They challenge me to want to be better, lead them better, and learn as much as I can. No two days at rowing are the same, even though the sport is literally just doing the same movements over and over again. 
 
 I'm also one of the only people who aren't in the Navy, or fishing, that gets to work in a boat every day! That is very special to me!

Work_Story:
In my line of work, I get great satisfaction in making sure children and adults are safe from online abuse and bullying. I work as a community moderator full time and it has its ups and downs. A community moderator is someone who monitors Facebook posts, comments that are posted on blogs, message boards, websites, chat rooms, etc. As a community moderator, I sometimes will settle disputes, remove posts that are offensive or those that break forum rules and also put posts into their rightful categories. Many jobs that involve being a moderator are posted online and are very easy to find. The difficult part is getting your foot in the door. It took me almost six months of constant emailing and applying to land a job as a moderator. I can tell you that it was worth the work I put in now that I have the job. I have many opportunities to move up in management and the jobs are constantly changing due to social platforms constantly changing. I am also a content moderator. A content moderator serves two primary functions - to protect and promote. While screening, monitoring, and approving content sound like easy tasks, keep in mind that content comes in many forms namely articles, images, videos and audio clips. This is why I have to be on their toes around-the-clock. I ensure that all information and data uploaded by users won't violate intellectual property rights or contain inappropriate content that may scare away users. When it comes to promoting, Content Moderators help build up an entertaining and compelling experience by keeping content focused on the interests of your community. Plus, they also respond to feedback or posts from the community. This job can be very demanding at times as you are keeping an eye on so many things and you don't want to miss important issues. I have been assigned to jobs where I am working for one company and moderating their site and other times I have been assigned a few jobs to moderate a site, forum, and social media posts. It's fun and exhilarating to be in so many places at once and to interact with so many different people. I love being able to do this job from my home. I get to stay home and set my own schedule while working with companies all over the world. I make sure that harmful comments are removed in an immediate fashion so that the forum can keep moving. If you think about it, if you have a blog and it is very busy, then you have to have a set of eyes on the content at all times or things can go south very fast. Most companies reach out to staffing companies of moderators to handle this issue. I have found that you can make the most money by doing this yourself and not go through a staffing company. You need the experience for a while, then you can step out on your own.

When it comes to promoting, Content Moderators help build up an entertaining and compelling experience by keeping content focused on the interests of your community. Plus, they also respond to feedback or posts from the community. This job can be very demanding at times as you are keeping an eye on so many things and you don't want to miss important issues. I have been assigned to jobs where I am working for one company and moderating their site and other times I have been assigned a few jobs to moderate a site, forum, and social media posts. It's fun and exhilarating to be in so many places at once and to interact with so many different people. I love being able to do this job from my home. I get to stay home and set my own schedule while working with companies all over the world. I make sure that harmful comments are removed in an immediate fashion so that the forum can keep moving. If you think about it, if you have a blog and it is very busy, then you have to have a set of eyes on the content at all times or things can go south very fast. Most companies reach out to staffing companies of moderators to handle this issue. I have found that you can make the most money by doing this yourself and not go through a staffing company. You need the experience for a while, then you can step out on your own.

Overall_Why:
It is the responsibility of a moderator to maintain order during discussions and to see to it that all participants behave with courtesy. Forums involve the exchange of ideas whereby participants air their views and sometimes engage in debates about the identified topics. Therefore, there is a possibility of the discussion becoming unruly particularly where the forum topic is controversial. Moderators order the forum by keeping the discussion civil and constructive. They share the rules of the forum, which may prohibit abusive language, derogatory comments, and such other offensive behavior. This makes my job extremely meaningful considering I could keep someone from feeling bullied or harassed.
