Work_Story:
Job title: Quality Assurance Manager.
 My job at work consist training, audit preparation, document development and control as listed below in the following categories, this is done on a daily base, monthly and annually as well.
 At the beginning of a typical day I ensure that the production for that day is verified and that all personal are in formed of the product that is to be produced. To verify that all product to be use are not expired products and that we do have everything that we need for he manufacturing of the product. 
 The documents that will be used for production are updated and this is to be done by viewing the information in the footer of all documents.
 Document control is enforced The methods and responsibility for undertaking monitoring activities, verifying, maintaining and retaining records shall be documented and implemented. All records shall be legible and suitably authorized by those undertaking monitoring activities that demonstrate inspections, analyses and other essential activities have been completed. The methods and responsibility for designing, developing and converting product concepts to commercial realization shall be documented and implemented. The specifications for all raw and packaging
 materials, including, but not limited to ingredients,additives, hazardous chemicals and processing
 aids that impact on finished product safety shall be documented and kept current. Verify compliance with the regulator and that all customer requirements are being met at all times. Products and/or processes of co manufacturers that are considered high risk shall be required to undergo an audit by the supplier or other third party agency to confirm compliance to the regulatory and agreed.arrangements.
 Ensure changes to contractual agreements are approved by both parties and communicated
 to relevant personnel. 
 Review process controls at control points in production to monitor product safety, identify when a process is deviating from set parameters and make corrections to
 keep a process under control. This is done by using the Standard Operating Procedures which are approved by FDA for the safety of food that is produced. Through out the production this is being done by my department employees and myself. 
 Check all raw materials, ingredients, packaging materials, and services that impact on finished product safety shall be supplied by an approved supplier.
 Inspecting of the raw materials upon arrival for character defects and documenting according to supplier. If there are a large number of character defects at this point the supplier is contacted regarding this issue and a note is placed on the excel describing the problem. This information is used as for evaluation of good supplier.
 Reworking operations are supervised by qualified personnel assigned and trained by myself to ensure the highest quality pf a product is being produced and shipped to various customers. 
 Reworked product is clearly identified and traceable according to the information as listed on the rework product document form. This information is reviewed and used by me for quality control purposes and can be accessed if a problem should arise from the production daily code.
 Each batch of reworked product is inspected or analyzed as required before release.
 iv. Inspections and analyses shall conform to the requirements outlined in the standard operating procedures.
Overall_Why:
My work allow me to be a part of the quality of product that is produced, as well as the daily challenges that makes my job enjoyable to me.
 Establish that the Quality (Management) System is achieving the expected results and meeting
 the company regulator requirements, continuing to conform to the Standard, continuing to satisfy the customers' needs and expectations, and functioning in accordance with the established Operating Procedures which is the bible in my line of work.
 Exposing irregularities or defects in the System, identify weaknesses and evaluate possible
 improvements. Reviewing the effectiveness of previous corrective actions, and to review the adequacy and suitability of the management system for current and future operations of the company.
 To review any complaints received, identify the cause and recommend corrective action if required.
 up on reviewing the finding of internal/ external audits and identify any areas of recurring problems or potential improvements reviewing the reports of nonconforming items and trend information to identify possible improvements.
 Conducting Internal audits of the Quality System are the functional concerned is adhering to the company regulatory as it is written as a guide for procedures. Writing a comprehensive Audit Program that compiled at least a year in advance however, should particular needs be identified, and the frequency of audit may be increased at the discretion of the Quality Assurance Manager.
 Audits are undertaken by auditors who are trained in auditing and not directly responsible for the
 functions being audited within that Company. However non conformance observed is brought to the attention of the Quality Assurance Manager, and is recorded, documented and subject to timely
 corrective action to ensure full rectification. This is very enjoyable for me because of the standards which I oversee.

Work_Story:
I currently work as a medical appeals writer for an independent LLC company. I found this job opportunity through chance through word of mouth, as my employer is the fiance of a friend of a friend. My employer is a very nice gentleman, who is a lawyer, in another state. I contacted him via email and text, had a phone interview with him, and signed confidentiality documents to access the secure medical server. Although the pay for work as a medical appeals writer is minimal, it looks good on a job resume, and is helping to prepare me for my long term goal of return to work as a physician. As a point of relevant information, I have a medical degree, but no medical license, and am working towards taking steps to rectify this and return to clinical practice.
 
 Regarding the medical appeals work, I enjoy the ability to set my own hours and do the work at my own pace. I am essentially on the "side" of the hospital and am arguing that an insurance company needs to pay for a patient's hospital visit. The insurance company hires someone like myself on their side to argue why the visit was NOT medically necessary. An independent reviewer then reads both papers and decides whether the hospital or the insurance company is responsible for paying for that patient's medical bills.
 
 To give more detail, y job responsibilities include reviewing documentation from one hospital visit, summarizing the patient's presentation, the clinical rationale, and the actions taken by medical staff, and then arguing why the visit was medically necessary, with supportive documentation from nationally accepted standards for clinical admission and well-respected clinical literature. Although an important resource if provided by my employer (Milliman Care Guidelines for admission- a highly expensive and secretive copywrited set of legal codes to justify medical admission), a lot of supportive documentation is done independently by myself, by searching for clinical management guidelines in Google Search from nationally respected organizations such as the American Heart Association (AHA) or National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI) to prove medically necessity for admission.
 
 Although I am an incredibly slow worker, I am very thorough, and enjoy that academic challenge of fighting these cases. I intended to work more cases a week (I currently average 2-3 and wanted to increase to 6-7), but I find the work can be draining, especially when I know there was not a valid reason for admission and it is my job to try to successfully argue to the contrary.
 
 So far, after four months of employment, my employer seems happy with my work. But I admit, I have not gone out of my way to seek feedback to avoid rocking the boat. My appeals are getting shorter (3-4 pages of text, not counting the supportive legal documentation, instead of the 5-6 pages of text they originally were) which I see as a good thing, as I tend to be unnecessarily verbose. 
 
 The best part of my job is getting a text from my boss that I won a case. I have yet to tally my wins and losses (I think because I'm scared of the outcome) but I estimate it's about 50% of the time. I receive a small bonus when I win a case, which is sufficient motivation to provide my best work. The worst part of my job is clearly arguing a case that I know will lose and feeling like a schmuck. 
 
 Although there are presumably other individuals that work for this company, it is really quite an independent profession and I communicate only with my boss, accessing these documents from within a secure server and uploading the finished product to this secure server. I wish I had known about this job 10 years ago because I enjoy it and anticipate continuing it for several more months to years.
Overall_Why: I find my work meaningful for selfish reasons- because it is affording me skills of medical research and critical thinking that will prove useful when I return to work as a physician. In addition, while there is some meaningfulness to completing medical cases that "deserve" to be covered, I find some cases that I know should "lose" to be draining and not meaningful work. For instance, a case that I found meaningful was a young African American child presenting with sickle cell crisis and hospitalized for three days for fluids, antibiotics, and breathing treatment. This case unquestionably deserves hospital admission, per clinical guidelines, and it is absurd that the insurance company denied it. On the other hand, a COPD patient who had no signs of clinical distress but felt poorly, who was admitted for a one week exacerbation with no real medically necessary treatment (as steroids, fluids, and bronchodilators could have been provided in an outpatient setting) truly did not meet medical necessity. I feel poorly arguing this case because I feel that I am selling my values for the sake of a dollar. Still, it is my job to ague even "bad" cases, so I do find some meaning in doing the best possible work in a bad situation, knowing that no one else could argue the "bad" case as well as myself. I am unsure if the outcome of the cases affects the amount of money that the hospitalized individual has to pay. I think it must, as I have certainly gotten hospital claim denials and have had to pay more money than originally intended. If that is the case, it would ad more meaning to my work, as there is more invested in the outcome of the case.

Work_Story: I work for a Medicaid and Obama Care HMO company. We are located in {city}, {state} and abide by state and federal medicaid and marketplace guidelines. Our job as an HMO is to not only provide insurance for low income and disabled people but also to help manage them as far as setting up appointments for them and gathering resources. We are affiliated with the local Children's Hospital so we are considered a non profit organization. My job title is lead pre authorization specialist. My role is to received prior authorization requests from facilities and doctors office to see if it meets for medical necessity. This means that we review cases such as surgeries and diagnostic tests to make sure that it is actually needed. For example, say a doctor belives that a person would be a good candidate for gastric bypass surgery. Our job is to review the doctors reasoning and medical documentation to figure out why we should cover this.Sometimes a procedure is just not a covered benefit all together and we need to go by state and federal guidelines.My role requires me to be in a lot of contact with providers. They usually have a lot of questions such as is authorization required and is this a covered benefit. We also need to watch a members eligibilty as with Medicaid it can literally change HMO's month to month. Also, high dollar members usually end up becoming fulled managed by the state. Things can change daily in my business so it is very important to pay close attention. I need to be very careful in processing these authorizations as the only way we are paid as a company is by making sure that we are following the laws to a tee and making reach to members. If we do good, the state pays us a nice chunk of money and if we fail we do not receive any of those bonuses. It is strange to deal with providers at times because I am not in a customer service role. My job does not require me to be nice to these people are actually prefers that I am stern. It is a business where we are considered the bad guys. Although I an sympathize with providers and with patients, a big part of my goal in my career is saving money the best way that I can. The good thing is, we do make up for it somewhat by going out in the community and bringing people together. Often times we will work food and health drives and get to meet the community that we support. It's always a great thing to interact with the people that we are trying to help and putting a name with the faces. All and all it is a fun and exciting business to work for but it can be scary. My job is at risk if there are any Medicaid or Obamacare cuts and I could always end up landing in the same spot that these members are. Nothing in this business is for sure and that makes it very scary. It pays great and it truly is a good job. It's stressful though. Often times I exhausted and nervous at the end of the day. I don't feel secure and that is probably one of the worst feelings in the world. In the end anything can happen with any job. Just need to keep hope!
Overall_Why: In some ways I really do.I feel like we give members and people in our communities a lot of hope and love and guidance. I feel like people depend on us and appreciate us. In certain ways I do not feel like that. Everyone always seems to have it out for themselves and working in the non profit insurance sector is no different. Everything is surrounded with the dollar. I feel like sometimes we don't allow certain members to get things not only because they may not need it but because it could cost too much money as well. Usually, we take the initiative by trying to get them involved in other programs rather than going with the more expensive surgery. We also don't take weird and strange cases into account. If something is not covered it is just simply not covered no if ands or buts. I don't think it's fair to have the state completely in charge of what we should pay or not. That I don't think is a great thing. Then again a lot of these people have no bills and only a three dollar copay so they should feel pretty lucky and blessed. I supposed just like any other company there are always great things and there are always negative things. All and all I think that we do a lot more good than harm and I am proud to work where I do. Without us there would be even less resources so I try to take that into consideration.

Work_Story: Work is mostly constant but some days are different than others. The biggest difference that my work experiences is the change of seasons. The weather is so different between summer and winter and it has such a huge impact on our sales. It's really bad during the winter, especially when it snows. Some days we don't even get any sales, maybe one or two customers. I guess we make up for it in the summer time. In the summer, there are just constant customers. It's like they don't stop coming and the check out lines are always nonstop. We have a meeting at the beginning of the day before the store opens and we basically talk about our projected profits and what is expected and at the end of the day our sales numbers are announced over the intercom. My favorite part of my job is the customers. I have constant, returning customers and they always love to see me working because I am very helpful and nice to them unlike some of my other coworkers. So many people get away with so much crap at my work. There's a lot of drama there to be honest but it's a paycheck for me. I do my job, and that's just it. I'm supposed to work the register, and I do, but sometimes other people don't do their jobs so I have to make up for my lazy coworkers. So I'll shut off my light and leave to help someone. Other times I do have to leave to do other duties such as, bringing in carts, moving heavier things that others cannot, taking tests for work related things, sometimes I have to run to the back and grab an order for a customer. I help out in other departments even when I'm not asked to. I'm usually all over the store, always asking if anyone needs any extra help or sometimes just to get off the register if it's slow and obviously I check to make sure it's okay first. I have a couple of coworkers that I've made friends with but not very many compared to the amount of people that work there. One thing I like to do is play this game, work related (challenges between stores in the area) and you have the opportunity to win prizes and score for your score and at the end of the season whoever has the most points wins a bigger prize for the entire store. I really enjoy when the summer season comes around and we get all these new workers, so it kind of helps with the extra stress of it being so busy. I recently lost my manager and we picked up a new one and she is seriously the best thing that has ever happened to the store. She does the jobs of the higher ups that don't know what they're doing. It's a shame but if she wasn't there, the store would go down the drain. In my opinion, I think she's raised sales because she's capable of doing things that others cannot and will not. This has impacted all of us because our bonuses have gone up significantly. I like that we also get raises every six months. It's not much usually but it's something and our bonuses make up for it. I've learned a lot since starting at the company. It's a hardware franchise so I've been able to benefit in areas of my life that does include home improvement skills and knowledge of tools etc.
Overall_Why: I find it meaningful to an extent. Within myself, for myself, it's meaningful because it's a prideful job because I do the best that I can do. I put in 110% each and every day. As far as it being meaningful to others, I would hope so because I believe I am one of the hardest workers in the store and without me, I feel like there would be some struggles within. A lot of people aren't as knowledgeable as me, men and women, so a lot of people come to me or call me when they have a customer that they can't provide any answers to. It's a good feelings for me knowing that I am needed and that I can be a useful tool to others, not only customers but coworkers as well. I find the work I do meaningful. I do my job to the best of my ability and that's all there is to it. I'm not out to satisfy anyone else, the satisfaction comes from me knowing that I do all that I could do to have the best results.That's what makes it meaningful to me. The prideful feeling that I get from doing my job, knowing that I got to help someone make decisions about huge decisions they are making for home improvement, knowing that I hit a sales goal within my register, knowing that I did the best that day. It's not a physical advantage or meaning, it's more of an emotional sense of empowerment.

Work_Story: I work as a security guard for a large gas company. The company I work for imports/exports/and stores Liquid Nitrogen or LNG. My job is to ensure that the facility stays secure at all times. The facility sits on the Gulf of Mexico and is therefore located in a prime area for easy access. The facility has a ton of different security features. One of the most prominent is the wall that surrounds the whole facility. There are also numerous offices located throughout the facility. In order to access the facility, there is a one-way road that leads to it. It is the only road that comes in or goes out. One of the biggest duties is ensuring that only authorized personnel have access to the plant. The way this is done is there is an entry point that looks almost like a military bases checkpoint. When someone arrives at the checkpoint, they are checked to ensure that they have the proper credentials for access to the plant. After verifying that they have the correct documentation, they are allowed access to the plant. Some people cannot enter the plant by themselves. They have to be escorted because they do not have the proper clearance to enter the facility. There are always 2-3 guards on duty and one who is a supervisor. I am the shift supervisor for my shift. The shift supervisor is responsible for escorting the people throughout the facility. Another duty of the supervisor is to train personnel. On a daily basis, I have to sit down with my subordinates and train on a topic. These topics range from terrorist topics to technological topics. Since the facilities security equipment is very technical, all guards have to be completely trained on the use of the systems. Another unique aspect of our job is that we have to be trained in firefighting and medical assistance for the entire plant. Not only are we guards, we are also responsible for any fires or medical emergencies that may take place while on shift. One of the huge things with protecting a high-target facility like the one I work for is that anyone who wishes to do harm can easily access the plant with proper knowledge. My job as a supervisor also extends to patrolling the facility and ensuring that no unauthorized individuals are on the property. The biggest drawback to this is the we are not armed. If something was to ever happen, it would not be a good day for us. Of course, if terrorists want to cause harm they will always have a plan regardless of what we are doing to protect the plant. As a shift supervisor, another duty of mine to fill out all shift paperwork and to make sure there are no errors within any of the paperwork. Not only do I check all paperwork, I have to have a subordinate check it for errors before submitting it as well. One of the last things I have to do before my shift is over is to give a shift briefing to the oncoming shift. This includes everything that happened throughout my shift and anything that is pertinent. Before leaving my post, I have to send an email with how many people entered and exited the facility during my shift. After everything is completely check and confirmed, I can leave for the day.
Overall_Why: I find my work meaningful in certain ways. It is mostly meaningful to me when thinking about what I am actually doing as my job. In the world today, there is always a threat of attacks taking place. For me, I know that I have a responsibility to protect something that is meaningful. Natural gas is one of the most useful sources we have today for energy. Knowing that I am a part of something that is making a difference is meaningful to me. Something else that I find meaningful is the relationships that I have formed with the people I work with. We have to take care of one another and make sure that we keep ourselves protected and also that we are knowledgeable. Acquiring the skills for my job has been a fun experience, but at the same time, I know that I am responsible for not only my life but others lives as well. If something was to happen to the facility that I am set to monitor and protect, it could cause many casualties within the local area. If there was an attack on the plant, it would cause many deaths in the city. This is mainly due to the pipeline and the connection to other facilities within the area. There could be a huge catastrophe if something was to happen to the pipeline and knowing that I have the responsibility to ensure that no wrongdoing is taking place around those pipelines is meaningful to me. I find my work to be fun and boring at times, but in the end I am protecting my community and the people I care about most.

Work_Story: I work for and EDI company that is worldwide out of {city}, {state}. I have been with the company for fifteen years and have worked my way up the company ladder. I started out working third shift monitoring the files being processed and if there was a problem, escalate it to my manager. After doing that for a year I was promoted to Network Operator and moved to first shift. My job was to answer incoming phone calls from customers and help them with their communication problems and/or help them to determine why their files were not processing. After proving myself, I was promoted to Network Analyst and started learning about doing system upgrades and installing new proprietary software.I am very proud of my accomplishments with learning around unix servers and installing and back out software without having a computer science degree. All of my training has been on the job training. I am on call 24 x 7 because our network never stops. So when most people go home from work, I have to keep my cell phone on in case there is a problem. I have recently gotten involved in Quality Control of new software because of my excellent troubleshooting skills. I meet with the director of programming and talk about the problems some of our customer experience and offer solutions that will fix the problem. I then, in turn, consult with the programmers with my vision and they in turn write new code to solve or enhance the code. After they have written the new program, I install it on my test Quality Control system and basically try to break it. Also, by installing it on the test system, it give me an idea of what it will be like to install it on the production system because we don't want the production system to be down for any length of time. We typically do new software installations on the weekend in the wee hours of the morning around 1AM or 2AM but then we also send a mass email out to our customers giving them a heads up that we will be down for a short period of time. Another part of my job is to be second level support to our first level support team. So if they have a problem they can't resolve, they will bring it to my department and one of the Analyst will either assist the first level employee with finding a resolution or we will get on a conference phone call with the customer to try and troubleshoot the problem. If the problem cannot be resolved, we try to give them a work around until we can explore what other options we could use to resolve their problem. As with any software related company, there are always times when the system goes down either by human error or a software or network related problem. When this happens, our team goes into "Root Cause" analysis mode until we can figure out how the problem happened and if there is a way to keep the problem from happening again. One of my best assets is my intuition. I always make it a habit of randomly searching the system to ensure that everything is running ok. I have found many of problems that way and corrected them before they became "big" problems.
Overall_Why: Yes, I feel that my work is extremely meaningful. I affectionately call myself radar because I sometimes know when sometime just doesn't seem right and I fix the problem before it manifests itself into something that could get out of control. I feel like I am an important part of my team and company and find it very satisfying to be appreciated by my co-workers and manager. Since I have been with the company for so long, I have a better understanding then most employees on how everything works and how everything is interwoven. It is very satisfying when a new employee comes to me and asks my opinion or my help in resolving a problem. If we can't resolve that problem, I put on my thinking cap and create a temporary workaround to the problem or ask the customer to handle the transaction a little differently. The customer is not always happy about that but then I investigate into whether other customers are having this problem and if so, talk to our programming director on a possible software resolution to the problem. I have a great relationship with my competition counterparts and I will actually call them and we will exchange our challenges and resolutions for problems. This amazes my manager that I have that kind of relation with our competition and of course, my company values that. The fact that I am respected and included in team management meetings makes be proud and makes this job very meaningful.

Work_Story:
I work as the office manager for a non-profit that provides services to refugee families. I both do direct client service, in addition to general non-profit management and administrative tasks. Occasionally, I also contribute to the organizations social media accounts. 
 On the services side, the majority of my day involves helping clients that come to our office, the vast majority of whom have limited English language abilities. They come with mail that needs to be translated, forms that need to be filled out, or appointments that need to be taken. Most of the assistance that they need revolves around the day-to-day tasks of running a family within a fairly new, bureaucratic society. As overwhelming as it is for the ordinary adult to navigate the automated menu options of their insurance company, their frustration is even bigger due to their language and cultural abilities. In short, a significant part of my day is spent on hold. Occasionally, I also get to work on more enrichment and development oriented programing, such as English conversation groups, hiking sessions for teenage girls, or computer coding for beginners. While all the work is important to me, it??s these kinds of activities that keep me at my job. 
 In terms of supporting the organization itself, my day to day tasks are much more varied. Sometimes I have to spend time away from the clients to work on a grant proposal, or otherwise look for sources of funding. (In a way, my work funds my own position.) I might also spend time attending community meetings representing my organization. As we are fairly new, we are still building alliances and both advertising ourselves as a resource, and seeking out resources we can refer to our clients. I very rarely can attend meetings that happen during office hours, since we are short-staffed and lacking in volunteers, but I will often make meetings with individuals for coffee or lunch and brainstorm with them about ways we can collaborate. I also contribute to our social media presence. Occasionally, I??ll post updates about the office to the community in multiple languages, or ask for more volunteers. Rarely, I??ll post asking for people to donate or contribute towards the specific needs of individual families, but because we have no storage space or any other way of sorting or storing donations, we can??t collect donations on a routine basis. 
 My time is not spent evenly between client services and organizational capacity building. I spend about 75% of my time with clients, and 25% of my time with other tasks. I am the only paid employee. The two co-founders also contribute daily to the office, but because they still have other full-time jobs, they are often multi-tasking or working long hours. There are also a regular stream of volunteers handling less sensitive client needs and other administrative tasks. At any given time, there are between 2 ?? 5 people in the office. It is a fairly small space; I sit at the front desk and there are 4 other stations to help clients at. Overall, everyone gets along well and things go smoothly. Everyone is meant to be independent and take the initiative, although easing in new volunteers is always a difficult process. Ideally, the office expand both physically and in human resources, and we can expand the range of services we provide. Ideally, I??d like my role to evolve away from administration more towards program development, but as it stands, it is an excellent, fulfilling place to work.
Overall_Why:
Overall, I find my work incredibly meaningful. I literally get to spend my day in the service of others, helping them feel supported and empowered in an environment that can be hostile to refugees and immigrants. It is not an ordinary office job; I'm not working for a doctor or for an insurance company. The service aspect adds a deep element of meaning and fulfillment. 
 
 At the same time, it is a challenging and engaging position, which also adds a lot of meaning. I speak multiple language, and I am pushed in my knowledge and ability and forced to practice languages I might not otherwise get to speak on a daily basis. I also am forced to learn about systems and resources I wouldn't otherwise be familiar with. For example, when I was first hired, I went through an in-depth training related to how social benefits work, such as welfare or subsidized housing, so that I could answer people's questions and help them fill out applications. I'd never really tried to navigate these systems before, and even after the trainings, I had to push myself to research as much as possible to learn about them. 
 
 Perhaps the only aspect that makes this job a little less meaningful than it could be is the fact that we can't do that much program development yet. We don't have the funding or the staff. However, I studied psychology and am learning about how to empower children and families, and I want to provide not just daily services that satisfy their immediate needs, but also activities that will lift these families up in the long term.

Work_Story: I'm a home healthcare Nurse. I currently provide daily medical care for between 6-8 disabled or elderly adults in their own homes. I see each of my patients for approximately 45minutes to 1 hour per day 5 days a week from Monday thru Friday. When I first arrive at each patients' home I ask if them if they have any specific health concerns they need me to be aware of. If they do I then handle those tasks. If they don't I am then able to move into my routine medical checks. I check and document their heart rate,temperature, blood pressure and in the case of diabetics their blood glucose levels. All of this has to be documented daily in my logs. After the daily checks I'm then able to move into the more complex medical issues. Changing or cleaning any catheters or bandages they may have. Performing an overall full body check of their skin to ensure they aren't getting any bed sores or have any injuries they're unaware of. Next I check and dispense any medications they may require. Finally I'm normally able to spend 15 minutes or so just talking to them. Finding out about their day and their families and friends or any other concerns or needs that they may have.Hours vary depending on patients' needs, especially those who need around-the-clock care. Most home health aides work a regular 40-hour work week, but some work weekends, nights and holidays. This job requires strength, both physically and emotionally. Work days consist of long hours on your feet and possible heavy lifting, as well as the emotional stress of working with the sick and elderly. Aides should take every measure to prevent the transmission of minor infections and major diseases, such as hepatitis.This profession may have appeared on an episode of "Dirty Jobs " because aides are usually required to empty bed pans and change soiled bed sheets. Patients may also be unpleasant, disoriented or irritable at times. However, even with the demanding nature of this job, it is extremely rewarding--many aides gain great satisfaction in knowing they have truly helped those in need.Home health aides may go to the same patient's home for months or even years, but most aides work with several different patients, sometimes on the same day. They generally work alone with periodic visits from their supervisor, so you need to be OK with working alone with the patient. Aides are given detailed instructions on how to care for each individual and are expected to follow them accordingly. They are responsible for getting to patients' homes on their own and may spend much of their day traveling, so you might want to trade your pick-up for a hybrid if you're getting into this line of work. In many cases, you don't need a college degree or a high school diploma hanging on your wall for a job as a nursing, psychiatric or home health aide. However, hospitals may require you to have training and experience. Nursing care facilities often require workers without experience to complete at least 75 hours of training and pass an exam within four months of getting hired.
Overall_Why: I find my work to be extremely meaningful! I impact the lives of my patients and their families on a daily basis. Some of my patients which I see on a daily basis, may be patients for months or years. I build a special bond with each of them and they become an important part of my life. After time goes by with some of them we almost stop being a nurse and a patient and it becomes more of a friend type relationship where we feel open enough with each other to share our thoughts and feelings about a wide variety of subjects. I've learned so much about life from my patients that I can never repay them or thank them enough for the wisdom they have imparted to me over the years. I really can't think of another career field that I would consider more meaningful than the work I currently do. Even though I'll never get rich from my salary I already feel wealthy from all the time and emotions I've invested in each of my patients. Hearing their life stories and learning about everything they've experienced in their lifetimes is the best possible reward for my time and dedication. I would recommend this career field to anyone who wants to help people and feel a sense of pride and fulfillment in their job. It's truly more than a job it's a way of life. If you want to help people and feel good doing it I would consider a job as a home healthcare nurse.

Work_Story: I currently work at Medieval Times in {city}, {state}. It is one of nine castles among the United States and Canada that specializes in entertainment. The castle employs various forms of work within it, each divided into different departments. There are the knights and squires, the show cast, the food servers, the cooks, the photo department, the marketing team, the bar, the office workers, the gift shop, the event staff or security, the maintenance crew, the sound and lighting team, the stables workers, the cash control team, and the wardrobe staff. Each department works independently as well as intertwined with each other. For example, the knights and squires work closely with the stable hands to help maintain the horses, but they also work with the show cast who are putting on the performance. Essentially, the entire premise of the business is to put on a dinner and show to entertain guests. All of the other departments work towards making money for the corporation as well as adding to the guest experience. I personally work within the gift shop, which is the retail section of the castle. We sell souvenirs and weapons to guests to elevate their experience within the castle. Some of the items we carry include figurines and statues, light-up toys, journals, jewelry, glassware, paperweights, and magnets, as well as a variety of swords, daggers, and shields. We also offer services to help recognize guests for an additional price, including having the actors say what a family is celebrating during the show and having guests participate in a knighting ceremony. During a knighting ceremony, the reining monarch will knight someone (anyone of any age) and explain what they are celebrating by coming to the castle. The guest will also be given an item of their choosing and a booklet that contains a scroll explaining what they are celebrating as well as a picture of their knighting. Lastly, they will also receive a special announcement during the show. This event (knighting) takes place before and after the show and is a great way to showcase a special time in guests' lives. During this, we also commonly receive engagement requests, and the show cast will help make that proposal extra special. Every now and then, the castle is rented out for private affairs such as for large groups or weddings. Ultimately, the castle operates during specific days with shows ranging from one to three times a day. Every 5 or so years, the entire script and show changes as well in order to keep our guests coming back for fun. In the gift shop department, it is your basic retail, but we also sell many weapons. We sell full-length swords, not sharpened, as well as daggers, letter openers, shields, and other interesting items. Our guests must be eighteen years or older in order to purchase that merchandise. I personally am a team lead within the gift shop, so my specific tasks revolve around managing the team, checking in shipments and distributing merchandise, customer service, and guest resolutions.
Overall_Why: I find my work meaningful in the people I work with as well as the experience I am able to give guests. It always brightens my day when I see families or individuals having a fantastic time, especially because of something I was able to do. I especially love the team I work with. Although this work is like any other in retail, and as such I do not find it very meaningful in the specific line of work, I do find meaning with the relationships and dynamic within my department. The group of people are supportive and entirely wonderful, making work very enjoyable. Other than the warm fuzzy feeling I receive while helping people, I do not find this job to be entirely meaningful. It is retail with the goal of selling as much as you can. Coming from a lower socioeconomic status than many around me, I always feel upset watching kids who really want a toy become discouraged when their parents say no, especially since it is expensive just to come to the show. While the experience is worth the money, I understand that it is a struggle sometimes just to come up with that money. I also do not find it very meaningful because, other than making someone's day, we do not do anything profound to better the world. I also see a lot of people, well between 1,000 and 4,000 people a day, which tends to show me the worse side of humanity in general.

Work_Story:
My current job contrasts greatly with my first job. When I was 18, I went to university for my undergrad in engineering. Since my parents were unwilling to pay for all of my college tuition, I decided to work part-time as a sales representative at a sales store. However, I had never worked in retail before and was truly shocked by the experience. For the first few months, because of the fact that I was desperate, I decided to endure the "pain" for the sake of the money. Towards the end of my freshmen year, however, I could not handle the stress from school, stress from family, and the various problems I had to deal with at work. Not only was I being over-worked, my supervisor and his boss was unfair, and unwilling to work with me or any of my co-workers. I was forced to stay extra hours even after closing, for instance. The work environment was very toxic in general, and very unhealthy for my personal life. After 6 months, I had enough and decided to give in my 2 week notice. My only regret is that I didn't make this decision sooner. This job was the opposite of the job I would later get at a company that I currently work for. 
 I've been in the same group of 4-5 people ever since I started to intern with the company I'm working for now. I started the internship my junior year of undergrad, with a few friends who were in the same degree I was in (mechanical engineering). After graduating, we were offered a job to work as mechanical engineers. We are currently working for a healthcare company. We do various projects/tasks, but are currently working on maintaining quality standards for a certain product after its initial product launch. I love my work environment and every aspect of it. My co-workers are great, my supervisors and superiors are understanding, and lenient. I have good pay and good hours as well as great benefits. However, I do run into problems every now and then. For instance, we had a project that we had been working on for a few weeks. There were a few details that my co-worker did not fully complete/understand. As a result, he was stuck on his part. The deadline was fast approaching, and since I had finished my parts ahead of time, I decided to help him out for the sake of everyone. However, after I did so, he responded saying that he did not actually need my help. He stated that he had already asked someone else for help and could've easily finished it himself. He did not even offer gratitude for the time I put into the project on his behalf. As a result, I was a bit angry and refused to ever help him out again. 
 Besides this one co-worker, however, I have an amazing time at work and work very efficiently. I look forward to going to work every morning and I put in a lot of effort to make sure I do my job. I get to take breaks as I desire, and can leave early if I did what I was supposed to do for a specific day. The salary I get allows me to live a comfortable life while also being able to save for retirement. I go out atleast once a week with my co-workers and have met some of my closest friends at my workplace. At this company, I can also climb the ??ladder?? very easily, and see myself getting bonuses and promotions in the near future.
Overall_Why: I find my work very meaningful. Some of my main values as a person is to be a good person, and to help others. The work that I do helps me accomplish and carry out both of these values. I treat everyone at work really well, and go out of my way to help my co-workers, whether it is related to work or not. I will put in extra effort if one of my co-workers can??t put in time due to unforeseen circumstances. My co-workers will also do the same for me. Additionally, since I work at a healthcare company, the work that I do directly impacts thousands of Americans. If I do my job well, then I know for a fact that I am helping thousands of Americans who have problems with their health. While the impact isn??t as direct as, say, a doctor, it is still very effective and I can clearly see how the work I do benefits other people. Additionally, because of the high salary that I get that allows me to live a comfortable life, I can help others in need by donating my own money. I frequently donate to various charities that I have researched. Helping others allows me to be a better person, therefore, I try to help others whenever I can. Additionally, I??m very passionate about the work that I do. I??ve wanted to be in this line of work ever since high school. As a result, I enjoy every day at work and look forward to my doing and completing my tasks.

Work_Story: My full time job is as a Strength and Conditioning Coach. My job is to design and implement strength and conditioning programs for various types of people. I work with a wide demographic when it comes to clients. Morning and afternoons I mostly work with athletes. My down time consist of training stay at home female clients and male and female clients that are retired. In the morning I start at usually 5:00 am. I begin my day by training the morning rush. I train anyone that wants to train before they start either school or work. I own my own business and employee 2 other people. We can take a total of 9 people at once. The morning rush crowd is always the most energetic crowd believe it or not. They are always prepared and happy and actually enjoy being at my facility. We start our day off by running all our clients through a dynamic and static warm up. These drills are usually dictated by how that specific client is training that day. If they are doing a lower body program the warmup will mostly consist of movements that warm their legs and core temperature up. If it is an upper body day the client will spend time doing upper body drills followed by increasing the core temperature. After about 10 to 15 minutes the clients should be fairly warm and ready to go. We then start into their individualized program. Each client has their very own workout geared to their specific goals for themselves. For a client doing a lower body intensive day they would start off with a big movement such as a squat or deadlift. After completing the desired amount of sets and reps they would move on to accessory work. Accessory work consist of movements that are still beneficial but wont be as taxing (normally) as a squat or deadlift. These movements consist of multi joint movements still such as various lunges, various squats, various hip hinge patterns (Romanian Deadlift, Good Mornings, Bridges, Hip thrusts, etc), and even machine exercises like the leg extension and leg curl. That is basically what an lower body program looks like for most people. These programs are all different as I stated before. If someone has an injury we work by rehabbing that particular injury and also by working around the injury as much as possible. That's basically how all my programs are designed for everyone I train. This is a ver small glance into how a lower body program may be performed. My other duties at work consist of cleaning, inventory, and payroll. These things are usually pretty straightforward. Cleaning really can stink in the winter because of all the wet and salty shoes that travel through my facility. Inventory is quite simple because we never need anything different. We usually just need cleaning supplies and various things such as toilet paper and paper towels. Payroll is also very straight forward because my employees work the same hours each and every week so it's always very consistent when it comes to time and pay. I usually do a light cleaning at the end of the day. On the weekend I do a full scrub down of the entire facility. Payroll is performed every 2 weeks. Inventory done at the end of the month every month.
Overall_Why: I find my current career to be highly meaningful. Everyday I wake up and go to work I'm there to make my clients better in some way. My clients pay me to meet a specific goal they set at the beginning of their journey. These goals always range between a few things. Most of my clients are there to increase their athletic performance. WE do this by getting them stronger and faster in their particular sport and position. I've never failed at making an athlete increase their performance in one way. Athletes are usually the easiest client to train because they are very self motivated and want to get better. They wake up everyday wanting to be the best that they can be. Another goal of a lot of my clients is fat loss. These clients are typically older in age. Usually 30+ years or older. These clients are hit or miss and sometimes struggle with achieving their overall goal. Sometimes the work gets too hard and they struggle to find a balance of work, family, and hobbies. I always give my 100 percent effort when training clients and I expect them to do the same when they are with me. I do find my work meaningful though. I change peoples lives for the better in the end. The only thing is they have to help me along the way. I can't be the only one changing their life. They have to want to make that change in order for the change to actually occur. I would say most people would also agree with me when it comes to defining my job as meaningful.

Work_Story:
I am a Real Estate Broker in {city} {state} and surrounding areas. My work generally involves helping peoples buy/sell their homes. For example, If I have a client who is looking to buy a home I will search for them listings that are up in the area and price range they want. Following this I would show them properties that I have found. If they are interested in a certain home then we will put in a offer to the sellers.I represent the buyer in terms of negotiating the price etc. If a deal is made I will draw up real estate contacts for the purchase of the home. 
 
 If a client is looking to sell their home then I would first take a look at the house and let them know somethings we might need to change to make the home more attractive etc so buyers would be more interested. For the price of the home I look at the area and what homes that a similar have sold for in the past 6 months or so. I promote the sell of the house through different things such as open houses. These are usually help on Sundays and are open from 10-5 where the home is open to the public and people are free to come alone or with their agents to take a look at the property. One of the most important things in terms of helping a client sell their home is after someone is interested I would be in contact with escrow companies, home inspectors etc to make sure the terms of the purchase agreement are met beforehand. 
 
 Another important factor in my job is being able to network and build a base of clients. My job is based on pure commission so its extremely important to get "your name out there". Being able to find clients that are interested in buying or selling their homes is the hardest part of being a Real estate agent. Other factors that make the job kind of difficult it the current housing marking in my area. There a lot of people moving to Seattle because of so many huge companies but there is a lack of homes that are for sale. The price of homes has also risen a great deal the last few years so people are generally not wanting to sell their homes and are waiting to see how high the prices will get. 
 
 My typical day consists of going into the office around 10am and going over and emails or reminders I have previously planned. I search the MLS software which provides me with any new listings that I am not aware of. If I have any potential clients I generally call them at least once a week to see where they stand or just to touch base with them. If I have any appointments with my clients I will either meet them at certain properties. My days are generally the same, I am either showing certain properties to potential clients or I am contacting other agents to set things up.
 
  The main responsibilities of my job is to basically provide guidance to clients and help them buy or sell at the right prices and the best possible terms. Providing a good market value or clients who are looking to sell their homes is also very important. 
 
 This is a basic outline of my duties as a real estate agent.
Overall_Why:
I find my work extremely meaningful bemuse I help people in terms of finding or selling their homes. Owning a home is one of the largest purchases that couples make so its very important to me to be able to provide them with the best service that I possibly can. Its very important to me to be able to help others find their happiness. Being able to build a network of relationships with clients and other people in the field is very meaningful to me personally. For example, finding the "dream" home for first time buyers is always great because I am able to guide them with my knowledge and help them find exactly what they are looking for. To be able to see how happy they are when all contacts have been signed and the loan is completed is job to see. A lot of times when clients are nervous or confused about buying/selling their homes its meaningful to me to be able to assist them and provide them with any questions or concerns they might have.
 
 Overall I think that my work is extremely meaningful to me because I am able to help clients with anything that they need. From helping finding their dream home or being able to give them details about escrow companies etc is something that I really enjoy doing. I know that there are a lot of agents who are just trying to get commission as fast as possible and don't really care about their clients but that's definitely not me or how I look at my job.

Work_Story: I am assistant director of a local dance studio. We have classes in 8 different styles in 4 different age/skill levels, as well as 3 different competitive teams in 4 different styles. My job is basically to assist the owner/director and to assure that everything goes smoothly. That can consist of anything from maintaining costumer accounts, sending out bills, choosing and ordering costumes, registering for competitions and conventions, maintaining the social media accounts, and talking to students or parents when issues arise. I work from home during the day, and in the evenings after school hours, I work from the studio. I am not there the entire time, mainly when my own child is in her classes or if I am needed. I also go in on Saturdays, as our company ballet classes are held on that day, so I go in and make sure everyone pays their weekly ballet fee, shows up on time properly dressed, and that the ballet mistress gets her payment. During competitions, I make sure all company members are accounted for, hair and makeup is done, that there are no costuming issues, and that everyone has ate before performing and that they have proper hydration. With the younger ones, I also have to make sure they have a bathroom break as well. I watch as the director and choreographers run their dances before taking the stage, pointing out anything they may miss that needs correction. I calm nerves of the dancers and the parents. I give pep talks to those who don't think they did so well. I take pictures and post results on social media. For recital, I make sure all costumes are ordered, which usually we run about 450-600 costumes per recital. Set up the photographer for the program book, gather all the photographs, assist the program book person in putting together a 200 page program book. I design and order the tickets. I coordinate our recital rehearsal by making sure everyone is there, communicating schedules, feeding kids who are there for long periods of time, talk with parents to soothe any fears or concerns they may have. During the actual recital, I do have my own kid to tend to, but always end up helping others out with hair or costumes if needed. I also am responsible for cutting all of the music and providing it to the sound people. After recital, I coordinate the clean up. Also, I spend a lot of time helping the director make decisions, such as which teachers to hire, to let go, and which students get to move up to teacher assistants. All in all, I enjoy it. There is something quite amazing about watching all of this work come together onstage in a 3 minute dance, to see how hard these dancers work, how committed they and their parents are to dance life. The joy on a kids face the first time they get a skill, its quite a feeling to experience it over and over again. But now I am past 500 words, but it says I need 3000 characters. So which is it? I would have probably worded things differently had I know there was a character count as well as a word count. Should have said that to begin with.
Overall_Why: Dance is something that not only inspires people, but it can help them cope as well. I have watched kids with low self esteem come into our studio, thinking that they wont be able to accomplish much, become so much more self confident with every little piece of gradual growth they find. Some kids come from homes that really arent conducive to personal growth, and dance is their safe place. They are able to come to the studio and leave it all behind, they get lost in the music and movement, they take their fear and their anger and they push it through their body and transform it into a beautiful art form. That in itself is quite meaningful, to know that I help this place be what it is, so that they are able to have such a place. To know that I work with such talented people, to watch them grow and achieve so much, is really an honor. We have had students come to us with no skill in their entire bodies, and have been able to help mold them into budding stars. We have dancers that are professionally working as choreographers, some that own their own studios, and some that are working as professional dancers on dance teams, on Broadway, and have gone off to join prestigious ballet companies. Even though that is amazing, it is still the every day joy you see on the kids faces that makes it the most meaningful.

Work_Story:
My work is very meaningful. I work full time as a Social Worker for the state of {state}. I am able to help others and provide them with resources to help them out of poverty. My clients usually consists of women and children. These women are usually single parent households which makes it hard for them to provide for their children. My typical day consists of following up with clients. I go over their information such as number of children, programs applied for, and current income. With this information, I am able to point them in the right direction. Many of these programs are government aided programs which is also known as Welfare. Programs offered include Medicaid which is health insurance for low-income families and children. SNAP is also another program offered for many of these families. SNAP is Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program which provides low-income families with a monthly allotment towards food. We also offer SNAP benefits in the form of cash for families who are homeless and not able to eat hot meals. 
 My clients often come from neighborhoods stricken by poverty. I often perform house visits to check on my clients. We offer a wide range of programs in order to point the children in these neighborhoods in the right direction. There are summer camps that children are able to go to and also extracurricular activities for the children to participate in. 
 This job is my passion. I have always wanted to be able to help others. I remember when I was young, my family was going to move into a homeless shelter because we had nowhere to go. We ended up living with my Godparents. A few years down the line, I witnessed a close relative living in a homeless shelter with her 4 children. She was single, had 4 children, and no money. I believe it was at that moment when I realized I wanted to be the person to help people out of these situations. No one should ever have to struggle and have trouble providing for their children. Children should not have to suffer when they have no control over their financial situations.
 Although my work requires a lot of energy, I would not trade it for anything. My job actually matters in people lives. I am not just a Social Worker to these families, but I am someone who is helping them have a better life. I work with different organizations which also provides housing for a lot of these families. Sometimes it is hard seeing different families struggle so much to provide for their families. I often meet the children and see so much potential in them. But when their in poverty, it is hard for them to reach their fullest potential. This is where I come in and offer aid or programs to help make their lives a little more better. These programs are designed to be temporary in order to help people get off of their feet. I have seen people abuse this system which makes my job somewhat hard. I have to report them to the fraud department and they are investigated. This usually results in them losing all government aided benefits and now the children have to suffer the consequences also. 
 I work with a team at work but at the same time I often work alone. All workers have their own clients so we are usually to busy to work together. The only time we work together is when we have to switch computer programs or if we're searching for a new solution to a problem.
Overall_Why:
My work is very meaningful. My job is dedicated to helping people. We offer programs to help lift families out of poverty. We also provide clothing, food, and shelter for families that are not able to afford it. For instance, in the winter months children often go without warm clothing. We often have clothing drives in order to provide warm coats and clothing for children. During Christmas we offer families in poverty different programs in order to get gifts for their children. Many families I work with consist of single women with children. They are low-income and cannot provide essential needs for their families. On Christmas we were able to provide over 30 families with gifts. Not only gifts but also Ham and canned food items that were collected during our annual food drive. 
 My work does not stop there. As part of my work, I often volunteer at homeless shelters. This gives us an inside look at what is going on in our communities, and gives us ideas on how we are able to fix the problem. I am able to build a relationship with a lot of the families that I work with. I perform home visits just to make sure they are doing well. 
 The hard part about my job is seeing how these families are struggling. There have been times when I went to do a home visit and the children do not have a bed to sleep on. The houses are bug infested and the neighborhoods are not safe at all. It's my job to get them out of these unfortunate circumstances.

Work_Story:
I have two jobs. I am employed with a company in which we provide care for individuals with physical and learning disabilities. I started working with this company 10 years ago. At this time I was a lifestyle teacher. In this role, my husband and I would take adults with disabilities into our home and provide all needed to care for them. Many people understand it best when we describe it along the same lines as foster care.My first individual that lived in my home needed assistance with daily acts of living, behavioral assistance, and medication administration. All caregivers are required to take medication administration classes before administering medications to clients. This was a test that required multiple hours of training and if you failed you, you were not able to take the test again. All teachers, including myself, are required to take many tests and go through extensive background screenings and drug testing. Teachers also had a social worker monitor their home by conducting unannounced home visits each month to check on the welfare of the individual and to make sure that the home had no state violations or unannounced house guest. The teachers are compensated well for this job. Most teachers make from $5,000-$8,00 monthly. After teaching for four years I moved into the social worker's role of the job and was assigned a caseload of 16 homes. This job not only included unannounced home visits but we also had continuous paperwork to do on a monthly basis. Individuals also receive money from the state and one of the things the social worker was responsible for was counting the cash during the home visits and then running monthly audits on each individuals spending for the previous month. The teacher would turn in all receipts for all purchases made by the individual and the social worker would complete an audit which included line itemization and balancing all funds for that month. These forms were always maintained in the individual's record along with any other information and were subject to a random annual audit by the state. The social worker was also responsible for making sure that the teachers maintained training compliance. If a teacher needed to renew training, the social worker would schedule the state approved and required classes and ensure the teacher completed the classes to remain in compliance with the state. The social worker also had quality evaluation tools that were to be completed each month in the home. Each month was assigned a different tool. This spread the required quality evaluations to be spread across the year. Completing the tools monthly provided a smaller chance of tools being overlooked. The social worker would also complete a medication review each time in the home too. We would make sure the controlled substance was documented and signed off for as well as nonnarcotioc medications. If we found that a home was out of compliance during our visit, we would report to our supervisor. If we had reason to believe that an individual was in harm in a home, we would remove the individual and place them in one of our emergency respite family homes until the issues in their homes were investigated. This job required a lot from the social worker. This was a salary position, however, the social worker was always on call. In June 2017, My family decided to move to {state} after the unexpected loss of my 28-year-old son. I retired as an employee of the company and was then offered a position where I was able to work from my {state}. home as an internal auditor. I now audit all records, visits, medication records and finances for 5 social workers in the company. This takes some of the workload off of them and allows me to still hold full-time employment with the company I love and have tenure with. I respect my employers because they provide for their employers. They offer many benefits to their employees and ensure that they provide all tools necessary to make each employee successful in their role. They provide a company vehicle, scanner, printers, paper supplies, iMac and any tools I may need to complete my work. I see myself working for them until I am able to retire. 
 I am also part owner of a small business that does commercial and residential renovations specializing in 3D marbling and Epoxy flooring and countertops. This is a start-up business that my husband I run and operate and we have seven employees.
Overall_Why: I do find my work meaningful for a few different reasons. I now audit all monthly records of individuals with disabilities. I do believe that my hands on role as a social worker who was in the home a lot each month were more meaningful but it was also exhausting. You never knew when an individual would go into crisis and you would have to drop everything and go assist the family. I grew very tired physically over the years of doing this job and now I feel I can still watch over these individuals by reviewing all of their medical, financial and monthly paperwork. I have caught issues in the paperwork that turned into a need for an investigation. I have also caught some people "fudging" work hours which in turn brought up questions of who was providing care for the individuals in this home. I have also discovered discrepancies in medication reports that brought on investigations and it was discovered that an individual was not getting their medication as they should have been getting it for a month. We were able to correct this and follow all needed protocols before it became a serious issue. I take great pride in monitoring these items because I know that things can be easily overlooked and having an additional set of eyes on paperwork gives a better chance that a mistake is caught in its early stages and it is stopped before it becomes a huge problem. Although I do not find my work to be extremely meaningful I do find it to be meaningful

Work_Story:
I work at a podiatry office. I have not yet received the position I am looking for but everyday I work towards my goal. At the moment I am working as the intake nurse in the office. I am the person that annoyingly asks you stand on the scale and take your height. Don't get me wrong, I like working with the patients and the great thing about a podiatry office is we see the same people on a fairly consistent basis. Which is awesome, because I genuinely like building relationships with our patients. I chose working at this particular office because I had major ankle and foot surgery and can relate with the patients' pain and aggravation. Once I take people into the exam room I take blood pressure and check bandages and sutures, go over any diagnostic or lab results and take a brief history of the patient's health concerns since surgery, or their last visit. I find it easy to talk to everyone about what they are going through because I have been in their place, and my surgeries were even done but the very same doctor. I can often give advice as to what will help eliminate pain, help injuries heal, or just be compassionate about what someone is going through. 
 
 My job is laid back as we only schedule patients every half hour to give everyone time to prepare and take care of the discharge properly, and also to make sure we do not have to rush around. Due to our great scheduling department, we have a lot of down time to do what needs to be done around the office, with minimal stress on the office staff. It gives me plenty of time to talk to the other girls in the office. Which is great because they taught me how to use the computer system, outside of the education I received in school. I really like scheduling appointments, dealing with insurance companies and calling in prescriptions. I know that sounds lazy but like I stated before I have had ankle and foot surgery multiple times and it hurts for me to stand for long periods of time. The down time also allows me time to study and continue my education, as it is important in the healthcare field to always remain current on your credentials and the goings on in the medical community. 
 
 My job gives me some flexibility in schedule also because the doctor is in surgery all day on Friday, which means there are no patients in the office that day. I have the opportunity to work it as a full day, half day or take the day off entirely. I am paid to really do nothing on Friday's. We answer phone calls and assist patients with documentation they may need from previous appointments but it is completely casual and laid back. Our busiest tasks on Fridays are catching up with MedTrak by entering the missing data and refilling prescriptions for patients. This is my first job working in a doctors office and my desire is to never work anywhere else. The girls I work with have became close friends and I even consider the doctor I work for a good friend. Our kids are friends and his wife hangs around the office a couple days a week. Being the intake nurse is simple and basic but I feel like I am able to make a difference in people and hopefully make their days a little brighter. I am working toward sitting at a desk setting appointments but until then I am content with what I am doing. Everyday I get a chance to learn and become more efficient and knowledgeable in my field. The doctor I work for is well connected and I hope working for him will advance me in anyway possible, or at least provide myself and my family with a safe and secure financial future.
Overall_Why: I do find my work meaningful,I find the entire field meaningful as it's healthcare and that is an important source of life- at our core. At all of societies core. The doc I work for is a young guy, maybe been in practice six...seven years or so and he is on top of the cutting edge podiatry speaking. He even initiated a request for a stem cell study to aid in some of the healing processes of his patients. He also likes to work with the patients on "state's insurance" or Medicare/Medicade type programs. He says that since his parents were immigrants and came to this country with nothing- subsequently becoming physicians themselves, he owes it to the lower income caste to fulfill exceptional medical care at lower or no costs to the patients. It really does make a difference. Like I stated before, the relationships that I have acquired with the patients of our practice has become the backbone of why I do what I do- and how I will stay engaged and practice longevity in this specific office. I think if I ever left this specific practice, I would go work in the Federal Prison System as a health care technician of sorts there- not only because of the student loan forgiveness, but also because I think it would be equally as meaningful and the relationships, while governed and professional, will be equally as real. Being able to provide excellent medical care to people who are incarcerated and, essentially, wards of the state, I hope to help people feel more human and in touch with the "real world."

Work_Story:
Prerequisites

Adjacent to this same period, and moderately a lengthy time before a hobby of mine, rather a passion, live in computer technology. Sometimes, I could be found dismantling a VCR, clunky desktop, or trying to figure out exactly what makes everything tick, sort to speak. I didn't realize how my passion would combine with my family's current occupation.
 
 
  As I proceeded to age I was hired by {company}. Starting in a military base taking inventory, measurements, along with weighing each unit by use of a forklift. Most units were deployable by plane, but definitely heavier then you'd believe.   The Promotion

  It was determined that my additional week was purchased back, which included an ample improvement in earnings and a formal title. Commercial Technical Manager would identify me for several years and into present time. The project soon to launch, no wonder they acquired my time back from me.
