Overall_Why:
Absolutely, I feel a definite sense of purpose from my current profession. It is pivotal that I suitably adjust employees upon how to carry out everyday operations. Our Clients deserve their individual vocational spaces to remain respected, relocated, and as operational as before the replacement. My team is responsible for conducting themselves in a professional manner while conducting their work at an accurate and suitable velocity. Not just my family relies on my decisions determined by my outlines and dispositions, other men plus their relatives remain affected if an incorrect order is presented. It may appear minuscule, however, I assure you, much more obligation is incorporated than anything generated personally could possibly be explained into words.
 
  Consistently advancing a clear devotion aimed at personal and professional improvement shows to signify my strength. At times, a much-needed reduction in vanity and egotism doesn't hurt, by what my spouse proclaims. I have quite a family supporting my determinations involving my professional viewpoints. They are the only thing I find more meaningful than my profession.
 
  Humbled today by various accomplishments achieved in my not so distant past has shaped who I am now. Listening has always benefited me more than talking and understanding how to support certainly has guided me on how to manage better. Anyone that has progressed a sizable length had to originate from someplace before they could start. Remaining vigilant for knowledge and wisdom will be something planted in my values. I remain respectful of anyone in authoritative positions than my own and those who are older than myself.

Work_Story: I work as an office girl for a local concert sound and lighting company. our office is open monday through friday from one in the afternoon until five pm, monday through friday. summer is our busy time, while winter is more for maintenance work on our equipment. I come to work before the office opens so that I am there to answer the phone. when I get to work I check the fax machine, make coffee, answer or print out any emails that have come in overnight. I handle the shipping and posting of ebay sales for old gear. my main job is to handle communications for my boss, who is the owner. my paperwork includes handling the mileage reports for the government on our semi trucks, which has to be reported regularly, I handle the payroll for our crew of guys, and keeping expenses in order for the tax professionals that he hires. I act as a shield for my boss so that he can get important stuff done, then concentrate all of the outside noise into a short summary when he's ready to address it. he built the company from the ground up, and has been in business for 30 years. He did not have a secretary before me, I had to implement many systems. when I was first hired he dumped out his desk drawer on my desk, it was full of receipts for the year. He was not sure what I would be able to help him with when he hired me, he just knew he needed help. on a lot of shows that we are hired to do our guys have to drive in the semi trucks across many states. I will arrange the hotel rooms and printed out routes for them before we all smart phones. one time I accidentally printed out a route for them that took them through canada and they called me from the border. I had to be a lot more careful after that, they were not happy with me. we have a regular crew of guys that work on things that require skills like running the sound board or the lights, or that know how to set up a stage, then there are workers that come and go that are there to do the heavy lifting. during the summer there are a lot more workers because we are so busy with festivals. that makes payroll a lot more fun. payday is on monday at 5 because monday is like our friday, there are less shows in the middle of the week and more on a weekend. I am the only lady in the business and everyone has been very respectful to me. my boss smokes his cigars out in the warehouse because he knows it bothers me. It gets very cold in the building and i have to dress warm in the winter months. my office is in a building behind my house so it is very convenient, and my boss is very understanding if I need to take days off or run home for anything.
Overall_Why: I do not find my work very meaningful. There is no room for me to grow, this is it. I will never advance or earn much more than this. I will not learn very much from the work I am doing, or grow as a person. I am not helping people or animals in any meaningful way. Music is good for my community, so it's nice that I am helping to creating a space for families and friends to enjoy a good time. There is also a lot of drinking that goes on at events like this, which isn't that great. It is nice for me to be able to contribute to my home and finances, it is great being so close to home so that when my family or animals needs me I can drop everything and be there for them. The work itself is pretty tedious but the environment is nice. I enjoy my workspace very much but I don't find it to be very enriching. My father also works at the same company, he builds speaker cabinets in the warehouse and runs the soundboard at local shows, so it is nice to be surrounded by family and there is a great sense of community. We all live on the same block, so my coworkers are my neighbors and that is meaningful to me, that we are all in this together. The successes and failures of the company are successes and failures for each of us that depend on it.

Work_Story: Currently I work as the controller for a local government agency. The purpose of our agency is to provider Mental Health and other related Human Services to our county members. We are a semi state/county organization where we receive about 95% of our funding from the state/feds and only 5% from our county. I am responsible for keeping all of the books accurate as well as submitting several annual reports. I am responsible for balancing the checking accounts to both the county's books as well as our own with the bank statements. I am responsible for annual time studies to determine how much money will be allocated to each department as well as the expenses. I am responsible to monitor funding to the budgets and make sure that we do not go over budget in any line item. I also am responsible for determining payroll splits and entering all entries that are paid by the court house into our books. I must monitor all expenses to make sure that they are expensed to the proper department. I am responsible for maintaining the integrity of the books as well as fully understanding the software that we use. I also am responsible to make sure that my department is staffed as needed an to make sure all employees can take their vacation days, PTO days, and other holidays off that will not effect the way we do business. I am responsible for promotions, and annual reviews within my department. I work on a daily basis Monday through Friday and work seldom on weekends or evenings. Some of my main concerns are to monitor that programs will have enough money and shift funding as needed on a monthly basis so that we provide all legally required services to our members. I am responsible for reviewing contracts and making sure that all fiscal responsibilities are met by our providers. I must annually review their financial statements to make sure that they are legally doing everything correctly. I am responsible for meeting with various departments about productivity and to monitor the productivity of each department. I must report this information to the CFO, Administrator, and department heads as required. I work on several group boards to make sure that fiscal is providing all of the correct and needed feedback on a regular basis. I am responsible for the training of my staff and for various software permissions that new hires need to have allocated to them. I am also required to remove their access upon termination. I am responsible for invoicing the Federal Government to be paid for the services we do on a quarterly basis as well as the state. When funding comes in I am responsible to make sure it is allocated correctly to our department and other various county entities who work with our department for funding. My job is very busy at times and there are times of year where it is very relaxed. There are many times that I am asked to review reports that will go to outside agencies for accuracy. I work with various in county and out of county auditors in different departments looking for information. I also have the responsibility on a daily basis of checking time sheets and for approving payroll on a bi weekly basis for my staff and sometimes other department staff when their manager is on vacation.
Overall_Why: It is meaningfully for the people we serve. I feel that I do a very good job making sure that people have the funding to obtain the mental health, intellectual disability, drug and alcohol, and early intervention services our county residents need. One in every seven county residents obtain some sort of services through our office. I work very hard to make sure that they all get what they need. I am sometimes required to be very creative in my financing options to ensure that all people can be served. I also must ensure that the county has enough funds to pay for services and to terminate services to prevent the taxpayer from having to pay more than their required taxation. I feel that I work very hard to do all of these things and by working hard I am doing a huge service to both the residents, board members, and tax payers that are within our county, state, and country. I work with the Department of Justice to help rehabilitate offenders to prevent them from re-offending. This is important as our job is to help people, not imprison them and charge the taxpayer large sums of money to have them their. I also work with other agencies, aging & children and youth, to ensure their members obtain the services they need as well. I am a steward of the public, a civil servant, who wants to help my community and family to ensure all needs are met.

Work_Story: I work for a salon/spa/retail company. As far as the structure goes I have a district manager and a location manager. After that there are three assistant manager which includes me. We have five shift managers and maybe ten sales associates. On the salon side we have two salon coordinators (who answer phones and book appointments) and around fifty salon professionals. About thirty five of those salon pros are hair stylists. We have five nail technicians, six estheticians, and three massage therapists. The salon section of the store mostly manage their own schedules and wages. We help them build their business and take a portion of their earnings in exchange for work space. They pay the store on a tier system based on the how much revenue they bring in and how many repeat customers they have. They tend to get along for the most part but there is always someone bickering about something. Usually a new salon staff member with no experience has just graduated and doesn't have any clients so we have them shadow a master stylist or nail tech, whatever the profession may be, and book them clients that want to pay staff level prices. Our pricing for all salon pros starts at staff level, then senior, and then master. We have one esthetician that makes $80,000 just waxing eyebrows. I would say we have a pretty successful staff as far as the salon goes. The salon coordinators stay pretty busy answering the phones and since we only have two salon coordinators us managers have to pick up the slack and take over so they can take breaks or if we need to cover a shift or even a gap in the shift. Actually Sundays we don't even put one of the salon coordinators on staff since sundays we are only open 9 hours in the day. The retail staff is mostly young girls in college. We have one older woman who has been with the company for 15 years. They are a lot of fun and seem to enjoy what they do. I spend more time with the retail staff than the other managers. We play a lot of games to help them learn the products and give incentives for certain sales goals. We have a decent size sales floor and carry over 10,000 items. There is a surprising amount of information to learn about all the different specialized items and I try to make it as fun as possible. We set daily sales goals and usually meet them everyday. We are number two in the company for highest retail and salon sales. I spend some of my day filling in corporate paperwork, taking care of deposits, ordering supplies and collateral. I do a weekly inventory on the salon supplies and get two deliveries to make sure we always have the right amount of items based on the services we will be offering. Since i have been doing it for awhile now i have gotten familiar with the colors and supplies for the particular season that we are in and its a bit of a predicting game every week. I usually am the one opening the store and after I get the daily goals laid out and morning paperwork done I make sure the salon has fresh towels ready and start the coffee for our early bird appointments. Normally first thing in the morning there won't be a salon coordinator there so I start calling clients to confirm their appointments and check in clients arriving until a salon coordinators shows up. After they arrive I start doing laundry and make sure the retail staff is doing okay. I make sure everyone knows where I am so that I am accessible to anyone at anytime. They depend on me to handle all kinds of things like computer issues, upset customers, product issues and product knowledge. I feel like we have a pretty good team overall all and find most of the people pleasant to be around.
Overall_Why: No I do not. I think my idea of extremely meaningful work would be something that related to working with children or some type of environmental work. I am really concerned for the planet and how we have treated it and feel like that would be the most meaningful line of work I could get into. For it to be meaningful it would need to have lasting effects that would make a change or a difference in the world. My job helps to satisfy people's vanity issues and people's need to belong. The work I do may make someone happy one day but doesn't make a real difference in the world. It surely isn't helping the condition of climate change. The chemicals that go into these products are harmful to the environment and quite honestly harmful to us as humans. For example, most of the moisturizers we sell, which could be considered high end, have liquid plastic in them. While this makes your skin silky smooth it also traps free radicals in and that is not good for the body and actually makes you age faster. I am not helping the people I sell these items to, in fact I feel like I am hurting them buy not explaining the ridiculous ingredients put in these products. For a while I felt like maybe i could make a difference and start warning people about the potential dangers of the products they were interested in and I started explaining to customers. The majority of people looked at me like I was crazy or flat out told me that they don't care. It got to a point were I decided to only talk about these type of things with customers that specifically asked. This is the kind of thing that makes me feel like this job is not particularly meaningful.

Work_Story: I work as a shipping clerk for an aerospace company. My daily tasks include receiving in of product from my company's overseas facilities into our inventory system, verifying counts and product certifications in accordance with industry standards. The parent company has facilities in France with additional locations in India, China, Morocco, Germany, Poland, Turkey, the United Kingdom, Canada and the United States. Because of the international span of this company, the receiving process is different based on whichever plant the products come from. Along with receiving products, I also help with the shipping to our company's customers, as well as shipping between the different plants and facilities of our company. With shipping, there are procedures that differentiate depending on which facility the parts are going to be shipped to. Parts are allocated to different customers based on the need of the customer, and are processes, counted, packaged and shipped in accordance to their, the customer's, specifications. The type of bag, amount per bag, type of box, amount per box, how many boxes, which shipping company, how many days from order request to shipping, how many days to wait before shipping, customs claims if the product is going to ship internationally, and a variety of different procedures depending on each specific company, product and method. I work what is considered the American standard schedule, Monday through Friday, 8 am to 5 pm. My work environment is a large manufacturing plant, where I am required to wear safety equipment because of the nature of the work. I am required to wear ear plugs, safety shoes with non slip soles, both water and oil resistant, and steel toe protection, and safety glasses. There is security clearance that is present because of the sensitive nature of the products being produced, shipped in and out. The aerospace manufacturing industry is very protective of all operational flow in order to prevent any FOD, foreign object debris, or product that does not pass industry and customer specifications for inspection from going out the door to be used in the air vehicles. I also conduct daily cycle counts of the over 10,000 products that are valued at over a million dollars worth of inventory. Because most of the products are counted and distributed by weight, there are often discrepancies in the counts of products that are in our inventory. Most shortages or overages of products are with low cost items that in the long term are negligible and not detrimental to the overall accounting dynamics of the company. However, any shortage or overage of any product that is worth a significant amount of money per part, or an integral part for operational performance of the aircraft, is investigated heavily in order to determine where the fault lies and how to remedy the situation and insure the future orders and shipments are held to standard and comply with customer's desires. While being a dull and tedious job, it is vital that my daily tasks are performed correctly and in a timely manner to guarantee the customer's can continue with their operations.
Overall_Why: I do not see any value in what I do at my job other than to insure that the company I work for maintains its profits and the higher executives keep getting their salary. My job function is as a low gear in the cog of a larger machine, and while my work goes into a lot of airplanes and ensures the safety and proper operation of aircrafts, I do not feel a personal satisfaction or connection to the end product of which I produce. I do not feel like what I do directly impacts and world as a whole, even if it does but just to a small degree. My daily tasks leave me feeling as if I am just going through the motions and not progressing towards anything my myself personally. I do not feel like I am making a difference, either large or small, in any capacity for the betterment of society as a whole. When I go home at the end of the day, I shed all feelings and thoughts of my work and job and do not want to fixate on them any further. I think that a meaningful job or career would leave a person with a feeling of excitement and fulfillment, and a person would not dread their daily commute to work or feel like they need a drink at the end of every day in order to release the tension and stress of the daily grind of work.

Work_Story:
Currently I am employed full time at {company}. I have graduated from college with a B.S. in Psychology and am transitioning to obtaining a Master's Degree. When not in class or studying, I can be found at {company} working as a To Go! Specialist, Bartender, Host, Production Specialist, or Certified Trainer. So far I have been with the company ({company}) for 6 years and it has been my main financial source of income throughout college. 
 On a daily basis I interact with guests and typically provide a service for them (if placing an order, I input their request, offer suggestions where needed), and insure they receive what they preferred. One rule is to always maintain a positive attitude regardless of how stressful a situation may become (either through the behavior of a guest who cannot be satisfied or through the sense of feeling overwhelmed by how many tasks must be completed with 100% accuracy within a specific time frame.
 Although each role I play varies in what is required, I strive to do my best, with the best attitude, as a true team-player, and in a genuine manner. Because I am a "seasoned" employee, and take on various leadership roles, it is important to me that I make a difference and set a positive example not only for co-workers, but also every person who walks into the restaurant. 
 Typically I work 10-12 hour shifts and, if time allows for it, get anywhere between a 10 minute to 1 hour break. If I am working in production, I am clocked in by 8.00 a.m., if working as a ToGo! Specialist, then by 9.00 a.m., if scheduled as a bartender, then 9.30 a.m., as a Host, then 10.00 a.m., and, if scheduled as a Certified Trainer, then typically by either 9.30 a.m. or 3.00 p.m., depending on which shift I train during. 
 Overall the job is pleasant and enjoyable; I always say "it is what you make of it" and this holds true in my experience. In stressful situations I try to maintain a positive, energetic approach and have found that using certain sounds/animations when completing given tasks helps not only myself, but others as well because it can radiate the sensation to them...this in turn, knowing that I did something to assist someone else's own emotional regulation...their ability to believe in themselves and realize that we're doing our jobs and it can be fun, in turn has it's own reward. Simply put, attitude goes a long way. 
 There have been times when I have felt frustrated with the job, either due to how management was handling the business (i.e., behaving hypocritically, no longer perceiving employees as humans who can physically handle only so much before rest and sustenance are required, etc.) or because it felt that employees were constantly taking on more than individuals should...such as under staffing a shift but expecting, for instance, half the amount of people to do a job that requires the entire amount designated.However, regardless of any frustrations, large or small, I aim to remain responsible for myself and my own actions, those things I know I can do, and realize that, though i might enjoy this job and feel comfortable with this job, it is not where I see myself within the next 10 years...once my continued education (Master's and Ph. D.) are completed.
Overall_Why:
Overall I find my work moderately meaningful. My goal in life is to help others, in any situation, and this job has provided such an opportunity. Through the experiences I have had as an employee, I have interacted with a large variety of individuals (either as co-workers or guests) and learned much not only about social interaction, but also about myself and capabilities. It is meaningful in the sense that I can be part of a team, I am continuously learning new things, exposed to new things/people/possible experiences, and, with the more I know, the more I can teach others who are either interested in having a wonderful dining experience (either in restaurant or out of) or providing the experience for those who have contact with our business. 
 Through employment at Olive Garden, I have assisted with setting up/serving food donations at various locations throughout the community and, though it is not coming from my pocket, I can see the impact it has on those who receive the food or service. Being able to be apart of a job that allows me to be part of a "giving back" experience has, in my opinion, value. We are not "changing the world" on any large scale, but we can acknowledge that our efforts make a difference. 
 This may not be the life career for me, but it is meaningful in that it has provided me the opportunity to rise to various types of challenges, both from internal and external forces, and grow as an individual.

Work_Story:
I currently work freelance in the film industry in {city}, {state}. I work in the Production Office of various movies and TV shows, jumping from show to show every few months. The Production Office is the nerve center of any production - we distribute scripts, schedules, call sheets, production reports, and many other documents, we prep all the various meetings, we plan for large scouts of various locations, and we generally keep the ship running while the crew on set shoots the scenes. 
 
 This is an extremely fast-paced, energetic and stressful environment. Multiple departments will rush in throughout the day, asking our help ordering equipment, rushing film to set, or booking flights for cast and crew. Many small emergencies pop up throughout the day, all of which need to be handled right then and there. This is definitely an environment where I have felt stressed, anxious, and agitated - but part of what you learn in the Production Office is that calm always wins. I take a deep breath, I try to remind myself "It's just a movie," and then I force myself to logically think through the problem at hand. This is an invaluable skill, and one that the higher-ups tend to value, since there will always be such situations.
 
 We really help coordinate all the logistics of the production, especially the rental of equipment needed on set. There are a wide variety of cranes, dollies, cameras, lenses, and generators that are requested on any given show, and it is important to know what they are used for and which departments rely on them. Again, there are always last minute requests for equipment, as the schedule is always changing. We have to simply remain calm, call the vendor (sometimes after-hours) and try to book a very specific piece of equipment for use first thing in the morning. We like to think we pull off small miracles on a daily basis.
 
 One of the other main things we deal with is ensuring that the cast remains comfortable, happy, and actually shows up on time. The Assistant Directors (the people who run the set) come to us to books car services, hotel rooms, or to fulfill any other minor requests (such as a particular snack) for the cast members. After all, we can't shoot a scene if the actors don't show up.
 
 This is not a job I envisioned myself working, but the Production Office is a great way to see how each of the other departments on a movie really work - and work together. It's a great vantage point from which to learn how a movie truly comes together. This particular department is a great introduction to the world of producing, and many people in this department go on to become Production Supervisors, Unit Production Managers, or Producers. Having an understanding of different departments' needs, and an idea of what all this equipment costs, is an invaluable skillset to bring to the table.
 
 I've learned an incredible amount over the years, and every show brings with it new challenges and different personalities. It's an interesting environment to work in, especially because as a freelancer, I get to work with many different people form a variety of backgrounds. Even though it's high-stress and sometimes too fast-paced, I can't imagine doing anything else.
Overall_Why:
While I quite enjoy the fast pace and the crazy requests that come our way, I can't say that this job is particularly meaningful. When it comes to my deepest values, I would consider a job meaningful if I were actually helping people in need, making a difference in my community, or somehow effect change on a national or global issue.
 
 As it is, I constantly remind myself throughout the day that "It's only a movie," and on some level that demeans my own work in a way. Entertainment is certainly important, as everyone needs an escape, but I don't believe that a movie or TV show that I work on is particularly effective at actually changing the world.
 
 My current job consists of keeping individual egos in check, while also making sure that small groups of people have the equipment and tools they need to complete a very specific task. While this may be meaningful within the context of the movie, or within the film industry as a whole, there is no way to claim that my job aids my community or makes a measurable difference in the world.

Work_Story:
I work as a Reference and Outreach Librarian at an online, for-profit university. I would characterize it as both repetitive, challenging, and innovative. While it is definitely not the type of school I thought I would end up working in, it is definitely the place I am meant to be at this time.
 
 Many aspects of my job are quite repetitive. Most of my day is spent answering student reference questions via phone, email, and live chat. I can count on at least 2-3 frustrated and unappreciated students a day, although most of my encounters with students are quite pleasant. And even though the questions I get are quite varied in terms of topic, they can often be answered with the same techniques and recommendations. Most of my students are looking for general information that can be found in a handful of our larger databases, and I provide almost the exact same database search tips and strategies to each student: format your keywords this way, tweak these settings, sort your results like this, etc. Yet while it can be very boring and repetitive to me, it is often groundbreaking and life-changing for them.
 
 My job can also be quite challenging. For one thing, it's often hard to mentally rise above the aforementioned repetition. When I am designing instructional aids and videos, knowing that most of my students struggle with the basics of information searching means I often have to hold myself back from adding more advanced content to my materials. It's hard enough to pack the basics into a short instructional session; going beyond basic in a meaningful and interesting way is often impossible. I also struggle with figuring out the best method for my instruction. The widely varied student base means that I have to reach students with vastly different age ranges, experience levels, and life experiences. The entire student body is also online, and the classes are asynchronous, so I rarely, if ever, meet or learn specific details about individuals. In fact, much of my instruction takes place in email or video form, so I rarely know if my efforts have been effective.
 
 Despite the repetition and challenges, the exciting thing about working for my school is that it is quite innovative. The more traditional institutions of higher education at which I have taught in my past have often moved at a snail's pace in terms of change and innovation. In my present position, however, I am encouraged to pursue the latest forms of software and technology in order to reimagine traditional library instruction for an all-online, digital student body. My students are located all over the world in all time zones, and I am one of many people working on a plan, a curriculum, and learning aids to help deliver quality high-level instruction to students that is accessible, effective, and innovative. 
 
 My organization and the team of people with whom I work are very responsive to our student's needs, and our organization runs at a very fast pace. If an idea or an instructional component doesn't work, we don't wait until the next semester to make a change. My students get the very best of myself and my work in real time.
Overall_Why:
My work is meaningful because I spend anywhere from 50-80% of my day helping others. As a Reference and Outreach Librarian at an online university, I deliver information literacy instruction to students via chat, phone, and email. While I occasionally have to deal with a few students who find the library too difficult, I am able to help the students to whom I talk find the resources they need to complete and be successful on their assignments. Most of the information I give out on a daily basis is pretty basic, but for some of the students who reach out for help, it is life changing, if only in the moment. 
 
 As with all jobs, though, a lot of my work doesn't feel meaningful when it's happening. I also have to communicate with faculty members and administration who don't really understand the purpose of an academic library. For them, I am just a support person instead of an important part of the educational process. The library is a place they send their students for help with homework, not a place where real learning happens. This ongoing struggle to define the work of an academic library and librarians is tedious and results in a lot of unnecessary busy work and assessment. And at an online university with a digital library, where we don't have a building--an academic hub or home--to call our own, it's very easy to feel somewhat overlooked.

Work_Story: I am a Band/Choir director in a small rural public school. Our school district is a high poverty area with around 75% of the students on free or reduced lunches. This is the method by which many programs or additional aid is determined. We have been very fortunate to receive several large grants to help our district this year. Band programs in the rural areas have greatly suffered over the last 20 years. I have taught for 34 years and I am seeing the end days of many programs. I will quite possibly be the last band director in my school district which had a proud tradition of music. Small school have many barriers in their way. Fewer options for scheduling. More State and Federal Mandated classes. Fewer teachers willing to work in the rural areas. Many don't have the dual certification needed. No funding for basic equipment. I start my mornings working with middle school choir. Then I become a High school choir teacher. This class is for students who need a fine arts class but don't want Art or Band. Sometimes this is fun and sometimes I hear the "do we really have to sing" question a few to many times. The next hour jumps back down to the youngest students I teach. 6th grade beginning band. This class used to be taught at the 5th grade level every day and now is 6th grade every other day. Well most of the time we meet two or three days a week. The best thing about this age is they really want to learn and are excited at least until their parents are unable to afford a good instrument. The cost of a good instrument might run $400 and up. Online services sell my students cheap junk for $50 to $150 that the parents are just sure will be fine for them to start with. I spend half of each day trying to get these instruments to work. Then I spend the rest of the hour trying to help them get a sound out of them. Next hour is my high school band. At this point those students who have made it through with hard work and parents who have invested money in them we have a good hour. After an advisory period and lunch I have my select choir a plan period and 7th and 8th grade band. It doesn't matter what you teach or how good your students are last hour of the day with middle school is always going to be a long hour. After you stand out side and make sure the kids get on the buses it is back to the band room and work with students in small groups or individually. Weekends there are often practices with the most dedicated students who have qualified for select bands and choirs. Of course there are parades and competitions, concerts, fundraisers and lesson plans. This year we have managed with donations to add chromebooks for our high school students. This has allowed me to start teaching music theory and assessing their knowledge through software programs. his however leaves me in a difficult position. I need some money for software, but I still need music and all the band equipment and furniture that was purchased when I started here 24 years ago when the community and school had better finances are getting old and falling apart. So instead of deciding I'm just retiring.
Overall_Why: There is nothing more important than the education of our children. Only with music do you enable students to utilize all areas of their brain. In addition to teaching "music". we teach our students how to work with each other to produce a good product. There is competition within the group but there is more "teamwork". Other life skills have been making the students "hem' their dresses or pants and make sure they are ironed properly. After giving them deadlines I bring in a few iron and ironing boards, Needles and thread and tape measures and start teaching them the basics. My students take my classes for a variety of reasons. Some because they see the importance of music. Some because they are extremely talented and just love music. Some plan on being professional musicians. Some take the class because it fits in their schedule and gives them the chance to stand and move around more than than a regular class. Then there are the really strong Academic students who take music because it helps them relax and relieve their stress. Hopefully I have been a positive influence in my students lives. I know I have enjoyed them. Even after they have been gone for years I still think of them as my students when I see them years later. Nothing besides teaching gives you this type of connection. Then you get to teach their children and grandchildren.

Work_Story: I am a Process Analyst. My work consists of many tasks throughout the day. Most of my day is spent reviewing technology and its use in the work place. The company that I work for is the biggest IT firm in Europe. It started getting a foothold in America about ten years ago and has become a force in the business world. I support many different clients. Each of them has a specified technological need. It is my job to analyze that need and implement a reasonable solution in a quick and cost effective manner. I am then responsible for the creation of training materials. In that process I must develop and fine tune everything so that it can be explained and used by the average person. I then have to work directly with our recruiting department to gather a group of suitable people for perspective employment. That is easier said than done. Once that task is accomplished I have to work with the client to determine the workforce needs for the contract. Through an established process, it is my job to select the individuals that have made it through the screening process. These are my first trainees. I then have to present the material that I have created, and test their ability to retain and use the knowledge. Once it has been proven that they have been successful, I immerse them into the needs of the contract and monitor their progress. I solicit feedback from the client and their consumers. After a period of time I am able to sit back and evaluate each person. When I do this I ask for their opinions and document their responses. I then compile all of the data and report back to my superiors and the client. This is usually done over the course of several meetings. We are then able to construct a model of where things are, and where they need to be. It is then my responsibility to take that information back to the team and make changes as needed. One of my favorite parts of my job is having the opportunity to spot and nurture potential leaders. I am responsible for monitoring talent, and promoting accordingly. On the flip side of that I am also responsible for culling the force, that is removing people from the team that are determined to be a detriment. While that is not fun it is most assuredly needed. Once a sustainable team is in place, it becomes my job to establish and monitor their performance metrics. That way I have specific data on hand and can account for everything. The goal in the end is to keep the client and my superiors happy, and at the same time keep good hard working people employed and healthy. This plays out every day of the year and has for the last two. I'm gearing up for some changes in the future but this is what my future will be based on. This has been the most gratifying experience of my life.I did not expect that this would be where I ended up in life but it sure makes for a full day. When the numbers are in the sweet spot for the client, and my superiors then life is easy. When they are not that is when things can get a little interesting. Our team is like a well oiled machine, any change no matter how small can have devastating consequences. It is my job to analyze those consequences and keep the balance.
Overall_Why: My work is extremely meaningful. It is nice to have to face each day with a challenge. I get to work with some of the most amazing clients in the business world. I get to work with some of them most amazing talent that is out there. I get to work with new technology and create training material. That is one of the most meaningful feelings that I have had as a civilian. No two days are the same. Each day comes with its own unique challenges. When I go to work I am usually excited to be going. When I come home at night I am usually fulfilled by what I have been able to accomplish throughout the day. I don't feel like anything has been wasted or left on the table. I would only change a few things. I wish that I had a more direct impact on people, face to face. Most of our meetings are held over the computer. I do not find that to be very meaningful. I would place less emphasis on the bottom line, and more emphasis on the people that I work with. I find the job that I do to be very meaningful but the pay certainly leaves something to be desired. In the end it turns out to be a balance of human feelings and corporate dollars. By having the ability to keep all of these things in balance and working in harmony I feel like my time at work is meaningful.

Work_Story: I work as a Realtor for a big real estate company in Southern {state}. I work out of an office with other agents where we each have our own workstations to do our work. I can work everyday some weeks, where I work floor time in my office for about three hours per shift. Sometimes no other agents want to do floor time, so I will work in the office all day trying to attract clients for myself or my team. Since my office is near the beach, a lot of tourists and locals with walk by my office and look at the listings in the window. When they do, I will try and talk to them or follow up with other clients I have met. When clients want to look at properties which is a main part of my job, I show them beautiful coastal mansions and homes that are for sale that they may qualify for. I first get them qualified so I know the price range they can afford, then I will pull up properties in that area, and take them to see it. I have a lock box key which enables me to enter properties with the owners or listing agents permission. I can pull up a lot of details on the properties so the clients will already have an idea of what they are seeing. If a client does like a property, I can write up an offer for them and submit it. In my office, I will write up the offer and do other paperwork. My office has a computer room where I can also use desktop computers to print out the documents I need and fax anything. Besides homes, I may show upscale condos and even investment properties. I also look for clients who are looking to sell their homes. I can represent them as a listing agent and help them sell their home. After looking at comparable properties, I can help them to distinguish a good selling price. I am constantly on my phone talking to other agents and calling back clients. I had to get a license to do real estate by the state of {state}. I have an office manager I work with also, who will take calls for me when I am out of the office in the field showing any type of real estate. Sometimes I will throw mixers to meet new clients and just talk to existing clients about real estate. My office has meetings once a week where we go over contracts and new information that should be helpful. Sometimes, I will have clients just hop into my car and I will drive them from property to property or they will follow me. I also use an in house lender to help me get loans and he helps qualify the clients so I can pull up the right properties for them to see. I help clients buy their dream house they can afford to live in. I also work in a team within my office where I may refer my team members clients for a referral fee. I enjoy doing that also sometimes rather than always showing properties. I will do floor time and get the clients information and have one of my team follow up with them for a nice referral fee. Although the coastal areas are my favorite, I will show properties all around the wealthier areas because those are the areas that interest me the most and I have the most passion to show. I work for a broker who is a very kind and sweet person and helps me whenever I need it. She is who holds the meetings and will teach us agents new laws and updates to contracts. I will also send clients properties off the MLS to their emails so they can view the property online and then make a list of which ones they want to view.
Overall_Why: Yes, I help clients meet their goals and accomplish what they always wanted, and that is being a homeowner. The money motivates me also. I can make a lot of money per deal and that just keeps me energized and happy that I chose the best career for myself. Seeing clients happy and excited makes me feel like I am doing something meaningful for society and everyone involved. You know when you meet me, there is a good chance I can help you get that real estate loan or help you buy that property you always wanted. Giving someone that satisfaction makes me super grateful that I am able to bring joy to others. ALso passing a really hard test that not many people pass was a big accomplishment for me. Without that license, you cannot do the work I do, so that really makes it meaningful that I am in a position that I earned it through hard work. I get up everyday, where an expensive suit and tie and nice shoes to work each day. The broker said I was the best dressed. That means a lot to me. It shows I have passion for the job and respect for myself and that comes from being happy with my job. The money I make shows that the job is meaningful and worth it to do. I feel like I can accomplish so much by just working hard at my job and doing something that I am good at. I save people sometimes that need places to live and make it happen for them so they are not stuck. I enjoy helping these clients not just for the money but it feels good to help.

Work_Story: Well my work is a central part of who i am. I wake up most every morning and head off to the place where i love the most my very own restaurant that i own and run with my spouse. I can't imagine what my life would be like without my restaurant every since i was a small child it has been a big dream of mine to own my on restaurant where i could serve people and bring happiness to others through the power of food. My job is all about cooking and managing the restaurant it definitely has it's daily challenges but to be honest i wouldn't have it any other way. I consider my work to be a form of art and craft i take pride in cooking great dishes that people will remember and tell all their friends and families about it. I love seeing the reaction from people of all different races and cultures enjoying how my food brought them together. It isn't smiles and happy faces all the time. Running a restaurant has its ups and downs just like any other thing in life i feel like its one big roller coaster with good times and times that make me wanna down right walk away for good. I have had lots of times when customers would have various problems with the food not being cooked to their liking and that's perfectly normal to expect people to have their own judgments and tastes. All in all running the restaurant is a huge part of my life basically i would say it is almost all of it outside of alone time with my wife. Cooking is most of what i love and enjoy about my job been then there is the business side of my work which if am to be completely honest is handled mostly by my wife i did not do well in school as i only have a high school diploma so i am not so great with numbers where as my wife is a business major at a prestige university. I honestly would not be able to run my restaurant without my wife or my employees that work hard for me each and every day. That brings me to the next part that i like about well i love about my job and owning and running my own restaurant is that i am giving other people chances for work and opportunities to make money i have about 3 or 4 kids that i gave jobs because they were drop outs from school and had no other way of making any kind of living and it keeps them from stealing and off the streets and shows them the value of working for a dollar. I feel like my job while giving me and my wife happiness provides just as much happiness that it does to me and gives it to others and that makes me really happy and i couldn't imagine doing anything else. I actually have plans to have a child of my own some day and while i would want that child to be able to pick or choose to follow there own hopes and dreams i couldn't think of anything that would make me happier than my kid choosing to follow in my foot steps and run the restaurant for ages to come and pass it down to their kids and their kids pass it down to theirs a family tradition. I also love to give elderly people and people who have severed in the military or arm forces daily discounts on any dish from my restaurant
Overall_Why: Yes i feel like my work is extremely meaningful. While it may not save lives or cure cancer of anything it brings people together and everyone needs to eat don't they? I feel like the power of food is extremely underrated and to be honest going out to eat at a restaurant is more than just eating food it can be to celebrate a wedding anniversary or a birthday or any kind of special occasion it can even be to celebrate some kind of holiday that certain people celebrate. I feel like restaurants bring people together like very few things can do and it's really more than just sitting down and eating, its how was your day? it's will you marry me? it can also be happy birthday.
