Work_Story: Let me tell you about a day in the life of what makes me get out of bed every morning and just like everyone else, grudgingly groan the whole commute there: My job. It starts at the early peaks of almost every morning (except weekends) around 2am and doesn't end until 5:30pm or until shift change arrives. A large array of clinging, banging and other loud noises fill the room upon clocking in and coming through the bio hazard safety doors and blood and urine vials are almost in every corner of sight you can think of. Microscopes at minimal volume are scanning blood and urine for traces of infection or mutated cells in multiple departments and the hustle and bustle of busy workers feet are in a flurry. Have you guessed who I am yet? If you guessed a Medical Technician or a Lab Technologist, then you are correct! I work in a level 1 Trauma Hospital in Ohio and we're the only level 1 trauma center in the Tristate region. We see thousands of patients a day both impatient and out, and draw hundreds and hundreds vials of blood to analyze for diagnostic testing. We're here to help our patients receive the best of care at the quickest pace to speed up their recovery time and to have more time to enjoy themselves. I myself, analyze the blood/urine/feces/ect that is drawn and sent to the lab for clinical analyzing for any discrepancies so we can correct them and administer the correct medication to start the recovery process. My job is full of stress and a lot of overtime is put in for our patients to ensure they will never doubt our results and our dedication for their health and safety. On average, I work roughly almost 56-60 hours a week to help fill in for lack of staffing in certain departments but the day tends to go by relatively quickly because we're all running around trying to get results processed for our doctors and patients as soon as possible so they can start a treatment plan. I work roughly 12-13hr days and most of that time is spent in front of a microscope or a computer looking at cells and results to ensure they're accurate according to our hospital reference ranges and policies and to also insure sample integrity, gotta make sure those nurses and phlebotomist are drawing good samples for testing or else they get rejected and will have a delayed result and we don't want that. I get one break a day and it last 30 minutes, it's not the best, but we're a 24hr lab/hospital and we don't get very much time to just sit around and play on their phones or whatever everyone else does when it's slow at work. If I'm not analyzing blood, I'm starting quality control on our instruments to ensure they are properly working, releasing reliable results, and functioning the way they were manufactured. A lot of work goes into working into a lab and lots of people don't understand the pressure we're put under to perform and get results out. I hope this statement can help you understand more about my job and some of the stress it comes with. Have a blessed day!
Overall_Why: Honestly, I love my job. Despite some days where it's extremely hard to get out of bed from lack of sleep and overwork, I find it more than rewarding and very positive to my own personal way of life. Passion and integrity as always been something I am very keen about and I finally found a profession where I can shine and help others, even if it's behind closed doors where we don't receive a lot of credit. Lab work can have a lot of up's and down's because of our profession, but honestly, what job doesn't have good days and bad, we're not perfect. Being attracted to helping others fills my heart with purpose and fulfillment and it makes me more than happy knowing I helped a person get better or even helped make them aware of what's going on in their body so they're not in the dark their whole life and in a constant state of panic. I try to take everyday head on and look towards the positive sides of life and look at it as it isn't so bad out here, so if I can help someone in the slightest way, I am happy. I'm happy that what I am doing is making a difference in someone's life and that is the greatest feeling personally I think anyone can feel. Hope this kinda gives you an understanding of who I am as a person.

Work_Story:
As a hybrid Systems Administrator and a Change Coordinator, I create Change Requests, which are tickets created through an application called Remedy that receive approval from the department head in order to make changes to the functionality of a server, network, and/or an application. In order to create these change requests, we must add full documentation and details to the tickets. Examples include the server(s), applications, and/or code that will be impacted, the length of time the outage will be, back out plans in case of issues, which those back out plans must be attached to the ticket, what testing we have done prior to rolling the changes out into production and those testing plans must be attached to the ticket, have we informed the staff and customers prior to rolling the changes out to the customers, how to perform those changes and ensure the changes that will be made have have been attached to the ticket, what time the change(s) will be made, what day the change(s) will be made, and who will be making the change(s). 
 
 These change tickets are related to either departmental requests, staff requests for better functionality, and/or requests made by customer needs. The Servers are Windows-based and are virtualized systems, which are systems that run multiple operating systems so that our systems can run faster, are redundant in case one of the virtualized systems fails and it can crossover to another system if a failure occurs, and the applications are company owned, as well as cloud based products. We utilize SQL for making code changes internally and through the cloud based products. The cloud based products used are all external cloud products, which allows our company to edit the code, settings, users, and networks, as examples, so the functionality meets our business demands in the financial industry. The networks we use are both internal and cloud based and we also us wi-fi devices, too. The company utilizes the Citrix Server for logging into the company securely so the company applications and systems can be accessed, such as e-mail, financial records, applications, etc.
 
 In addition, as a Systems Administrator, we purchase laptops, desktops, monitors, phones, printers, headsets, and other accessories, such as tablets and cell phones, for our staff and management to use to perform their daily tasks. Tablets are only allowed for management and executives since they are on the go constantly. Cell phones are for the IT staff and management since, again, management is on the go quite often and the IT staff rotates their on call schedules. We utilize a third party service to make these purchases and primary stick with only one brand for each of our pieces of equipment for laptops and desktops, tablets, and cell phones. We use the Dell brand for our laptops and desktops. As far as tablets, we use Amazon tablets and we use Android smartphones. For the most part, we use HP printers since they tend to work best for our company. The headsets are of the Microsoft brand. The phones we purchase are Avaya phone systems. System Administrators are also part of the software installation and imaging of tablets, laptops, and desktops throughout the entire company prior to shipping or site delivery of the equipment. We must setup the tablets, laptops, desktops, and phones on the domain first and their specific applications based on the staff role prior to shipping or delivering the equipment to the staff member.
 
 As far as setting up new users, we use Active Directory for setting up new users, apply their group policies, and assign them to their respective location(s) and printer(s), as well as any other permissions required, such as certain applications, the computer name they will be using. Active Directory can also be used to reset password, lock and unlock user account, monitor server security, and monitor special services. On that same note, we can disable a user using Active Directory. As an Admin, we monitor system performance through event logs on the server and we utilize a monitoring tool called Tivoli in the event there is a system event, e.g. intrusion or too many unsuccessful login attempts and/or network related issues, such as network communication issues or a network intrusion has occurred. There are also event logs that are manually created through our cloud based products that are directly sent to the IT staff regularly so we can monitor unwanted traffic, intrusions, unsuccessful logins, etc. We create daily, weekly, and monthly backups directly on the Windows servers and on the cloud infrastructure through Amazon Web Services.
 
 Other assigned duties include creating and updating documentation on SharePoint so others in the IT department have access to the same information; knowledge transfer and we are all on the same page. On that same note, in the event of a rare instance of an event, other IT staff members can look up information on that rare instance and know how to fix it immediately. In addition, when a workstation, server, or a cloud based product goes down, the System Administrators are called upon to act quickly to identify and fix the issue. Once the issue is determined, the Admins must get the workstation, server, or cloud based product back up and running as soon as possible and make sure it is functional. In some cases, a simple reboot of a server or restarting a service or an application will fix the issue. In other cases, we have to read through the code to determine where the issue is. In more severe cases, we have to determine if it is a network related issue or if the cloud based product is suffering an outage. When the workstation, server, or cloud based product is back up and functioning again, a mass communication is sent out to the executives, management, and the appropriate staff explaining the root cause of the outage and what was done to fix the issue(s). On the contrary, if there is a network or cloud based outage, we also have to send out a mass communication stating as such to the executives, managers, and appropriate staff letting them know of the outage and when we provide the next update, in addition to letting them know we are monitoring the situation closely. 
 
 Other situations include monitoring a ticketing queue where staff members submit tickets for workstation issues. In these cases, there may be an issue with their workstation. Examples may include they lost network connectivity, an application has bugs where we have to determine where the issue is, they need an application installed, they have malware or a virus on their computer, they need an e-mail recovered, they need to upgrade or new equipment due to a failure or issues, and so forth. 
 
 On the servers and cloud based products, we monitor the CPU usage, check for runaway processes and kill them if needed so the systems and applications do not get bogged down throughout the day or evening, check disk space and clear up any unwanted space so we do not run out, check all mail servers to ensure they are not filling up too quickly with mail that is overly large by staff members. If mail is overly large by certain staff members, we will contact those staff members and have them create a .pst file and move the large files to that or have them backup the large e-mail to a network drive. If they do not know how, we will ask them to submit a ticket through Remedy so we can assist them with this task. Admins also look for unattended sessions on servers. If a staff member has been logged in for a lengthy period of time and we notice they are sitting at idle, we will boot them off of the system since only so many connections can be logged into the system at one time. As far as printer spools, these are checked to ensure the spools are clear from unwanted or unattended jobs so staff members can print what they need to print throughout the day. If jobs remain the print queue that have been left there too long, it will cause a backlog of jobs. 
 
 On the servers, temporary files are removed on a daily basis to allow the system to run smoothly. On that same note, if a staff member complains their system is running too slowly, this is one of the first steps we will take (clear cookies, temporary internet files, and reboot the system) to see if this makes their system run faster.
Overall_Why: I find my work very meaningful because it allows me to control what privileges users are allowed to have throughout the entire company. For instance, if a new or an existing staff members asks for a specific application on their system, they must get approval from their manager and that manager must come to the IT Systems Administrators to give approval directly to us first. As far as managers, the manager's manager must provide approval. Executives do not need to obtain approval. For the most part, we have control of the systems, laptops, desktops, headphones, phones, printers, cell phones, and tablets and how staff members have access to the applications and systems so no one has access they do not need without proper permissions. In addition, my team is very knowledgeable in all different types of IT and we share knowledge with one another constantly. We are very cohesive in nature which, in my opinion, is very rare in the IT industry. We help one another out whenever possible; crisis or no crisis. We all bounce ideas off of one another. I love the cohesiveness and attitudes of all of my coworkers and my direct boss. My boss does not question any of our abilities and believes in our efforts to better the business for our customers. We very rarely make mistakes, but when we do we own up to them immediately and we come with immediate resolutions to those problems and/or ask for others opinions on how to resolve those issues.

Work_Story:
I am self-employed full-time as a foreign exchange trader and also tutor graduate students in finance. I have an MBA and have attended graduate school for Computational Finance which combined finance, math, statistics and computer science. I like to take a very analytical and comprehensive approach to my work and my education has been invaluable in this regard. 
 There are only a few major currencies that are traded in the foreign exchange market: Euro, US Dollar, British Pound, Swiss Francs, Australian dollar, New Zealand Dollar and the Canadian Dollar. I focus on the ??majors?? exclusively as the other currencies are not quite as liquid or just too volatile. Managing risk is the hallmark of every good trader and I tried to adhere to good risk management principles first and foremost. Good risk management essentially entails keeping your losses in check/under control and generating as high a profit as possible for the risk taken. 
 I have excellent charting and trading software and have developed my own models for trading. My day starts with running the latest data from the charts through these models and looking for trading signals. I don??t believe in trading frequently, only when the conviction level is very high. I have found (the hard way, unfortunately) that patience is infinitely better than making split-second decisions while trading.
 I love working for myself because I can keep the focus on the markets and my knowledge and understanding of it rather on issues like office politics, professional rivalries, jealousies and such. I have worked full time for major financial firms and while you do have more stable income and job security you also often spend considerable time (at least, in my experience) on the aspects mentioned above which tend to act as a drag on your sense of fulfilment/achievement. I can honestly say that I spend virtually 100% of my time in learning, improving my professional abilities and executing my plan or very close to it.
 Unlike most other professions being a successful trader does not, at its core, require you to have good interpersonal skills, work well in a team etc. ?? if you have sound ability to analyze markets, spot value, buy low and sell high you can be a successful trader. At present, I have little or no social contact at my work ?? I like to protect the models that I have created from wider dissemination and so have not taken on any partners although that might change down the road. I do have substantial interaction with others in my profession at trade shows and the like as well as through my tutoring work.
 Working for yourself also comes with other challenges ?? there are no ??benefits?? that you don??t pay for entirely yourself. Health insurance especially is a major expense. But there are other perks that are invaluable. You get to set your own work hours and even decide if you want to work or not any day or even any month. Travelling is my passion and I have travelled the world. All told, I wouldn??t trade what I do for anything!
Overall_Why:
My work is extremely meaningful to me because it keeps me close to my chosen professional field which Is financial markets, and my knowledge and analysis of it. I am free to apply any concepts from any field that makes sense and don??t have to prove it to anyone but myself. I believe I have well above average creativity and this works particularly well for me. 
 As I had said in the original write-up, I spend virtually no time in extraneous activities like office politics etc. I learn constantly and do better because of my knowledge and skill rather than anything else. Besides, learning is rewarding in itself and as I grow older I find that increased knowledge makes for a more meaningful life.
 Since I am self-employed my work gives me a sense of having built something instead of just helping others build their business. I am a hard-worker and am therefore more driven to work. I have often had to push myself creatively to solve my work-related problems and have had to exercise all of my faculties in the process. I am not sure everyone gets to do that. I do hope to go on to bigger and better (and hopefully more meaningful) things from here.
 I have the freedom to choose when I work and the flexibility to do things outside of work that I enjoy. I love to travel and have travelled throughout the world. I can work from any place with a good internet connection. My work has, therefore, enabled me to escape from the 9-5 paradigm at a set location. I can work in the middle of the night if I choose to.

Work_Story:
I am a translator of Tibetan and Sanskrit languages. My work in Tibetan is in both oral interpretation and written translation of classical, medieval, and contemporary literature. My work in Sanskrit is exclusively in the field of written translation.
 
 In the field of Tibetan written translation, my specialty is in the religious literature of the Nyingma sect, particularly the prophetic traditions and the tradition of Dzogchen (great completion or perfection) and its various literary traditions. Though this is a fairly narrow specialization, because of the vast prolixity of Tibetan literature, the Tibetan cultural reverence for written texts, the general embrace of heterodoxy, and the isolation of many sub-sects, this specialization encompasses a veritable ocean of texts in which to wade. The general classification and taxonomical studies of the literature (let alone translation of said literature) will not be finished in my life time. Add to this the fact that this particular sub-set of Tibetan literature is constantly growing due to the Tibetan embrace of an open religious canon, and the work seems to have no real end in sight.
 
 This particular set of Tibetan literature has multiple indigenous claims as to its origins. The Buddhists claim that it comes from a kingdom north of ancient Indian that was known to Sanskrit speakers as Uddiyana. The pre-Buddhist peoples (more akin to shamans, though they adopted many of the outer rituals of Buddhist due to various periods of religious persecution) know as the B??n (a word that has no clear etymological root in the Tibetan language) or B??npo claim the tradition arrived from a place called Taziq via the ancient kingdom of Zhang Zhung. Until recently, both Uddiyana and Zhang Zhung were thought to be mythical kingdoms, but recent research has shown significant evidence to the contrary. Uddiyana is likely to be a located in the Swat valley of lower modern day Afghanistan. The presence of Buddhist statues and other archaeological evidence would seem to corroborate this. The existence of the kingdom of Zhang Zhung was corroborated by the discovery of manuscripts written in the language of their people that are decipherable and do seem to contain references to the literature. Furthermore, research into the area where the kingdom is said to have existed has uncovered peoples there who claim to speak a language called Jang Jung that is clearly very closely related to the language of these manuscripts. Additionally, the Taziq referred to in the B??n literature appears to refer to a part of ancient Iran, and some Persian scholars seem to think that B??n would be an approximate pronunciation of the ancient Persian translation of the Sanskrit term Dharma. Needless to say, these discoveries have further opened the already endless task of my profession.
 
 With regards to oral interpretation, I am employed by various English and German speaking groups when they have occasion to seek an audience with Tibetan lamas or wish to receive spiritual teachings from them. This is a difficult task given that the philosophical language of Tibetan is still primarily classical, and so when teaching, a hybrid of colloquial dialect and classical terminology is presented for me to translate. And despite what is claimed by some linguists with limited work in the field, Tibetan is not a standardized language and the saying, "Every valley has its own dialect and every lama has his own Dharma" seems to hold true.
Overall_Why:
Yes, I find my work to be exceptionally personally meaningful and satisfying. The work I'm doing helps make available an entire tradition and its lineages of thought that still survive today. These philosophical traditions are just as rich and rewarding to engage with as the Western philosophical tradition, and they are just as important, in terms of quality of thought. By making these texts available to non-Tibetans and those not trained in the traditional manner, it makes it easier for the written tradition to survive and easier for living teachers of the traditions to use these translations as teaching aids for their students who cannot read the classical language.
 
 Beyond this, as someone who loves languages and historical research, there is an endless amount of unexplored territory for me to immerse myself in. Additionally, the challenges of translating a highly sophisticated philosophical literature into a readable English text is very rewarding to me. There is a tendency in the writings of this tradition to elide the use of grammatical particles and markings as well as nominalizing and verbalizing indicators. The resultant language is often a serious of propulsive and poetic phrases composed of single syllable words that are simultaneously verbs and nouns. It is a difficulty and pleasure that I have yet to encounter in any other language or literature. Furthermore, it is impossible to perfectly translate the effect of it on the mind of the reader ?? that the whole meaning, every single noun of a sentence is an active verb acting upon all others. The great pleasure of translation is in trying to find ways to replicate these types of effects in completely different languages.

Work_Story:
I have been with my company for many years, over 30 to be exact. I have preformed many roles over the years. I started in the warehouse, and within 3 months was promoted to outside sales. After 5 years of doing sales on the road, I was promoted to branch manager. I was the branch manager for 18 years and was instrumental in starting a new initiative in the company. I was eventually promoted to be a regional sales manager specific to the new initiative and I am still in that role today.
 
 I get to travel a fair bit and that is good and bad. I??m sure I could travel more if I wanted to, but my location is not very convenient to travel and any trip is a long one from my home base.
 
 What I do is more business developmental than pure sales management. I look for opportunities and try to bring all the needed resources to bear so the I am acting more as a facilitator than anything else. I assist with technical needs as well as finding the financing that will enable the project to be built. I also look for special projects that can be outside of my area of expertise. I really enjoy learning about the technical aspects of products or solutions and then figuring out how to monetize them so that they pencil.
 
 I am currently working on a new water treatment system that is modular and can filter down to 1 micron. The cost per gallon is comparable to normal sources of city water, but this can be deployed anywhere and use almost any source of polluted water as feed stock. It is financeable and can be leased. It can be a game changer for many different parts of the world as well as many different industries here is the USA. These types of projects are fun since they are not mainstream commodity types of projects.
 
 My day to day is not as exciting. I work for a fortune 500 and there is a lot of paperwork and bureaucracy involved in what I do. It was much worse when I was a branch manager as there was always someone form corporate asking for something. It was a never ending battle to try and keep everything in order, keep my people motivated and still have time for customers. Since we have so many field locations in North America, headquarters is mostly out of touch with reality. I can get very frustrated with the lack of vision and understanding due to the lack of field experience.
 
 I have met the CEO, and he is a good guy, but he does seem to grasp what goes on in the day to day operations out in the field. I have tried very hard to communicate back up the chain of command, but I think those messages get muted along the way. But, from my experience, my company is not much different than any other big company in that they struggle to effectively grasp the burdens that upper management task the field with and expectations are not realistic.
 
 Locally, I have had to fill many other roles as I am the most experienced manager in my geography. I am called on to mentor, babysit, counsel and guide other departments. I have to bear the torch for the company as I am a known commodity in my local market. Many customers and suppliers seek me out for guidance and insight.
 
 So, in short, I have had many roles in my 30 plus years with my company. I have some flexibility to enjoy what I do while delivering value to my company. As frustrating as it can be working for a large organization, the benefit of having a large pool of resources makes it worthwhile in the long run.
Overall_Why:
The initiative I started was renewable energy and related fields. It makes difference to me because we need to be less reliant on foreign oil. It makes me feel like I have helped spread the word as my company was doing nothing in this field when I first started down this path. It took years of hard work and dedication before the company recognized the opportunity and potential of what I started. I also pride myself in mentoring as many of the younger folks as I can so that they can have an easier path than I had to endure in order to move ahead in this industry. I had no one that I could turn to and was on my own to figure things out when I first started. Information is more readily available today, but firsthand experience is still the best teacher.
 
 On the other hand, some of the things I have to do feel meaningless as they don??t provide much of anything tangible that can be measured outside of my company. We get involved in things that don??t seem very important in the big scheme of things. So days it feels like I am just earning a paycheck.
 
 Overall, I am happy with my job and my career. I didn't think it was influencing people or making the world a better place, but as I reflect, I think that I am doing meaningful things and helping folks along the way.

Work_Story:
For my day to day job I am a Daily Service Provider for a nonprofit organization in {city}, {state}. I have been doing this job for just over 4 months and have grown to enjoy it quite a bit. My job has a lot of different working parts to it depending on who you work with. 
 
 
 To better understand what I do I will explain what a Daily Service Provider is. I work with special needs individuals in the community, and help them integrate better into the community in any way that they would like. I also sometimes help out with day to day tasks, things such as dishing, laundry, and meal planning. I thoroughly enjoy this because not only do I motivate the people I work with to have better health and goals, but it also helps me stay on track with my own.
 
 I am there to do basically anything that they need, while encouraging independence where it is possible for the consumer. We may play their favorite games, watch their favorite movies, or even call delivery in and watch their favorite sports. As long as they are experiencing happiness and not self-destructive or harmful in anyway I have done a great job.
 My job isn't just limited to things around the house. I also take them grocery shopping, maybe to the movies, or even to catch their favorite artist live. I really enjoy the time I have with the consumers that like to exercise, because we can go to the gym together and push each other to make our bodies healthier. 
 
 Of course my job also has some parts that become difficult. I am responsible for making sure that at the end of the day the people I work with are doing the best to take care of themselves. You cannot always predict what people will do, or what their illnesses maybe cause them to do, so you need to be prepared for accidents to happen. It's not that uncommon to have human waste or vomit to clean up depending on what medications people are on, or how they respond to their environment. 
 
 Any challenge of every day life is a possibility that we will face when working with consumers. That is something that we will gladly work on together because in my position I understand that everybody needs help in one way or another. For the people that do not have the ability to learn how to or control their bodies need our assistance 24/7. That is a part of the job that every person may experience.
 
 Another portion of the job is the On Site On Call time that you are working. During these On Site On Call shifts you are able to sleep for six to eight hours, depending on the individual consumer that you are working with. In case of an emergency you are there to help them and make sure they receive the proper care that they would need.
 
 My work is incredibly rewarding at the end of every day. I know that I make a significant difference in the lives of those that I work with, and they enjoy my company for it. I feel like the people that I work with are just as close as my family and friends are. Helping other people grow and do better is the best feeling I could possibly experience. I feel as if I am growing through helping others grow. 
 
 I see myself staying in this position for a long time and potentially moving up through the company further. I want to help the people in the community as much as possible to feel like they belong as much as anyone else. I want to see more people engage in these activities through volunteer work to understand that these people are not much different from you or I.
Overall_Why:
This is a very clear cut yes for me. It is because the work that I do is life changing for the individuals I do it for. Some of the people that I work with depend on me in various ways, and I am happy to help them because I know I am making a serious difference in their lives. 
 
 The majority of the people that I work with actually say this to me verbally. They explain how I am able to help them in various ways. For one consumer since we have started working together they have lost 23 pounds by going to the gym and eating better. For another nonverbal consumer we have started sign language. The different ways that we can positively impact their lives are innumerable. 
 
 The family of the people that we work with also see it this way. Because they are not able to constantly be their for their loved ones, or give them the proper treatment that they deserve, they look up to us to help them excel in life. They express their gratitude to us in different ways. It is always amazing when a guardian or parent of an individual that you work with tells you that they see improvement in their child and thank you for it directly. 
 
 That is just some of the ways that my work is extremely meaningful, but really at the end of the day it is helping me grow as a person, helping my community grow, and helping improve the lives of those that I work with.

Work_Story:
I am a fourth grade teacher. I am the literacy and science teacher. I co-teach with the other fourth grade teacher in my grade level. I teach 21 students in my classroom and switch and teach 22 other fourth graders. I am new to this district. I taught third grade prior to moving to this school.
 My school provides supplies and materials that I need to be an effective teacher for my students. There is an amazing support from everyone working in the school. I am new at the school and people come up to me and ask me questions about areas they need help with. Everyone is welcoming. My school make me feel like a strong community. 
 
 I am passionate about the school I work at because I also graduated from the city I now work in. I was so excited to find out that I could give back to the place that taught me so much and made me feel like a real person. Because I moved to the city in high school I was able to develop and push myself in ways I never thought I was able to coming from a small town. 
 
 I have always been passionate about working with children. Working with children everyday is a true pleasure. I am passionate about my job and am so thankful to be in the place I've always wanted to be. 
 While doing my job, I also coach the schools 5/6 grade boys basketball team. This is an additional two hours a week outside of my time in the classroom. This is an amazing way to help support all students in the school. The boys I coach reach out to me when they have having a hard time in school. 
 
 My school is supportive and encourage me to try new things. They offer support and suggestions if I ever seek it out. The students are very well behaved and crave knowledge. I am passionate about teaching them everyday. The parents of my students are open and love to communicate with my about their child's needs. 
 At the start of the year I was sent to a conference and it was paid for me. My grad. school classes are also paid for my the district. The district has in service days where we are able to choose the activities that we participate in. There is a very diverse choices for all desires throughout the district. THey also provided up with breakfast and lunch during the day. We have a vendor fair. We were able to get samples of teaching materials for out classrooms use. 
 
 The district reimburses me for any expense that comes out of pocket. At the start of the year, i was able to purchase almost $3,000 worth of books for my classroom. I only have to do duty for an hour a week beyond my teaching time. This happens during my free time ( I have a period of 100 minutes alone). I love my job and really look forward to working here for many years to come. The most important part of my job is I feel like family and all my input is appreciated! I am so thankful for having been invited to work in such a great school. I hope more people are as lucky as i am.
Overall_Why:
I make a difference in my student's lives. My students love coming to see. I love teaching them. I find that they are some of the most passionate students and they want to learn. They are always willing to help each other. They say kind things to each other. They share their personalities with me and everyone around them. 
 I love working with my students and the teachers I work with throughout the school. Everyone is welcoming and always willing to help. My job encourages me to do my best job everyday. 
 My job is able to pay for me to further my education. They provide opportunities to grow as a profession. I may not make the most money, but I love my job. It is rewarding every day. I feel support everyday when I walk into the school. 
 My school provided all reading teachers in the school with a substitute teacher when we had to complete out reading assessments. I have never had a school do that for me. The school has trade days where we get an extra day off if we work later than normal. The school pays us if we ever stay late for meetings.

Work_Story:
I am a corporate recruiter for a transportation company. I review applicants to see if their work/life experience matches job specific tasks that make them the best long-term candidate for any given position. It is my responsible to fill a career and not just a job opening. I work within federal, state and government guidelines to ensure that the candidates that are offered employment can pass extensive back ground checks, pre-employment physicals and required drug and alcohol testing. Once a candidate has been chosen I conduct all onboard training utilizing different mediums for presentation from videos, to power point presentations to printed handouts. I ensure that the candidate understands the requirements of the position and all state, federal and company rules and regulations that must be followed. I then provide site specific training to ensure that each candidate performs the position with a high level of safety commitment to minimize workplace injuries.
 
 I follow up with candidates at 7 days to review training and any deficiencies that may exist in job understanding, benefits and pay.
 
 I follow up again in 45 days to ensure that the candidate still has a strong commitment to the career and address any issues they may exist.
 
 I monitor all job postings evaluating and rewriting ads to ensure the highest level of applicant submissions to give a broad basis of candidates to interview while also making certain that any ads are for only active positions and we are not in a position of "application collecting"
 
 I review, critic, rewrite and publish forms and policy in regards to all aspects of the transportation industry as it relates to our company and work with outside agencies and workgroups to improve upon the industry as a whole when it comes to best practices.
 
 I report to upper management on all open requisitions and how they applicant process is working from the number of applications received, what advertising avenues are producing the best results, to the recommendations for the best candidate for the position. I report on the length of time it takes to take a position from the release of the requisition to the hiring of the candidate and the success rate of candidate retention as it articulates to the overall company goal for profitable and success.
 
 I provide safety training for all departments working to ensure that all employees are maintaining a high level of commitment to company and personal safety as well as product and process knowledge through various mediums from online training, email, printed handouts, power point presentations and physical presentations.
 
 I work with upper management to reduce work-place injury and work to move an employee through the work injury process from investigation to treatment to return to work/termination of employment.
 
 I monitor employee complaint system and work with upper management to determine course of action and work to ensure that employee needs are balanced with company rules and policies.
Overall_Why: I love my work. I have a strong commitment to connecting individuals with long-term careers and not just a job for a check. I have a strong commitment to training an individual and empowering them with the tools needed to be a successful part of the team and empower personal growth and development that in turns benefits the company as a whole as you foster long-term committed employees that enjoy what they do. I enjoy being able to empower individuals through difficult times by ensuring that they have a voice and feel heard and understood and balancing that with fostering company growth. I love the challenge of finding the right words to place in an ad that will attract the career minded individual that wants to invest in their future long term and not just live paycheck to paycheck - the ROI is amazing when everyone is invested with intentionality. My work is extremely meaningful to me as I know that I work diligently to match individuals with a job that matches not only their current skill set - but their potential while being mindful of their hurdles and working with them to learn strategies to overcome the hurdles.

Work_Story: I work as part of a firm that does local advertising and graphic designs for local businesses. I was one of the founders of the establishment along with a few of my friends. After a few years, we have been able to successfully establish ourselves as a trusted and sought after company for local businesses who need help with trying to promote and market themselves to the general public in our city. I think one of the things that makes our business so successful is that a lot of the people who work at the company have a great understanding of the local market and cultures. That type of insight really helps when it comes to garnering attention and appeal for some of our clients. And one of the best things is that a lot of our clients always have something good to say bout us to other businesses. While every employee in the company does a little something different, everybody at the firm usually works as a team when it comes to the projects and clients we get. I am in charge of the creative direction that we usually take on various projects and campaigns. I also usually do most of the networking. But like I said, most everybody at the company is usually involved with all aspects of our projects. While all of us have experience in different fields like graphic design and marketing, some are more focused on a particular field than others. The first steps to our projects after we have talked to a client is to have a meeting with everybody at the company regardless of their department. It is during the meeting where we brainstorm and exchange ideas on the best ways to help the client market and promote their business. After everybody at the company has had a chance to suggest their ideas and contribute their input, we then move on to making a decision on the best way to step forward on the project. The executives at the company, including myself, will then make the final decisions on how the project is going to be handled. After that, I will then take the creative steps to start the project. I have experience in art. Most of my work usually involves the manual aspect of creating designs for our projects. After I am happy with some of the initial designs of an idea, the next step in the process is usually for our graphic designers to start putting the final steps on the computer in regards to designs and other marketing aspects. Other steps that involve talking to possible partners and issues is usually handled by myself and the other executives as well. As a project is in the final stages, the client is usually given the opportunity to give us their input and opinions in regards to how things are turning out so far. As different departments take care of different parts and stages of the project, when I'm not partaking in helping out, I am actively networking and making sure that we have a steady flow of possible clients. One of the best parts of the business is being able to get to know the local people who run businesses in our city. I think my people skills is a big reason why my company has given me the responsibility to do this aspect of the job. It's also something that I like to do as well. Other things I will also do throughout the day is research the businesses that we are working for and seeing how to plan for the future when it comes to marketing strategies. People in our local community have not only been supportive of our work, but also very happy with it.
Overall_Why: I definitely find my work meaningful. I am able to use a multitude of my skills to make a living. And one of the best parts is that it is something that I love to do. I have always loved being involved in art. It is great that now as an adult, I can actually use art for not only a profession, but to also help run a company. It's a great feeling that something I am good at is also helping others. Also, being able to help local businesses become successful due to the work we do for them is something that I find to be really fulfilling. When I am able to help our clients run a successful business, not only is it good for them, it's good for the local economy as well. Helping the local economy is always a good thing when it comes to my line of work. I also love to talk to the local community when it comes to the research I do when it comes to figuring out how to approach a new project we have or client. Seeing the local community find out about a new business or service is a great feeling. I think that seeing a project go from the brainstorming stage to the final stages is very satisfying. I hope that this is something that I can continue to do and that my company is successful. My company being successful not only means that I can continue earning a living doing something I love, but it also means jobs for our employees.

Work_Story:
I work as a freelance illustrator and animator. I work primarily with twitch streamers and youtubers to create emotes, icons, banners, logos, anything they might need to better brand themselves. I can create animated logos and shorts for their channels as well. I also do regular commissions, like illustrations for every day people.
 I get a lot of my clients through social media and word of mouth. I post my work as I finish it on outlets like Twitter and Instagram. People find me on there or through the people I work for, and contact me about pricing.
 I charge flat fees instead of an hourly rate. I do give discounts if multiple things are ordered at once.
 Programs I use the most are: Photoshop, Premiere, Motion, and Toon Boom Studio. 
 When planning out my projects, I use Air Table to breakdown the project in depth and set the deadlines. I track my progress in there and mark projects complete as they come to an end. This keeps me organized and gives a visual aid to my current projects.
 I received payment up front, along with any instructions I need for completing the project. I gather reference images as needed, especially if it's something I haven't drawn a whole lot or don't know well. I use these to create my vector drawing. I adjust line widths as needed and then export to begin coloring in Photoshop. After I have the base colors down for the image, I go through and add shadows, highlights, and color the lines so it's not a harsh solid black. I inspect the overall feel and make sure the image feels cohesive. After that, I send it for approval to the client. If they want something adjusted, I work on that immediately, sending an update once it is finished. Once finalized, they receive their files in whatever format they need and I am free to make a post on social media of my new work.
 With animations, however, it can take a bit more time. First, I need to create a storyboard. From there I make an animatic to adjust timing. I begin animating the frames and keeping arcs of motion and making it overall visually stimulating. I export the frames and color them, then composite everything together in Premiere. If I have any additional motion graphics to add, I go in Motion and create them, then do my final composite. I send a preview to the client and then make adjustments according to what they want. Once they approve the final cut, I export the file in whatever formats they need and then I am free to post on social media to promote it.
 The important thing with my work is to maintain a solid social media presence. I can't get new clients if I'm not actively presenting my work and seeking them out. The more active I am, the more chances people have to find me. I've been lucky in regards to word of mouth over the years, but in the last year I've began taking my social media presence very seriously. Hosting giveaways, interacting with others, posting my work, and general tweets about my life are just some of the ways I promote myself and get my stuff looked at.
Overall_Why:
Yes. This is what I studied in college and am passionate about. It gives me the freedom to make my own schedule and take on as much work as I would like, while also giving me a creative outlet. I get to draw and animate things I may not have if I was left purely to my own ideas. But being commissioned by others, I have the chance to create their vision and bring it to life. 
 It also gives me the opportunity to try new styles - I can create new characters I wouldn't have before, new animals I've never tried - in Disney style, Ghibli style, etc. The biggest thing is I don't limit myself. I try slightly new styles with every project to expand what I am capable of.
 And beyond that, I feel satisfaction in seeking out new knowledge and harnessing it. When I master a new way to do something, I feel successful. When I finish a project, I feel a sense of self-worth. I feel proud when I hear how much a client enjoys their commissioned piece. I feel like I've brought joy or happiness, in some small way, to them and whoever else will see my work. It's not ultimately about what I accomplished, but what I contributed to the greater whole and what I gained and learned about myself in the process.
 Overall, it's the most satisfaction I've had from any job. This is me using my talents for good and expanding upon them every day.

Work_Story:
I am an officer in the United States Army and have been for almost twenty-one years. Over the span of my career, I have held over twenty different jobs, ranging from entry-level, direct leadership of a small team, to corporate-level leadership of a large organization consisting of over seven hundred people. Despite the vast differences in the types of work roles I have held in this time, there are three lessons about working in the Army that have proven consistent throughout. First, The Army expects its leaders to act, to get results. Second, the Army expects its leaders to care for their people, fairly and humanely. Finally, the Army expects its leaders to learn and grow as they progress through their careers. These three lessons are, perhaps, the best way to describe my work.
 
 The first lesson I learned as a young leader was the importance of accomplishing the mission. At first, I did not understand why my supervisors stressed results so often. As I gained experience, particularly after I led troops in combat, I understood clearly why the Army stresses results so much. Put simply, they matter. When placed in the context of combat, getting results equates to the difference between accomplishing the Nation's goals and failing to achieve them. It equates to keeping your troops alive, or sending home needless dead. These are the stark realities of this line of work, and why the Army stresses getting results so much.
 
 The second lesson I learned growing up as a leader is to take care of my troops. They are the ones who bear the burden of getting results, accomplishing the mission. They are the ones who must work tirelessly, without losing focus, to keep each other alive and to stay on task. They will perform magnificent feats when they know a leader cares deeply for them. They will act, sometimes without question, when they trust a leader. Now, there is a unique aspect to this particular lesson. Taking care of troops in its best form means pushing them to their limit, training them relentlessly, and holding them to high standards. These seemingly harsh measures actually forge the bond between them and with their leaders, and these measures are the surest way to keep them alive. They understand this, and they respond positively to the leaders who push them.
 
 The final lesson I have learned during my career is to reflect, to study my craft and improve myself. Leaders cannot be static. They must learn, adapt, and grow as they progress through their careers. Without growth, leaders place themselves, their mission, and their troops at risk. The world is changing. Technology is changing the character of warfare. Society is changing who enters the military. Leaders must keep pace with these changes or risk complete failure on the battlefield.
 
 These three lessons, things I have learned over and over during the past twenty-one years, describe my work in the United States Army. It is all about getting results, while taking care of my troops, and improving myself.
Overall_Why:
I find my work extremely meaningful. The work I accomplish on a daily basis is important to the security of my country and the safety of my family. It fills me with a sense of purpose and belonging. It makes me a part of something important and larger than myself.
 
 Looking to the work I have done in the past three years in particular, I have found it to be most meaningful and rewarding. It involved shaping young troops to become capable leaders through personal interaction, development, and setting the example. It involved preparing large organizations to perform under the extreme stress of combat through rigorous and frequent training. It involved preparing families for extended deployment and then providing for their care in times of need. Accomplishing this work reinforced my own beliefs and values. In turn, I was able to impart my beliefs and values to others and forge a bond with them.
 
 All-in-all, I believe that my work gives my life meaning. Nowhere else could I live out my values and beliefs. Nowhere else could I forge a bond with others by applying those values and beliefs. Nowhere else would I have the opportunity and privilege to lead large organizations, to shape my professions future leaders, and to care for our families. I cannot imagine finding satisfaction elsewhere, and I am extremely grateful for the opportunity to serve these twenty-one years. I would do it all over again, if I could.

Work_Story: I studied long and hard to work doing what I love which is working as a chemist in a lab. Luckily I have even been able to work in a start up as the lead chemist which is extremely fulfilling. The majority of my work consists of working a fractional distillation set up. Fractional distillation is rather easy as it involves separating compounds based on boiling points. The procedure starts with the set up of a two-liter two neck round bottom flask. A solution of various compounds is used to fill the the two-liter two neck round bottom flask. The flask is then loaded onto a mantel. A mantel is a very nice piece of equipment which essentially heats flasks to a desired temperature, much like a hot plate however it allows for greater control of the temperature as it has a control system which allows for a more precise heating. The round bottom flask is usually mounted on some sort of frame while it is loaded on the mantel to provide support, in my lab set up I just use two rectangular steel support base and two rods. Using a three prong clasp, I am able to secure the flask using one of the necks. I then attach the remained glassware needed to complete the system. For a standard distillation, as well as the one I work on, a fractional column is attached to one of the necks of the round bottom flask. This piece of glassware is key to actually doing the distillation. A condenser is then attached to the column which is connected to a modified round bottom flask called a cow. Aptly named, the cow has utter like prongs which allow the connection of different single neck round bottom flasks. And so a this point the main frame of the distillation system is assembled and partially supported using the steel rods and bases. The remaining steps for setup include attaching connections to the mantel thermal rod, and independent thermal rod for determining the temperature at the distillation head and a connection to a vacuum. This set up is vital for a successful distillation as it creates a closed system in which separation can occur. Following the set up the mantel is used to heat up the flask at various temperatures for various lengths of time until the desired compounds are separated. I don't know if this is exactly what is expected from this survey, but for the majority of my day my work entails this procedure and hardly an interaction with people while I do it as it take about six hours to complete. I of course take diligent notes and modify the technique is required per distillation and solution to be separated. Like I mentioned, I work in a start up which requires wearing many hats, including accounting and various miscellaneous tasks but the procedure that I described engulfs the majority of my forty hour work week. The accounting aspect requires the creation of various excel spreadsheets and calculations but I have created templates for the majority of the tasks that I can usually complete said tasks relatively quickly. Ultimately, I hope to eventually go back to school and obtain a PhD.
Overall_Why: I don't know that I find my work extremely meaningful, it is fulfilling in the sense that I am doing something with the degree that I worked so hard to complete. I suppose that for me to consider my work extremely meaningful I would have to get a PhD because unfortunately it doesn't matter how much you actually know until you have a document that says you know it. I mean the stuff I work with helps people but honestly it doesn't help many people. To be meaningful to me I would like to work on something that could help many people. I mean a degree in chemistry is not really synonymous with helping people but I love chemistry, it makes the most sense, I mean it literally describes how the world works at a molecular level! So I just knew that I had to work in a lab in order to be fulfilled but it has not completely fulfilled my desire to help people. But if I had to choose again I would still pick this route. I have discovered that it will be a little harder to achieve my "life goals" if you will, but it is still something I thoroughly love. Extremely meaningful, this is such a complex concept all on its own but to apply it to something that someone does might just put it over the edge. I want to think that I am currently working at a discipline that is at the very least just a little meaningful, but is question might just have trigger an existential crisis for me.

Work_Story: I own a retail pharmacy. A lot goes into running a pharmacy, but the work with customers is the most fun. It doesn't feel like work to me. People bring in prescriptions, I type the prescription and tell the customer if the Rx went through on their insurance and what the cost to the customer would be. That is where things get tricky. I feel more like a deal broker than a pharmacy employee. The doctor writes a prescription for what he/she wants the patient to take (often influenced by a drug rep). The insurance has its own idea of what the patient needs and what they will cover. The patient just wants the best medication they can get without going broke to get it. I am the only one who can see every angle and find a solution that everyone is satisfied with. Often the answer is to omit insurance altogether. Many people don't realize using your insurance is not always cheaper. We can sell a great number of different medications surprisingly cheap. I also work to align a customer's medication so they can pick them all up on the same day. If a person has 4 different medications, it is easier for me and for them to pick them all up once a month instead of making multiple trips to the pharmacy. I also set customers on 'autofill.' I know that a female is going to take birth control each month, same with insulin, blood pressure, blood thinners etc. I know when you should run out of your maintenance medication so we fill these medications a few days before the customer runs out so it is ready when they walk in. The part I really hate is dealing with the insurances and trying to explain why they are doing what they doing when it really makes no sense, they are 100% profit driven. The insurances pretend to care for the patients. The insurances give the pharmacies a 'star rating' which is total B.S. Insurance says its to let customers know which pharmacies ensure 'compliance' in other words, the insurance sees a claim go through each month for your blood pressure medication, so they consider you 'compliant.' If a customer has a lapse in days supply then that hurts the pharmacy rating. The problem is, many times the insurance is setting the customer co-pay higher than what the Rx would cost without insurance at all. With the customers' best interest in mind, we give them the cheapest price which is not to bill insurance. Now the insurance considers us a bad pharmacy and the customer 'non-compliant.' When in fact we are all fixing a problem they created only to be labeled subpar by the insurance companies. Insurance won't let it be about the patient. Pharmacy has very little to do with medication anymore. Pharmacy schools no longer teach all of the drug interactions because there are too many for a person to realistically learn. The computer does it most of the work. 90% of our job in pharmacy is to keep insurance from ripping off the pharmacy and our patients. Insurance has a number of tactics they will use to frustrate the doctor's office and the patient until they give up and give in to what the insurance wants to 'pay' for. The cost of drugs like inhalers and insulin are insane. It is very disheartening and I don't see it ever being solved. I am actually looking to get into another career field. I'd love to be able to reinvent pharmacy one day but that will take millions. I do want to restore the field back to its rightful place where it is about patients and community. It is about gaining more time on earth and improving the quality of that time. Niether is happening now.
Overall_Why: The nature of the career started out meaningful, and there are days where I can really make a difference and in pharmacy you are usually the last person to deal with the patient after they've been to the doctor etc. They usually feel they have spent lots of time and money but were rushed through the doctor's office, not seen for long, not listened to and then rescheduled to come back for the same experience in a few months. I do get to take my time with patients and listen to them, explain things that weren't explained by a nurse etc. Patients worry a lot about not being able to afford their medication. What really makes me feel good is to help find a drug that will work for them and that they can easily afford. Seeing the relief on their face and usually their spouses is very rewarding. Their entire demeanor changes once they feel like things are going to be okay. In pharmacy, we are the closest to the patients. However, the drug companies and insurance companies are making it more and more difficult to do this. The job would be greatly rewarding if it could be what it was meant to be and not to be an extension of billion-dollar clueless greedy corporations. I spend so much time running in circles to try and meet their demands that are in the insurance contracts that no pharmacy has any say over. The contracts I'm sure are illegal in some areas but no one can afford the legal fight. I want to be able to help people without someone who is familiar with stockholders, not patients telling me what needs to happen, how, when and if it needs to happen at all.

Work_Story: I work in an informal office setting with three other people. I am a graphic artist at a print shop, but my duties range from customer service, answering phones, making copies, design, printing, bindery work, shipping, billing, and general cleanup of the office. We are a corporate owned company, but are a fairly independent off-shoot of the corporate office. My office manager reports to corporate office, yet I report directly to the site manager. Many days I'm given duties that would normally be considered that of an assistant manager and put me in the middle of employee disputes and the chain of command. Many days I end up with paper cuts or even cuts from an exacto blade as I may spend some of my day cutting up large posters and signs, applying prints to foam core, and adding hardware for hanging. I'm in charge of managing inventory for one of our fulfillment programs. This entails me counting inventory on a regular basis and re-ordering stock as needed to meet my clients' orders. Some days I may vary duties back and forth through out the facility, but other days I may just sit at my desk the majority of the day and design or typeset logos, flyers, brochures and business cards. Some days are spent loading and reloading the digital copier to get all orders printed in a timely fashion. I even end up doing some light maintenance on machines in order to add toner, fix jams, and calibrate the color settings. We have a large size laminator that allows us to easily mount posters, and also to create a hot-laminated sealed edge piece. The days when you load the laminate backwards and it accidentally glues to the rollers are especially difficult. We see many visitors every day including the mailman, UPS driver and Speedee delivery driver. We have a great relationship with all of our vendors. Interestingly enough, the company that I used to work for prior to starting this job is actually one of my current vendors, so I have a lot of inside information that has made me very knowledgeable in performing my duties of selling, pricing and ordering promotional products. I've built a great relationship with many of the local businesses, most of whom only want to deal with me when they come in for a project. We work with the local school, coffee shop, restaurants and shops, in addition to insurance companies, chiropractors and city hall. My job has been a great confidence boost to remind me that I can still be creative, yet also produce final pieces instead of just being a production artist with no creative opportunities. I feel I am very comfortable in my job duties, but somedays do get bored. I make a long drive to my job every day since I purchased a home nearly 90 minutes away from my job Ideally, I would love to be able to work from home someday, but with the amount of physical labor I do on any given day, it's not possible. It doesnt stop me from asking every so often. I do not see myself staying there more than a couple of years. I need to get back on my feet after buying my house, and wait until my children are old enough to drive before I can give up the commute.
Overall_Why: I do believe that print media is a dying industry with all the advances in social media and internet news, media and web. But there is still a great need for a printed piece to accompany people in some professions. The local schools have stopped printing lunch menus and newsletters, but they still need to have health information cards for students to fill out. We produce a lot of forms and invoices in carbon copy that a lot of companies with older generations still run, so they dislike new technology. I feel that as my demand will lessen even more over the years to come, what we do print is a greatly appreciated by all customers that we see on a daily, weekly, or monthly basis. In another facet of our job, we print stock signage for several local grocery stores that they print sale signs on. It is a great need for them to not use electronic signage yet. Although I do see that business shifting more and more as well. Promotional products are another area that are still very useful and in demand for clients to give out at local parades in the summer, and also to get their name in peoples hands. I love providing someone with a pen, can cooler, chip clip or even tote bag that has their logo on it and they will love to hand out as a freebie to all their customers. I'm always happy to help with any project that comes my way.

Work_Story: I'm a mobile DJ and run the business I do that work through. I offer various services including bar trivia, karaoke, weddings, club DJ, etc. I'd say it's pretty rewarding because I get to be creative and make people happy. It does have it's downsides, though. I typically deal with mostly drunk people and they can be pretty needy and annoying. While I do drink, I don't get drunk and I have zero patience for people who act out when they're drunk. Ultimately the way I accept that this is still a good career choice is I realized that everyone's job has at least one thing about it they don't like. So other than that it's fantastic. Trivia is easily my favorite part about my job. I come up with some pretty good questions that people really enjoy. My show was even voted the best in the city I live in by a local magazine. Stuff like that keeps me going through the more aggravating parts. I'm also very fortunate that in my industry, there aren't too many people who take it that seriously. Most people who host trivia or karaoke do it as a part time job. This is my full time job and career choice and like everything in my life, I take it seriously. That dedication shows in my work. Recently I realized that this job has changed me, as well. I used to love going out and listening to music. Since that's my entire job, I don't go out anymore and I don't listen to music. When I'm in the car I just listen to podcasts. It's a weird effect but it's not one I mind. I do enjoy the music while I'm at work. I guess that's another thing that changed me too. I went from liking mostly rock and metal to enjoying pop songs and bangers. Lately I've been liking the songs that I know will get people going and dancing. Still, though, I don't listen to them in the car. Another great thing about my job is that I get to hang out with my friends. Since I don't go out often, my friends come out to my shows to hang out with me. My shows are also where I met all of my friends (aside from the ones I've made before I worked in this industry). What a lot of people don't realize is my job also requires a lot of work done at home. The amount of time preparing for trivia, club DJ nights and especially weddings is more than people expect. So even though my total hours working outside of the home is around 25 hours, adding the time working and preparing for my shows at home brings it to at least 50 hours. I don't mind the hours though because it's work that I'm making for myself. Most people won't put in the extra time and energy to make their shows better. It's because I work at home too that I'm the best at what I do. I guess that also makes me more frustrated with other people too. I see most people give minimal effort in their jobs and then complain all the time. I have a pretty fun job that most people would love to do, but I don't complain about things that are within my control. At least I don't think I do. When I was younger I wanted to be in the Airforce. In high school I wanted to be a Director. After that I thought I'd do something in computers. After all these changes, I've never had a job/career that I was so dedicated to. One that I would commit my entire life to. I love my job and the work that I do.
Overall_Why: I do mostly because this job allows me to express myself. It sounds silly to say that most of what I do is trivia, karaoke and club DJ, but there are a lot of things that are going on that are not apparent to most people. I consider myself a smart guy. With trivia, it's challenging to come up with a game that is difficult enough to have fun, but not so much that people feel stupid. Add to it my own brand of humor and each game is an expression of myself and the style that I go for when entertaining people. With karaoke, I manipulate each singer's vocals so they sound as good as possible. I make people happy and feel special. It may only be for a moment but when I see them completely immersed in the song and lost in the moment, I know I've done some good. For all I know that could be the best thing that has happened for that person all week. At the very least it's not the worst. Finally, with club DJing, that one is easy to explain. I make people dance and forget about their problems. When I train DJs to work for me, I always tell them to keep it nice and loud and avoid silence at all costs. The reason why is because silence is when they can hear their thoughts again. If they're able to hear their thoughts then they remember where they are. If they do that then all the problems in their life have the opportunity to come back in their head. When I'm working and I see people lost in the moment, it makes me feel very fulfilled.

Work_Story: I currently work as a lactation consultant a large women's hospital. I work alone on an overnight shift. My main duty is to see the mothers who may qualify for the WIC program. I am there to target these low income mothers. It has been proven that low income mothers do not breastfeed their babies as long as other mothers, if they breastfeed at all. I educate and assist these mothers in any way that I can. When I come into the office at night, I go through the current patients for the day. I look in the computer to see who has been seen and who has not been seen. Of the ones that have not been seen yet, I will visit them. There are also many that usually need a follow visit to be done that night. I will look up their feedings and any other information that may be helpful to me. I like to read previous notes left by the other lactation consultants and their nurses. This information gives me a good idea of where the patient is in breastfeeding and how much help they have received so far. I will look at birth weight, weight loss, or weight gain. If there is a large amount of weight loss, I can gauge if feeding is going well or if possibly there may be a need for intervention. I also look at the feedings so far. This is very important on how I will start my visit with the mother. If I see many breast feedings that have been good, meaning baby is sucking and swallowing at the breast, I may not need to have to assist so much. If I see a lot of poor feedings at the breast, I will most likely need to do a lot of teaching at the visit. I also need to see if the baby is taking any formula. If I see a formula feeding mom, I may just need to do education on breastfeeding and its benefits or just educate the mom on how to deal with milk coming in if she does not wish to breastfeed. If a baby is being supplemented, I need to know how it is being done. A baby is is taking expressed breast milk can be fed this milk by many alternate feeding methods. Some may feed with a cup, a spoon, a syringe, or a bottle. Some methods may affect a baby going to the breast and latching correctly. A cup or a spoon is the ideal way to feed a baby with any supplemented expressed breast milk or formula. If a baby is taking a bottle, it is possible that this could affect their learning to suck on their mother's breast. If needed, I will teach the parents all of the alternate feeding methods in order to find one that they are comfortable with. Another important factor is the parents social history. If I have a very young mother, maturity may be a factor. In contrast, An older mother may be set in her ways. If this is their fist baby or their fifth is very important. It is helpful to know if their previous breastfeeding experiences, if they have any, have been successful. If a mother is on any medication, legal or illegal, is extremely important. Some drugs contradict breastfeeding and others are very safe. If a mother is using illegal drugs or has a history of drug use, I will have to approach the situation very differently. If a baby is going through withdrawal, feeding can look very different than it would for a healthy baby. Homeless, depression, and anxiety are all very helpful things to be aware of. At times, I will come in contact with an abuse victim. Knowing that there may be some post traumatic stress associated with this is very important. These mom's I have to very careful not to touch unless they feel completely comfortable with me doing so. When I visit a mother's room I introduce myself and ask how feeding has been going. This will usually start a conversation about the past feedings. Mom is able to explain how she feels things are going and is able to ask any questions. I will need to probe a lot about the feeding because some mother's may feel feeding is going well, when in fact, it is not. I ask about many questions to find out how the feedings have been going. How confident mom is with latching the baby? Does she feel comfortable holding and positioning the baby? Does the baby latch quickly or does the baby fuss a lot at the breast. How does the latch feel? Is she feeling any pain at all at initial latch or throughout the feeding. What does the pain feel like? How long does the baby stay latched? Can she recognize an active feed versus a comfort feed. Is she hearing any swallows when the baby is at the breast? How is the baby's output and if the baby is meeting input/output goals? These questions will vary depending on the answers and need of the mother. If help is needed, I am there to assist. I will help with teaching the mother and significant other how to position and latch a baby. We will go through the many different positions and I will help mom with all of them if needed. If a baby is fussy and ready to feed at the time of the visit, we may only review one because the baby will not stay calm enough to try all of them at that particular visit. Every situation is different and I have to adjust my teachings as needed. Some babies may latch right onto the breast without difficultly or much assistance. Other babies are more tricky. I could have a very sleepy baby or a baby just a few hours old. These babies do not show much interest in feeding and no matter how much you try to wake and them and get them to feed, they will not. In these circumstances, I teach mom hand expression and offer baby drops of colostrum. If mom is unable to hand express on her own, with permission, I will demonstrate. I always try to have mom mirror back what I am showing her. Hand expression can be difficult. Even with demonstrating to her, she may need additional help to pull the colostrum out. If dad is in the room, I will ask him to assist mom. If he is unavailable or unwilling, I will assist mom as needed. Some mothers can catch onto how to hold the baby quickly and others not as much.
