Visits can range in time greatly. I like to do a lot of education as to why we are doing things the way we are, so the mother can feel confident. Confidence is very important with breastfeeding. Parents will be home without assistance in just a few days and will need to learn how to breastfeed on their own. I always try to be very hands off because of this. I like the mother and father to learn what and how to feed the baby with as little hands on help as possible. Some women do require a lot more hands on help than others, but I always try to have them do it first. If we are unable to latch a baby, for whatever reason, at my initial visit, I have them call for me next visit and we try again. I will make visits throughout the night and sometimes go back for every feeding. There is a lot of education that falls under learning how to breastfeed. I like to teach moms how to hand express their milk because every breastfeeding woman should be able to do this. If needed, I will also show them how to set up and use a breast pump. There are a lot of different pumps available now, but the most popular ones all have the same concept. If the mother has her personal pump with her, I will set up and show her how to use this one. I will review all the different cycles and speeds. I will also review cleaning of the pump and how to put it together. I will review storage guidelines for expressed milk if necessary. If she does not have her personal pump with her, I will set up the hospital grade pump and explain its use. I always go over amounts expected so moms will not be discouraged when only a drop or two of colostrum is expressed the first day. The majority of breastfeeding women will need to pump their milk at some point and this is a crucial skill to learn. I will also discuss normal newborn behavior and what to expect the first few days. The first 24 hours most babies will be very sleepy and not show much interest in feeding. I encourage them to watch for feeding cues and when they are observed, they should attempt a feed. If baby is unable to latch, hand express and offer drops. If no feeding cues are observed after three hours, attempt to wake the baby feed. Again, if unable to wake or latch a baby, hand express and feed drops of colostrum. We will discuss feeding cues and what they look like. The most common feeding cues are the baby sticking its tongue, hands to mouth, and sucking on hands. I encourage as much skin to skin as possible. This allows mom to notice early feeding cues and breastfeeding is proven to be more successful when a baby is fed with early cues versus a baby's late cues or crying. We will discuss the baby's input and output and how this will show if feedings are going well. How many wet and dirty diapers the first few days is the main way to show a baby is feeding well. We have a breastfeeding log here at the hospital where parents can mark off any feeds and any diapers. Now there are also countless apps available to do the same thing. I encourage parents to keep these logs and bring them to their first pediatric appointment. This is very important information for the doctor and they will surely ask for this information. Many parents have an array of questions when I enter the room. I am available to them to answer whatever they maybe questioning. I always give the parents outside support systems for when they go home, if they were ever to need them. A lot of my job is offering support and empowering women to give them confidence that they can feed their babies and their body has enough milk to feed their babies. After a visit, I will chart the visit and any feedings that may have occurred. If a patient needs more follow up, I leave the information for the day lactation consultants.
Overall_Why: I love the job that I do. I am there when parents and infants are the most vulnerable. I can be a deciding factor on weather this baby and mother dyad is going to be successful with breastfeeding. There is no better feeling when a baby latches for the first time and you see the mother and/or fathers face. I am able to empower these women to fulfill a goal. I am there to guide them into something they want for themselves and their children. I am the one who is right there in the middle of the night and they are ready to quit. They are sore and tired, but I help them continue on and do what is right for them. I feel as if I am the rock for them sometimes. I can help them through a tough time. If unable to latch the baby, I can give the alternatives to just get through that feeding. I offer them hope and support. I educate and reassure them that things will get easier. Breastfeeding is shown to be beneficial to mom and baby. There are so many diseases that can be prevent with just breast milk. I am not only helping the family, but also helping society.

Work_Story: I am a restaurant manager in a quaint, downtown area in {state}. I have been working there for about 10 years; I started off as a server and bartender, becoming a manager only 3 years ago. I enjoy my job and I look forward to going to work. The owners, a married couple, are great people that would give you the shirts off their backs. I typically go in every day at 9:45 and I work until 4:00 pm, sometimes 4:30. We are closed on Sundays. Sometime I come in as needed, or take phone calls if someone has a question that they cannot answer themselves. I take pride in my work and I like feeling needed. The owners are very involved with the day to day operations, and I take over their tasks when they go on vacation or just need a break. This includes ordering the food, alcohol, chemicals for cleaning, and any other odds and ends that are necessary. I do not make the schedule, the owner does; I do check over it for her and make any adjustments that are needed. Monday is the only exception to my 9:45 am-4 pm hours; I work a double shift that day and stay until we close at 9 pm. I am behind the bar that entire day. I make anywhere from $150-$300 for that entire shift. You might be wondering what a typical day consists of for me. I arrive at 9:45 am, clock in, fix myself a cup of coffee, and get the schedule; I write down who is working and I assign the sections. I base it on who wants a "staying section," meaning they stay until 4 pm, and who wants a "leaving section", meaning they leave around 2 or whenever it slows down. Some sections are bigger or busier than others so I also evaluate the crews strengths and who can handle which section. After that is situated; I start making tea, and setting up the tea station. I stock napkins, cups, fill up the sugar bin and also the box we keep the tea bags in. Next, I go into the dining room and wipe the bases of the tables off. I then start putting down the chairs. I'm really funny about making sure the tables are exactly where they are supposed to be, I hate it when the dining room isn't perfect. I windex, I clean bathrooms, and sometimes I sweep and mop. I basically open the front of the house with 2 other people, while there are 3 doing the back of the house. We open at 11. We serve a variety of hot and cold sandwiches, wraps, fried shrimp, crab cakes, kabobs, salads, shrimp and fried cheese grits, salmon, and we have two cuts of steak. We run a $5.99 lunch special Monday-Friday that includes a tea. We are probably the busiest restaurant in my town. We are known for our friendly and efficient staff our fair prices, waterfront location with patio seating, and our consistency. I interact with a large base of "regulars", customers that come in more than three times a week. We even have a few that stop in every day! As a restaurant manager, I still wait tables during my managing shifts. I wouldn't have it any other way. I start off with a smile, an introduction, our specials spiel, and I ask what everyone is drinking. I get the drinks, give them time to look at the menu, answer any questions, get the tables order, key it in to our computers, and bring them the food once it is ready. It may not sound like much, but it is very fast-paced and difficult. Not everyone is good at multi-tasking and staying on top of a busy lunch rush. I am fast and efficient. I enjoy my job and I like to think that people can notice that. I have been working there for quite some time by some standards. It's without a doubt the best restaurant I have ever worked in, and I love my job and my customers.
Overall_Why: I do find my work very meaningful. At my very core, I am a social, active person that demands instant gratification.I am happiest when I am making others happy. I do not like being confined or restrained. A restaurant is the perfect place for me. It allows me to be around people of all different backgrounds, and interact with them on a personal level at times. I have free reign of the dining room floor, I do what I need to do to make the customer happy, and I make most of my wages by the day. Earning money after each table satisfies my need for instant gratification. I don't have to wait, I get it right then and there. I feel like I would not like to be confined to a chair or an office. I am much happier being myself and not having to put on a facade to please someone. As a manager, I get to make decisions and delegate tasks as needed. There is a refreshing "openness" to what I do. It feels like each table that I serve is a clean slate. It's a new experience, it's a new chance to talk, it's a new chance to be my best, it's a new chance to earn a tip. It's almost hard to explain. Being on the move is so liberating to me. Due to the qualities that I possess, being a restaurant manager and server is more meaningful to me than I ever thought I could deserve.

Work_Story: Firstly, this is an interesting question because people in my line of work are constantly made fun of for how arrogant or full of ourselves we are. For this reason, and because that's mostly true, I never bring up my work in a social setting, and its an accident if I do. Whenever someone asks me about my work, I'm sheepish and lead with, I work for the government. If they persist, I tell them I'm in the military. If they wonder still, I'll tell them I'm in the Marine Corps. Normally, by this point they're sufficiently weirded out by my elusive answers or just don't care to go farther...but in the off chance they do I reveal I'm a pilot. My job is to fly for the Marine Corps in a host of different mission sets. This, though, is only half of my work, because I also have a 'ground job', or a job that doesn't entail flying, but rather contributes to the overall operation and running of the unit I'm with. My ground job is in maintenance where I manage a department that repairs the aircraft when they break or requiring replacing of sort. My job requires a ton of schooling, and that is pretty long. It's a pain while you're in it, but you don't realize until you're out of school just how great an opportunity it was. Flying is challenging, at least in the beginning, because it's a combination of skills you don't have with physiological factors that you're ability to cope with and adapt to will either assist or greatly hinder your ability to learn the massive skill set you've undertaken that you have no idea how to accomplish. Aside from your steep initial learning curve, it begins to taper out once you get some experience and flight time under your belt. This is why you hear the airlines talk about experience in flight hours and their requirement for flight hours before you can join or progress in their ranks. After the task of maintaining balanced flight becomes simple enough, you progress to much more complicated tasks such as flying on instruments alone, all the way until you can fly your aircraft and fight the enemy or conduct your tactical missions simultaneously. This task, and the qualifications you achieve which indicate you level of skill and proficiency in the aircraft are how my job measures one pilot to the next (excluding ground job). On the ground job side of things, this is more a manpower and funding necessity than anything. The Marines pride themselves on doing more with less, but the only way they can stake that claim is by spreading more work over the workforce they currently, have, which is exactly what they(and no other service) do. This is one of the reasons its common knowledge that the Marines have the lowest quality of life and highest workload, in general, of all the services (I'm not bitter or anything). But really, it's a supremely lucky job to have. Few other jobs in the world have the responsibility of a pilot, it is a significant undertaking. Much is expected because even more is entrusted to me. My job has more good days than bad, but it also takes a positive mental attitude to get through a lot of the difficult aspects. But, my job shares a lot of similarities with my friends' jobs, i.e. bad bosses, bad hours on occasion, and the like.
Overall_Why: My work is meaningful because there is a really high amount of responsibility that comes with it. It's designed that way from day 1 it almost seems. It takes 2 years just to get your wings and call yourself an aviator. I think this is why so many aviators seem cocky or arrogant, it's a hard feat that few attempt and even fewer succeed at. Then those that go on and reach the apex of that profession are in an extreme minority. So, I find it meaningful, not just because of the pursuit and time invested to get there, but also because I make decisions every day that affect the lives of others and that's a very real and rare position to be in. I'm not sure I'll ever get paid to be in that spot again, actually. On the ground side of my job, I find it meaningful because every day I get the chance to meaningfully impact the lives of the people who work for or with me as I like to think. They rely on my ability to mentor, guide, learn, and instruct them. Their experience, their lives, are directly impacted by the amount of work I do for them and on their behalf. Work that, frankly, I'm not required to do at all. So on the ground side of things, I would also say that my work is meaningful. A personal value of mine is that you should leave a position, place, whatever, in better shape than you left and always lend a helping hand if you're in a position to do so.

Work_Story:
I have worked in higher education for the past 22 years in various capacities and currently serve as the Executive Director for Associated Student Incorporated (ASI) at {company}. Currently, I oversee student government at {company}. On each of the 23 {company} campuses, ASI is a separately incorporated 501(c)3 non-profit organization and we function as an Auxiliary of the University. I report to both the Vice President of Student Affairs and the ASI President. 
 As the Executive Director, I am an ex-officio member of the ASI Board of Directors and in my role, I am the primary advisor to not only the Board of Directors but the Executive Board and five ASI committees. In total, I advise, mentor, and work with 28 students. I meet weekly with each member of the Executive Board to assist them with projects and issues they are working on. I also attend each committee meeting where I serve as a resource for their endeavors. I am responsible for all administrative functions of the organization while helping the students enact their strategic plan/yearly goals, advise them on how to navigate the University, assist them in planning programs, events, and campaigns for 10,000 students, and work them with to develop our legislative agenda. In addition, I am the budget manager for the ASI Budget totaling $3.7 million dollars. I provide the Executive Board monthly updates on their spending and help them to plan for upcoming expenses. I also supervise a part time administrative coordinator and 2 student workers.
 Each year, I work with the Executive Board to plan 2 retreats which we use to train and develop our Board members. In the Fall, we work on team dynamics, vision and goals for the year, leadership development, and parliamentary procedure. In the Spring, we focus on team dynamics, leadership development, program planning, and Spring initiatives. 
 Due to my dual reporting, in addition to my work with ASI, I serve on the Senior Management team for Student Affairs. I sit on various University committees such as Food and Housing Security, Homecoming, GradFest, and the Commencement Reception. This year, I helped to secure a $102,000 grant to start our CSUB Food Pantry to help address the food insecurity issues our students are facing. In the first semester, we have served 712 unique users. I also recruit, coordinate, and train 55 volunteers for our monthly Food Distribution. 
 Another large project that ASI has taken the lead on in coordination with the President's Office, Student Affairs, Student Union, and the Student Recreation Center is a student referendum to build a new student union and recreation aquatic center. I am helping the students to design a 4-week campaign to build the reasoning why we need a new facility, how this new facility will impact the campus community, and why we need to do this project now. I will also coordinate 2 days of online voting including multiple polling location on campus.
Overall_Why:
I find my work to be extremely meaningful. I work at a Hispanic Serving Institution (HSI) where 70% of our students are first generation, 77% receive a Pell Grant, and over 40% are food and housing insecure. I have been able to create programs and opportunities for students who have never had access to many of these resources which are common on many campuses.
 
 My position in ASI was vacant for 2 years and the person they had in the past wasn't a higher education professional. Since coming to CSUB, I have been able to infuse leadership programming into our retreats, to develop a new way to structure the ASI Budget, to revise our ASI Bylaws and codes, to develop a computer refresh plan for the organization, to implement a transition program for newly elected Board members, and to plan an end of the year banquet and swearing in ceremony. I have been able to bring stability, fresh ideas, and a spirit of collaboration that was not present before.
 
 In my role, I helped to secure a $102,000 grant to start our CSUB Food Pantry in order to address the food insecurity issues our students are facing. In the first semester, we have served 712 unique users. I also recruit, coordinate, and train 55 volunteers for our monthly Food Distribution. 
 
 It has been very inspiring to see the impact my work is making on the campus community and I feel so fortunate to be able to make such a difference every day.

Work_Story:
My current role is embedded within the Human Resources department within a financial institution. I am personally responsible and held accountable for recruiting specific financial professionals within a very targeted segment. I'm tasked with a wide-breadth of responsibilities covering a multi-state footprint and a large headcount. 
 In addition to my actual job description my work at the core level involves a number of key elements. Communication is at the top of the list and one I work to refine and hone every day as it pertains to my audience. I have to ensure that if I'm presenting to leadership, I'm presenting with a goal of influencing decision-makers. If I'm interviewing a candidate, I want to ensure I am evaluating their skill set, their ability to make an impact, and their ability to fit culturally within the organization. Managing communication to our team is also another aspect of how I utilize this very important strength.
 Partnership would probably be another high on the list requisite of what my role entails. Much like in a political environment, I need to have the ability to reach across a variety of channels and levels of hierarchy to be effective. In some regards it's important to recognize when to take a step back and reposition your messaging so it aligns with your intention. Other times, it's more efficient and effective to be direct, to the point, and straight forward. Understanding how to approach your partners is an important foundation especially in my role.
 Metrics! I would say metrics is a huge part of what I do on a day to day basis. Once established, it's important that I am providing a solid return on my value add to the client, candidate, or customer I may be working with at that moment. If I leave a scenario and don't feel I've given my best, I often times make a note on where I may need to self-improve or invest in learning a little more about what changes I may need to implement. In addition to ROI, I need to ensure I'm utilizing the available resources I've been provided to do my job effectively. Sometimes when faced with an abundance of resources, some of us tend to function MORE effectively with a focus on 1 or 2 versus too may. That said, understanding why in a corporate culture we need to be mindful of an over abundance of resources is important.
 Probably of the utmost importance, I focus on the people. The people I work with, the people I want to work with, and just the human face of the corporate world on a daily basis. All too often, we get caught up in the rat race of the work world and it's important we stay real. It's important we stay engaged at that core human level. The level that enables us to reflect care and concern, or at the very least just a genuine interest in what everyone in your work "group" or "network" is responsible for during their workday. 
 Because of the work that I do, I am responsible for ensuring people are making a wise decision not just for their career path but for their client's future's as well.
Overall_Why:
I do find my work extremely meaningful. The reason for this is that my work, my role is about engagement with people from all walks of life. I am providing value to their long term goals, their client's long term goals, and the firm's culture ultimately. If I'm able to influence even one individual a day (a week, a month, etc) then I have made a positive impact on their world. In turn, they too have made a meaningful positive impact on mine. I'm personally invested with the individuals I partner with, I provide support to, and my circle of trust. That is an important role to play when you're in a corporate culture.
 There are of course, elements to my work that have an opportunity to be improved but all I am able to control is what is within my control. Therefore the work that I do, I tend to make sure it is meaningful to me. 
 I spend 8-9 hours a day at my job, I have the opportunity to work from home full time (and I do), and I also get an opportunity to travel here and there which enables me to see places and meet people I may not have otherwise had the chance to engage with.
 I do my best to make sure that if I find myself in a space that I don't believe is meaningful within my work day, that I make the effort to change that space. To change my approach so that I am securing meaning, adding meaning, and affecting meaning is what makes my work meaningful to me.

Work_Story: I am in charge of strategy at a mid sized (400 person) government agency in {city}. We fund organizations to deliver workforce development services (job coaching, resume review, etc), occupational training, business support (starting a business, marketing, financing, etc), help connecting to government contracts, and business attraction in commercial districts. My role is time work with the heads of each of four divisions (workforce, business services, neighborhood development, and government contacting) to create 5 year plans that outline the strategies (aka approaches) we will use to achieve milestones and work towards the Agency ideals. We have never had exactly the kind of strategic plans that I'm trying to create. We have had annual work plans, or PowerPoint decks outlining overall objectives, or high level "guiding principles" but never actual multi year maps that articulated the milestone we were trying to achieve in a specific period of time and the method (approach, not activity) that we were going to try to use to get there. In some ways it is lonely work, because although my boss is super engaged in this (and devotes a lot of her time to working with me and her direct reports, the leaders of each of the four divisions), I am the face and focus for this work for most of our staff. I was supposed to hire a team to work with me on this, but I couldn't find the right people at the level salary we were offering (we are a small government agency, and there is no flexibility in the range I can offer people). So I never hired staff. That has made me both less effective and less well regarded. I feel like I am failing. Of our four divisions, one went very well and the plan is complete (our highest performing division, and the best leader of the four). The second is mostly complete, but the division head has done nothing the roll out or implement the plan with the staff. She uses the piece that isn't done as an excuse. She is supposed to have three senior leaders under her, that manage the teams. One left in the first week of November, and no replacement has been identified. She's openly warring with another. The third is about to go out on maternity leave. So my work with that division doesn't seem totally successful, plus I don't have a ton of faith in implementation. I'm working on the strategic plans for the third division now. It's tough because their leader is generally adverse to defining anything. For example, in our working session on one of his strategy maps today, he kept insisting we make more "broad" what we were going to do to ensure more businesses were represented on BID boards (as opposed to property owners who are the supermajority on most BID boards now). It feels like he talks in circles and has difficulty saying anything definitively. I can imagine that has served him well in other (political) circumstances, but he's gotta know that we can't create a strategy map that is full of meaningless platitudes. It's supposed to be a document representing the specific things we will accomplish by year, and the actual way (approach, not activities) that we expect to get there! And the last division is the toughest case. It's the division my boss and I know best (she used to run it, and I was her chief of staff). It also has the absolute smartest leader (by far). Unfortunately, that division head has been failing, and not coming to work, and very clearly (to me at least) ignoring most of her responsibilities.
Overall_Why: Overall I do find my work meaningful. This is largely due to four factors (in order of importance to me): 1) I believe in the mission of my Agency to help individuals and businesses earn enough money to be safe, successful and self sustaining in New York City; 2) I believe in and take pride in our commitment to focus our efforts on those that have the least; 3) I think that most of the staff at my Agency truly cares about and for the mission; 4) I see a way I could contribute to the Agency, the staff and the mission by using my brain, that would result in something that would actually be impactful. Of course there are parts of my job that I worry may make my work meaningless. My top two concerns are: 1) my boss and the Agency head (her boss), seem to keep promoting or supporting people into senior leadership roles who are not competent. It's widely known (I think) that one of the four division heads is a lame duck/do nothing, and that another has lost the confidence of both my boss and the Agency head. That's 50% of our leadership! And no amount of an excellent strategic plan will have any impact if our leadership can't drive change. 2) I don't know what I'm doing. No one ever taught or showed me this before. Our Agency never did plans like this before. None of the division heads seem to be able to articulate strategies even for their current portfolio (never mind the next five years).
JOB_TITLE: executive director of strategy and impact

Work_Story:
I am currently a School Counselor in a public high school. I have held this position for close to five years. Prior to that, I worked at the same school as a Business/Computer teacher. I have always had a passion for working with others and being a leader, so becoming a teacher was a natural fit for me. I truly enjoyed the eight years that I spent in the classroom. I worked with a variety of academic levels, teaching everything from Microsoft Office courses to Web Design courses. I also taught a Microsoft Office class for students with learning disabilities. That was perhaps the most rewarding for me. After several years in the classroom, I longed to make more of a difference in students' lives, and wanted to reach more students at one time. Thus I decided to become a School Counselor. 
 
 My job entails a wide variety of responsibilities. I am charged with ensuring my students' academic and mental health needs are met, as well as participating in school activities. I currently service close to 400 students, and must make sure that each and every one of them are taking the appropriate courses. When students are not successful in the classroom, I reach out to their parents/guardians to develop a plan to help them get back on track. I help students prepare for their post-secondary plans, including, but not limited to, college, the military, and the workforce. I write many letters of recommendations, and help students apply for the programs they want to pursue. Because I am a Counselor at a Title I school, I service many first-generation graduates, and thus have to educate many of them on their opportunities after graduation.
 
 In addition to the academic setting, I helps students with mental health needs. I am often the first point of contact for students who deal with depression, anxiety, self-harm, or suicidal ideation. I work with the families to ensure the students' safety, and provide community resources to those who are trained to address the critical issues. I feel that mental healthcare is becoming a very big need in schools, and we could often use someone trained specifically in that area to help our students. Because of the number of hats that School Counselors must wear, we feel very thin in our ability to truly help so many kids. 
 
 Many times we have students who are facing great challenges at home. Again, at a Title I school, this can often be magnified. I have many students who are working jobs until late at night to help support their families. I have students who are in fear of deportation, even though they were brought here at a very young age. I have students who are dealing with abusive home lives, in which case we have to involve the Department of Family Services. 
 
 One of the other big parts of my job is to be an active member of the school administration team. This includes supervision of students before school, between classes, at lunches, and after schools. It also involves participation in extra-curricular activities, such as attending awards events, sporting events, and other school-wide activities. I participate in planning meetings for staff development, staff growth, etc. 
 
 I absolutely love my job, but it is very mentally draining. The impact that one person has on a child's academic success and mental health is a heavy weight to bear. There are often nights where I wake up in the middle of the night worried that I have not put a student in the correct class, or worried about a child who is going through a rough time at home. I wouldn't have it any other way, however. I see myself continuing in the realm of education until I retire.
Overall_Why:
My job is very meaningful. Although it sounds extreme, it is true that there are childrens' lives at stake every day. I have students who have suicidal thoughts, and I have students who are facing abuse. I have to make very quick and important decisions daily about how I will handle these situations. I often second-guess myself on choices that I make, but you have to feel confident with the choices that you do make. It also helps that I have a team that I can bounce ideas off on a daily basis. 
 
 In addition, I feel that the role I play in my students' academic careers is just as meaningful. From helping students pick classes, to helping them focus on a path they want to take, to helping them plan for their post-secondary options, each of these things will have a great impact on them down the road. 
 
 I often have students come back to visit, and to tell me about their successes, or even their heartaches. Some took different paths than they originally chose. But when they come back to tell me about these things, it helps me see the role I played in their lives, no matter how tiny. As a teacher, I felt my job was meaningful, but I always though I could do more. When I was making a choice about what path of education I wanted to go into, I felt that I could find more meaning as a School Counselor, as opposed to something else, such as administration.

Work_Story:
I'm a partner in a large regional accounting firm, specializing in nonprofit organizations, their for profit subsidiaries and donor issues. I advise my clients on how the tax code applies to their existing and planned activities. The areas of advice vary widely as I'm an advisor to my clients and coordinate services from many specialists. The organizations I serve span from newly formed organizations of all sizes, small community organizations to very large foundations, universities, hospitals and retirement communities. 
 In addition to consulting work, I supervise several teams who serve the compliance needs of these organizations. The teams of employees I'm responsible for are located in several offices, so we work virtually together to gather information, prepare, review and sign annual returns. Part of this process includes answering questions on information to be gathered, how the returns relate to similar organizations, how changes in the tax code affect the organization, and what IRS and state regulator activity to be aware of.
 In order to appropriately advise our clients, I spend time weekly reading articles, court cases, rulings and commentary on issues affecting tax exempt organizations, employers, individuals and for-profit businesses. I write articles as a contributor to our firm's newsletters, and identify opportunities for people on my team to author or co-author articles and alerts.
 I am a seasoned presenter for our firm and outside conferences. I regularly prepare and present sessions on a variety of topics affecting tax exempt organizations, in person and by webcast. As a highly rated speaker, I have many opportunities to teach during the year, and focus on conferences or sections of the industry where I have significant clients or experience to share.
 Several years ago I moved to my current location which is no a large metro market. I do not live in an area where there are many clients of the size and complexity the firm expects and have chosen a narrow specialty, so I travel frequently to visit clients and present at conferences. This has resulted in my clients being serviced by teams who are physically located in many different offices, which provides opportunities for people in the firm who want to work in this specialty area to do so without requiring they relocate or be employed in a particular office.
 In addition to supervising teams in my specialty group, I have a responsibility for the people employed in my local office regardless of the group they work in regularly. As the only partner in my non-metro office, the employees there look to me for information and guidance on a regular basis. I participate in weekly and monthly meetings regarding the operations of the office and region and assist where possible with administrative tasks. 
 Part of my responsibilities are involvement in regional team selling with my partners and marketing meetings to strategize pursuits. I host monthly check in-calls for the firm to connect similarly experienced people serving tax exempt organizations and the high level people in their teams.
Overall_Why:
Yes, I find my work extremely meaningful. I chose accounting as a career in high school as I connected with the logic of bookkeeping and enjoyed working with numbers. As I progressed through college, I chose tax as my focus as I appreciated having a set of rules to work within in the form of the Internal Revenue Code, court cases and rulings. Once I graduated and received by CPA license, I worked on a variety of clients for several years before being introduced to nonprofits. When I was given the opportunity work with these tax exempt organization, I learned everything I could about this specific area of the Code and set it as my goal to become a nationally know speaker on tax exempt issues. I attained this goal several years ago and continue to have many opportunities to share my knowledge and experience with clients. 
 Working with tax exempt organizations as their business advisor is very satisfying as I see the good these organizations do to help people in a wide variety of areas, and I have a part in helping them fulfill this purpose. I have the opportunity to work with donors who are very generous in supporting charitable activities they believe in. I have the pleasure of working with people wanting to start charitable organizations to fill a need they are passionate about. As a teacher, I'm given a platform with an audience who is interested in knowing more about this specialized area as well.

Work_Story:
I work in education in a urban Pre-school with ages 3-5 as a Instructional assistant (Paraprofessional) Monday through Friday from 7:00 a.m -3;15 p.m. and some days beyond school hours.On a daily basis, I work closely with the classroom teacher, reinforcing the classroom teachers instructions by giving the students individual attention and hands-on-assistance, and leading small group instruction either through reading stories, providing writing assistance, helping reinforce language skills, numbers, and alphabetic knowledge. I maintain classroom equipment, manual and electronic files, and records on a daily basis. 
  I help other staff as needed with clerical duties such as organizing paper work or files, photocopying or producing specific materials needed in the classroom. I sometimes provide one-on-one assistance to students that need modified learning activities,also help the classroom teacher target specific students that may need extra academic intervention, and also administer individual assessments on students that are sent to the state that shows their academic growth, or student weaknesses that may need to be addressed throughout the school year. I assistant in maintaining discipline in and outside the classroom. I attend weekly Professional learning committees with other instructional assistants and classroom teachers, so we can share ideas, compare data on academic growth of the students. I assistant with planning after school functions such as parent teacher association,talent shows, and holiday assemblies for students and parents as needed. 
 I sometimes fill in as a sub when other classroom teachers are not present for the day, or provide coverage for the school secretary when needed. I assist in providing technology to the children use of computer activities or lessons. I supervise the students outside of the classroom while on the playground, during bathroom breaks, lunch time, and field trips as needed. 
 I attend meetings and in-service presentations outside of work hours that provide relative information to my job functions that helps promote and achieve professional growth.
 I meet with parents or guardians in their homes to discuss their child's progress and needs, and suggest ways that they can also help the child promote learning and normal development in the home.
  I provide the students the ability to have what they consider a school mother, by offering conversation, compassion, encourage social interactions,provide them a creative learning environment, and treating their emergency situations as they may arise. I regularly schedule the children times to meet with other school personal such as the school nurse, guidance counselor, and behavior specialist on a rotating basis. I have to maintain a high-level of respect for diversity, excellent listening skills, positive reinforcement skills, patience, understanding of different learning abilities. I provide feedback to the classroom teacher on children that I work with on a daily basis so that she can monitor and document any potential concerns, I plan along side the classroom teacher daily so that we can prepare a enriched lesson to the students on the following day. I occasionally may assist in the computer lab teaching the students how to use the computers and software the right way.
Overall_Why:
I think my job is extremely meaningful, I have little children that look up to me to give them their foundation into school. They are excited to learn new ideas and concepts, and to me there is no better feeling with I see the smile on their faces when they have begin to master these concepts.I am give them the opportunity to feel safe, and feel love being that they are so young and sometimes their first experience away from parents. I couldn't imagine this feeling satisfaction that comes over you when you are doing something that is rewarding to you and the parents or guardians in the children's lives.
 I think working with children brings out the best in me, even when I don't feel like getting up going, or things in my life are not going so good, I can go to work and they give me life. I love my job and have felt more and more compassion for what I do year after year. I don't think there is any other job that would give me the feeling of being accomplished in what I do, and you see instant results everyday in the progress of the children. I love when parents and other members of the community thank for what you do everyday making a difference in the lives of so many different children, and it's not because you have to do, it is because you want to, and it gives you joy, and brings meaning to your life everyday.

Work_Story:
I am a full time ESL (English as a Second Language teacher). I help foreigners learn English including speech, listening, writing, and grammar. I work for a few schools since I don't have enough classes with either one alone. While I teach most levels of learners, my main focus is with intermediate to advanced learners.Specifically my specialty is teaching business English which is a subset of general English training. Most of my students are business professionals that need to learn or improve their English for work. The student in this case are very motivated because they know learning English will improve their opportunities at work and their employers want them to have this skill. I mostly teach in the afternoon and evening as this is when the students can take the time for their lessons. Lessons are usually one to two hours long.
 Some classes use books that are provided by the school and are based on the students' level. Other times I must design the lessons for the class. So besides actually teaching in the class, a lot of my time is spent preparing lessons for each as class. It is important to prepare each class so that the time in the classroom is efficient and purposeful. Classes usually dont go very well if I dont have my lessons planned out. Other parts of my job are to grade tests, review homework, and answer student questions. At the end of the month or end of the course I also have to submit my reviews and grades of each student. This also includes giving each student feedback as to how they are progressing. The schools use this to decide whether the student advances to the next level or not. Besides working for the schools, I also teach as a freelance English tutor. THis means that I teach private lessons to students outside of school. These may be students who need more help, focused learning, or to prepare for an exam. Here again, I need to not only teach the lesson, but need to prepare the lessons beforehand and review material afterwards. Most of my private classes take place at a coffee shop or another public place that is convenient to meet with the student. I usually. look for a place that is quiet and has wifi so that I can use online material for my lessons. Since I have several private students, I need to constantly work on my schedule and contact my students about class times and location. Many times students will need to change or cancel the class and I am constantly reworking my schedule. I spend a lot of time exchanging emails, instant messages, and telephone calls, with my students to confirm classtimes and to possibly make changes. Since these are my private students, I also need to handle billing and collecting my fees. So bookkeeping is also part of my job. And finally, I am always looking for new students so i need to spend time advertising and marketing my services.
 So how did I get into teaching English? Well I worked for a long time as a technician. I studied this in college and had several jobs in the the telecommunications field. I enjoyed it for some time but the industry got a little unsettled and through no fault of my own, i lost several jobs over the course of 3 years. THe companies either went out of business or scaled down.
 Teaching is something that always interested me. I like interacting with people and I like the feeling that I am helping people. SO when I was let go from my last job, I decided to take the time andtake a course in teaching. It was a great experience even though at first I wasent sure it was really for me. I was quite nervous when I first began teaching in front of a class full of students. But slowly after more and more time teaching and gaining experience, my confidence level rose and I really started to enjoy the experience. Many of my students come from all over the world so I not only get to teach but I also learn about thier individual countries and cultures. I have worked with students from Europe, Asia, Africa, central and south America. The great thing is that they all bring something different to the class so none of my classes are everr the same. They all have a little different feel to them. Most of my classes now are taught with people from MExico. SO my classes typically have students with the same culture. THis doesent however diminish the diversity in the class. Most students have differnt stories and reasons why they are taking the class. THere homelife is different and they may have very different jobs. So there is still a lot of things to make the class different.
 Teaching English can be hard at times but I enjoy it. I like creating classes and coming up with different topics
Overall_Why: I find my work meaningful because I provide a service that people are in need of. My students want to learn English for various reasons. They come to my classes with expectations to learn more English and improve their ability to communicate better. Learning English is very important for them because English is a very important language to know in the world today. It is the language used for business, commerce, social engagements. Many people want to learn English so I feel I fill a need that is wanted. People appreciate a goomy work meaningful becaused teacher and I am often thanked for helping my student learn and improve thier language ability. It is very rewarding to see when my students are able to use more English to express them selves. It is something than we can see with results right away. As they improve, their confidence level goes up and they really feel like they are getting better.I find my work meaningful because it improves peoples lives. Maybe they can get a better job and make more money. Maybe they can communicate with people in a new country or with new friends. Having these skills really makes their lives better and they can use the skills. For this reason I think teaching is very meaning ful. It give people more opportunity and abilities to do more in life. If it helps them make a better life that is the reward I see for being a teacher. SOmetimes I keep in touch with my students and see the results later on

Work_Story: My full time job is for a natural gas utility company as a Operations Foreman. My job entails supervising 6 employees daily activities. Coordinating their daily schedules, scheduling their customer appointments and making sure that they have the parts needed to complete the job. I handle all pre planning of jobs by visiting customer locations to locate where our gas main is in relation to the customers house, looking for any and all possible issues that would hinder or prevent the job from going smoothly. I ensure that all paperwork is completed prior to job start and that all required paperwork is done and filed at job completion. I make sure that all work is completed in a timely manner and done to exact company specs. I am responsible for ensuring the proper training and certification of all of my employees by scheduling their training classes, making study time available, scheduling testing for certifications and keeping accurate records of all current certs. I am responsible for the accurate time keeping of all employee time sheets, approval and submission to the payroll department. It is my job to ensure that inventory is accurate and constantly stocked with all needed parts for uninterrupted work and emergency response. I handle all aspects of company and union interactions by coordinating with the local union steward. It is my job to make sure all tools are calibrated and in working order by company standards, keep all records and make them available to state and federal officials when required. I am required to keep myself qualified in all aspects of my job just as my employees are and that requires a considerable amount of time. My working hours are 7 am to 5 pm, i receive a one hour lunch break and once a week i get to take a half day since most of us salaried employees work far more than the 45 to 50 hours a week we are supposed to work. I have a company truck that i get to take home each night along with a company phone and laptop. I oversee several classes of workers who work shifts that cover a 24 / 7 / 365 schedule to maintain constant emergency response and ensure uninterrupted gas flow. All scheduling of equipment maintenance is handled by my office along with transport of the equipment to either an outside vendor or our internal maintenance dept. I am required to work a rotating one week on call shift as the response foreman for all after normal working hours response to emergency situations, coordinating all response efforts and maintenance efforts needed to make the situation safe. I then fill out the required paperwork and emergency logs, sending copies to the area superintendant for review. The hiring, discipline, and firing of employees in my shop is also my responsibility as well as annual reviews and mentoring. It is my job to create goals for my employees as well as plans to achieve those goals. It is my responsibility to ensure that contractors and other utilities can dig safely by locating the gas mains belonging to my company.
Overall_Why: The work I do is meaningful but not extremely meaningful. The work that i do closely related to public safety but it isn't as closely related as police or firefighting work or paramedic work. It is my responsibility to ensure that my employees are fully trained and certified to safely do their jobs as well as spot checking their work for adherance proper procedures. I am responsible for the odor level of the natural gas that flows through the system so that it can be readily detected by the average person should there be a leak in their home or place of business. My employees handle the maintenance and repair of pressure reducing regulators and pressure relief valves that protect the gas system from dangerous over pressure conditions that could cause leakage or severe damage. My employees complete quarterly system patrols looking for leaks with specialized leak detection equipment to ensure that we locate and repair leaks before they become a public safety issue. Beyond the safety aspect the delivery of a a clean burning, safe, efficient, and cost efficient fuel source is satisfying. Knowing that cheap and effective heat is available to my friends and neighbors is a good feeling.

Work_Story: I am a self-employed currency trader. I have been trading full-time for six years. I started with penny stocks. I joined a chat room in 2012 called "Promotion Stock Secrets." In that room we discussed penny stock promotions. I would buy the "Awesome Penny Stocks" promotion, which was a penny stock pick which was named about once every two months. I would make money a few times and lose on the rest. Soon I decided penny stocks were too volatile since I was consistently losing money. Around 2014 I joined a chat room called "Investor's Underground" and I had a little more success. However, it occurred to myself and my partner that blindly following the trades of stock gurus like Nathan Michaud and Timothy Sykes was not all it was cracked up to be. Then came the real revelation. This revelation was that there are actually people in the world that have written books on something called "Price Action" and "Trading Psychology." This was a dream come true. I was mesmerized by the depth authors like Mark Douglas and Jack Schwager had in their explanations of this mysterious mindset called "The Professional Trader." I was hooked. I read, but understood very little at first. Then the brokerage I was using ran into some problems. The CEO got into trouble with the law regarding something to do with penny stock promotions, and the reason I was using the brokerage was that the "Pattern Day Trade Rule" did not apply to me while I traded there because it was an offshore brokerage. So, fearing the brokerage would shut down and take all my money, I withdrew it and thought about what I would do next. Luckily for me, there was something called FOREX, and I did not need to worry about the "Pattern Day Trade Rule,' meaning I could trade as much as I wanted, and in small share sizes with large leverage. By this time I was heavily into the study of trading psychology. I had made money and lost money, and trading was what I had been doing full-time for about four years after 2012. I loved trading a great deal. There was something about conquering myself, like climbing a large mountain, that really got me going into this field. Around 2016 I discovered Al Brooks' "Price Action Course." It turned out that learning how to trade takes years, and that one cannot simply learn by study but has to practice with live trades as they learn. So I bought his course, and studied for hundreds of hours. Price action trading is difficult, very difficult. But soon, I was awake at the right time (London Session, midnight, pacific standard time, daily) and built a schedule and a life around trading. I had been doing nothing else for four to five years, and this was my life. Like any startup company, profits are hard to come by, but I remained committed. It turns out that only about 7% of traders stick with the profession after five years. My partner and I are still at this full-time. We have had people supporting us the whole time so we did not need to engage in any other profession. Now, about to start our seventh year as full time traders, life is exciting in the sense that we are a rare breed: we are nearing the point that we can finally turn a profit. My partner trades stocks and I the currency market. Price action trading has been remarkably interesting. What is so exciting is that all the so called "experts," such as college professors and the like, say that the markets are completely random and that there is no way for humans to make money. My partner and I are proving them wrong as we are nearing a profitable business using price action and indicators. I owe the world a great debt, in the sense that we have such wonderful people backing us in an endeavor that few get to realize success. Now, in 2018, we are upon our seventh year as a traders. As they say, it takes about 10,000 to master any field, and I have about 7,000 hours of face-time with the markets, and am loving every minute of it, losses and wins. I spend about 12 hours a day in front of the markets, which averages out to about 60 hours a week, and I love the work I do.
Overall_Why: I find my work extremely meaningful because it is a journey into self-discovery and self-realization. I learn much more about myself doing this than someone else might, simply doing work for someone else. It is my business. I am the employee, the boss, and the chief strategist. The profits are mine, and I can make money or lose money, based on my own performance alone. I can read from any teacher I choose, at my own pace. And since I work for myself, I take complete responsibility for my own work. Price action in currency trading means a trader can be right about the market, maybe 60% of the time. This has to be true, or the trader on the other side of the trade would not take the trade. The people I am trading against have to have a chance to make money. No perfect trades exist, because for me to be perfectly right, the person on the other side of the trade has to be perfectly wrong, so perfect trades cannot exist. Trades are at best educated guesses. And this requires one to think in probabilities. This means I need to think of trades as batches. The next trade's outcome is random, but the next 100 are not. Therefore, this profession allows me to learn some unique skills that most never get a chance to learn. Along with that comes the need to focus on the process, not just the outcome. Also, using my own money to trade, I must detach myself from my capital, meaning I must treat this as a game. If I think of my capital as something I can buy something with, it hurts to lose. But I have learned to think like a professional, knowing that in the end after a series of trades, I will be the winner.

Work_Story:
I am a proposal development manager. The average person has no idea what that means, so I will explain. A proposal manager leads a team of people who develop responses to Requests for Proposals (RFP). RFPs are part of a competitive procurement process used by government and commercial companies. An agency or business issues an RFP that describes what type of work they need a vendor to perform. It describes the scope, provides background on their needs and challenges, and identifies what information they want vendors to respond with. Companies like mine respond to RFPs in order to win new business.
 
 As a proposal manager, I help my company to develop compelling documents that comply with the RFP requirements and persuade the potential new customer to choose our proposed solution. I don't do this all by myself. I work with a team of writers, graphic designers, solution architects, pricers, sales leads, technical SMEs, as well as legal and contract management resources. My responsibilities include developing a proposal schedule, developing the response outline and making author assignments, planning milestone draft reviews, training content writers on proposal standards and writing strategies, and executing proposal activities against the schedule. While I am not accountable for whether we win or lose the deal (that is the responsibility of the Sales leads), I am accountable for us "answering the mail" i.e. compliance. 
 
 To achieve compliance, I work with the assigned authors to help them to interpret the requirements for their assigned section, so that they know what they should be writing about. As they develop their content drafts, I conduct informal reviews of their drafts, perform edits and rewrites as needed, and provided them with feedback on how to improve the content. I work with all of the authors through multiple iterations of each section's draft. 
 
 As the draft content matures, we provide milestone checkpoints for our sales leaders to review the drafted content to see how the proposal is progressing and to provide suggestions on what changes the authors should make. We typically conduct an average of three draft reviews on each proposal. The turnaround time of the opportunity (aka "deal") is what dictates how many draft reviews we conduct. If it is a "quick turn" deal (less than 3 weeks long), then we may only have time for one formal draft review. Alternatively, if I manage a "long turn" deal, then we would do four draft reviews. 
 
 No matter how many reviews are done, as a Proposal Manager, I am responsible for conducting a Compliance Review to verify that all requirements have been addressed and that we described our approach in a compelling way. This compliance review does not count as one of the formal draft review cycles described above, it is done in addition those reviews.
 
 Once I have verified compliance, I then send the document to legal and contract management for approval. Following approval, I then work with a printer to do the final print production, followed by submission to the client.
Overall_Why: I think the work I do is very important work that has a direct impact on the bottom line of the company I work for and I see the positive correlation between what I do and the benefits that our employees get when the company succeeds. However, if I use the definition of meaningful provided on the previous survey question, then despite the importance of what I do, I would have to say I do not find my work extremely meaningful. I transitioned into this area when I was only with the company for about 8 months and wanted to try something new. As I started to do well and excel in quickly learning how to do the job, I was fueled by how quickly I was able to advance, earning three promotions and reaching the executive level in just four years. However, once the whirlwind rise settled down, and another four years had passed of just doing this job, I feel completely unfulfilled. I'm not doing what I'm actually meant to do. I've realized I was more excited about advancing in the company quickly, than actually doing what I enjoyed. Now it's gotten to the point where each work day feels like I'm just trying to make it through to Friday. So I went back to colllege to study Forensic Accounting and just graduated with my Master's degree. Now I'm planning to change careers. This time I want to do work I love, not just look for quick promotions.

Work_Story:
I am a paramedic. I went to school for a year to become an EMT-I back in 2000. An EMT-I is an emergency medical technician-intermediate. A few years later, I went back for 18 months to become a Paramedic. There are different areas to work as a paramedic. I have worked most of them. I currently work for a private care ambulance service. We do a wide array of patient care and transport. I can break these down into non-emergent, emergent, and critical transports.
 Non-Emergent transports are the main type of calls that we perform. The skill level that I am trained for is not needed in this area. This area is the main source of income for the company. The company makes on average 350 dollars per patient. The majority of the calls are dialysis transports. We pick up patients at their house or in nursing homes and transport these patients by stretcher to a dialysis facility and later return the patients. These patients typically have a basic assessment and vitals signs performed. We transport to the hospital if we notice any abnormal changes. If the patient is stable then we continue to the dialysis center. We weigh the patient pre-dialysis and post-dialysis for the evaluation of fluid loss. We also transport patients to doctor's offices and transport patients out of the hospital to the appropriate facility. 
 Emergency transports are not as abundant in the private sector. We do provide back up for congested 911 areas. Typically, our emergencies arrive from nursing homes. The ambulance unit responds lights and sirens to the nursing home and will locate the patient. The nurse will be contacted and report obtained while the second crew member begins assessment and evaluation. The patient is evaluated for life threatening illnesses and treated for any conditions. The patient's symptoms are treated prior and during transport to the emergency room. Treatments range from oxygen, iv placement, medication administration, cardiac monitoring to cardiopulmonary resuscitation. A vast amount of the educational background and protocols are implemented to assure that the patient's condition are improved. 
 Lastly, critical care is the most advanced type of transport. Critical care transports are typically a hospital to hospital transport. The patient requires a paramedic that is trained in critical care. The patient is typically intubated and on a ventilator. The ambulance service has their own ventilator for these type of calls. The patient also is typically on several iv medications and sedated. The report is obtained from the RN. The respiratory therapist is also in the room to confirm the transport ventilator settings are the same as the hospital ventilator. The patient is moved to the stretcher and all of the medications and pumps are attached to the stretcher. The patient is then connected to our ventilator and transported to the destination. The paramedic must closely monitor the patient during the transport. The medications must be maintained during the transport and the ventilator monitored for failure. If ventilator failure fails, then the patient must be manually ventilated by bvm. These calls typically start out at 1500 dollars and increase based on the equipment that is required.
 I enjoy being a paramedic at my current workplace. We perform a variety of different paced jobs. There are calls that range from basic transports to calls that require a lot of knowledge and treatment to save a person's life. The vast array of situations keep my mind sharp and fulfilled.
Overall_Why:
I find my job meaningful because we are providing life. We pick patients up in their worst time and try to make them better. That could be sitting on the floor and holding the hand of a mother that just had a miscarriage or saving a father from choking on a steak. I went to school for two and a half years and I feel that I use every bit of information that I have learned to help people. My education is not wasted on a meaningless job or task. I fell that if I was to go work one day and save one life then everything would have been worth those years in college. 
 I always state this, "Think of you most loved person and place them in the worst situation possible; who would you call to help that person out?" Everyone always says 911. That points out the fact that a Paramedic is always the first line of contact. I have never heard anyone reply doctor. This is because the doctors don't respond to house calls. The doctor is obviously more qualified to do the job but will never do that job. I find that meaningful because paramedics are the highest level of care in the outside world. We specialize in all fields of medicine. We also have to make point decisions without any help from a higher trained person. 
 I also find the psychological care that we provide as meaningful. We give families hope that was once lost. I enjoy seeing the relief in a person's expression once we arrive or correct the problem.

Work_Story: I work for a nonprofit organization that services several different types of facilities. We have two workout facilities, several independent living buildings a couple of assisted living facilities and some nursing homes. We also support and run a local charity that feeds children who may not otherwise get to eat after school and on the weekends. My job title is Technical Support Analyst. I am in charge of two other employees and answer directly to the Vice President of Communications. My days usually consist of several things which I will detail in the next few sentences. First, I administer our e-mail and active directory systems. This includes setting up new users in both systems, assigning permissions, and removing people when they leave the company. Second, I oversee our desktop deployment and support. To do so I administer a Windows update server (WSUS), oversee reloading and deployment on all machines, and document and verify their respective locations. I also administer our mobile device inventory which includes some cell phones and other devices like iPods and iPads. We also provide technical support to all the people living in our facilities which I oversee. This includes any computer issues they may have or issues with printers, televisions or any other electronic device that we believe we may be able to fix for them. Another one of my responsibilities is organizing the technical aspects of our building projects. As a growing company, we often have several building projects going on at the same time, my responsibilities there include making sure phone lines are installed, data lines are installed, nurse call systems get installed and work properly, coaxial cable is run for the televisions, and various other audio and video services are working. I often act as the sole liaison between vendors and our company, organizing site visits, training, and support from these vendors. Often times I will travel to these vendors for specific training on their hardware or software so that I can be considered a local expert on those items for our company. Another of my tasks is to oversee our nurse call systems, this is very important as without them we do not meet the state requirements for care in a nursing home or assisted living facility. These systems include call lights outside the rooms, pull cords near the beds and in the bathrooms by the toilet and shower, and pendants so that if the resident is not in their room and have an issue we can quickly find them and attend to their needs. I feel that I am a very valuable and almost irreplaceable part of this company, though no one is truly replaceable I do my best to be as close to it as possible. As a whole, this company is probably best considered a mid-sized company with over 700 employees and multiple campuses. All of this can be at times a little hard to keep up with but because of the value I place on my job and the value that my company places on me I have been very happy here.
Overall_Why: I would say that overall I do find my work meaningful. Now there are many day to day operations that I take care of that seem monotonous and boring, but there are other times when I get to work with a resident and take care of that issues that make my work feel very meaningful. Think of it as helping your grandparents learn a new technology that they are struggling with. Most of the time it is not overly difficult to operate the technology items that your grandparents have but because they didn't grow up with a keyboard or phone connected to their hands some technology can seem overwhelming. My position allows me to interact with a lot of people from a different generation that grew up completely different than how I did. In my opinion, this is one of the best parts about my job, not only do I get the satisfaction of helping them with something they were struggling with, but I also get to learn their stories. People my age and younger really have no idea what the older generations went through to get to where they are today. The things they persevered to make sure that we could still live free. Taking the time to sit and talk to them and hear and really understand where they've been and how they got to where they are really is a great part of my work. I think without that aspect my job would get pretty boring.

Work_Story:
I work as a 7th grade social studies teacher in an a public school in upstate {state} . My job is very challenging as well as an enriching one . My school district is predominately made up of economically disadvantaged students. There are a lot of behavioral issues that are of concern for the district that help tp shape how we teach in the classroom. Classroom mangement skills is key tool needed in order to successfully get through a a day of teaching. I have a reputation amongst the students for being stern but fun. Without order and discipline, education is impossible. 
  As a social studies teacher, I love my job. I enjoying teaching the kids how to love history. To understand its value and also to see the fun it can be. We have various projects that I try to make interactive and exciting. One year we had a presidential debate in the classroom. Students were allowed to dress as there favorite president and give a debate speech. This was an extremely great expereince for me as well as the students. 
  When I am not teaching or preparing my lesson plans, I have team meetings with other teachera on my floor. We discuss issues that might arise with the students as well as comparing notes and information about changes going on in the district.
  My job has changed a lot in the past few years in terms of curriculum changes. The is definitely more of a focus on state testing and adhering to Common Core standards. The major problem I have with the common core is its focus on restructuring how teachers teach students. I must attend several workshops throughout the school year to learn new teaching techniques. As of right now these changes have made the test scores go up nor have they increased the confidence of the students. I try to keep the creativity alive as much as the curriculum allows. There are guidelines that I must directly follow but I try add extra flare to keep the students interested.
 Teaching is passion that I love, it makes me feel alive to stand in front of a room of students and engage them. I enjoy public speaking as well sharing new information with my students. Get them to care about issues in the world past or present. More importantly getting students to stimulate and challenge their brains. Some students have gone own to major in history when they went to college because they were inspired by class lessons when they were in middle school.
  I have been teaching for ten years and have reconnected with former students who told me I had a big impact on their education. I enjoy knowing that I am a positive force in my students lives.
 I enjoy giving back to the community by developing young minds. These minds will make and shape the future of the world. It is important that they have a great start. I enjoy being a 7th grade social studies teacher and would not change that for anything in the world. All though I believe my industry is underpaid monetarily we are enriched with respect and love from our students which last for a lifetime.
Overall_Why:
My career is meaningful because I get to help students mature into great adult leaders. As an educator my job is to enrich the quality of my students lives. I provide them with the academic and social skills needed to become contributing members of society.
 My care is also meaningful because I am in service to the entire community. A student that I have today may turn into a judge or criminal based on how I helped or hindered their development. Being a teacher is a calling meaning you must enjoy doing it to be successful at it. There is a part of teaching that enriches the educator to do it. I sometimes joke that I maintain my youth and vitality through being in school for my entire life. First I was a student and them I became the teacher but the environment remains the same. Teaching is meaningful to me because it is mutually beneficial to me as well as the students. I also understand that is a necessary good and service. Where would this world be without teachers? How would knowledge be interpreted and passed on without us? The only reason one might questi on the value of teachers is because we are paid so little in comparison to professional althletes and hedge fund managers. I believe that is due to society's devaluing of education for the masses. Most teachers teach in public or small school districts, we are not teaching the children of the elite. We are trying educate future middle class people. Sometimes my mind jumps into a conspiratorial realm about this topic. All in all my job is extremely meaningful.

Work_Story:
I am a project manager in the commercial marine section.
 the core hours are from 8AM to 4:30PM, but are not limited to it. Since the ship schedules doesn't know any work hours or holidays.
 Since I am a project manager I am leading, together with my colleagues, a team of 7 workers and distribute them on the specified job. 
 My usual daily work is to get in touch with the customers and offer them our services, basically marketing. Also, to establish the relation I am in frequent contact with them. Either this customers or new ones are sending me requests for their ships and need to be planned and quoted accordingly. 
 If the customer is satisfied with the offer I'm starting to plan the job. Which materials do I need, how many guys on the team for the job. We are getting in touch with the agency so we can keep a track of the schedule of the ship so I can plan the right timing to attend the ship. If the ship is coming in in the night I will plan the team to rest over the day, to avoid any fatigue but of course to keep the hourly rates under control. Depending on the job I will also attend and plan the job on site or just send the team.
 Once the job is done I am preparing a service report that gives the customer a full picture what was done. I am also working very close together with classification societies, so if the ship is requiring a surveyor??s attendance I am contacting them upfront to plan the job with them in advance, to optimize the timing as well.
 The range of work is quite wide. 
 My responsibilities are Firefighting equipment, general repair, technical solutions, such as monitoring systems, vessel inspections, lifesaving appliances and the list goes on.
 Coming back to the daily work. 
 The first thing in the morning is to check the emails, if I haven??t done it already on the smart phone. Usually we are receiving about 300-400 mails per day. Working with 6 persons on the emails we are making sure that nothing is missing.
 After the emails and after the workers are set up for their jobs, I might have time to get into the marketing. Not on a daily base but frequent the inventory need to be checked to, so I can reorder equipment that is frequent needed.
 Also, I need to keep the track for our quality system. Audit??s are in need to be planned. That means the quality manual which ensures our processes of work are optimized must be up to date. Improvements are a dynamical process. All that is check by a classification society. Certifications are the most important subject in our business, just to be able to perform the requested job. 
 I try to plan my day, so I can keep my worktime somehow under control. On some days that is not possible and on other days that are not so busy, I might go home a bit earlier to compensate it a little bit. I was a superintendent in Germany before and had a minimum of 10-12 hours day. I decided to change the side from the customer to the service side, so I have way more flexibility and much more important, the time with my family is may be not that much more, but for more intensive.
