It is a difference if I see them every night just for 2 hours, or one day or even to days not but on the next day 4-6 hours. So, I can do something with them.
Overall_Why:
It is way more meaningful than my Job before. In the previous job I had a very decent salary a company??s car travel over the world, but I couldn??t share it with my family since there was no time for it.
 Is the current occupation my profession? I would say not a 100%.
 I do this, so I can have a decent life with my family. This job is the perfect compromise for myself. If I would go with the most meaningful job I could imagine I probably would invest in real estates. But the risk is to high, so I decided to have this job.
 Other than most of the colleagues from the marine section, I have nothing to do with the water than my job. Other colleagues are having sailing boats or are living on the water or something like that. Not me. I saw and see this business as such and not as a hobby I made to my profession. 
 I am working to live, not living to work. Whatever gives me the best balance between my job and my private live is the most meaningful for me. 
 Anyway, I was moving my family from Germany to the USA, so I can do exactly what I do with the best possible balance. I guess that is the most meaningful subject at all. I do see peoples risking their relations just for the job. Is it because the job was everything for them? Of course, it is a lot of fun to be out with the guys and do the jobs. And the feeling to have a job done right in, or below the budget and exactly how it was planned is a very good feeling. But just because of that, I would say it is the most meaningful work I could do. Ask the peoples what the would do if they win the lottery. Most of them will tell you they would continue to work. Not me. I would quit right away and start investing the money in more profitable things.

Work_Story:
I work as a swing manager at a popular fast food restaurant. The hours are long, but it pays the bills. I work together with a team of ten other managers, and we supervise approximately seventy crew members. We provide fast, quality, and friendly service at a reasonable price. We operate under the assumption that the whole is greater than each individual. This means we must work together to accomplish our goals and targets. We have targets every peak time (when we have the most business) for time of service, time to make the food, and other things. We provide incentives for the crew to motivate them to meet these targets, such as free dessert or a free meal. It really does work, and sometimes drives a competitive spirit between the different stations to have the lowest time. 
 Most of my work involves supervising and managing the crew on the floor during shifts. I do have other duties, however. I am responsible for counting the waste and inputting it into the system at the end of my shift. This means marking down everything that is trash, but food waste so we can account for the loss. We put them in special buckets for ease of counting. Inputting this waste into the system is relatively easy, but tedious. I actually enjoy doing it after the fast paced and loud shifts that I usually work. Additionally, I am responsible for what we refer to as "Food Safety". Food Safety is a procedure we use to ensure that the food we are serving is safe for consumption. We cook meat and test its temperature to see if it has reached our standard for safety. If it has not, we know we need to adjust the grills to either a higher temperature or a longer cooking time. 
 The actual managing portion of my job is perhaps the most difficult. It is difficult to orchestrate so many people in a stressful environment. I have to make sure that I have enough staff to cover the different areas of kitchen, front line, and drive-thru. I need to put people in places where they are strong so that service is as fast as possible. It is also my job to make sure there is a good rotation of people to go on their lunch break, without leaving us short. With so many people in a space working fast and getting frustrated, there is a lot of drama. Accusations fly around, unsubstantiated rumors spread like wild fire, and people gossip all of the time. I do not approve of these things happening, and I do not participate. We are taking measures to help calm these things, such as more open communication during shifts and during staff meetings. We also have started a group chat where we can all openly say things about shifts and anything else we need to talk about. It also helps to have a record of what has been said and when we have said it. I do not always love my job, but I like working with customers and I have made some really great friends there. I have worked at two different stores in my career, and I much prefer the one that I am currently working at. The general manager is much nicer and straight forward, whereas at my old location the general manager was stubborn, mean, and did not listen to his staff of managers.
Overall_Why: I do not find my work very meaningful. I do not help anyone beyond providing food. I really wish to work in a technical field and help people that way. At my current job, I feel it is just a step job, where I can step up to something that is closer to my values. The job of swing manager I feel values people, organization, teaching and guiding. I do not feel like this aligns with my values and goals. I am very much an introvert, so I do not feel comfortable being so close to people and guiding them to succeed. Often times we employ people here as their first job, young high schoolers. This means we have the task of shaping and instructing them in how to conduct themselves in a professional manner. This is often a difficult task. Additionally, as I mentioned before, there is a lot of gossip and drama going around. It seems that in order to succeed in this field, one must be good at handling drama and the people involved. I prefer to just stay away from all of the dramatic things happening. I do not like to get involved in the petty arguments and the silly fights. I wish to have a job where I do not have to worry about things like this. I value peace and understanding, that is what is meaningful to me. The chaos and stress associated with my job to not lend themselves to this, so that is why I do not find this job particularly meaningful.

Work_Story: I'm a network administrator at a lighting manufacturing company. My official job requirements are to manage, maintain and upgrade the server infrastructure. I also maintain the network. I take care of minor user issues and many other technical tasks. Usually day to day tasks include resetting user passwords, fixing slowness on peoples computers and general troubleshooting of applications such as: microsoft word, microsoft excel, microsoft outlook. adobe acrobat, shoretel communicator, firefox, internet explorer, google chrome, java applications and more. I work with a team of other technicians. We have 2 other network administrators. We three specialize in different areas though. One of them specializes in network devices. He came from the Red Cross originally and is very good at what he does. He also manages mostly our linux systems. The second network tech handles mostly the minor issues as well as the application Alchemy. Most all users in the company call him with issues. He is very slow and bugs me all the time for stupid issues that he already knows how to fix. But, I think he just likes to talk a lot. I am the last network admin. I specialize mostly in windows systems and phone systems. What does this mean, exactly? Well I manage all user accounts and the operating systems for all users as well as servers. This includes me upgrading the servers to different OS systems, or migrating server systems to new versions. For instance, recently I worked on an exchange problem. It is more complicated then it sounds if it didn't already. I had to first fix our DNS systems which is the name associated IPs, ex. google.com is actually at times 172.217.14.110. So for us email, has multiple names associated with it, such as, mail.minkagroup.net, autodiscover.minkagroup.net, auto.minkagroup.net, etc. So, I fixed that and then I had to work to link the two exchange servers together. Once they are linked together, I spent the majority of time moving mailboxes of users after hours to the new server and then the following days troubleshooting any issues this created. Finally, I had to begin decomissioning the old server. This involved first making sure all relative information was added to the new exchange server. Make sure mail was being forwarded and successfully delivered on the new exchange server. Then once all this is confirmed, I had to uninstall the exchange applications. Then I removed from the domain the exchange server. And finally shut it down and deleted the associated exchange server account. That was just one project. Also, with the phone systems I have to manage the users and make sure all phones receive IPs, have clear calls, and are setup properly with voicemail. This is all related to the shoretel system on Windows servers. It is more complicated then just these few things as we have a call center with call ques and rules that apply depending on different situations in the organization. So with all this barely scratching the surface. This is what I do.
Overall_Why: I do find it meaningful more than the average job I would say. I grew up loving computers and I believe that computers are the way of the future. Some day computers will take over most of our day to day tasks and unfortunately some of our jobs too. However, being the guy who, manages, maintains, upgrades and fixes these systems, makes me feel a sense of stability. It also makes me feel like I'm involved in the future. I feel like I do something that is useful and have meaning in my job in supporting my company as well. For instance, recently with some upgrades to monitors and computers I was able to increase the production of our employees and making them generally happier with their own systems. This makes good employees which makes them work harder. So both in production of the systems increase as well as increase in hard work from general employees I'm able to really make an impact in my company. This job is also meaningful for me in the way of my family. I'm able to fully support my child and wife. My wife does not work and in turn can focus entirely on our child. This is the most meaningful part of my job. As my daughter grows old I want her to learn the meaning of her moms devotion to her and mine as well of course. She is our meaning in life.

Work_Story:
I am currently working at a technology start up based out of {city} called XXXX. It is a very small company so far of about 22 full time employees, most of which who are data scientists and software engineers. They created an actuarial tool to help predict which health insurance is best for each individual. This is particularly interesting as we have collected data on who selected plans we suggest and who didn't. On average the clients who chose our recommended health insurance plans saved an average of $2,000 per year! Those who selected the plan we didn't recommend over spent an average of $600 per month. That's not to say they selected a bad plan, just that they could have saved money instead of uselessly spending. I am the office operations lead and executive assistant to the CEO. We operate business hours from 9-5 in a large office in center city. On a day to day basis I handle the CEO's scheduling, calendar and travel arrangements. I also handle payroll and HR. We are currently undergoing a series B capital raise which has me working on gathering "due diligence" for our investors. I assume once this capital raise is over my duties will die down again. 
 On a day to day basis I try to plan out our culture and activities in the office. Before I started most employees worked from home and it was my goal to get them to come in more often and enjoy being in the office. I introduced Minute to Win it Mondays, where we split up into teams and play short games against each other. It has really worked as far as getting employees to come in on Mondays! I also implemented team lunch every other week..and guess what? Every one shows up for that! I recently planned out our holiday party. I had a small budget to work with so we kept it in office, I had dinner catered in and brought in a murder mystery dinner theatre group. I was really surprised at how well that went over! I also hold culture club meetings bi-weekly where myself and a few other employees try to plan out fun activities for our office. Next week we will be volunteering at a food bank, we've done this once before and it was surprisingly more fun than expected. 
 I also manage office supplies as well as pay the bills. I coordinate all receipts on our google drive and use a customized website to keep track of accounting. I order supplies on amazon and staples business advantage. I also implemented lunch and learns on Friday. Employees take turns presenting to each other on whatever topic they find interesting or relevant. Most of them have to do with agile process and calculator updates, I recently did one on public speaking and previously on resume writing. Topics vary week to week but we try to keep a few of them not so data science driven to enable everyone's involvement. 
 Today I will spend most of my day following up with client calls and collecting due diligence for our investors. Currently, we are in need of two software engineers. We haven't quite received resumes for the talent level we're looking for so I'm going to go to a few engineering "meet ups" next week to see if I can network with {city}'s finest talent. 
 This task has really shown me just how easy my job is...
Overall_Why: I would say that my company does extremely meaningful work. I am always amazed at just how important they think their work is, trying to save people money and get them the right health insurance. My previous job I managed a health insurance brokerage where my employees sold Medicare (MAPD) plans to those on Medicare or Medicaid. This is usually a simple job however each individual is different and you have to do your best to put them in the right plan so that they stay there and you make a commission. This tool is extremely important to brokers and individuals with health insurance. I personally don't find my work as meaningful as I am not helping build the tool. I do keep the company running and the lights on by paying the bills, but I am not directly impacting our customers. I know that the CEO appreciates my efforts as it enables him to sell and raise capital instead of wasting his time scheduling travel and calls. I do find worth in that. I also think I have improved the lives of those I work with, they enjoy coming to work and have become friendlier with each other since I have started. I find meaning in that, not everyone enjoys their job, I think that is an extremely important part of your life. You spend the majority of the day with those you work with, and if you're not enjoying it then your life isn't as fulfilled as it should be.

Work_Story: I currently work as a Youth Guide for the {company} in {city}, {state}. Basically, I am a children and teen librarian and have worked in this position for a little over three months. My main focus is on programming and I implement about eight programs each week for youth and families. Regular programs include storytimes for babies, toddlers, and preschoolers as well as weekly teen and tween offerings. I also host our family and all ages programming like art and game nights. My programs are designed to inspire creativity in our community. Other than programs, I am responsible for working the floor, maintaining our branch's marketing efforts, and other duties as assigned. Working the floor means working the front desk, children's desk, or as a rover. The front desk is where most of our daily interactions with customers comes from. We create new library accounts, provide assistance in locating items, or a wide-range of technical, directional, and general assistance. At the children's desk, I typically am watching over kids that are on the computer and playing with younger children as they come in. Our goal is to provide an enthusiastic, personalized experience for everyone. While roving, you basically walk the floor, interacting with customers and helping those that need assistance. For marketing, I create our press release, flyers, branch calendar, and input everything on the library's webpage. Other duties as assigned can include dealing with behavior issues, checking in books, creating large kits for programs that will be floated to other branches, and looking for community partnerships. I also have a lot of meetings, in my opinion, with other staff across the system. Children's Guides meet once a month for a 3 hour meeting, teen guides have a monthly two hour meeting, and I also have a monthly meeting for summer programming. The meetings consist of at least one person from each library branch. We use the time to discuss important topics, plan for the future, and share our successes. I also have meetings that pop up often for various other committees. Right now, my branch is currently in a hiring process for two full-time positions. I have the opportunity to assist with one, for a full time concierge position. I have been reviewing applications and resumes and will begin phone interviews this week. Another really fun part of my job is creating Experience Zones in the library for customers to explore. Each week, myself or another staff member, comes up with an idea that can be put out in the library for customers to interact with. It can be anything from a collaborative crossword puzzle to a bunch of play-do. The goal of these zones is to spark curiosity, allow for play, and provide interesting topics for customers to peruse. Overall, I enjoy my job, but I still have a lot of 'new job' kinks to work out. My branch is a small staff at 6 people currently, but we are hiring for two full-time positions and one person will be returning from maternity leave next month. Being full staffed again will be nice and I feel that I will be able to get a lot more accomplished.
Overall_Why: I think my work is extremely meaningful to my community as I provide a free service to the public. We become daytime shelter for homeless individuals. We provide free resources to everyone that comes in. My programs are some of the only free youth programs that a family can attend in our area. My storytimes offer caregivers the opportunity to educate themselves on literacy best practices. After school programs keep tweens and teens off the street. I often provide snacks at programs because we have a lot of hungry kids. I take my work very seriously as well which I think contributes to how meaningful I find it to be. I contribute to my field often be it through offering presentations, writing on my blog, or posting to social media groups. I think sharing my expertise helps others find meaning in their work. I think the most important thing I do is connect customers to the information or help that they need. On an almost daily basis, I help people find employment, connect them to community services, or provide assistance in basic computer issues. For many that come through my library's doors, the computers in our lab are the only computers they have access to. We have kids, teens, and even college students who use our services to complete homework. I am happy everyday to go into work because I know that I am making a difference in what I do. I make a difference for the people I help, for the people I educate, and for myself.

Work_Story:
I am a doctoral candidate in organizational behavior at a Tier I research institution, conducting research on leadership and work/life issues. My job consists of two distinct, yet often intertwined, components: research and teaching. On the research front, I conduct research, typically using surveys and experience sampling methodology, on leadership, the leader as a holistic person, and how work and non-work domains interact. Within this research space, I have multiple manuscripts under review at top management journals including Academy of Management Journal and Academy of Management Annals, with working papers targeting additional publications such as Journal of Applied Psychology in final preparation for submission. My typical research day, which has been the bulk of the winter break for me, has involved three major components: data collection, data analysis, and writing. Across multiple different projects in different stages of development, I have engaged in the collection of experience sampling data, the analysis of existing data using multilevel structural equation modeling, and the writing of a manuscript for another project.
 
 On the teaching front, I teach Human Resource Management, a required course for Management undergraduate majors. My duties with respect to this component of my job involve preparing course materials (e.g., written assignments, syllabi, teaching materials such as Powerpoint slides, and in-class activities), instruction, grading, and dispute resolution as needed. Instruction is perhaps the most enjoyable part of teaching for me, as it gives me the opportunity to not only interact directly with my students, but also share my experiences and knowledge from the workforce as well as sprinkle in some of my own research and research interests (often insidiously, such that students are not aware that they are being taught "research"). However, one difficulty with this portion of teaching (the instruction portion) is conveying difficult concepts to students. For instance, some of the more abstract concepts are those typically found in academic research, and can be difficult to convey to students in a concrete manner. One example of this is explaining performance measurement, which involves discussing constructs, construct validity, contamination, and deficiency in measurement. However, these are the challenges upon which I thrive.
 
 Another component of my teaching job involves grading. There are a few components to this, including devising a grading scheme (or rubric), as well as actually grading written assignments. The University has designated my course as a "writing intensive" course, such that students are expected to engage in a certain amount of academic writing over the course of the semester. As such, my grading is quite labor intensive, as I must read through dozens of student papers, and provide feedback not only on course concepts, but also on writing quality and clarity.
 
 Finally, with regard to dispute resolution, there may be instances where course or University policy is broken. For example, it may be, given this course is a writing intensive course, that a student plagiarizes all or part of a written assignment. If this occurs, I must intervene and have difficult, awkward conversations with students that are often dispute-laden. Further, I must also involve the University in the process, adding additional difficulty.
Overall_Why:
I find my work meaningful for a few reasons. First, on a personal level, I value hard work and dedication. This job, given its characteristics and the demands it places on its occupants, requires many long hours of work that may or may not pay off (e.g., getting a rejection). However, since I simultaneously value intellectual stimulation, this job personally challenges me and is meaningful to me. This is furthered by my ability to teach. I have long enjoyed and valued teaching and instruction. In fact, my first concrete vision of a career (in junior high school) was that of being a history teacher. While that did not come to pass, my love of teaching has remained. To this end, in prior jobs I have greatly enjoyed my ability to train and develop my subordinates, and this enjoyment has spilled over to my career in academia.
 
 On a more social basis, it is sometimes difficult to see meaningfulness in research, especially given the veritable cornucopia of work bemoaning the scientist-practitioner divide. That being said, I find my research personally meaningful given my past history in management, where I labored under the assumption by the organization that managers and supervisors are not "people," but instead simply mindless robots that have no life, experiences, or feelings outside of the workplace. This is what drives my research interests - to learn more about the leader as a person as opposed to simply focusing on "leadership" as a set of social influence behaviors. So, to answer the question, I find this job very meaningful.

Work_Story: My full time job is a mix of transcription, linguistics, foreign languages, grammar, literature, and crowdsourcing. I alternate between different companies to fill out my work. I am NOT describing MTurk as per your instructions. My training and job is rooted out in noticing details such as word usage, writing, enunciation, verbal / grammatical cues in a given sociolinguistic background, concerned with technicality / proper usage, and so on. For one job, this involves giving English lessons outside of formal settings on my own time while interacting with the speaker's native language. I am oftentimes not respected because some people, especially younger ones, think they know it all. It is not until they are older when they may perhaps realize they know nothing or perhaps not... Anyhow, the lessons are administered virtually. As an aide and a work of love, I slowly also begin to learn the functions of the origin language to ingratiate the non-native speaker more easily into the target language (English). For example, I may say, "In German, you show DER, DEN, DEM" as declining articles; however, we know that Russian doesn't use articles but the words inflect internally. Then we attempt at a conversation and I try to gently correct the speaking and field questions at the drop of a hat with no time to anticipate the question at times. There is no real way to seem gentle while correcting as it must be done immediately and how the speaker responds has to do more with how they internalize the act of being corrected. Arrogance cannot be corrected, at least, not by me. For another job, I transcribe. This involves having a bit of ewquipment though the cost cannot always be reasonable and so I forego some tools. I rely on language knowledge, language cues to fill in blank areas / unsure responses, I take into account my familiarity with accents given in a certain language and which language this is to identify translation errors and why they are occuring. This way I can document the audio. However, my perfectionism isn't always rewarded because not all people are aware of technical correctness in linguistics and other people, though of a lesser knowledge, can seem more established than I or more sympathetic due to reasons beyong my control. Therefore, as usual, I can be ignored even though I'm correct. Such is life. In the third job, I evaluate websites for clients. I judge the website appearance at the start. Things such as how color scheme affects or interferes with marketing, what background is best for a user, and how visuals can highlight or hide information come into play. Then I move on to issues such as functioning and technicality. Can I see a certain function button clearly and does it work I click on it? Does it redirect? Is the link broken? i also give examples of times where website design features (including all stated above as well as others which are glossed over) affected me as a consumer, my brand loyalty, and willingness to use the site as a return customer. I only judge language, audio, websites, and queries. I don't judge people because I'm trying to survive and go on to the next level of life -- I hope.
Overall_Why:
i would like to do more in my field. i used to be a professor. now the job market online has become impossible even for entry level work and i have been working online since 2007 due to agoraphobia. i am being shut out of more and more jobs. employers want the highest degree with the most experience for the smallest amount of pay. literally my whole department was fired and we were all replaced in the same role, same duties, and same payroll by people who had a PhD. I see entry level work online asking for a degree above an MA. I am smart, i want to work, i have a purpose for my income and it's essential to my happiness and love life however, all of these fall short due to the greed of employers dangling the work at home option to millions of people who WANT to work at home while i NEED to work from home. i faint and have seizures due to panic disorder. so, no... everything is failing at the moment. and i told you why. 
 i have a great deal of training and experience in my degree. simply because i cannot leave my home without struggle severely form a condition that has gone on since childhood should not shut me out of a whole career. even finding menial jobs online is a horrendous task. sometimes the things i am ask to do i find humiliating for the sake of working at home. this is nothing immoral but just humiliating and exploitative considering my seriousness, work ethic, knowledge, and experience.
