Overall_Why: I do find my work meaningful. I ensure that invoices are taken care of so our population of seventeen thousand employees are able to continue their insurance. I handle all of the Personal Time Off plan enrollments which allows employees to utilize their sick and vacation time, which is important for our employees. I think I add a lot of value to my department with my technology knowledge, and ability to pull needed reports ad-hoc as needed for meetings. I enjoy the people that I work with, which normally makes me want to work harder so that things are easier for them. We work well as a team. I'm encouraged to do things that fit my personality and also help the organization, like leading the Fun Committee for our Building. I'm given the ability to use my technology skills for my own work as well as to help others within in the department and that is very fulfilling. I am given flexibility to get my work done as well has handle my work life balance. The ability to work from home has been an amazing way to make this job more comfortable. They allow me to find my own processes to make things easier for me and that is a wonderful advantage to my job. I get to create my own documentation and my manager does not hover or micromanage, and it makes my job much easier. It makes me more comfortable to be at work and want to continue working here.

Work_Story:
I currently work in a women's healthcare center and my specific job is patient services specialist in the patient administration department for a well known state affiliated non profit in {state}. I have worked in this office for the last four and a half years at this point. My primary job in the health center is checking in patients that come in for appointments, I also collect information about their insurance plans and check on whether or not their plans are active or inactive and if they have a deductible or coinsurance. One other aspect of checking in patients involves confirming their demographics, such as the correct spelling of their names, the correct mailing address for them and how they most prefer to be contacted and who they want to use for their emergency contact and whether we can share information with that person. I also collect medical history information from everyone and hand out patient surveys or other forms. I make sure that the medical history information gets to the correct staff person. After the patients are seen by the nurse practitioner and their appointments are finished, I make sure when patients check out that the clinician has entered in all of the correct charges for all of the services they received during the visit that day and then I confirm and create claims for the visit that then go to the insurance companies to be processed. Other tasks that are included in my work is scheduling future appointments for patients and generating reports that contain show rates, walk in appointments that are taken for the manager and the higher ups in the organization. I am also responsible for keeping the reception and waiting area stocked with appropriate supplies for everyone such as paperwork, worksheets, pens, labels, tape, stapels and other office supplies. Reconciling money and opening and closing a cash drawer is also part of the work I do regularly. I have a drawer of $200 dollars that I count at the beginning and the end of my shift to ensure that no money is missing or no other issues are involved. I handle this cash but also I collect credit card and check payments as well.
 The last part of my job is to serve as a representative my health center location in organization wide committees that looks at the employee experience across the organization. I attend meetings and provide all sorts of feedback to a lot of other staff members and administration members in the whole group. This puts me in a leadership role somewhat but also allows me to speak up for the needs of my coworkers and location. I serve as an ambassador for the organization wide surveys and other things that involve getting employees to participate in. I get to be exposed to things happening in the organization earlier than most of the other staff and I get to vote on proposals that are brought from employees and seek to improve the quality of the work place. At this point my response is over 500 words but under 3000 characters and I don't know how much more I can write about my job.
Overall_Why: My work is incredibly meaningful to me for several reasons. I work with women who are making challenging medical decisions that are inappropriately politicized. I also find the fact that I work within the community that I grew up in and that means I am serving my neighbors and have a personal investment in how the community is served. I also find meaning in being able to interact with all types of different people and from different backgrounds. I love customer service even though people can be very terrible sometimes. I get job satisfaction largly from the type of conversation and interactions that I have with my patients. The type of work I do isn't exactly safe and their have been individuals who have been killed doing what I do best extreme anti women people. I find that the work I do is more meaningful to me because there is opposition from other people who don't believe that women can control there own bodies and make personal medical decisions based on their lives and the human right of bodily autonomy. My job has meaning because I also agree with the values and belief's that the organization I work for also holds important. I think even though the specific job I do may not be super hard or very challenging the type of interactions I have with the patients that need our help makes me feel very happy about what I do and that alone gives my job meaning to me and keeps me working where I work.

Work_Story: I work at {company} in {city} {state} as a PC Support Specialist. Some things I do include traveling to our other 21 branches and training employees on how to utilize certain software. This software includes Office 365,SharePoint, and Microsoft Windows operating systems. While at these other branches I also update the computers and replace any of the old devices that need replaced. When I am in my office I am typically responsible for helping people who call in with questions or IT related issues. When I am not dealing with any IT issues, I am usually working on updating our SharePoint or Website. We are an electrical distributor which means that we need to keep our prices and inventory up to date at all times in order to ensure that our customers are getting the best and accurate rates. This is not my only full time job however as I also run and operate my own business. I create websites for clients, manage the social media pages they have, and also create graphics for them such as logos, posters, and banners. The process of my work involves searching for clients and doing things such as meeting with my clients. I graduated Thiel College in May of 2017 with a Bachelor of Science degree in Web Development and interactive media. I attended high school in central PA (West Branch). I became interested in web design early in high school, where I taught myself how to code in HTML and CSS. Since then, I have explored Java, PHP, C++, and SQL. I also have a strong passion for social media, where I run numerous pages. Lastly, early in college I became attracted to graphic design. I took the class and received an A+. I was the captain of my college cross country team and also a member of the radio station at Thiel. I hope to use my web skills to produce high quality websites for those in need. I am continuously coming up with new and creative designs so my clients always have the newest and best service available. When I finally have locked down a client, we meet somewhere and go over some specifics such as what services they are looking for and what the expectations are for the finished project. After this I go home and begin brainstorming on potential things that I could include in the site. This includes the color, the site layout, and some pictures that should be included in the site. When it comes down to the designing. I get on my computer and create 2 or 3 drafts of the site for the client so they can have a variety and decide which one they want to move forward with. When the client and I have met again, we will advance to the final stage of development before finally deciding on a finished product. Payment is produced and the website is published and made live. This is something that is maintained monthly in order to keep a lasting relationship with my clients. When it comes to the social media aspect, we often meet up and develop a business or marketing plan on how we want to move forward with advertising. Sometimes its as simple as posting updates on Facebook/Twitter everyday and sometimes it is more complex and requires setting up targeted audiences and advertising.
Overall_Why: I find my work to be meaningful because it helps other people with their websites. When it comes to my job at the Hite Company, I am helping people understand how to work a computer and organize files within the systems. This is something that they can not do on their own and would often times struggle with. There is so much going on today that it can often be difficult to balance a schedule. At a great rate, I can provide people with a professional website, social media presence, and/or modern look so that they don't have to worry about it. I think this is very meaningful because people never have to worry about finding solutions or spending money outside of the company to get help. Another reason I think this work is meaningful is the fact that people are always counting on my to boost their business, something that I take great pride in. This work has to be meaningful because so many people are relying on me for help and that is something that makes me feel confident when doing my job. You can usually tell when looking at someone if they really have any faith or confidence in you and seeing that people do makes it that much better. Seeing the faces on my clients after they open that new website for the first time has always given me confidence to continue doing what I do because I know that I will be able to succeed and help.

Work_Story:
I work at a non profit animal shelter in the Midwest. The shelter cares for cats, dogs, pocket pets, reptiles, and barn animals. My job is considered both desk staff and animal care staff. This means in the morning I clean the animals' enclosures and check them over, and then once cleaning is finished and the building is open to the public, I process adoptions, conduct adoption interviews, and take in surrendered animals from the public when people can no longer take care of their pets. 
 I am also in charge of a satellite location for the shelter and work there routinely. There the staff is in charge of everything from medical, grooming, behavior, training, volunteer training, and more for the whole location. We have access to veterinarians and medical staff for medications and emergencies and access to a grooming station if need be. This location has only cats and small animals such as rabbits and guinea pigs and hamsters. Occasionally we will also take in reptiles for temporary holding until they can be transferred to a more appropriately set up location. Sometimes we do stray intake there as well, which means taking in strays if the public brings them to us. We get everything from dog and cat strays to parakeet strays and turtle strays. Sometimes these are animals that people just set loose. We cannot turn animals away and even say this on the website. There is no fee for someone bringing in strays but there is a surrender fee if someone is surrendering an animal. Small animals are $15 and cats are $25 and dogs are $30. Kittens and puppies are five dollars per animal, but we will take them in free if the people will let us fix the mother cat or dog. Barn animals and reptiles have other special surrender fees depending on circumstance, size, etc. Occasionally my shelter handles hoarding case as the animal control unit for my city is connected to our nonprofit animals shelter. Recently we had a hoarding case and took in 80 sugar gliders. 
 Our adoptions are very strict to make sure animals get a good home. Cats are $100 and dogs are $125. Puppies are $200 because so often people adopt puppies because they're cute and then return them when it's too much work. We have a high price to hopefully avoid those unserious adopters as our goal is to keep a pet in a home permanently. Our adoption application covers where a person lives, if they have landlord's permission, if everyone agrees about the new pet, how they will train the new pet, what other pets they have in the home, and a veterinarian reference. Because most animals go good in pairs, we have a program called a bonded buddy program where the first pet is the initial price, and the second pet is only 25 dollars. So basically one cat is $100, and you can get a second cat for $25. Our staff will help find two cats that get along and will be good to live together, if two pairs of cats aren't already bonded in our shelter bonded buddy program. This is one two pets come in together and their former owner request them to live together.
 My job can be emotionally taxing and the pay isn't very good, but it is a nonprofit and more a job you do because you like working with animals, not because you like lots of money.
Overall_Why: I find it very meaningful. Essentially I feel like I help save animal's lives. Some come in to us abused and battered and broken and to see them come out of their shells and gain confidence and trust in humans again is the best feeling in the world. Some of the animals we take in, especially from hoarding and abuse situations, have never met a nice human in their life. Likewise, some people we adopt to just need a friend they have never had before and an animal can provide that companionship and love for a human of any age. From a child getting their first pet to our customers that adopt a cat for their widowed and elderly grandmother. Animals provide so much for people and I feel like my work is very meaningful to both humans and pets. We right wrongs and find happily ever afters. We are a nonprofit and get so many donations and it's good to see the good in humanity. People care about animals so much. The best feeling is when we have a long term pet adopted and the adopter brings the pet into visit us later, or sends us photos and emails and stories of how they are doing.

Work_Story:
I am a middle school theatre teacher. In order to get this job, I needed both a Bachelor's and a Master's degree, and it took three years of working in related fields full time before I was able to actually get the job I wanted-this one. I had worked in education for several years previously, but never full time. 
 
 In theory, my job is 7:30-3:30 every day. In actuality, it is more like 7:00-6:00. I spend the time before school getting prepared for the day, making coffee, meeting with students, and giving my students a safe and comfortable space to prepare for their own days. My classroom often has about ten young people in there before the school day begins, giving them a chance to gear up for the work of the day ahead. I teach four separate subjects throughout the day, plus a study hall, which is more than anyone else in the building. I also teach more hours in a day than anyone else in the building, due to a variance that pays me a small amount of extra money to take on that additional class period. This is not something that I do really by choice, but more because the demand for my class is high and I want to allow students the opportunity to take the courses. After school lets out at 3:10, I set up for the following day. 
 
 If I am in rehearsals, which I am for about half of each semester, I then head to the auditorium and meet my students. I am in charge of 25-50 students during this time, and I was only recently given another adult to help out--and then, it's at the expense of my department. The students and I work together from 3:30-5:30. We talk, do warmups, and run the show we are producing at the time. I have students in charge of all of the technical aspects, as would happen in community and professional theatres, so I train them on all of those aspects of the job as well. By the time the show opens, it is definitely theirs and not mine. 
 
 I love my job. It does tend to take over my life, but I love it. I have tried other careers and none of them made me want to go to work every day the way this one does. 
 
 If I could change something about my job, it would be the pay. I know that's a really common theme from teachers, but I think the commonality of the statement is very telling. I work 11 hours a day for 10 months out of the year. I am not paid for the two months I am not on the clock, but I am still working during those months, both at a second job and on things for my classroom. The only way to move up the pay scale is to pay for additional college classes, but I do not get paid enough to take those classes. Just this week, I contacted my district to see if continuing education classes would count. These are not free or less work than other courses, but they are less expensive. I was told no, that I must take graduate courses for this. Graduate courses are often three times more expensive than undergraduate, and five to six times more than continuing education. There is a weeklong course I hope to take this summer, and since I would have to be on-campus the whole week, the course will cost around $3000. That is a month's pay after taxes.
 
 But, I have done other things. I actually didn't originally major in education, but in theatre itself, and I worked professionally in theatre for awhile. I do not regret that, as I cannot look at my life and wonder "what if?" And it made me realize that I simply don't like adults that much! I get to spend my life with the coolest young people, helping them through the moral and emotional challenges of being a "tween." And I can tell you, kids are so much better than they were when I was that age. We were hateful. I am not saying they are perfect, but I can say I don't see "burn books" like we had. When a child finds themselves feeling hate towards another, they often come to me and ask for help in how to not feel that way. How great is that?
Overall_Why: I find my work extremely meaningful. I teach young people about theatre, but more importantly, I teach them about each other. My theatre class is inclusive, meaning students from all walks of life are in there. I have students who can't afford the $1 daytime tickets for our shows and students who are wearing new Jordans. I have students with Down's Syndrome and nonverbal autism, and I have students who are so gifted that there is a very real chance they will change the world with what they create. My students are Christian, Jewish, Muslim, Hindu, Sikh, and atheists. My students come from all over the world and speak many different languages at home. I teach middle school, so many people think this "doesn't happen," but I have students who come out to me as members of the LGBT community each year, and I get to work with them on being okay with all of that. And I don't work in a big city! Sure, we occasionally have conflicts caused by misunderstandings between groups, but they are rare and they are talked out rather than fought out. And, at the risk of patting myself on the back, I think it's because of the culture of respect I create in my classroom. We are there to learn from each other every day. I freely admit when I am ignorant on a topic and I show the students what it looks like to find the information and ask for help. As a result, I often see students doing the same! I can't wait to see the people these kids become.

Work_Story: I am currently a senior chief in the United States Navy. I have been in the Navy for 14 years now. I am in patrol squadron 26. This squadron is currently stationed in {city}, {state}. I am the senior chief in charge of the night shift maintenance department. This means that I have to make sure all of the operations that are scheduled for my shift are ready to go. This includes making sure all of the flights that are going to be taking off during the night shift go off smoothly. If there is an issue with a plane that needs to be taken care of before it can take off, then I contact the shops that need to work on the plane to make sure all gripes are taken care of get done so we can have the plane for its flights. I also have to take care of all the planes that are already in flight and due to return on my shift. If they need to come back due to an inflight issue, then I have to try and get that fixed as soon as possible. If upon return, they have some issues with any component of the plane that will cause issues for its next flight, then I have to make sure that the department that needs to fix that issues gets on it. I am also in charge of making sure that the flight schedule for the next day gets released to the appropriate members of the squadron. I also have to make sure that all the personnel issues that come up during my shift get taken care of. If there are any prior issues involving members of my shift, I need to get updates on them and relay any new information up the chain of command. On certain days I am the leader that has to make sure that the training for each department is getting done. We have training on Mondays. I have to make sure the departments actually do training and its gets logged. I also have to carry out a fod(foreign object damage) walk down once a week on Mondays to make sure that the flight line is clear of any dangerous objects that could cause harm to personnel or the aircrafts. My job involves a lot of technical things that have to do with the planes in our squadron. There is also a lot of coordinating with each supervisor of the various shops that he have to make sure our work mission for that evening is being carried out. I get updates from them to see where they are at for each project that they are taking on. The main thing for me is to just oversee the nightly operations of the squadron. I have to make sure that we are making progress to have as many airplanes safe for flight as we can. I also have to make sure that this is done is the safest way possible. At the end of the shift, I update the flight board. All the planes that have been fixed have their statuses updated. The ones that aren??t fixed, are passed down to the next shift. Finally, before we leave I have to make sure that all tools have been accounted for. This job is very rewarding and I am always happy to help my country in any way I can. It all starts with making sure that my team is ready to work and they have all that they need to accomplish their jobs.
Overall_Why: When it comes to my work I find it very meaningful due to what I do. I think that anyone that is in the armed forces is doing a meaningful job. When it comes to representing your country there is a sense of pride that one cant really explain. You have to do it in order to feel it an know what its about. The things that I do and the people I work with are like a second family to me. Once every year and a half they are my family on a full time basis. We go overseas for a six month deployment and we are away from our traditional families. So the people who I work with and call my shipmates, are the family that I have at that time. These people come from all walks of life. They are young and older. They are from all races. Some were born in this country, and others are foreigners who have come to this country to call it their own. In the end they are all great Americans who want to serve their country with honor, courage and commitment. For the most part, you will not find a greater group of individuals. This makes this job extremely meaningful to me. Just becoming a team with so many different individuals in order to help carry out the naval missions of this country is amazing. Having a chance to see the world while wearing this great uniform is an experience that no one can underestimate.

Work_Story:
I work as a TCA or a Tourism Compliance Agent. The job reminds me of one of those sitcoms about working - like half the stuff I put up with couldn't possibly be happening in real life. My job is split into two parts - field work and office work. Now I work in the county's planning and zoning office. It is a large office with a general area and office off each wall. Now everyone who works inside this office permanently can be summed up to being a man-child most days. Every time I come in to get anything I can assure you what I find is priceless. 
 Now the majority of what I actually do is 90% field work, which I have a love/hate relationship with. Since this is the majority of my job I'll be describing most of it. I wake up in the early cracks of dawn and drive out a remote cove where I live. Now this cove is one of the most highly populated places in the world for wild bears. I spend my day monitoring tourist and especially tour guides to make sure no one tries to provoke or pet them. No I also make sure the rich millionaires that surround this cove don't freak out on the tour guides. If anything I am a glorified babysitter of millionaires and tour guides trying to make sure tourists get their photo-op with a cute bear and then go home. 
 Now I spend most of my time in my car filling out paperwork or reading the newspaper. The cove is in a remote area so there is no cellphone service or anything to do. I get paid a lot to not lose my mind mostly. When large tour buses arrive I monitor the entire area (about a few acres). I stop tour guides from taking large groups onto people's private properties. I encourage tourists to use the restrooms provided instead of bushes. I also take a lot of statistical data of incidents and the amount of people visiting the area. This data is used to get money and grants to improve the area to be able to handle the large influx of people. 
 I have no real power to actually stop anyone from doing anything. I'm there more to encourage people to behave. I have a little jacket and look very official. But if anything major happens (for example, a tour guide pulled a local from her vehicle and screamed at her for interrupting his tour) I usually call my boss, the state troopers, or the forestry service depending on who is involved. It's a pretty comical job and pays well. No one in the office ever really has a clue what were up to. We register drivers, manage the area, and create the presentations that produces the programs funding. It's definitely a great job most days. I get paid lots of government money to hang out in the great outdoors and see bears and wildlife every day. 
 Now during the summer field work gets so intense it??s all I do. I wake up - head out to the cove and then eventually come home and go to bed. I make a ton of money in overtime. After the summer ends I go back to the office for the winter and compile all the information I??ve gathered and create presentations for different government bodies that fund the program. I??ll spend weeks slowly typing in data into a spreadsheet because we gather all our data by hand since no electronics work and my bosses don??t want to invest in any of the systems in place to help collect data. After I create these presentations (which thankfully my higherup gets to present instead of me) I go to work on improving our program and creating better systems to take care of the most common incidents we have occurring. Right now I??m documenting issues with space for pedestrians over this bridge that is due to be renovated. We??re currently demanding that they widen both sides of the bridge and create walkways versus the current version where tourists just walk in the middle of a busy highway. It??s not the most exciting work ever, but the people in the office keep it fun and I have a great time, which makes it all worth it.
Overall_Why:
I think that my work has some meaningful aspects to it. I spend my days mostly in nature helping others connect with wildlife and stuff that goes beyond the cities and boxes that we've built ourselves into. At the same time I find the most of my job is about as meaningful as TSA is in the airports. I am as much part of the bureaucracy as anyone else is anymore. My job seems very pointless in the grande scheme of things. I believe at the height of it is that I exist to fill the requirements of some insurance agency who wanted the tourists to be most 'protected' near the bears. Not that I have any ability to stop a bear attach or anything of the like. I think there is a lot more meaningful work I could be doing but this doesn't correlate to how much I enjoy my job. It's silly and some days I want to quit, but for the most part it keeps a smile on my face and it pays really well. It could be more meaningful, but at the end of the day I feel like most meaningful work never pays enough to keep you fed and housed. It may feel your moral-happiness bar but past that it won't do much else. 
 
 My job is silly, I don't think it could or would even be automated. Humans would never let robots heard them the way they let me slip in an advice them that petting bears is not a good idea. In a way maybe I mean something to them, but my job means nothing to me.

Work_Story:
I don't know if I can get 500 words out of describing my work. I manage the kitchen in a steakhouse/rib joint. basically we have hand cut steaks, chicken, pork ribs, and beef ribs. We also have the usual side and salads, but it's pretty much a standard steak and rib joint. Since I'm one of two kitchen managers I split the time required to run a kitchen with another guy. A single person couldn't run a kitchen 7 days a week, obviously. 
 
 I guess I should start with my responsibilities on a day-to-day basis. On a typical day I do a variety of tasks including, but not limited to, vegetable prep work, meat prep work, inventory management in house, ordering, delivery of inventory, managing the staff of the kitchen, managing the time sheet (work schedule) for my team, covering absences, cooking, restocking, and whatever miscellaneous other things happen to go sideways while working. Twice a week I receive a truck loaded with the cold goods we need for our work including vegetables, meats, and frozen items such as ice cream and whole/half chickens. Once a week I receive a truck of dry goods and canned goods. Once every two weeks a professional knife sharping company comes to swap out our knives. They pick up the knives we've been using and drop off freshly sharpened knives for our prep and line cooks.
 
 A typical day for me usually starts between 4 pm and 5 pm. I get in and immediately check in the with morning manager and kitchen staff to see if anything untoward has occurred that might need to be handled before the dinner rush. If we are out of something or low on something but we have the materials, I will hop in the back and knock out whatever prep needs to be completed. Usually this involves making large Tupperware bins full of tossed salad. People often refuse to believe it, but the salad you get in a restaurant is usually created by chopping all the lettuce, cabbage, and carrots and dumping them in a large steel sink together. Then, the sink is filled and everything washed 2-3 times draining the sink between washes. Then, it is all removed and placed in a large Tupperware bin and placed in a walk-in cooler. Extra salad ingredients such as tomatoes and onions are kept separate until the salad is actually assembled for the customer. In the first hour of work I usually knock out this (if it's needed) and any other prep work such as cutting meat, putting dry rub on ribs and putting them in the steamer, etc. I also might have to leave and go to a nearby grocery store to get any items of which we are completely out (usually lettuce).
 
 Once I'm sure the kitchen is stocked and ready to go I check the line and make sure every station is clean and fully stocked and then I go into the office and check the inventory and schedule sheets. When the dinner rush comes I'm back in the kitchen filling in the gaps on the line and making sure the kitchen runs smoothly. A kitchen is a place of extreme tension and I'm a bit like the conductor in an orchestra. 
 
 Once the dinner rush is complete I can relax a bit. I double check the stock and make notes of anything that we might be running low on while my cooks clean the kitchen. Then, we usually go to the bar and have a few drinks and watch television once the restaurant is closed.
Overall_Why:
I don't think my work is very meaningful. I don't really do anything special and the food we serve isn't super amazing. I don't create anything unique and I don't make a lot of money. I could be replaced by another person and the place wouldn't skip a beat. I feel lucky to have the job because there are a lot of people in my area without work an there is a bad heroin problem in my area, so I know how valuable the job is. However, I do not feel in any way that what I do has meaning.
 
 I supposed I would only view a job in which you're helping people in some way as having meaning. In that sense, I'm not sure working in any restaurant has true meaning or a lasting impact on the people who work there or the patrons. I used to be an English teacher in China and I felt like that work really had meaning. I felt like I made a difference in the lives of the people I taught and those with which I worked. However, at my current job I feel like I might as well be a cashier at Wal-Mart. There is nothing about my work that gives me a sense of pride or accomplishment. I am not proud to tell people what I do and nobody admires my work or aspires to become what I am.

Work_Story:
I've been working at my current job for over twenty years. My job title is Senior Programmer Analyst, which is a primarily a computer programming job. I am part of a small IT staff of a Customs House Broker/Freight Forwarder. What my company does is move freight in and out of the United States on behalf of our customers. This requires that we get government approval to do so. We must send information to US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) as well as other ancillary government departments, such as the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the National Highways Safety Administration (NHTSA). They then in turn, must send approval and other information or requirements, back to my company for us to process. Our customers are generally small, local businesses but we do have customers that are nationally recognized companies. 
 
 As one of the most highly regulated industries in the US, getting approval from government entities in a fast and efficient manner requires technology. That's where our company's IT staff come in. We have developed our own custom interface with CBP (based on their strict guidelines) that allows a free-flow of data between my company and their computer systems. CBP in the last two years, recently upgraded its software, which required a complete rewrite of the software on my end. I programmed approximately 95% of the necessary changes, which took me almost 2 years. The interface with CBP is simple flat files, with data elements in specific positions. So, it's fairly straightfoward to program. However, it was the number of transactions and lengthy testing that made it probably my largest project in my career and definitely the largest in my current position. 
 
 Our software is written in a little known software by Progress software called Open Edge. It is a Relational Database Management System and language that allows fully-functional integration to various database, websites and processes and other technologies. I've been coding in Open Edge for all of my post-college career (almost 27 years) and my company has been using this software as long as I've been working for them.
 
 Besides our interfaces with CBP, my company has also developed its own software for our own internal users, so that they can collect and manage the information required by the government, as well as information for our customers. When I first started working for my company, I was tasked to develop the main "modules" for our business, an Export module and an Import module. These modules are still in use today and as there are changes in internal procedures and external rules, I have made the required changes along the way. Not to mention the various bugs that occasionally creep into our system. I've also developed various offshoots of these modules that have managed the operations workflow, to help our operations staff complete their jobs more effectively and efficiently. I've also developed Electronic Data Interfaces (EDI) to facilitate the collection of information to and from our customers and our overseas agents. 
 
 Other members of our IT staff have developed web-based applications for our customers, which allow them to book and track their shipments, manage their purchase orders and generate reports. I'm quite proud of the work I've done for my company and the technology that allows us to stand out in a crowd of competitors.
Overall_Why:
I wouldn't say that I find my work extremely meaningful in the grand scheme of life or the world itself. When I think of meaningful work, I think of work that truly transforms people's lives, like doctors, nurses and therapists. The people that work as police, firemen, the men and women that serve in the armed forces, who put their lives on the line to protect and serve the public at large. 
 
 But on a smaller scale, my work is meaningful to my company. My company heavily depends on the automated products that I have designed and developed to get their jobs done in an effective and efficient manner. Like said in the previous page, interacting with the multiple government entities as well as our customers, requires technology. I've written the majority of programs and modules that my operations staff uses in their day to day job functions. And as the laws and requirements change, our software must change with it. 
 
 My company is a family owned business and has been in the industry for over 100 years. Our CEO is the granddaughter of the man who started it. She recently pulled the entire IT staff except our director (7 people) into my bosses office to personally give us our Christmas bonuses and to tell us how much she appreciated all we do for her company. It did make me realize that I (and my entire department) are appreciated by the owner and management for what we do. 
 
 And there are those occasional times when users are ecstatic about a change that we have installed and helps them to do their jobs better or quicker. It's those times that I do feel my job and what I do is meaningful to a small part of my world.

Work_Story:
I am a business owner. I co-own an urban farm with another woman (not life partner). We grow nursery seedlings, very niche vegetables, culinary herbs, red raspberries, black raspberries, currants, gooseberries and other specialty berries. We also manage a small covey of quail in an enclosed aviary and sell their eggs.
 Farming is not a profitable business, but it is full-time work. We work year-round to meet meager budget goals, and our end product is high quality.
 I create home-processed products from the dried raw agriculture products in order to add value. Examples are blending dried herbs with salt for finishing salts and blending different dried herbs into herbal tea which we package for sale.
 As a business owner, I'm also responsible for maintaining records, data input, filing taxes, brand creation, graphic design, communications, inventory management, job training, transportation and any other task you might imagine that is required to run a business.
 In our co-ownership arrangement, the "business" role I most often find myself in is Creative Director. The "farming" role I most often find myself in is nursery and small livestock manager.
 As nursery manager, I am currently clearing out the high tunnel and greenhouse and generally tidying things up. Those tasks include washing all seedling supplies - trays, cell tray inserts, six-packs, watering cans, soil buckets, etc. I'm also in charge of installation maintenance of the inflation system to keep our double-walled high tunnel inflated for proper heat insulation. Once we have seedlings in pots, I'll be monitoring the temperatures in the greenhouse and high tunnel, adjusting supplemental heat and ventilation as necessary, and watering twice daily.
 In order to have nursery stock to sell, I'll be seeding trays of herbs such as mint, catnip, chives, parsley, several varieties of basil, and others. Those trays will germinate inside a room in my house under grow lights. They'll stay inside, putting on growth, for several weeks. Then we'll transplant them into larger 3.5 inch pots to finish growing out in the greenhouse and high tunnel. I'll start about 40 trays of seedlings this way.
 We also order seedlings for woody perennials to transplant and sell. We'll order about 1,200 seedlings this year, caring for them inside for a few days, then transplanting them and moving them outside to the greenhouse and hoop house.
 As far as small livestock management goes, the day to day is fairly simple. Daily, the quail are fed and watered, and the eggs are collected. Every few weeks, I shovel out their bedding, moving it to a designated composting area, and replace the bedding with fresh pine shavings. When quail die, which occasionally occurs from them startling and hitting their head, I bury them in the yard. We refresh the covey about every other year at which time I care for the hatchlings in a brooder outside. 
 The eggs from the quail must be washed in order to be sold or eaten. I wash them in a premixed egg wash solution, let them air dry, and package them for sale by the dozen.
Overall_Why:
Yes. I find my work extremely meaningful. The act of raising food for people to eat is my highest calling. It is extremely satisfying to think of people that I know and love being nourished by the food that I have worked hard to grow and raise. I take great pride in ensuring quality and nutrition in what I grow, because I know that it will have a positive impact on those who will be eating it.
 It's important to treat the earth as a partner in my job, not simply as something to extract resources from, so I find a lot of pride in the methods we use to decrease negative impact on the earth.
 The agrarian way of life, feeding ones family and others, has become less valuable to society as we depersonalize the people whose hands create the stuff we consume. It's too bad, because there is a lot of purpose found in creating things - shaping them with ones own hands. 
 Farming requires unending attention and unwavering commitment, it requires problem-solving and flexibility. If I found no meaning in the task, then I couldn't continue to do it. Mega corporations that are moving heads of cattle through a production line or keeping pig in farrowing crates and focusing on maximizing potential profit cannot possibly keep a meaningful connection to the product they're creating. In that regard, I desire to stay small as I farm, to remain connected with customers and the resources that I'm using as I feed us.

Work_Story: The line of work that I am in in something that I have been doing for a little over nine years now. I am a flight engineer. Its a very complicated job. It takes a lot of skills and concentration in order to be someone that is going to be able to keep up with all of the inner workings of an aircraft. I started out my career in the Navy. They were the ones that trained me to be a flight engineer. I went through a variety of schooling in order to finally get certified by the Navy. After I decided to get out of the Navy, I took the necessary classes that I needed to take and got certified to work in the civilian field. I found a job working for a private company here in South {state}. I love {state} and wanted to move here. This job was perfect and allowed me to live where I wanted to. I also get paid a decent amount of money which is never a bad thing. I also get to travel a lot and enjoy the world. Since I was already doing that in the Navy, I wanted to be able to continue the traveling and seeing all the different parts of the world that I can. I got spoiled and couldnt see myself doing another job that would take that away from me. I lucked out with this amazing job. Now that I have talked about my job and how I got it and why I got, I will get into what it is that I actually do. For the place that I work my job is simple. The actually job duties are simple. The carrying out of them is what can get complicated if you run into issues. I am one of the engineers that are called upon in order to perform ground turns. You need ground turns when components of a plane have been changed. The changes that are made have changed the integrity of the aircraft, thus they need to be tested. You do that by performing the ground turns. You have two types of ground turns. One if if the low powered variety. This means that there want a major change done. You are just checking to make sure the new product works as its intended to do. The other type of turn is a high power turn. This means that a major component was changed. You have to break it in and do a variety of checks to make sure that its working within the limits that its supposed to be. After the turn is done, then the plane is going to be ready for a test flight. A test flight is only performed for the planes that have had a major component changes. These planes have done all of the ground turns that are needed. They have made any adjustments during those turns that are needed. Now that the turn phase is over, its time to move to making sure that the new component is going to be able to handle a flight. Its a short hour long flight that will test out the component in the air. If everything passes, then the plane will be labeled safe for flight. This means that it can no be used to do whatever is needed of it. I am also in charge of fueling up these planed when they need to be fueled. There is a training component that comes with my job as well. I train certain people when it comes to certain parts of the plane. This allows them to be qualified for many things in order to make them more valuable to the team.
Overall_Why: I find my work to be very meaningful. I am someone that has always wanted to be involved in a career that brings some sort of meaning and enjoyment to my life. If not, then I will hate it and I wont last at it. Before joining the service I chose this career. I felt that it was something that was not going to be easy. However it was going to be very rewarding. When leaving the service I knew that I wanted to continue to do this job. I felt that I had enjoyed it so much that is was something that I was going to have to keep doing. I could not see myself doing something else. I just had to find the right landing spot for me to leave the service. Once I found that, I was all in. I feel that the work I do helps out the world. If there weren't any flight engineers, there would be a huge issues when it comes to having safe planes for flight. This is a tough a dangerous job. One that keeps you away from family and friends at various times. Its some thing that you need to make sure you love and have a passion for. That is something that I have for this job. Making it a meaningful job for me. One that I get to do something I love. With amazing people around me. I also get to benefit from it with good pay as well as seeing the world.

Work_Story:
I work at a {company}. My job title is "Assembler" but my job entails many things. My main job is to make sure all the products that go out to the floor are built properly and set up. I will build things for customers if they are ordered such as furniture, toys, and many other things. Much of my job also requires me to fix and maintain the store and things in it. Currently I am in the middle of painting the entire back room, which is over 5000 square feet of wall space. I am the first one called to fix things around the store that are not working and I do just about anything that does not involve electrical work. Having me at the store saves the store a lot of money because I do things that are not very simple and required a lot of learning on the job.
 
 My bosses are great and I really enjoy working with them, they seem to always have my back when I have a question or need help with a project or any type of scheduling issue.I have worked there for two years so far and I may be inline for a promotion to a manger very soon. I enjoy interacting with customers on a daily basis, it makes me happy to be helpful to these people.
 
 My coworkers are very fun and interesting people who have taught me a lot about the store and the things in it. I have a man who was hired not long ago to be my assistant and he is very nice and helpful. He is a bit older and does not have the ability to do all of the things that I can but that doesn't stop him from attempting to do things to help me out.
 
 I am also the store's expert on bicycles. I started out with my main job being to build all of the bikes that the store sells but now that I have a man who is there to assist me I have decided to allow him to do this so he can learn to do something on days that I am not there to help him out. 
 
 I don't make as much money as I'd like but I know that if I keep working as hard as I have been that I will get a promotion and begin to make money like believe I deserve. I did just find out that I am receiving a bonus and a pay raise which will be very nice and help me out a lot because I need money to pay for school this semester. 
 
 Overall I really have enjoyed working at {company} and I would recommend it to others if they were to ask. When you look past the people that go out of the way to make the job more difficult for the employees it is really a great place to work and I think many of my coworkers would tend to agree with me. Many of the people have been there for over 20 years and I think that says something about the culture of our store being so positive. We did get a new store manager about a year ago and it has really been a big improvement, I see many people being much more motivated to work under her and it shows in the way the store feels and looks.
 
 One thing that needs to be done to improve the store is that it needs a bit of a remodel, there are some things that break constantly and are not good looking for the customers and they do receive complaints quite a lot. Luckily the store gets a remodel this year and that will turn the place around from a physical standpoint. Anew coat of paint and new fixtures will make the place feel brand new.
Overall_Why: I think my job is pretty meaningful. Every time that I help someone and see that I made their day a bit better I feel like my job has some meaning. Being able to see the before and after of things that I have done at work is great and really motivates me to keep working hard. Seeing how I took something that did not look good and turning it into something that I can be proud of is one of the best feelings there is for me. The fact that my managers trust me to complete difficult and important projects with little to no supervision show that I have a meaning to not only my self but to them as well. Especially when I'm building bikes for kids and I get to see them pick out the one they like and the face they make when they find it makes my day every time, so for that reason alone my job has meaning. But then again meaning isn't really all that important to me in a job, life in my opinion does not really have a meaning and I can still take a lot of enjoyment in the regular things that I do just about every day. Knowing that my coworkers care about me is also something that I find meaningful. I've made many friends at this job so far and it makes the day go by so much quicker since I get to spend it with people that I find fun to be around.

Work_Story:
I start my day about 20 minutes earlier than the rest of the workers in my department because I am a member of the lead workers. I meet with the manager of my department and other leads to discuss what the daily objectives are, we also learn what our expected end time will be. I will work anywhere from 8 to 10 hours depending on the amount of product that needs to be shipped out. We also talk over any difficulties we may have had in the previous day??s tasks and if we foresee any obstacles in today's assignments. I then go to the computer type up an assignment sheet list of all the skids to be completed and who is doing what jobs for my 6 people team. I also have to type in an order for the forklift driver detailing what skids my team needs and if there is any products to be filled.
 With that done I hand out the task list to my team and we begin to work. I spend the day moving skids from the drop zone to my work station where I inspect the boxes for damage and if there is any damage to the skid. Any boxes that are damaged are moved to my table, and if the skid is damaged I have to move undamaged boxes to a new skid. I then have to measure the box the part is in find appropriate sized boxes and gather them to my work station. I then open damaged boxes examine parts and if they are good rebox them. If they are not good I scrap them out using a scan gun, throw them in the recycle bin and go find the amount of product I need to replace them. I also have to print out new labels for the boxes and restack them on the skid. After a skid is complete I scan the master label back into the scan gun, enter a new location for the product to go and take my floor jack and move the skid to the load out zone.
 I also may have to work on returns. These skids are not just one product but multiple different products that have been shipped back to the warehouse as a return. I have to scan in each box of product and the quantity it contains check boxes and parts for damage rebox if the box does not meet quality standards make new labels and scan the product into a new location. 
 My day is only broke up from these task by 2 fifteen minute breaks and 30 minutes for lunch. Then after everything is done clean up my work area, print out everyone's accomplished work pages and turn them into the manager. Sometimes on rare occasions when they have heavy volume or a large amount of absentees, I have been asked to fill in for other lines and departments from working at the outbound doors helping to scan products into trucks to working the lines of boxing out products. If that is to happen I will learn that at the morning meeting and after I get my team situated I will do that for the part of the day I am needed. If it is working on the line I will stand in one position for an hour loading a certain couple of parts as the boxes move down the line. I will 
 switch after the hour and either load different products or take finished boxes off the line and stack them on a skid where it will be picked up by a fork truck.
Overall_Why: I do not find my work all that meaningful because to me it is just a job that gets me paid. I do not have a passion for it and do not feel any since of having done something that makes a difference in the overall meaning of the world. I do the best job I can and achieve as high as I can because I get a paycheck for doing it and the other people in my company depend on me to do my best to help them do their best. Most of the work in my job will one day be automated and the majority of it will be able to be accomplished by a machine so my importance in the company is disposable, that takes away some of the meaningfulness of it to me. To me to be meaningful would mean the job gives value to or in someones life. My job is a paycheck because the most meaningful work I do or could ever do does not bring income into my home, it is in being a good wife and mother. A decent friend and a respectable human. The most meaning I can pull from my job is when I can help someone else's day go better, help them out and make their job easier to accomplish. For the most part the job I do makes money for rich people who already have tons of money. If I decided not to do my job anymore quite or just stop working hard it will not change the fact that the shareholders and rich people at the top are still making money.

Work_Story: I am communications manager for a generics pharmaceutical company in {state}. I write things, all kinds of things. I do memos, video scripts, tip sheets, organizational announcements, intranet copy, training programs, user guides, posters, social media posts and charts. I started in September and it is going ok. I have my own office, which is nice but lonely. I bring my lunch to work 3x per week. I'm not allowed to work at home and for the first time in my life, I have to punch a time clock as a professional worker! I miss the people at my old work and I am counting the months -- 93 -- till I can retire early and travel. My job is important though because I am now my family's sole breadwinner. It is tolerable. It is good hours, nice boss and easy work. I am paid fairly and sometimes I have interesting projects I do wish I had a better commute. Right now, it takes an hour each way, but it is better than my previous commute which was 2 1/2 hours each way by bus into NYC. This work is not really meaningful for me. I would prefer to work for a non-profit but I can't afford to. Also, it is increasingly difficult to get a job since I am over the age of 50. This company is owned by two Indian brothers who are gazillionaires -- they built all of this from scratch in only 15 years. The company is due to merge with another comapny in another few months and then we will have American management. I hope that I will still have a job. I think so. I am engaged right now in putting together a rebate event for our Long Island manufacturing facility. WE are getting rebate checks totalling $600,000 from the local power company for installing all energy efficient processes and equipment. We have invited the Governor of NY to attend and say a few words but I don't know if he will. I hope so -- he might be president of the US someday. At the event, I will probably have to be the master -- mistress? - of ceremonies. I'm a little nervous about that but should do fine. I also have to write the remarks for our CEO. I have no problem with that. So, in summary, I like my work fairly much. Sometimes I am bored. Sometimes I am lonely. But it is a paycheck and a fairly good one; I get a full range of benefits; and I go home at a reasonable hour. I am looking forward to my vacation in Hawaii on April 1 and I look forward every week to the weekend. I close my door and eat lunch at my desk most of the time. I have not made any friends here which is surprising because I am very quick to make friends at all my other jobs. I would even say I am beloved at my previous places of employment. I don't see any opportunity to advance here, unfortunately. This was promised to me when I interviewed so I am disappointed - sometimes angry -- that I was deceived. But, it is unlikely I would easily find another job with the same benefits and salary at my age so I will probably stay. I have been doing communications for more than 20 years and I am ready to retire and try something else. I was unemployed this past summer and I was overjoyed -- I was just so, so happy! I am frequently bored at my job, hence why I checked out mechanical turk.
Overall_Why: Not really. A few things bother me. First, the company pays its manufacutring workers $11.00 an hour in Long Island and New Jersey -- not a living wage. It is against my values. Second, we are the largest manufacturer of opiods and my sister died of a heroine overdose. Third, the owners belong to the nearby Trump golf club and are Trump supporters. All of these things are contrary to my values. I do sometimes find meaning in writing things that enable employees to feel better about their jobs, but I don't get to do that so often here. There is more of an emphasis on money, working all the time, material things and lack of diversity. Also there is only one woman on the leadership team, none on the board. There is never talk about developing women or fixing or changing that.
