Work_Story: I work as a paralegal for a large financial services firm. We are an in-house legal department that handles everything from customer disputes, employee disputes and regulatory issues. I serve four attorneys and in my role I draft legal documents, file legal filings, work on discovery issues and also perform basic office tasks such as filing, etc. I also serve in a customer service capacity as I answer phones and inquiries that come into the department. Another task that I am responsible for is the service and response of subpoenas that are sent to the firm. I have served in this capacity with this company for over seven years. I have performed in a role similar to this most of my working career as I have always worked in compliance related positions. In a typical day, I answer emails, I answer the phone and I perform some level of task related to my cases. For example, in the early part of a case I am responsible for reviewing the claim, assessing it for validity and reviewing the client and information available. Then I will begin gathering the applicable documents such as account paperwork, branch files, account statement and similar items. These items are all considered discovery and will later be produced to opposing counsel and as a result they need to be organized, catalogued and reviewed as they will be an issue later in the case. I will at times discuss these items with my attorneys and advise of my findings or seek guidance regarding future tasks. I will draft a small profit and loss calculation that incorporates the information from the claim and it will reflect the figures we have been able to ascertain from the claim filing documents at that point. This is also a time to discuss the possibility of obtaining a third party profit and loss calculation from a financial expert so as a result I would obtain quotes from experts we have used in the past and get those quotes to management for approval. I will also explore more of the possible discovery for the matter such as reviewing emails, conducting outside research and preparing the discovery for production to opposing counsel. Beyond that I will then start preparing for the mediation and/or trial to come so this becomes a time to create discovery exhibits, have preparation meetings and conduct final reviews of the matter and even formulate legal arguments. I will take notes on specific issues that may be harmful or helpful to the attorneys and they will use this information for the preparation of their arguments, etc. Additionally I will look for applicable case law that will either support claims we are making or negate claims the other side has made. This is done by accessing our legal research database and even searching for white papers or similar writings from respected counsels who have faced the issues prior. The information can also come from university law professors as well. With this information we can then hope to present sound arguments in the trail or mediation and achieve a more favorable outcome in the matter. Once the case actually goes to hearing I will accompany the attorneys to the hearing and serve in a support role and witness preparation role. I will chart the progress of the case and address issues as they arise such as reviewing case law for issues that arise during the hearing or collaborating or discounting a witnesses' statements. We will also formulate a timeline of events based on what we have received in witness testimony.
Overall_Why: I find at least some meaningfulness in my work from the standpoint of accomplishing tasks and serving the attorneys but I know that there is more meaningful work out there such as serving the community or healthcare work. There are times when I have accomplished a certain task or helped someone in particular or even been praised where the job has felt very meaningful or has left me with a sense of accomplishment. I know that the work is meaningful in the limited scope of the job itself as it is needed work and must be accomplished for the sake of achieving our goals and resolving the matters as we deal with. If I did not perform the work it would not be done thus there is meaningfulness in that. My work does not necessarily create great meaning or serve the world as a whole but it does accomplish things at least. I wish the work could be more meaningful for myself from a purely selfish standpoint such as making myself feel better or giving myself a better feeling at the end of the day. My work is meaningful to the company but it does not serve the greater good as a whole nor does it serve a greater purpose from the standpoint of fixing a greater social issue. However I think very few jobs are meaningful in and of themselves unless of course they are of the professions I mentioned above or they have altruistic or charitable goals.

Work_Story: My work is very meaningful to me. I am a home educator. I am also a self employed house flipper. My husband and I for the last 13 years have bought, fixed up, and sold property. We first buy them cheap but yet in a nice area with great resale potential. We actually move into the fixer upper and live there until we have the home fixed up and we are ready to put the house up for sale. While we are fixing up the home, I also am a home educator for my three children. I have a teenager, a pre teen and a 6 year old. Their schooling can take up most of the day. We are a Christian family and therefore our curriculum is usually christian based. My 13 and 12 year old are in 7th and 6th grade. However their geography, social studies, nature, science, and math are all done together in a group setting. They do their language arts, spelling, bible, all on a individual level. We love the Charlotte Mason style of homeschooling. My 6 year old is in the 1st grade and we use a Christian book curriculum for her that I teach her. My husband is a 100% disabled Army Veteran so he is also home to help with the schooling when and if needed. Now back to our fixing of houses. We try to get a house done in a years time. We always make sure that the should the house not sell fast or ever, that we would be comfortable living in the home for longer than anticipated amount of time. We have a realtor that we work with and she has been our realtor for 15 years so she knows what we do and what we wan when we are looking for our next home to fix up. Once we sell a house we look for the next one to fix up. Making sure the location is also appealing to us an to future buyers. We always do all fixes to the best that they can be done. We never lack or skip something because of cost or to save money. Our work is top notch and we take pride in the work that we do on our homes an we are proud to have our names on the resale end of it. We put up new drywall, paint, new trim, redo hardwood flooring or put in new flooring. All light fixtures are replaced with new as well as all accessories. If electrical is out dated, then new gets put in. Same as plumbing. Kitchen are renovated with new counter tops, appliances, faucet and sink. Furnaces and air condition is also up dated. Most every home we have done we have put new windows in as well. We make sure the exterior is redone if need and landscaping as well . We take tremendous pride in our homes and once they are perfect, we put them up for sale and move on to the next one. We use the same realtor to put our home up for sale as well. We are always looking at the real estate market. From the time we buy a home until the time we sell a home. We constantly know what is moving and selling and what is not. It has taken as few as 3 days to sell a home and as much as 8 months to sell a home. It all depends on the housing market at that time. Once our house has an offer on it we always give the buyer the option to buy the pieces or furniture or decor that the house it staged with and that gives me the opportunity to always keep things fresh for the next home we do.
Overall_Why: Yes. I find my work extremely meaningful. We after all do a job that we absolutely love and our children are able to be involved in the processes as well. They are learning valuable and meaningful life skills for now and for in the future. I also get to home educate our children. This is extremely meaningful as I get to included aspects that are extremely important to me that I feel is very important for my children to learn as well. I can also educate my children in a Christ like manner instead of a worldly manner that many schools not educate with. Having my husband and kids home while be able to make an income and be self employed and move at our own rate and pace in extremely meaningful for us as a family. I find great joy in what I do and how I am able to work. Being extremely meaningful to me means that we are doing and promoting some thing that we love and that at the end of the day we are help and find meaning in what we are doing with our lives and how we are impacting an affecting others as well.

Work_Story:
I work as a Free/reduced specialist for a school district. I help families that are struggling financially to get on our program so their kids will be able to eat while at school. What I do varies by what time of year it is generally. If it's the start of the year, I'm spending most of my time processing applications. I'm fairly new at my position, so I've made some changes to this process. Instead of primarily paper, I've been pushing for families to apply online. This has created a massive front-load of work for me at the beginning of the semester, but it's definitely worth it for the amount of time it saves me during the rest of the year. 
 
 In the following months, I and the employees under me work to help families re-apply if they haven't done so yet. Luckily, the grace period from the previous year does not expire right as school starts, but round the end of September. This gives us time to reach out to families to get their butts moving if they want to avoid lunch charges. This also includes a good amount of front-loaded work, but there's a direct correlation between how much effort you make at first compared to how much you have to deal with later. 
 
 Towards the end of the first semester, I begin performing audits of applications that I've received. We accept applications without proof at first because we just want to get the kids fed and taken care of. Once things calm down, the computer selects applications that may not be completely accurate, and those families are notified to bring proof of their income or state assistance. I used to have a more cynical view of families on welfare, but my tune has changed. Most of the time, families pass the audit and genuinely need the help. Those who don't pass the audit are mostly because of a mistake here or there on the application themselves. There are some common mistakes due to bureaucratic wording, and you can't fault many people for those mistakes. Then there are a few who never respond to the audit, and that's mainly because they know they're busted. By not responding, their kids lose their benefits and life goes on.
 
 Things finally die down a bit at the beginning of the second semester. I've begun to go out to the schools where my employees work so I can evaluate how things are going. Since I work in a central office, I'm not always able to see what's going on at the front lines. By going out to each site, I can get a better idea of how effective our current practices are, and if they need changes. I also evaluate the employees themselves and give them feedback. Most of the time, I have great people. I've gotten things to a point where I have to nitpick if I have to find anything to be critical of. 
 
 Towards the end of the semester, I'm already making preparations for the new year. I have to make sure that ever school is stocked with what they need to be able to help families apply for our program. We make good food, and want to make it as accessible as possible. We use a lot of from-scratch cooking, as well as fresh fruit and vegetables as much as we can get.
 
 I love my job, and can see myself doing it until I retire. I love helping the kids.
 
 I typed 500 words and it said I needed 3000 characters, so that's why my word count is higher.
Overall_Why:
Yes, I find my work very meaningful. My work has a direct impact on others in a positive way. Kids need food in order to learn. There's no way around that. If my work can help improve the accessibility of food to the kids, then they'll be able to learn better as a direct result. For many kids, this is the best meal (or even only meal) they'll have all day, so we try to make it count.
 
 For their families, it relieves a financial burden that they would otherwise have during a difficult time in their lives. Unfortunately, kids will be sent to school without food simply because the family had nothing to send them with. We will never take food away from a child, and always work to make sure the child has the food access that they need. If they're in financial trouble, we offer them benefits, if the family is well enough off, then we get on the parents to find out why the student didn't have any food (and we will still feed them)/
 
 This work gives me a sense of satisfaction. It makes me feel like I'm making a real difference. Since I live in the community that I serve, I'm able to talk to families to get their feedback on how we're doing. I should mention here that even though I talk to them about what I do, I always maintain confidentiality. I sometimes check up on some of the kids I know to see how they're doing. Some are doing very well, but others have had some issues. I don't think the kids realize just how many of us adults are cheering them on.

Work_Story:
I am a traffic control supervisor for a large bridge building company. I work mostly night shift during the construction season due to restriction on most roadways during the day time hours. I got into this business about 20 years ago when I started flagging for a utility contractor. I opened my own company about a year later and ran it for 10 years. I was offered my current job by one of the contractors I had sub-contracted with for about 5 years. 
 
 Before any job starts I have already put in a lot of work. There are meetings with the engineer and superintendent to go over all of the plans and figure out staging. We study all of the traffic plans and I create and submit anything that is missing so we have approval before start date. 
 
 A month or so before start date the owner, state, county, city, etc. will host a big meeting with the contractor and all of their subs. We will again go over plans and staging. They always make us spend 4 hours in a conflict resolutions class to address any problems we may encounter once the project has begun. 
 
 I usually have to show up on the project a week or so before the crews to coordinate all of the equipment being brought into the work site. These are usually large cranes and equipment so they need traffic control just for delivery. 
 
 The superintendent and I will begin to call the union halls to start manning up the crews. Everyone has to fill out the employment packages, go over safety regulations and drug test. This usually takes 2 or 3 days depending on the size of the project. 
 
 We start having the subcontractors bring in their office and personnel trailers and get them set up while we are setting ours in place. Some of the subs also have large equipment that will be brought in at this time too. 
 
 Once the project starts my crews and I are very likely to work 7 days a week, 12 hours a day for several months straight. We work with all of the contractors so we normally have to work no matter who is out there. 
 
 These projects are very exciting partly because of the size of most of the bridges we build but also because it is just very satisfying to see the finished product. During the course of a project the crews become very bonded. During that time period we see very little of anyone except each other. 
 
 Working on the highways at night is a very dangerous job and the crew learns very quickly to look out not only for themselves but for each other. We have all seen crew members hurt, a few killed and more car accidents then most of us would ever want to admit. 
 
 I have made a few safety videos with the state for flagger training. I am very dedicated to this cause because it can never be stressed enough how dangerous it is out there. Most accidents I have seen are caused by inattentive or impaired drivers. The crews have to understand they cannot count on the driver to do what they are supposed to do.
 
 I really enjoy this type of work and it allows me to vacation most of the winter months. I have taken continuing education classes throughout my career to stay informed about all the the latest leadership skills.
Overall_Why:
I think my work is pretty meaningful in the aspect that I help to build something that is useful and lasting. I have also had the opportunity to mentor several people and eventually place them in leadership roles. I am not saving the world or fighting world hunger so I would not say it was extremely meaningful. We work the DES to put welfare recipients back to work by hiring through their training programs. 
 This is one of the big failures of the work. I have never had one make it all the way through the program. I believe their idea is that if they teach them a trade they will be able to find and keep a job. The problem seems to be that they need to learn work ethic first. When I am out working at night or on a weekend I cannot keep someone I cannot count on to be there. I have no where to get a replacement and the whole crew ends up suffering. I was never able to figure out how to convey to someone who wasn't taught as a child what work ethics was all about. It didn't matter how well they performed when they were there if I couldn't count on them I couldn't keep them. I was always disappointed in not only them but in myself when I would have to let them go. I would always talk to their case workers to see if we could come up with a plan but always failed.

Work_Story: I work as a wildlife biologist, specializing in spatial analysis and the use of Geographic Information Sciences software. I work for a government agency, in a division with about ten people. I directly supervise one person, who I feel is much smarter than I am. I get along well with everyone that I work with and overall I enjoy my work, although I do wish that I were able to get out into the field more often. I am supposed to be focused on creating species distribution models, but I have managed to establish connections among the fisheries division and spend a good portion of my time working for them. I have worked on projects ranging from analyzing the numbers of people fishing on a lake by their home zip codes to using a side scan sonar fish finder to create a georectified substrate map of hundreds of kilometers of river, creating variables from that map and then using the maximum entropy algorithm to predict spawning habitat for an endangered species of fish. I also spend a lot of time prioritizing areas for conservation actions and money. Being in contact with the fisheries people and the habitat conservation people has introduced me top a lot of areas that I didn't learn about in college and I am happy that I am able to continue learning as I work. In addition to being able to learn this way, my agency offers many training opportunities and we are given free tuition at state colleges. I recently completed a second masters degree this way and am working on a third. I am working in the field that I got my first set of degrees in - wildlife biology and the other two expand on the subjects that I work on - GIS and Restoration ecology. I am given a great deal of freedom in choosing work tasks that I am interested in and am also given almost complete control over how I go about completing my tasks. My supervisors are very hands off in their approach to management, but they are always available to talk and to provide help if I need it. I have received 2 promotions since I came to work for the agency and have been with them for ten years. I am very happy with the people that I work with and the work that I do, however the opportunity to advance my career is limited because it literally requires an government action to create a new position, otherwise the only way to advance is for someone to quit or be fired.I recently was able to successfully complete a project in which I was the lead PI, and we are encouraged to try to publish the results of our work in academic journals. I have two publications now. Another aspect of my work that I appreciate is that it forces me to keep up with advances in technology and scientific discoveries in the wildlife field. We are moving towards working on climate change mitigation and I find that to be a fascinating and very meaningful field. Overall I am very happy with my job and feel that it adds meaning to my life and contributes to society. The biggest downsides are frustrations with policy and that the work can be a bit tedious at times.
Overall_Why: I find my work deeply meaningful. I grew up being exposed to the natural world regularly and cam e to treasure it. In addition, I had pets the entire time that I was growing up, from snails to dogs, and I love animals and care deeply about their well-being. My job allows me to directly influence the condition of the natural world, and the health and quality of life experienced by the creatures living within it. I get to interact with the public at times, and I create materials that can help to teach them about the value of the natural world, and ways that they can help to preserve it. I am also able to work on ways to help moderate the harm that climate change is likely to do. I feel that climate change is one of the most important issues facing the world today. It is vitally important that we find ways to present it to the public so as to erase their doubts and solicit their support in finding solutions. Climate change will cause huge amounts of suffering among the most vulnerable sections of the world, both in humans and in other animals. Finally, we are also experiencing the sixth major extinction. I am able to help provide models based on scientific reasoning to help slow this trend, if even in a small way. I believe that all life is precious, and that diversity in itself is all the reason needed to preserve species. I also hate the idea of the suffering of the individual animals as the extinction ensues.

Work_Story: I work full-time as a customer service representative for a small call center. I basically sit at my desk, waiting for customer service type calls to be routed to me by the sales representatives, although I do some order taking myself if it gets busy and we are short a few order processing reps for the day. Answering the calls to place orders can really be a pain, since I end up with the customers that don't know how to place an order, or what they wanted to order if they knew how. But sometimes they are very nice and it makes me feel good to be able to help them choose a gift for someone, especially someone who is convalescing in the hospital, for example. But my official job title is customer service representative, and that's the job I prefer to do because that's what I was hired to do. Doing customer service takes a special kind of person; someone is is patient, and understands the customer's needs. Not everyone can do this. I've gotten calls transferred to me that weren't even customer service related - just a rude customer that the order processing rep didn't want to talk to. Now, I can't say I blame this person for not wanting to talk to someone who is being rude, but to send them to me when I already have enough rude customers of my own, just isn't professional. Learning how to deal with difficult people is part of the job description of the order processor, and it makes them a better order processor once they learn how to do their jobs proficiently, so don't pass them to me. I've been in the position of order processor before and I proved myself, so that's why I was hired to be a customer service rep. I know how to listen to the needs of and then help the customers to the best of my ability. Yes, they can be quite rude sometimes, but I think in order for one to be a good customer service rep (or any type of retail representative) is to be able to put yourself in the customer's shoes. How would you feel if you ordered something that you really needed, and it's been on backorder for several months? And there's even a term for a backored item that may never come in - a backorder beyond hope. You just have to let the know that the company is doing their best to get the order fulfilled. You have to be able to handle these people with finesse, because you just don't know how they may react. They might have had a really bad day and their order not coming in that they've been waiting weeks for may be the thing that makes them have a meltdown. So always treat them the way you would want to be treated if you were in their place. You know what it's like to be a customer, you know what it's like to be put on hold, so put yourself in these peoples' place. Otherwise, don't be a customer service rep. I take pride in the fact that I can handle this type of customer. I try to make the customers feel that are in good hands with me and I will help them resolve their issues. I want them to feel better after talking with me, having put their minds at ease.
Overall_Why: I find my work to be meaningful when it has to be, which is not all the time. For example, if I'm talking on the phone for a half hour so someone who doesn't know the first thing about placing an order and expects me to walk them through the whole process, then that is not meaningful. That thirty minutes could have been spend doing something else. I see no meaning in wasting my time. But on the other hand, if I am truly using the time I was given to help someone, then I can see a greater purpose to my work. If someone is really struggling to find just the right item for a special person, and I can help them choose something and they are truly appreciative to me, then I feel at that given moment, that I helped make a difference in someone else's life. When I do that, I feel that my job has meaning. When I help someone with difficult order and their feeling quite frustrated and I can give them some peace of mind, that gives my job meaning as well. But I've really never had what one might consider to be an extremely meaningful work experience. Not in retail. If it was a life or death situation, and the product I'm selling them is going to save their life, then that would be extremely meaningful. I've yet to have that happen.

Work_Story: I am the clinic assistant in a middle school with approximately 1700 students. When people outside of a school think of the school nurse, what generally comes to mind is actually my job. The school nurse is busy meeting with parents and teachers, dealing with larger issues, and coordinating care. Meanwhile, for the day to day needs of students, there's me. I'm the person who is there in the school clinic every day. I'm the person who does first aid when a student falls, who takes their temperature, gives them a bit of health counseling, and maybe calls their parents if they're not feeling well enough to call themselves. A lot of the time, my job is evaluating students. I'm the person who decides if they're sick enough that they should go home, and while I'm not allowed to diagnose (thankfully) or really direct parents I can tell them what I see. One of the main parts of my job is administering medication, whether it be ADHD medicine a child needs to take before lunch, or an epi pen for someone having a life threatening reaction, I'm the person who has the medicine. I have to be on call and get anywhere on campus if there's a crisis. The last part of my job, and probably the trickiest, is health counseling for students. The nurse does the big deal things. She talks to students who are pregnant, or have cancer, or other really serious things to deal with. I'm more the person students come to if they get headaches a lot, if they're hungry, or if they're getting their first period and the whole thing is freaking them out. I spend a lot of time on what I call ??classes in common sense living?? talking to students about how getting up 5 minutes earlier or throwing a cereal bar in their backpack can mean the difference between a terrible day and ok one, or how it's ok if the period blood isn't always the same color. I teach them how to use non-cell phones, how to leave voice mails, and how to tell the difference between a crisis and an annoyance. ??No Billy, your leg is not about to fall off. You have a bruise though. Those happen when you decide to jump into a wall.?? The school relies on me to be something of a go-between, with parents, students, teachers, administrators, and the school nurse. I'm often the first person to see a problem or hear about it. Because of my relationship with many of the students they're used to coming to me with personal things, so I hear about issues at home, bullying problems, mental health worries and the like. Much of my job is listening to them, understanding them, and giving them some compassion mixed with tough love. Middle school seems to be where a lot of them first feel stress, first really jam a finger, and first really remember being sick, so to them it's a much bigger deal than it is to an adult. It's a complicated job with a lot of parts and a lot of responsibility, but it boils down to this. Care for the kids' health. Listen to them, and work with them to make sure they're healthy, safe, and able to learn. I'm there before they first arrive (usually doing paperwork) so that there's always a health person on campus when they are, and I leave when the last bus is rolling away.
Overall_Why: I think when you look at in the surface it looks like my work should be very meaningful. After all, I'm on the front line of taking care of kids, and often have to deal with some intense and hairy situations. I'm there to ease, improve, and sometimes save lives. The thing is though, I have a hard time with meaning. There are a lot of reasons. The first is simple. There are so many of them! Our school has 1700+ students. I see between 80 and 100 students a day. Often there are 4 or 5 in the clinic waiting for something from me at any given time. It's hard to connect when the volume of work is so high. It's hard to really be there for someone when at any moment I may have to stop listening because a new person came in and they're bleeding. I long for connection, and once in a while I still make it and find bits of meaning there, but it's hard at a school my size. Secondly, our rules are there for a reason I know, but I find myself tied down to them a lot. There's so much paperwork to do for every little thing, and without the proper form signed within the proper date range my hands are often tied as far as helping a student with an issue goes. That makes it very hard to feel meaningful things are happening when most of what I see is tied up with red tape. Finally, I have to be very careful about stepping over other people's toes and boundaries. These students have parents. They have families. There are teachers and principals and guidance counselors. All of us have our own area, but sometimes the kids don't feel comfortable talking to the guidance counselor but come to me instead. It's a balancing act between helping the student and doing it in the ??right way?? which also pulls me away from meaning. I still find some, but not as much as I think people expect.

Work_Story:
Currently, I am employed full time with an independent and assisted living community. It is a forty hour a week job. Overtime is highly discouraged and frowned upon. You must finish all your assigned days work with in the given eight hours of the day. Any rare overtime must get explicit permission from the supervisor for that day exclusively. 
  The department I work in is the house keeping / maintenance department. There are four full time house keepers, I am one of them. The community has one hundred twenty one or two bedroom apartments which we are responsible to clean and do the laundry for weekly. As well as maintaining all the common areas of the ten story building. The job is very labor intensive and is nasty dirty work. Since we are in central Fl it is simply very hot all the time as well. Sometimes stepping out on the residents private balconies to sweep and clean the windows in the upper ninety degree weather is a relief from the heat they have on in their apartment. Fortunately, some actually have on the a/c. The pay is very low. Slightly above Fl minimum wage even after working there for four years.
  My supervisor has stepped in and done the job from time to time and understands all the in's and out's of the job. He has a good balance in what he expects and knows how hard and nasty the job is. He does take up for us and defend us as well. 
  My day starts out with a scheduled list of 8 or 9 apartments to clean for the day as well as the common areas of the floor or floors I am working on. One of the house keepers does only the laundry. The other three of us do the apartment cleaning. There fore we start our day stripping beds and gathering laundry for her. Then we go back and make the beds. This will take an hour or more of our day. Then we move on to cleaning. It is a light house keeping. But, you tend to really care about these people as you get to know them and usually end up doing things for them that are not part of your job just to help them out. Most of them are wonderful people with great stories from their lives. They are full of knowledge sometimes. There is a lot to be learned just from observing and listening. After making the beds when we go back and begin to clean the apartments. Starting by taking out the many trash cans and replacing the liners. Old people tend to have trash cans everywhere. Even in the living room. The I move to the small kitchen where I wipe down the outside of the cabinets. Then clean the kitchen sink and the open counter space. I am not allowed to move any of their stuff. So, no straightening. Check the frig for spills to be cleaned and wipe down the out side of it. Then comes the bathroom where I clean the mirror, sink, counter, tub, shower walls, and inside and outside of the toilet. Which is often extremely gross requiring scrubbing with a pumice stone toilet stick. Do not understand why so many of them do not flush the toilet. It makes for difficult cleaning and I can not imagine living in an apartment with the toilet not flushed. The smells are awful to say the least. Then I move to the dusting. Since I can not move anything I must basically dust around their stuff. Since they have a ton of stuff sitting around absolutely every where this is a good rule for me. I have actually been called out by residents for throwing away used tissues before. Yep, I said "used" tissues. And used napkins as well. So many of them begin to hoard odd things like that. I try to keep them as free from these germs as possible with out loosing my job for throwing away their stuff. If they do not trust you they begin to imagine that you have taken or thrown away items of value to them. After dusting I move to the balcony to clean it. There I sweep and clean the inside and outside of the sliding glass doors as well as the rails the doors slide in. Then it is on to sweeping the entry way, kitchen floor and bathroom floor. then mopping all those areas. Next comes vacuuming the living room, hall and bedroom or bedrooms. Keeping in mind that I have a maximum of 45 minutes to clean each apartment and move on to the next one. this includes the extras I do because I care. Maybe they need to open water bottle lids for the week because they are to weak or to find lost things under their chairs. Move things they ca not because they are to heavy. It is really a judge for myself what I should and should not do. What I have the time to do. What will not take too much of my time. No one oversees my work on a daily basis. The residents, if they are home must sign my form stating what I did in there for them. the task are listed and I must check them off and get the resident to sign. I never ever list the extras I do. That would make them think I do not not have enough work to do and they would definitely give me more work to do every day. After cleaning the apartments I must clean the common areas for the floor or floors of apartments I cleaned that day. That envovles cleaning the pictures are wall decor, vacuuming all of the corridors, and cleaning the seating areas of each floor as well. Then the doors to the stair wells. There is far more work to do than can be properly done in the amount of time given. 
  Dealing with the emotions of this job is as tiring as doing the work. Watching the heart breaks and struggles of some people is very difficult. A lot are just very healthy normal people that want to live from from some of the living chores and house and yard upkeep. They travel and tell me their stories. It is hard when they die. Which they will because of the natural order of things.
  There are times I have been met at the door with a fist in my face from veterans with PTSD. Never hit or hurt though. Yet, it is still frightening. You just never know when they may slip and hit you. I try to clean for these people when they are not home for my own safety. They always recognize me and relax so far. Thank God for that. There are times that I have been aggressively hit on by perverted old men. They have waited for me in their beds beckoning me to join them throwing back the covers to reveal themselves. Thank God they were wearing an adult diaper. lol, yep, that saved me from the nakedness that time. I have been ask out. I have been touch and pressed against improperly. I have been ogled and had money stuffed down my bra. Sigh, it is like real life magnified. You learn who they are and how to handle them. And very importantly, how to avoid them. Telling the head of the community does no good. It is passed off because she does not want to loose any residents. My supervisors tells us to clean when they are not there and to get someone to go with us to those apartments. At least he cares. Taking someone else from their own job to go with you is hard because you know what it does to their day. And since you have to hunt someone down it changes the dynamics of your job as well. So, we share our experiences and work together to find solutions in every way possible. This really helps. It also builds a nice team work and bonding with each other. 
  When one of us has the day off or has a vacation, the other two of us must pick up their work along with our own work. And it must be done in the same time frame of an eight hour day. No overtime. However, our supervisor has worked out for us a way to make this work. He has instructed us to tell each resident what is happening. That so n' so has the day off or is sick and the other house keeper and I mus do their work as well as our own work. There fore we will be doing an extra light cleaning for them. They are always understanding of this. And generally very kind about it. Sometimes they will even tell us to skip them that day. Which is wonderful making the day a lot easier for us. Stripping the beds and making them can take two to two and half hours of the day. then you have five and a half to six hours to clean twelve to fourteen apartments. Our supervision does not require us to clean the common areas on those days. This is an incredible help. On these days we do not clean balconies. We do not dust. We do not wipe down cabinets and clean frig spills. this makes the day much much lighter and doable. Although, at the end of the day it makes you just exhausted. The two mile walk home for me on those days is much much longer. 
  On some days there are mandatory meeting for all employees or functions that we are required to attend. And yes, you guessed it, all of the work still must be done with no overtime. At least we are allowed to use the same guidelines as listed above for when some one is absent. It still makes for a very hard day. and the meeting are usually useless and extremely boring. The only good thing about them is that I get to sit down for a while most of the time.The other women in my department feel the same way that I do about the meeting and the job as a whole.
  If the pay were a lot better it would not be so bad. You get used to it and can work through things mindlessly as a routine if you do them the same way each and every time. It also helps you to remember if you have done something in a particular apartment since you do the same things over and over ever day many times a day it is easy to loose track of whether or not you have done something. Therefore the routine is crucial to getting everything done in and efficient and effective manner. If the residents were not so great as a general rule it would be a much worse job. But, it is very low pay for the amount of hard labor and nasty work that is required. If you feel bad or something hurts, it is too bad. suck it up buttercup and do your job or have no job. And the owner does not believe we should be paid more. He would rather just churn through new house keeping staff over and over than pay people any where near properly for the job done.I will not be staying in this job. He owns several communities and is far removed from what real life is like. His income provides a shied from life's normalalities. He actually believes his one housekeeper deep cleans his large home in thirty minutes each week. Guess he has never cleaned anything. It makes him very hard to work for. You must bite your tongue often and learn to laugh it off. 
  I have cleaned up after flu ran it's course in the building for a week. Those days are truly disgusting. You find yourself cleaning poop out of sinks, off of the walls, the floors, the shower walls, the tub and oh so many places on the toilet. And there is no short cut to cleaning that. On those days I just feel like crying. Very seldom does any extra help get sent. You must wear a mask which makes it harder to breathe and much hotter to work. You get called to clean things that are not on your list that is the nursing departments job to do. They do not want to do it. Therefore you must doe it on top of your normal and extra load. This can truly be and awful job. And I cleaned blood multiple times as well with no extra protection than my regular cleaning gloves. You learn quickly how to double them up and keep mask on your cart even if you have to buy it yourself since they mask are seldom provided by the company. Our well being is not really watched over very well at all. Some of the cleaners are very harsh and take you breath away. There is not a good air flow in the apartments to protect us from the cleaning fumes either. 
  When someone moves out that requires extra deep cleaning before they can move in. When possible we get together on these apartments and clean them as a team. It is much better work then. We work well together as a team with this group of women. That really helps.
  At the end of the day I must allot for enough time to get my cart and equipment ready for the next day as much as possible. The vacuum must be empties and the filters cleaned or brushed out every day or the vacuum will not work properly. Which just makes your job that much harder. You must throw out all of the trash you have collect during the day. This usually must be done several time during to day to keep up with it. All of my bottles of cleaners must be refilled and set up for the next day. I need to refill my paper towels, gloves and all sizes of trash bags. Put the dirty cleaning rags in the laundry to be washed. These will be folded and distributed the next morning for that days use. There is generally barely enough of these to go around so we are always asking for more of them. The mop heads are washed with the cleaning rags and retrieved the next day as well. The mop water is made fresh each day as well. This end of the day routine is a time we are all together and we use that time to share tips, info about the difficult people and just about our lives in general. It is a nice bonding time for us a group. The final prep before the day is also a good bonding time. And we use it to organize our days if things have changed for the day as well. Sometimes our supervisor brings us in donuts in the morning. That is always a nice surprising treat. 
  It is not uncommon to not have time to take an morning and / or afternoon break. The workload is heavy. We always take a lunch break. Of course, we tend to eat light because of the heat and hard work. Otherwise you get sick or really tired from eating to heavy. There is no place for us to take and break and be away from the residents. So, we usually eat outside on the covered patio no matter how hot it is. At least there is a slight be of privacy there because some of the residents are eating or napping then. 
  The residents come to love me and look forward to me coming as well. This is nice to see smiling faces along with the grumpy faces. Oddly, they are afraid of the PCA's. Though after seeing some of them in action, I would be too. It is not uncommon for them to tell me stories of miss treatment and ask me to help them with something the nurses aid should be doing for them. Something I do. Sadly a lot I can not do. They make me promise not to tell because the PCA's will retaliate against them. I do not tell because I seen it happen to them. I have tried getting them help and been given light flippant responses. So, I help where I can. The administrator has been fired. And a new will be coming. So, I hope the issues at my job will get better in many ways for me and for the residents. However, since the owner remains the same for now, I hold a wait and see attitude. 
  One thing that I love about my job is that I do not have some one constantly watching over my should. I do a good job and they leave me alone. I get to make a fair amount of decisions about how I want to accomplish the given task and freedom to do so as long as I get it done properly and in the time frame given. It is very nice to not have some one double checking me and watching everything I do all the time. It helps to make up for the poor pay and hard work a little bit. 
  This is not a job I would recommend to anyone unless they just did not have a job. It is by far better than not working. I started at the community as a part time receptionist. But, sixteen hours a week was just not enough working hours.
Overall_Why: No. overall I do not find my work to be extremely meaningful. It is mostly meaningless. Yes, people need they living areas to be clean and well cared for. they need to know that some one give a flying flip about them. they need a place to vent and share. a place to tell their stories and make new friends. Quite often this is me. But, quite often it is not. I see people abandoned by their families. It is heat breaking. But, I do not replace a family. I do not replace a good friend. They may actually do more and feel better if they cleaned their own place. It would make them move more and keep them healthier. So, while my job may add a bit of joy, ease and comfort to some people's lives. It is by far fulfilling or meaningful. I do not feel it has much to do with my life purpose. Except to provide me with income and some meaningful life lessons. I help some people here here and there. But, it is underpaid, and under appreciated. It is hard back breaking work with little to no reward. and little to no value to mankind in general. I do not feel I am giving very much back to society as a whole. This leave a very empty place in side me as far as meaningfulness to my job. It is labor intensive and not really very helpful in the long run when looking at the grand scheme of life.

Work_Story:
I currently work daily as an auto shop technician. Being an auto technician has 
  its ups and it has its downs and it also has its benefits. Let's start with the bad. 
  When it rains at the shop I work at, water tends to drip on you or drain into the shop onto the floors forcing us to work In it. We also like to help others out who wouldn't necessarily gotten help from other shop locations. When you do 
  a thing like that people sometimes think they can take advantage of you or maybe 
  even "strong arm" you, meaning try and 
  scare you into giving them their car without full payment. There are always costs to trying to be the nice guy all the 
  time. The shop I work at also isn't in the nicest part of town so we deal with a lot of drug users and drunks loitering and/ or theiving. Enough with the bad and let's talk about the good. The best thing about the shop I work at is that we are very well known for providing very high quality and effective motor rebuilds that will last you a lifetime. We have yet to have a negative complaint on a motor rebuild and we have been in business for 10 
  years. Also one of the greater things with 
  working at the shop I work at is that we 
  can kind of do as we please as long as our job is completed at the end of the 
  day. That means leaving for food or even leisurely time it is all great. There are so many ups and downs to being a technician and working at the shop I work at but overall I enjoy cars so the good outweighs the bad. Let me tell you how our shop looks and try to give you a 
  visual of our location. We are location on 
  the outskirts of downtown in a very

Although that can affect our business sales we have a very effective staff including myself working on advertising and selling old or new parts we have laying unused around our shop. We like to stay very organized and have a nice grass landscape with a gravel pathway for the cars to travel into an bay. Our garage has four bays meaning four cars can be inside at once. I have spent countless nights at my shop as a technician. There

You have to have room for certain things you didn't once before. There have been days I've wanted to quit or just not call at all and start over. Overall I enjoy my shop and being an employee there and although it can be overwhelming and frustrating I 
  couldn't see myself at any other place as an employee. Automotive Technicians are the people that repair your cars and keep them running properly for you with no problem. When working in this job you need proper schooling as well you cannot just jump in and work on a car you need proper training. Mechanics have lots of duties on the job in which involves a lot with computers and repairing parts for the client??s car. Mechanics earn a very good salary that continues to grow with the more experience you get in the job. 
  Automotive Technicians is a great career to have because you work with many tools to figure out the problem, you get a good paying job, and you will never have to worry about getting a job because 
  mechanics are needed everywhere. 
 Automotive technicians have to go 
  through schooling before becoming a professional mechanic. To start working you would need your high school diploma or something similar to that to be able to work as an automotive technician. Having 
  your high school diploma would be better to have than anything. else .because. it .could show that you are a hard worker. If you would want to get a higher pay thenn you would need to continue schooling and go to a training program that usually takes 6 months to a year to complete and in these schools there is mainly hands on learning there is not a lot of in class learning is all outside in the shop. Auto body repairers Are highly skilled craftspeople who repair damaged
 automobile and truck bodies, restores body metal to the original contours, and replaces
 non-repairable body parts.
 I chose automotive body repairer for my report because I like to deal with cars and
 I enjoy performing hands-on activities. I like the hands-on and mechanical operation of
 this career. Automotive body repairer is in the Industrial and Engineering Iowa pathway. 
 The National Employment and Outlook of this career is currently stable. However
 the employment rate is expected to rise with the amount of automobiles that are lighter
 weight and that are more easily damaged when in a collision. Also, other classes that pertain to the electronics of automobiles, like basic
 automotive technology and basic electronics. The changes in the industry have made it
 necessary for a person in this occupation to become skilled in the use of fiberglass plastic,
 aluminum welding, and other water-based materials.
 In this career, a body worker can do a wide range of things to the body of an
 automobile. Automobile body repairers are skilled craft workers who repair vehicle parts
 damaged by collision, rust, or other causes. They pull dents and make them smooth again,
 they replace parts and panels that cannot be restored back to its original look, and also
 smooth out creases and other various rough areas. Also, they do a lot of custom work on
 the vehicles. They have the ability to do custom paint jobs and install custom parts. After
 they have fixed an area, they will sand that area and paint it the color of the rest of the
 body. This career requires climbing, balancing, lifting, walking, and stooping. 
 They often work with tools such as drills, riveters, hammers, welders, files,
 screwdrivers, and sanders. Automotive body repairers must buy their own tools, but their
 employers will usually furnish power tools. Many workers have thousands of dollars invested into tools.
Overall_Why:
Auto Mechanics are Always in Demand
 Entering a career as a mechanic means guaranteeing a future of steady work. Slumps in the economy can affect consumers buying new cars, but vehicles will always require maintenance and repair. Changes that may disrupt other businesses have very little effect on the automotive service industry, so there??s really no such thing as a slow season for auto mechanics. The growth in the industry has also been steadily rising in Canada, which means there??s a need for auto mechanics now and in the future. Auto Mechanics Have an Amazing Collection of Tools.In most garages, mechanics are required to use their own set of tools. In fact, many even start building their collection of tools while attending mechanic college. If you??re a hands-on person, then imagine what you can accomplish with dozens of tools at your fingertips! Along with your knowledge and skills, your tool collection will be your most valuable asset and investment as an auto mechanic. Auto Mechanics are Self-Reliant. Once you become an auto mechanic, you??ll be entering a career where the nature of the work is independent. Even though garages employ several mechanics, you??ll be trusted to perform diagnostics and repairs on your own. Many mechanics love having this level of self-reliance. After an auto mechanic course or two, you??ll also be able to work on your own car. Detecting problems and doing repairs on your own car gives you the self-reliance of not having to pay anyone else to get the job done! Auto Mechanics are Knowledgeable
 When you start gaining experience as an auto mechanic or automotive service advisor, you become an expert on the subject of cars. After working on similar issues with a particular model of car several times, you will be able to diagnose problems much quicker. Many people rely on their cars, and with their knowledge, auto mechanics provide a service that is invaluable! Auto mechanics get asked for car advice a lot. Your knowledge can save friends and family a lot of time and money, whether or not you fix their cars for them. Your knowledge will expand over time, and putting your expertise to use is one of the many things that make this career rewarding

Work_Story:
I work in book publishing. I started in {state} about 24 years ago as a production assistant at {company} and stayed there for about a year before being laid off. I soon got another job, again as a production assistant, at a small unique publisher on the {city}. It was a family run business and in total there were 6 people working there when I began including the wife, daughter and son of the owner. I was a little wary of that set-up and there was a fair share of family drama from time to time but I was willing to live with that. The wife and daughter only worked part-time and the son was the Editorial Director. Then there was an all purpose assistant and my direct boss- {name}- the Director of Production. He had been the Director of Production at {company} for many years (he had left by the time I arrived at {company}) and was well respected in the industry. He was very knowledgeable and more importantly we got along very well. He taught me pretty much everything I know about the industry. 
 I did a variety of things when I first started and got to learn a lot about publishing in an intimate way. We had a relationship with {company} that was and remains the main part of our business. Over time the company grew and I grew along with it. Eventually we outgrew our location and moved downtown which allowed me to walk to work, which was very nice Subways are a nightmare- convenient but nightmarish. I was eventually promoted to Production Manager and had an assistant of my own, which at the time I thought was just great. As Production Manager I dealt with the day to day production of our books. Creating and maintaining schedules, placing purchase orders, maintaining our databases and the like. We worked with the MS Office suite and I became quite good at writing excel formulas to streamline our work as much as possible. I also spent a fair amount of time meeting people in the industry, primarily vendors, who were and would continue to be vitally important to our work. We were primarily focused on low costs, which actually is less of a priority for bigger houses than it was for us. The basic nature of our business at the time was to sell what are called bargain books. Basically, we would work with other publishers to acquire books that they had already produced and sold in what are known as the trade channels (hardcover and paperback books sold in the usual way). These books had sales histories at particular price points and we would take that information, combined with our preferences and whatnot and present titles to Barnes & Noble for sale specifically in their bargain departments. In every B&N store there is a section that sells these bargain books. They are usually hardcovers and they are sold for about one third of the original list price (somewhere in the $5.98-$8.98 area). If B&N liked a book they would tell us and indicate a quantity based on previous sales history and such. Then it would be up to us to go back to the original publisher and negotiate a price to buy rights to produce the book ourselves. The other publishers were generally amenable because they had pretty much already exhausted their trade sales and this was just another channel in which to sell the book, reaching a different set of customers. So they made more money (as did the authors) by selling the rights to us. We bought hundreds (by now thousands) of titles and it has been the lifeblood of our business. Sometimes the books dont do very well and they just have that first printing. But other books have done fantastically well and have reprinted dozens of times. Among the advantages of this business model were two main things- 1) We would always have an order from B&N before we printed anything and 2) Every book we produced was already sold to B&N who sent their trucks to our printers to pick them up. This meant we didnt have to deal with book inventories, remainders and other such cost drains that plague other publishers. I learned the intricacies of this business model as I was a Production Manager. 
 As we continued to grow as a company I was eventually promoted to Managing Editor, where I became more acquainted with that side of the business. This coincided with the beginning of our next big project with B&N- The B&N Classics. As you can imagine when you are just buying titles that have already been produced by other publishers there isnt a lot of editorial work to do. The B&N Classics changed that. Although we were just going to pick up public domain classic titles (Moby Dick, Pride and Prejudice, etc.) our main goal was to refresh the classics by adding a range of ancillary materials like timelines, new introductions, author biographies, discussions and the like. 
  This necessitated finding writers and editing the new material. As Managing Editor I spent a lot of time working with our authors (largely professors from prestigious institutions) and streamlining schedules with our various departments (Production, Art/Design, Production Editorial, Editorial). I enjoyed this experience and got to spend a lot of time immersing myself in classic literature. I remained the Managing Editor for several years as we got the B&N Classics up and running. 
 
 About 10 years ago I was promoted from Managing Editor to the Director of Production which is the position I currently hold. {name} wanted to retire and I was ready to run that department. I was a little anxious mostly because I didnt want to lose touch with the editorial side. But by this time most of the classic titles we were going to produce were already done and it had become mostly a reprint business without much for editorial to do. In all we had produced, I think, 185 titles. About a quarter of those reprinted regularly. So I returned to my origins in production and took up the mantle of Director of Production. My responsibilities are pretty much to oversee every book we produce, both from the classics line and the bargain line. We print about 80-120 titles a year amounting to roughly a million to a million and a half books every year. I'm responsible for all of it. I work closely with the Art, Production Editorial and Editorial departments to bring everything together. Most of the day to day stuff is handled by my Production Managers. I spend time working on special projects, negotiating contracts, establishing and maintaining relationships with vendors, interacting with B&N, overseeing our inventories (paper, books, jackets/covers, etc), overseeing our budget, approving invoices and other similar stuff. Things almost run themselves at this point. I'm not sure what else to say. At this point I'm only 1400ish characters in and I've been writing for almost an hour. I'm going to try to write more but I dont know if I'm going to make it to 3000 characters, which in fairness is much longer than just a page and a half. A 'page' generally contains 500-600 characters. I know this because at one point in my job I had to analyze character counts to estimate titles. So you have greatly misrepresented the amount of writing required. I thought you wanted 500 words, which is more like what 1.5 pages would require. But I tried to move on at that point only to be stopped by a message up top demanding 3000 characters. Its just not going to be possible to write tht much about my job. I'm going to write a little more now and hope I'll be able to move through this. I feel like I've done more than enough already.
 
  I do get bored sometimes but I've been given opportunities to do more interesting, less monotonous things as well. Helping to digitize all our books, writing jacket copy, participating in design discussions, proofreading and so forth. But I still have downtime and that's when I do some turking or other things. Our office is very comfortable and I am on good terms with pretty much everyone. We are now have about 45 employees and it's a fairly diverse bunch. The owner of the company treats everyone well and we sometimes have outings and what not- going to a movie, going to plays, dinners, etc. It builds cohesion and I've become very friendly with several co-workers outside the office. 
 Overall I enjoy working in publishing. It's fairly laid back, it's intellectual, it's got perks. I particularly like working for a smaller company and wouldnt really want to move to a big house or a place like B&N. But as I mentioned sometimes I get a little bored; it's just a quibble though. I consider myself lucky to have found such a nice place so early in my career and to have watched it grow and thrive has been a pleasure and something I'm proud to be a part of.
Overall_Why:
I cant believe you want more writing. I think I misunderstood the counter on the last page. I think I wrote a lot more than necessary. Anyway, I find my work meaningful because it serves the world. I think reading and literature are among the most profound things people can engage with. Being able to work on classic literature is a pleasure. I get a little less joy working on some of the idiotic self help books we do but we've also produced titles that have had a profound affect on me. I really dont have much more to say on the topic. I'm getting fatigued and I'm unhappy I spent so much time on the previous page. When I couldnt advance after writing 500 words and seeing you wanted 3000 characters I thought the counter was counting characters. I got to over 1500 words before I realized that the button was letting me advance and the counter was actually words and not characters. Grrrr. 
 Books ar life. Its amazing to me that people elected a president that doesnt read and probably never has outside of business nonsense and the like. His profound ignorance should wake people up to the fact that reading actually is fundamental (as they used to say when I was a lad).

Work_Story:
I started out working at a call center for a utility company in {state}. I worked there for about a year just as a call center rep doing customer service work. What was required of us was to answer the telephones and help customers with any questions that they had about their electric and gas bills. One day they had a position open to be a supervisor of the call center floor. I was one of the best workers there and I decided to apply. I got the job and that is what I do today. 
 
 It is a lot of work being a supervisor. I am currently responsible for over 200 call center representatives. I am required to make sure that everyone does what they are suppose to do on a daily basis. Throughout the entire day these employees have several questions about their current calls and I am responsible to help assist them in the best way possible. I also have a team of 25 members that I am personally responsible for. We have goals that we have to hit each month and I am required to train them or help them in anyway possible to help us reach our goals. 
 
 My daily routine consists of myself clocking in around 8am, monday through friday. I occassionally have to work Saturdays but that all depends on the goals we have set and how close we are. I post my teams daily stats to the team portal where everyone can see them. The stats are individual as well as team based and they show things such as average call time and how many calls taken for the day before. After everyone gets clocked in then I review my teams stats to see who is struggling and what areas need improvement. Then throughout the day I have each person that is struggling come to my desk for a brief coaching session. We try to come up with a plan that will help them succeed in the company. If they keep failing consistantly and show no signs of improvement then I have to write them up and eventually terminate them. After the coaching sessions are over with, I then walk the floor and help out people when they have questions about the calls they are on. Sometimes I have to take over calls when the customer asks to speak to a supervisor. I have had some interesting supervisor calls. The last thing I normally do before my day ends is listen in on a few calls to make sure people are doing what they are suppose to do. 
 
 Every once in awhile when it gets really busy the supervisors have to jump on the phone to help reduce the call volume. When this happens I normally get some interesting calls. The most memorable call that I ever had was when I first worked at the call center. Infact, it was the very first call that I took. The guy was clearly mental especially by the tone of his voice, and how upset he was about his address being mispelled on his bill. He kept saying that the word "street" should be abrevaited to "STR". He would yell at me saying "it is S-T- with an RRRRRR". Our system works directly with the post office so their was nothing that we could do. At the time I ended up transfering him to a supervisor after hearing 30 minutes of him cussing me out. Well that is the life of a call center supervisor, hope you enjoyed it.
Overall_Why: I find my work very meaningful because I help customers on a day-to-day basis. It makes me feel good to provide good customer service to people. Also it makes me feel good to help my employees succeed at their goals. I feel really accomplished when I get a nasty supervisor call and I am able to resolve the issue within ten minutes. I have been told that I have a calming nature and a nice sweet voice that tends to calm even the most angry of person. Sometimes though if they have mental issues even a nice calming voice won't help them. Majority of the calls that I take are from elderly people in Michigan. Many of them do not understand how the billing cycle works or why they received an estimated bill. I find it very meaningful when I explain to them how these things work and they finally understand how it all works. It makes me feel good to receive good customer feedback from my clients at the end of each call. I absolutely love when they compliment me and tell me that I am doing an amazing job and they appreciate people like myself. It makes me feel good when my team hits their goals and my boss comes up to me and tells me that I am doing a great job. It makes my line of work so much better and so much more meaningful then any other job that I have ever worked at in my entire life.

Work_Story: I am a nuclear medicine technologist. I work in a hospital and administer radioisotopes intravenously in order to diagnose disease processes. Nuclear Medicine is a part of our radiology department here. We work in tandem with other modalities as well as our radiologists in radiology to provide the best care possible for inpatients as well as outpatients daily. Long term as well as short term care patients are cared for in our department and we provide a wide range of nuclear medicine procedures that aren't necessarily provided at smaller hospitals. Many hospitals in the area don't offer nuclear medicine services due to licensing issues. We are a level one trauma center and as a result we accept many cases that are traumatic and require advanced care. These patients are typically admitted to an intensive care unit and are monitored very closely. These patients must travel with a nurse in order to have their exams done because of their unstable status. Since nuclear medicine exams sometimes take 2-3 hours, this can be quite tedious at times. Working closely with our nursing staff is paramount to proper care. Nuclear Medicine shows more physiologic change than physical change--we show metabolic activity in the body as opposed to just how we're built. This allows a doctor to provide more in depth care. For example: someone could come in with abdomen pain and their CT/Ultrasound/Xray would all be okay...but their nuclear medicine scan could show that their gallbladder isn't functioning correctly whereas their CT/Ultrasound/Xray would only show how they look physically. (This happened to me personally!) Our ability to show functionality changes people's lives regularly. We are also able to treat certain types of cancer, locate abscesses/infection, identify brain death, diagnose pulmonary embolism and locate areas of the heart muscle that aren't receiving adequate blood flow and need intervention. These are only a few of the things we look for on a regular basis. While we balance inpatient care quite regularly, we also have routine outpatient exams that we perform. Typically these require a certain prep in order for us to perform the exam. Usually that means the patient is unable to eat or drink anything after midnight. Sometimes our patients are required to stay off certain medications (thyroid medications, pain medications, iodine supplements) or it can interfere with the test's ability to show their functionality. It can get pretty tedious and frustrating for the patient at times, especially if they didn't follow the prep. Some of our tests require the patient to even eat a low carbohydrate diet the day before--and if it's not followed, they have to be rescheduled. Same goes for caffeine for certain exams. As a whole, my job is rewarding. I spend an inordinate amount of time with my patients and have the privilege of getting to know them a little better in my time with them. Typically this allows me to give better care and lessens the amount of anxiety the patient feels during their exam, especially if they were already nervous to begin with.
Overall_Why: Yes, as a whole, I really do. So many of my patients come in and are lost and unsure of what to do or where to go from their initial symptoms. some of them don't even realize that nuclear medicine is basically a harmless diagnostic tool, and when they hear our name it makes them panic even more (which is regrettable). I spend some of my time looking at obituaries and wondering of some of my sicker patients will be there soon, but I find reassurance in the fact that I know they're fighting and living their best lives possible. There are other situations where we are able to give people the pretty amazing gift of peace of mind--especially when they're familiar with the process. We do follow up exams on cancer patients to ensure that their cancer hasn't spread to any other portion of their bodies--PET/CT scans, Bone scans, MIBG scans...all these can be employed to manage cancer treatment and staging. There are also patients who battle recurrent infections and we are the only ones able to isolate and identify where they are "hiding" in their bodies. Sometimes people just present with fever and we are tasked with finding where and how this fever originated. It can be tedious but very, very rewarding when we are able to give someone better quality of life. So many times we aren't able to...but those moments where we can and we get a hug are just the best. So yes, it's meaningful to me.

Work_Story:
I work for a small, locally owned business in my city by the name of {company}'s. It's a business dedicated to the sales of computer components, such as memory (RAM), graphics cards, motherboards, power supplies and smaller accessories like fans, RGB strips, USB controllers, cables and so on. Another feature of the business is repairing and building computers, which is - in particular - my area of expertise. I'd say this service is pretty well-used by our customers, as we offer it for a relatively modest price that allows them them not to worry about the hassle of putting a system together themselves or replace a troublesome component in their tower. 
 
 The business is pretty small, really having only six employees, though that number has actually grown a bit from last year when we only had three employees.
 
 The owner there is heavily involved in the day-to-day workflow, as he can't afford not to be. This leads to a lot of cooperation and accessibility, making him easy to approach if there's an issue in the workplace. Though it's not a particularly conflict-heavy business among the employees, in my experience. Because the number of employees is small, it leads to a more tight-knit community among us. The owner is picky when it comes to who he hires. This isn't so much about skill level as much as it is personality for him. He believes most people can be taught what we do - and I agree - but 'you can't teach someone not to be an a-hole' is a line we've passed around a few times. And in this environment, the last thing you want is someone who's going to make our jobs more difficult.
 
 I'm thankful for this picky hiring, because so far the people I work with have turned out to be generally easy going sorts. It's definitely a more laid back environment, as our customer base is that way, and it allows for just a little less formality and stiffness in the workplace. Getting along with the other employees is a huge boon to our work flow. 
 
 I've worked here for just about three years now. It was only a few months after I started up on Mturk that I managed to land this job. I didn't start out as slow as some other employees, because I do have a fair amount of PC building experience under my belt, so there wasn't any need for a lot of training in my case. The only thing I needed to learn was working the inventory system and dealing with the checkout process. 
 
 About eight months ago, I was promoted to a managerial position. Thankfully it's not a huge addition of work given our size, but it has given me new responsibilities, namely in helping to train newer employees and generally being the sort of go-to person when the owner is busy. I make sure everyone is on task and delegated to a job within their skill set, and make note when someone is struggling to perform, to make sure they're appropriately taught all they need to know. So all in all, it's not too difficult of a job from what I was already doing. I'm thankful I have this position. Even if the pay isn't the greatest, the environment isn't too tense and the work itself is something I strangely enjoy doing.
Overall_Why:
For me, something is most meaningful when it provides a sense of 'completeness' with me. Like there's nothing, or very little else, in my life that would give me the level of satisfaction, of feeling like a contribution, of feeling like I've reached my goals. I enjoy my work and I do find it meaningful in that sense. I could certainly be doing some more mundane, in a more restricting environment that left me feeling trapped by the work. I'm very thankful this job doesn't make me feel that way. BUT, I can't say I find it extremely meaningful. 
 
 Extremely meaningful would be doing something I truly love, that makes me feel like I'm fully satisfied with the work, could do it continuously and never have any regrets about my choices in life. This job is still a job at the end of the day. One that fits well with me and gives me a sense of contentment for the moment. In the long run, however, I don't think it's workable as something I can rely on for my future. I don't make a great deal of money here. The business is small, and still trying to get off its feet in many ways. I'm a shy person, going out into the world and getting a job was a difficult task for me, and I'm eternally thankful to this place for giving me much needed experience in the work force, from which I can build upon my future, hopefully. This and my current living conditions with family allow me a stable living for the moment, but not an extremely meaningful one. That's something I've yet to discover. I don't know what exactly I need to find that would be extremely meaningful for me, but I'm not there yet.
