Hopefully I'll be able to find it, but until then I have my current place of work, and that's alright by me.

Work_Story:
At my work, we are primarily in the business of auto financing. I work in the back end of auto finance. This is where vehicles that have been repossessed or have been returned at the end of a lease are received, moved and eventually sold at auction or sold to a customer and/or dealer. These vehicles must be picked up from the repossession agent or from the dealership where they were returned. In the event that either a customer or dealership wishes to purchase the vehicle, that is where my work begins. We will receive money from one of these two sources. Once that occurs, all the sales paperwork is created and is sent by overnight delivery to the purchaser. When the purchaser receives the paperwork, I then contact them to ensure that they received the paperwork and that they understand how to fill it out correctly so that the DMV will accept it. The customer fills out the sales documents and then returns the documents. Once the documents are received back in the office, the sales funds are manually applied to the customer's account and the lease is fully terminated and completed. 
 This begins the second phase of the sale. Once these documents are received, I then am required to sign the documents on behalf of the company. These documents are then scanned, signed and sent back to the customer. In addition, the title to the vehicle must be requested from our title vendor to be sent to the customer or to their financing institution. This is a very basic overview as there are many different requirements depending on the state in which the customer resides. Illinois requires a special form to be signed and sent to their taxing agency. {state} requires 3 special forms plus requires that title be signed and delivered to the customer directly. The same person who signs the forms must be the same person that signs the title.
 In instances where the documents are sent but not returned, we also are required to call the customer on a regular basis. This is to ensure that the customer did, in fact, receive the documentation. Many times, we are required to contact the customer many times before they respond. In addition, we receive paperwork that is filled out incorrectly and must be resent to the customer to fill out a new set that is then returned. Customers also contact the department by phone, email and click to chat many times a day to find out how much it will cost to purchase their vehicles. We take those calls and explain the prices, sales tax, fees and anything else that they must pay in order to purchase the vehicle at the end of the lease. 
 My job is very manual and all documentation must be signed by hand and notarized as well. Customers must also have some of the documents notarized and this becomes a sticking point as many people do not know how to have something notarized nor do they know where to go to get someone to notarize their documents. Another sticking point is that no one other than a dealership or lessee/colessee may purchase a vehicle that is coming off of a lease. This also means that all funds must come from one of those sources. Many times, a third party such as the lessee's son or daughter will attempt to purchase one of these vehicles and they are very upset when they realize that this is not acceptable. Their money is returned to them but this rarely makes up for their perceived loss.
Overall_Why: Many of the customers who call or send in money to purchase a vehicle are very wealthy. They are accustomed to having the best of everything and they are not concerned with the vehicle. The purchase is almost an afterthought to them. While they are very appreciative when the process is smooth, they are also very irritated by the thought that they need to be bothered to sign and return documents. When these documents require notarization, that entitlement attitude really shows and they are very willing to insult customer service agents. There are other customers who are not wealthy and they are simply attempting to purchase their vehicle because they like it or a way over the mileage allowance and it would cost them more to return it than to purchase. These customers are typically very pleasant and are willing to sign what we need and return it. Unlike their rich counterparts, they are typically very nice to the agents partly because they are also agents at their jobs and understand more of the job. These people make the job feel meaningful as they are happy to do what they can but those who view agents as inferior make the work feel meaningless. The management tries their best to make sure all the agents feel as those their work is appreciated. This, too, makes to the work feel more meaningful but there is also a lot of favoritism showed that really lowers morale on the team and detracts from how we feel about our work,

Work_Story: I currently started a new job a couple months ago with a company that deals with the produce in Grocery stores. I work in the IT department there and support all the employees that use technology as part of their work. I have to say it is the best job that I've had so far in my life. First off, it's the closest job to my house that I've ever had. Its a 10 minute commute each day. Secondly, it pays the most money I've ever made at a job. Thirdly, it is the least stressful job that I have had as well. I work with a very small team of people. Our whole IT team is only 4 people including myself. They are some of the nicest people I have met since I've started working over 10 years ago. They are very helpful to me with any questions I ask, and I ask a lot of them. I work at the Help Desk and am always encountering new situations each day. It makes the job fun because I never know what to expect on a daily basis. They are also good to me due to the fact that they don't expect me to do too much right away. They start me off doing a lot of the easier tasks such as password resets. This way I can learn how to talk on the phone and get a lot of the basics down before moving on to more complex issues. I respond to emails and phone calls each day that range from a variety of issues. I also install software and set up new laptops and tablets for employees. This way when they get the equipment it's already configured and they don't have to do anything. One of my favorite things about working here is how laid back the work environment is. I am treated like an adult and am given a lot of respect and freedom when it comes to my job. As long as I am doing my job then there really aren't any issues. My manager is very understanding with taking time off and willing to work with my schedule in any way. I also like the fact that their is upward movement at this place as long as you keep learning each day. A couple of the people I work with started out doing Help Desk but now our in Admin and management positions. This is what I would like to do one day as well. It's nice to not dread coming into work each day and not feel super stressed out when you get home. Today I was the only one in the office for a good portion of the day and I managed to deal with it. I am proud of myself and feel a sense of accomplishment. It feels good to help people with their issues even if it's something small. I get all sorts of compliments when I help people with their computer problems daily. Just the other day I was told "you are my hero." Did I deserve that? Probably not, but it sure did feel good. I try not take things personal on the job and just be willing to listen to people and guide them to where they need to be. A lot of it comes down to how you say it then what you actually said. I've learned how to be very patient with people because thats one of the biggest keys in this type of job. Sure you need some technical ability too but if you have the right attitude and are good with listening to people and not being reactionary, you will go a long way in this line of work.
Overall_Why: I find my work meaningful because I help people on a daily basis. Sure it isn't the most important thing as far as saving someones life or anything like that, but it still is helping people. It really does make people happy when you can help them in small ways. It might make their whole day in fact or save them a lot of time and frustration. It can be overwhelming dealing with computer issues when you don't know a lot about them. I understand because the older generation just ins't as good with them as this generation. That's okay though because I find a lot of enjoyment in helping them in anyway I can. The reason it's more meaningful as my job I think is because I work for a smaller company. So there is more of that personal relationship with the customers. I talk to a lot of the same people in the short time that I've been here. We support a base of around 1000 people so that isn't very many when you think about it. I think it is more meaningful than other jobs I've have that were a similar role. In those jobs there was no relationship at all and it just felt like they needed bodies answering a phone. Here, I can take my time and really develop a conversation and check in with them about how things are going.

Work_Story: Wow, where do I start? Every morning I'm up at 5 am in the morning. My routine is usually slow but direct and to the point to establish my objective of actually leaving the house and going to work. However I have a wife and a daughter, and 2 dogs. These objectives can be tricky at times and require some love and care without losing my mind because I can't get out of the house. The love and care obviously and making sure I take my 2 dogs out, making coffee for my wife, and breakfast for my daughter. Dad of the year award maybe? After those objectives are established, I kiss my wife goodbye and pour some more coffee in my thermos before taking the dreadful journey to work. I can see my neighbors also frantically pushing there kids out the door into the minivans, to take them to school. My wife takes my daughter to school, which saves a ton of headaches of finding a parking spot and being late to work. Once I get to work, all the franticness goes away, because I usually if my objectives at home were met, make it work with 15mins to spare. This is what I call me time. Navigating new money making ideas on my phone, and sipping my coffee sitting in my car. Once the clock reaches 7 am, Its time to work. We recently had new ownership at our very small company which really changed, some for good and worse. So worse the money making ideas I navigate on my phone every morning, are becoming more in more in the central part of my thinking. So I and the colleagues all sit in our chairs waiting for our assignment. The owner usually sits in his office not saying much, as the ex-owner, now manager tells us about the day. Most of our work that I do is at the {company}. My job is a fiber optics specialist. It sounds fancy but really, I physically install fiber optics, install the connectors, plug them into our Liu fiber modules, and test them to certify or rectify any problems we may have. The IT department is one of the worst in {city}, which very outdated equipment. Our job is to rip out the old and install new wire for whichever facility needs it. I am sure you have seen us standing on ladders throughout the day opening ceilings? Today our manager got the word, that 3 fiber lines were cut by an electrician by accident. What made the problem worse, is it was a fiber line that we recently installed, in the underground tunnel. Those tunnels suck. They are hot full of spiders and other nasty things. So of course since I'm the only qualified fiber optics tech, I was assigned. I was allowed to take our laborer to bring in more cable from our van to the actual tunnel. Which is nice because my back is shot from heavy lifting. In summary, the install was completed in the timely fashion that is required by us and tested. Sometimes it's a regular 8hr day and other days its 10-15. The pay isn't the best, which is why I do mturk on the side, and it's always exciting to see bonuses from my requestors, because of the lack of care, my boss has for me and the other employees. I didn't even get a Christmas bonus this year. Not saying you have to give me a bonus, just showing an example of how people should treat you for your hard work. After work "typical day at CU", is what my colleagues would describe that, and I guess I'll use it to end this essay.
Overall_Why: At times work can be very interesting if I'm actually doing my job as a fiber tech. Most of the time I have to do other tasks that are not even close to what I signed up for getting the job. A great example of that is when we have to install tv cable lines at the resident housing which are basically apartments. Its very labor intensive, and dirty work. Then you have to deal with the residents themselves, who are upset because we enter their place to install better cable for there internet and cable. Some are so rude, they will not open the door. We have to get one of CU's facility workers to schedule a time we are allowed to come back and work. Actually, when that happens, I'm happy because I'm not crawling on the dirt, running a cable to the location. Lots of bugs and mice, very nasty work. When I actually do my job, it's great and rewarding. The cable pull itself Isn't fun, but the actual termination part is. I just put my earbuds in, sit at a table and start terminating the fiber. Sometime's I'll watch a show to speed up the day. My boss, of course, frowns down on me using my phone, but I'm so fast at installing he cannot come with an argument to make me stop. Obviously, I would if he threatened my job, but he won't because fiber specialists are rare, which makes it good for job security.

Work_Story: I've been a Mail Carrier in {state} state for 20 years, unfortunately as a "Star Route Carrier" (Government Contractor), which means NO BENEFITS. My last vacation was May, 1988. I MUST work six days a week or be in breech of contract. I sort, bundle, and deliver the mail and parcels to about 250 houses (only ten of them remembered me at Christmas). I was the ONLY Mail Carrier in {county name} to deliver the mail to every box on my route during the six weeks of snow emergency we had in 2017. I have a total of 28 years experience. I'm a "Government Contractor" now (in Wisconsin I was a Civil Service Mail Carrier, which has benefits and higher pay). My reward? $10.50 an hour, and I must pay all expenses for my vehicle upkeep (gas, expensive commercial auto insurance, repairs, and so on.) The IRS considers me to be "self-employed", and if I have any profits (I do not), then I would have to pay that all back to them! And if I don't have $400 profit in a year, I have NO Social Security, which I do not. I used to work in a food store on the night shift, but they went out of business in 1999. That was the last time I got Social Security. I have no sick leave, no substitute driver (I can't even afford to pay myself ... how can I hire a sub?). If I am sick or in a car accident, too bad. I must report for work, or hire a substitute driver, or authorize the USPS to hire one on my behalf (and pay them about 5 times what I make in a day!) There is a ski resort at the end of my route, and each day in winter is a hell, filled with hundreds and hundreds of skiing idiots, too stupid to stay in a warm bed, in their Audi's and BMW's, running me off the road as I'm trying to deliver the mail. You're supposed to be 155 feet behind the car in front of you when you are driving 55 mph, but these idiots drive about 20 feet apart from each other, like it is a freeway. And that 155 feet is for GOOD weather driving, NOT on ice and snow! Apparently these people don't have to work for a living. The minimum wage in {state} is now $11 an hour. I make $10.50, with 28 years experience, while the USPS (US Postal Service) hires new drivers from the unemployment office, and pays them $20 an hour to do a route half my size. Do YOU think this is fair. I should also mention that I carry all the mail up the Mount Baker Highway to the Glacier Post Office each day, which is at the end of my route. In other words, I carry about a ton of mail up the road while I am doing my own route, then pick up the {city}, {state} outgoing mail and try to make it back to Deming in time to catch the outgoing semi truck! If I am NOT back in time, I would be in breech of contract. How many flat tires have I had, or vehicles break down? It's happened dozens of times. Thenm I mkust try and get back home to switch to another vehicle, usually the tow truck driver giving me a lift. Amazingly, in 20 years I've only been late catching the outgoing truck ONCE (when I had a flat tire near Christmas AND deep snow)! And of course our "Postmaster" is a 31 year old punk with one year of delivery experience (and then he delivered pizzas for three years). He sits at his desk all day with his feet up on the taxpayer's property, playing with his magic telephone and looking at a computer screen. His wages? $68000 with full benefits. I make $28500. Again, I ask you, do you think that this is fair?
Overall_Why: I try and do my best, and of course I DO appreciate having a job. (In northern Wisconsin I threw 100 lb bags of rock salt all night for $4 an hour, sometimes we did 13 -14 hours of it). "Right to work state". I appreciate having employment, and I like to think that I am improving my social ability and meaning by interacting with people all day long. What would I do all day otherwise? I AM trying to be more positive in these last few years, not to lose my temper about idiot drivers and pencil-pushing bureaucrats. Of course, if each of the 250 homes on my route would just give me a lousy $5 at Christmas, then I could live like a human being. Instead, my home is at this moment 48 degrees. I turn off the heaters when I am at work to save electricity. I got rid of the cable tv years ago, and eat only once a day to save money for gas! But I have met some wonderful people, and worked with a few good ones. I'm learning to say "THANK YOU" each day, and to remain positive. No matter what job I have, I always try to work as hard and as fast as possible, and to do the best job that I can. I am presently the lowest paid Mail Carrier with the most experience in my county (and perhaps my state), but I still get up each morning and drive home each day to an empty, cold house (I can't even afford a dog and cat). But I am still off on Sundays, and can listen to music, paint pictures, and even make some "music" of my own on Soundcloud and Reverbnation, and so my job does allow me somke inspiration to be a better, more disciplined person.

Work_Story:
I work as a Patient Services Administrator for a small ENT specialty clinic in southeastern {state}. ENT stands for ear, nose, and throat. All of the providers in my clinic work strictly on problems with the ears, noses, and throats of human beings from all ages and all walks of life. 
 
 I have been working in this position at this clinic for the past 2 years. I had never worked in healthcare before, so there was a learning curve for me to get used to the medical terminology. My main duties are to answer phones and check our patients in and out. 
 
 Because the clinic is so small--there are only about 20 employees total--I (and most of my coworkers) end up "wearing many hats" throughout the day. I not only do the above mentioned duties, but I also assist our clinic's Patient Administrator/HR with any tasks she needs help with. That includes training fellow employees, creating/updating databases, helping sort her voicemails, fetching and sorting the mail, and even brainstorming ideas to help with the business. The Patient Administrator at my job is the main boss right under the doctor who owns the clinic. She really likes me and sees my potential, so she likes to give me responsibilities not normally given to people in my position.
 
 Back to my everyday duties, one of them includes answering the phones. This sounds simple, but it definitely is not. You never know what you are going to get when you pick up that phone. It could be something as simple as someone checking their appointment time or it could be someone who just had surgery and has a major infection and needs urgent care. My job is to sort through the calls and get the urgent ones routed to where they need to go, and also to take care of the more simple ones. 
 
 That includes registering new patients into our system. I get the patient's information (demographics, insurance, reason for visit) and enter it all in to our system. It is imperative that data be collected and entered carefully and correctly. While answering phones, I also have to deal with anyone who may come into the clinic. Thus, there is a lot of multitasking required. 
 
 Everyday I make sure I have all of the paperwork for all of the patients coming in that day. I have to keep up to date with any last minute schedule changes and I communicate with the doctor and her medical assistant often. We have several locations, and the one I work at is the smallest and in the best part of town. There is only one doctor at our location, so that simplifies things, but I am also the only office person there, so it can get hectic at times. 
 
 The doctor at my clinic location is a woman, and she is super nice. I really enjoy working for her and also for her medical assistant. There is no drama at work, which is amazing. Everyone just gets along and does what's best for the company. We all help each other out when we need it.
 
 I greatly enjoy my work. Not just because I like helping the patients, or because I like the detailed-oriented aspects of office work, but because I am appreciated by my employer. My bosses make it known that I am a valuable asset to the company, and for that I am grateful.
Overall_Why:
I would not say I find it "extremely" meaningful, only because it is not something I will do for the rest of my life. I am only an office worker. However, I do contribute to all of our patient's health, and that is very meaningful. I help patients and continually interact with them throughout my entire day. I am especially en-heartened by our elderly population. I have a lot of patience and I think that really comes through in my communication with them, and it is much appreciated. 
 
 Really, a lot of what I do is provide customer service. Many people in customer service these days are not very good at it, so I like to do the unexpected and actually help our patients out when they need it. It feels good to brighten someone's day, even if it's just a little bit. The patients that come into our office enjoy the honest smiles I give them and the usually give one right back. I am very genuine and honest in my interactions with people, and our patients can sense that. 
 
 Although I am usually very introverted, at work I "put on my customer service hat" and become more friendly and talkative. I enjoy that on most days and that helps to make my work more meaningful, as well. It is a way for me to get some social connection and to remind myself I am not alone in this world. We are all suffering at some point. It makes me feel good when I can make a suffering patient's day just a little bit better.

Work_Story: I currently work at an animal shelter. Not as a volunteer. I do paperwork relating to the adoption of cats and dogs. We get a lot of applications and have to vet them before we can allow an animal to be adopted out. Beyond paperwork, I also show the animals to prospective adopters. I also help to bring in New animals and I train volunteers to walk, groom and otherwise take care of the animals we have in our care. I don't have a lot of coworkers, but there are lots of animals. I get there at five am and some days I'm there until eight or nine pm and I often have to do a lot of work from home. Today was a busy day because we had an adoption event and we had a lot of people coming through. There were about fifty applications put through which is a lot more than we get on a regular day. Unfortunately someone on our blacklist showed up. A known trouble maker who has proven he is not fit to own animals of any sort. His house is dangerous and he's often beligerent. He tries to adopt multiple animals at once. I had to tell him he was not allowed on our premises and that we would not be adopting to him. He got angry and began shouting and the police had to be called. Thats not usual for a regular work day either. Usually it's pretty quiet, and filled with a lot of paperwork, writing, and not much time to think. My office is located at the back of the main office. It's pretty small and only has a desk, the chair, a window, filing cabinet and the computer. I've been working here for three years, and normally I find it pretty enjoyable. I get a lot of vacation days which is appreciated as well. My boss is an older man named Sam. He's worked at the shelter for 20 years. Most of the employees have only been there a few years like me. My boss is pretty great. He listens to his employees and takes into consideration our thoughts and ideas. My coworkers are all great. There is only one I don't get along with, and luckily for me I don't have to see them that often. It's definitely not a bad job, and I'm glad that I applied to it. There are a lot more benefits at this job than any other job ihave held, and I can see myself ending up staying with it for many years. Good prople, good location, something that I believe in and good pay as well. When I say something that I believe in I mean that I believe that rehoming abandoned animals is a very worthwhile cause. I truly feel that I'm doing good for the animals and for my community. I feel proud to be in the position that I am, and working at the shelter that I work at. I honestly can't complain. I hope my coworkers enjoy theirs jobs as much as I enjoy mine. I try to make working there as fun and laid back as possible. I'd never want it to be a stuffy or unhappy work place. Anyway thats all sbout my job. As i said it is a great place to work. I'm not sure there is anything else I can say about it. I'm pretty pleased to work there. I'm not sure I can reach 3000 characters, but I did hit the 500 words mark so hopefully that's acceptable.
Overall_Why: As i said in t he last writing task, I love where I work. Rehoming animals is so important. Every animal deserves to have a loving forever home and I feel proud that I can help make that happen. We have so many different animals that come through our doors. From the smallest, like mice to extremely large dogs and cats I work to get them a good home. And it doesn't just help the animals. It helps the community as well. It keeps animals off the street and makes people happy when they get to take home a new friend. I truly feel fulfilled every day that I come into work. It's meaningful for me, meaningful for my boss and my coworkers and meaningful for the community. We also save animals lives. Animals that might have otherwise died on the street or ended up elsewhere get to find new homes. Before I worked this job I was very unhappy. My life lacked a sense of meaning and purpose. Once I was hired my job and my life started to feel truly meaningtil. That is why I find my work meaningful. And I guess to answer the question, yes I find my work to be extremely meani ngful. The res not much else I can say That I didn't already say in the last writing prompt or in this writing prompt. I hope that I have managed to give you enough about why I find my job to be meaningful.

Work_Story: I work at an engineering firm located in {state}. Most of the company consists of engineers. The way I describe it is a four tier company. You have management, engineers, administration, and production. Roughly ninety eight percent of the employees have furthered their education and have received a degree of some sort. The engineers design a different systems. Production builds the systems. Our company specializes in building laser measurement systems and have huge success in the tire manufacturing industry. Currently, we are hoping to gain business in fields such as pipe building. My specific job is administrative work. I have my hands in everything. Accounting, shipping, janitorial services, payroll, and answering a multi phone system. Our home office (parent company) is based in {city}, {state} and have little companies spaced out through out the world. A lot of our technicians travel around the world and do so often. As much as I don??t like to admit it, the company is extremely biased. It seems like they could care less about your experience and expertise in a certain field. They really rely on the fact that people have a degree. Example: we have hired on several engineers who have had engineering degrees in mechanics. That is not the field that we specialize in, but they are hired anyway. We have also had a few hired on who have aerospace engineering degrees. Again, not the field that we specialize in, but they seem to take the job anyway. Our company also reels in young professionals. They tend to go to many college job fairs and do a lot of the recruiting there. I think it??s wonderful for students looking for jobs in the engineering field specializing in our area. A downside in that is they offer a sign on bonus. While the sign on bonus appears to be beneficial to a young college student at the time, they come to realize a year later, that this isn??t the area they would like to be in. My work houses roughly thirty people. It has a small company feel with corporate rules. Not very strict when it comes to time off, whether it be hour lunches, or two hour lunches. There are 5 people in total who are hourly employees. The remainder are salary employees. Another thing I must admit is that favoritism is a huge issue as well. If you are an engineer, then you get treated a lot differently then someone in accounting, and production. We are housed in a building that was built in 1901 which is pretty neat! They completely renovated the inside and placed engineering in the third floor, accounting/ administration on the second and production on the first. It seems as if production gets the crap end of the deal every time. Whether it be selection of shirts, or meetings. They seem to never be included. Which doesn??t necessarily seem to help the moral. We used to do meetings monthly to help keep everyone on track with shipments going out monthly, and what Parts should be ordered, etc.. Now that new management has stepped in, we don??t have meetings anymore, and no one is as friendly as it once was.
Overall_Why: In my opinion my work is extremely unmeaningful. There are some days when I have absolutely nothing to do besides answer a telephone (some days it barely rings) or clean up after our precious engineers. Example: one of the engineers cooked himself breakfast at work. No big deal right? They left the bowl full of scrambled eggs in the sink full of water. Well, as I previously mentioned, my role consists of a variety of titles. One of those being a janitor. It is expected of me to keep the Office space clean and tidy. They are never expected to clean because then that takes away from their role as an engineer. Prior to this job, I was a Lease Administrator at a movie theatre chain. To me, that job was meaningful. I was a property manager for over one hundred seventy five locations. I meant something to quite a few people. Here, where I work now, I??m licky if I get a ??thank you?? for pulling off the executive board meeting that takes place annually. Keep in mind, that board meeting takes place all over the world. We hosted it last year, and because I treated them with wonderful hospitality, they wanted to return. I would never get a good job, or a thank you, no matter how hard I work. I think that may be a huge factor as to why I feel like the work I do is meaningless. I??m in a position that they could replace at any given time.

Work_Story: I work for a company that's on a military base. The company usually get contract works from the military and thus we build engines or sometimes we take them off of planes. Most days are usually hectic because we're on a contract, and we don't have time to mess around. Also we're made to put our signature on every single think we replace, fix, or reassemble. That's only if you have a certification in Airframe and Powerplant. By putting our name and signature and various FAA documentation, they can track it back to whoever did the job just incase something happens. So if a lot of people pass away due to malfunctions on an aircraft and they find that it was your job that cause the problem and you didn't follow procedure. Then your certification is revoked and they give you the penalty of xxx amount manslaughter. So it's a scary job but someone has to do it for people enjoyment or security of our country. Anyways, on various day we usually do an overhauling of the engine. That sometimes involves in removing the engines and disassemble them. Once we finish disassembling, we often have to do service bulletin checks, as known as SB's and airworthiness directives, usually known as AD's. We go and make sure that most of the parts are complied with with SB's and AD's due to FAA regulations and the safety of society. Other days we usually have to do hot section inspection in which we take a bore scope and enter the fuel manifold area and check the compressor for damages. If damage is notice then we have to do a repair on those damages if it's within the limits. However, if it's not within the limits, we usually have to replace it. There are some days that we work usually three to four hours but then we don't do anything because of part replacement. We have to wait for orders to ship into the facility and that usually take weeks. Then there are days where we have to do Non Destructive Testing, aka NDI's, this is where we take the components that have failures in them and we do various testing such as ultrasonic, liquid penetration, magnetic particle inspection, and eddy-current. If there are damages during one of these inspection we have to replace them but there are times when we have to ship the parts and let other facilities test them for us. Then there's the fun part where we have to reassemble each peace and put it on the military aircraft, but before that we take the engine and put it on a stand. Once the engine is mounted on the stand, we connect every single hose that goes to it like fuel, oil, and any other electrical wires. Then we start that baby up and try to reach the minimum rpm for the engine and make sure there aren't any leaks or smoke. If everything works out perfectly then we take the engine and mount it on the aircraft. We also make sure everything is within it's correct torque when mounting bolts and screws. After that, we make sure there are no tools around left near the engine because most people forget things and leave their tools around thus causing damages to the engine or the possibility of killing their teammates. Once that is check, we do a testing of the engine on the aircraft to check for leaking and within limits also. After that is done then we ship it off. That's what I usually do for my work.
Overall_Why: I believe my work is moderately meaningful. With out country men or women doing these work. How would society benefit from traveling. These work also influence military which they use to protect our country if there is a war going on and etc. It might not be saving lives but it does bring many cultures together. Don't we all travel to other countries to experience the different culture and people. The different taste of food that we miss so much that we can't find anywhere else. I believe my work is meaningful because it brings people closer. Families that haven't seen each other for century because of an ocean between. However, there are times when I think my work isn't meaningful because I guess the hard labor that comes with it and the lack of trust of other fellow coworkers that might stab you in the back plus the worriness that I hope everything is alright with the aircraft for whoever use it. Other then that, I really love my job. It meaningful to me because I get to have money that pays off my student loan, buy a house, and a car. I find it very worthwhile that I get to enjoy a field that I find interesting or exciting. I learn new things every day. For everyday is a new learning day and an adventure. It helps me test my limits and knowledge of what I know. Thus I believe that yes this job is quite meaningful to me.

Work_Story:
I work as an attorney in a small firm that I began as a solo practice. I've been doing it full-time for two years now. 
 
 I decided to open up the firm before I had even finished law school. I put up my sign before taking the bar exam. I know this was what I wanted to do. At first, I didn't really know what to expect. It was like walking into the unknown, without any reference points. I had never had my own business, and I had never practiced law on my own. I based my decision on a feeling. Namely, I knew if I didn't open it, I'd regret it for the rest of my life. But, I did open it - and I'm happy I did. 
 
 Initially, I took every case that I could. I took evictions, auto-accident claims, contract drafting, immigration matters, and everything in between. I had no way to gauge the amount of work I had to do, and so I ended up working from 10 am, until 11 pm or later almost every day. To make things worse, I did not know how to filter cases, and ended up taking several horribly difficult cases for little or no pay. Eventually, I became better and better at picking cases, and learned a tremendous amount in a relatively short time. At the end of my second year, I began working with other attorneys and assistants, and was able to specialize on the area of law that I most enjoy - criminal defense. 
 
 On a typical day, I work on two or three client' matters. Usually, there's a court hearing in the morning, which usually takes about two hours. Then it's responding to emails and voice mails for another two hours. And finally, I have a few hours of drafting legal documents or interviewing clients. Around 7:00, I either head home, or spend a few hours catching up with some friends before having to wind down to do it again the next day. Compared to the first year, work in the second year is a lot less stressful, and is overall much more enjoyable.
 
 Like any work, the practice of law had both good and bad aspects. Personally, I cannot think of a better feeling than being in a courtroom and representing a client in a criminal proceeding. When you're standing at the podium in front of the Judge, with your client - who is counting on you to speak for and protect them, you have to shut off your mental chatter and be completely in the moment. You have to sense the room, and feel what everyone around you is feeling, and react to every change and unexpected thing thrown at you. If you try to memorize a script of what you're supposed to say, you are no longer in the moment. You become dry and insensitive to the feeling and requirements of each moment. It's a beautiful thing to experience, and I get to do it almost every day.
 
 As for the aspects that are not so fun, the practice of law has plenty. I know very few people who find joy in writing 40 page briefs, being under constant time constraints, and dealing with clients who always seem to avoid you when the bill comes their way . But, I believe that to have the good, you also need the bad. And overall, I'm happy with my work. I'm glad to have had the opportunity to share some of it with you.
Overall_Why:
Overall, I do not find my work to be extremely meaningful. Particularly, I've lost some faith in the structure of the legal system. I went into the practice of law to help people. With criminal defense, I felt that people who commit crimes are usually just people acting any of us would if we lived the same lives that they did. So I spent a lot of time and energy on each individual person, and tried to provide them with guidance or support so that they did not re-offend. I"m still close with many of them. Work was very meaningful at that time.
 
 However, I soon realized that you can't prioritize helping people to making a certain amount of money. To keep a practice afloat, you need to crank out cases at a certain rate. The more you help people, the less money. The more money, the less you help people. Eventually, I reached a balancing point. But now, I'm a ways away from the idealistic kid that first opened his office.
 
 Additionally, I don't find the work meaningful because it often times punishes clients who do not make enough money. About 8 months ago, I helped a kid who had a marijuana possession charge, but had a valid medical marijuana card. I spent a lot of time on the case, and he had faith in my work. When I went in front of the judge, he had already decided the matter with the wrong law. I tried to explain the issue to him, but he had already made his mind. 
 
 Over the next week, I spent dozens and dozens of hours in the next week writing a motion to reconsider, he was applying the wrong law. There was 30 pages of evidence, and every court who ever decided the issue being in agreement, that he applied the wrong law. I went to court two weeks later because the clerk could not find the motion. I looked at the file, and he had stamped "denied" and not ever read it. The client could not afford to pay for an appeal, and because the judge and staff didn't notify us that he just denied the motion, the 14 day appeals period had passed. I don't find meaning in the practice anymore, because it doesn't work the way that it should. And now, a kid that wanted to be a doctor, is a drug convict because I failed him.

Work_Story: I work for a company called {company}. The project i am on right now is doing sales support for {company}. I sit at my desk all day long and take a wide variety of calls. I track orders and provide shipping updates, I help process returns, i send out catalogs, and most importantly I place orders. {company} sells outdoor gear, clothing and equipment. I answer lots of questions about the various items I sell. I do comparisons and give suggestions. I talk to people all over the united states and Canada (although those are mostly call transfers) I talk to angry customers and happy customers. I try my best to make each person I talk to happy. I am required to do some soft up-selling of items that normally go along with what the customer has purchased. For example, If they purchase boots, I will try to up-sell socks. I fill out the orders and submit them for processing. Sometimes this can take awhile if the customer has a long list of items or they are unsure of what they want. I always tell my customers to take as much time as they need. Sometimes I am unable to assist a customer either because their issue is beyond my scope of support or because they will sometimes ask for a supervisor. I have learned not to take those calls too seriously. Often people forget that the person on the other end of the line is human as well. Some of the things beyond my scope of support would be orders that were delivered to the wrong address, orders not delivered by the delivery date, and sometimes it is just a random issue that i am not familiar with. One of the best parts of my job is that I work from home. I set up a very nice office and i put on my headset and off to work I go! I have a very quiet household of just my husband and I so working from home is very easy for me. I really love the fact that I can work in my pajamas and nobody is the wiser. I think that my favorite part of my job is getting to talk to such nice people and such very different people. My least favorite part of the job is probably the hours and shifts. I work late into the night and often on the weekends. We get especially busy around the holidays as many people call us to order Christmas presents. I enjoy helping people decide on what gifts to get for their family and friends. I like the calls where I help people track their packages, especially since often they arrive sooner than the person was expecting. Unfortunately sometimes it is just the opposite and the package is arriving late or has been discontinued. At this point I have to use my very best soft skills to calm and angry or upset customer. Sometimes a simple apology can really go a long way. Often in this case I have to do a transfer to a supervisor to help find a package, or if the item has been discontinued or back ordered I try to find an item as close to the original as I can. Sometimes that works in helping an upset customer and sometimes there is just nothing I can do. I think that I have a great job and i Love the variety that comes with it. When my line rings I never know who or what is awaiting me on the other end. It is a challenging job, but a great one!
Overall_Why: I feel like my work is meaningful to those who I talk to. Sometimes I feel like I am in a rut doing the same thing over and over just with different customers. But I try to remember that even if it is not necessarily meaningful to me, it is very meaningful to the person I am trying to help. I might be the person that has the biggest impact on them that particular day. I do solve peoples issues and problems on a daily basis. Fulfilling a need is the reason for my job. and if I am thinking like that then yes I feel like my job is very meaningful. I often wish that I could do a job that made more impact or had bigger influence on people. I know in my head that each persons job has meaning to someone and we are all part of the big picture, but even though I know it in my head doesn't mean I feel it in my heart. I do think that my job is meaningful but not nearly as meaningful as I would like. When it seems like you do the exact same thing day to day i think it is very easy to belittle yourself or the job you do. I feel some days like just another worker ant in a busy hive, but without all those worker ants the hive would fall apart, Mainly I feel like my level of meaningfulness changes from day to day

Work_Story:
My full time job relates to conducting academic research in quantitative psychology. I am a graduate student in my 5th year (of 6). I am about to defend my qualifying exams on Monday. I have the curse and luxury of being bale to work from home because my work can be done from a laptop. I do not collect fresh data sets, but work with pre-existing datasets. Most of the time, I use genetically informed designs to conduct my work because experiments are not feasible. They are not feasible for numerous reasons, including my interest in long term outcomes, such as longevity or mortality, and the use of predictors that would be unethical to manipulate. I've grown very interested in explanations for health disparities. I have published on outcomes related to longevity, sexual intercourse, mental health, physical health. I typically use sibling data, although I have published using married couples or repeated measures (i.e. daily diary) designs. 
 
 Some of my other work is more statistical, such as running simulations. In those cases you could argue that I am collecting my own data, but I doubt that it really counts as original data given that it is generated from distributions and a random number generator. 
 
 I do do more than just research. I have taught courses to gifted middle and high schoolers on statistics. One of the classes was titled "lying with statistics" and the other was titled "introduction to applied statistics". Both classes used the classic statistics book How to lie with statistics from the mid 1950s by Huff. I think it is a creative way to teach statistics because it teaches students how to use these tools in meaningful ways as well as think critically about their application.
 
 In addition to teaching and researching, I am still taking classes. This semester I am taking two classes -- my last two classes: one is on survival analysis and the other is on advanced structural equation modeling -- or perhaps advanced factor analysis. I cannot seem to recall at the moment. The course work is not my favorite but it gives my day structure.
 
 My days lack structure which is slightly problematic, but it does give me great flexibility. I really like that aspect of my job. I do not think I could get the kind of flexibility I have in an industry job, like I think I will have in an academic job. I also really like that I get to pick my topics of research. If I were in an industry job, I would have to work on whatever my boss wanted. In my job, I can pick a topic that is interesting (i.e. health disparities and their relationship with SES/socioeconomic status) . I do have to work more hours as an academic, but I find it fulfilling -- more so than I would expect in an applied statistician position or as a data scientist. The pay is lower, which is unfortunate, but that's why I mturk on the side. It helps cover the cost of conferences and some luxuries like starbucks. I also like to use it to get ideas about how reasearchers are gathering data and what kind of research questions they have. So I view mturking as a complement to my current job.
Overall_Why: I find my work meaningful because I get to conduct work on topics I find important. I look at understanding why there are differences in health within and between groups. My work could be used to lessen that gap -- perhaps even eliminate it. I feel like my skills applied to industry would result in a less meaningful job. In addition, teaching adds meaning to my work because I am helping the next generation of students become useful members of society. I am teaching them to be data literate in the world of alternative facts. I teach them to be skeptical of the material they read, learn to spot fake news, and learn to craft arguments using evidence. I think that by teaching them to understand how data and science can be misused they will better understand it and they will be inoculated to much propaganda. I want to development this course on lying with statistics at the undergraduate level. I think that it could be a good course for those that are afraid of statistics or just wary of math. In my class, I make sure to talk about stereotype threat, growth mindset, and other low effort interventions that can help student ssucceed, even if they drop my class. I also make sure to induce them to a variety of topics very early so that they know how much variety is out there. I know that I can't teach my students everything, but I can give them the tools so that they can teach themselves

Work_Story: Currently I work as an office clerk for a medium sized company, I basically do the odds and ends around the office all day. When I first arrive I print off calenders for all the conference rooms and then I mail invoices out to our customers. The rest of my day is scheduling meetings and answering phones. We have locations across the United States and I handle most of the customer concerns, I am mostly alone all day as I sit in a seperate area than the rest of the office. Basically my only human contact is when the phone rings, which I don't mind because I kind of, well I am a huge introvert. It is really relaxing to have such a laid back job where the only contact I have with people are employees passing through and remotely on the phone with customers. When there is down time, some of my coworkers turn to me to help organize their files, I really like organizing and can usually fly through those tasks and finish them quickly. I just started this job about 6 months ago, so far I am really enjoying it but it does worry me about how slow paced it is, a lot of the computer systems are outdated and many of my duties at work can be easily done with a computer with the right software.Whenever I find a shortcut on one of the systems, people who have been there for years tell me they didn't even know that was an option, its like my coworkers are set in their ways and do not want to look for or accept any change when it comes to the day to day operations. Sometimes my days are very tedious, I get there 10 minutes early, reorganize my desk that the cleaning crew always moves around, do the invoices, and just wait for a call or someone to need help on one of their tasks. My day is kind of a waiting game, the worse days are when no one needs my assistance, I just sit up there alone staring out the window, the silence almost fills the room enough to consume me and suffocate me. But those days often vary and are few and far between, I have noticed the worst part of my job is when the silence does set in, and someone bursts through the door almost giving me a heart attack every time. Often times when there is down time, I look down at the pictures of my dogs on my desk and think of being home with them, I would love the chance to have an office job like the one I have but have the ability to bring my dog to work with me. I always feel guilty leaving him for 8+ hours to sit in the quiet for most of the day, I like the noise from my fur friend, I love the sound of him running for me. I don't know how I wrapped around to talking about my dog but I somehow always do, I do in real life too, no matter what the topic is, or who I am talking to, buckle up because I have a whole vault of stories about my best fur friend. I honestly love my position at my company, I love my companies values (except they don't allow dogs), and everything they stand for, I am really lucky to have found this position and I am hoping to stay with this business for a long time. Although I am only 22 so the thought of having the job that I will have for the rest of my days already kind of suffocates me in it's own way, but that's another problem to face another day, after all, your twenties are your time for self discvoery, and most importantly, play with your dogs.
Overall_Why: In a sense I do find my work meaningful, when someone needs my assistance and I can help, it is a great feeling knowing that I did a job well done and that I am needed. But on the other side of the coin, my work does not feel extremely meaningful in the long run, I am not curing cnacer, or helping sick dogs, I am answering phones and organizing documents. What I do everyday at work is not going to have an extreme impact on the world, what I do everyday is not even going to have an extreme impact on my company, if my duties do not get finished, it will just go to the next in line. I often times feel like I am too knowledgeable for this current role, maybe that is why I do not find as much meaning in it as I would if this was my first full time office job. I have been around the block already, and I know all the tricks, if I were going to work everyday and at least learning something new or participating in new tasks I feel this position woulf be more meaningful to me in the long run. It may be that I am so new as well, maybe as I advance and make a name for myself in the company I will be able to explore different opportunites, and different skill sets, and for that I am hopeful for the future and look forward to what meaning I can find within myself and my company.

Work_Story:
I work for a company called {company}, or {company} for short. It is a company who's mission is to sustain the well being of the clients, who are mentally disabled. There are different career opportunities throughout the company. I work in the residential department. Clients are placed in homes with other clients with disabilities similar to theirs. Coming into the job I was trained on medicine certification CPR and first aid. I also learned what to do in emergency situations. I was trained on each individual client, their disability and their specific needs. I help my clients with their daily care needs. In one home that I work in I administer their medications, cook and serve breakfast, lunch, and dinner (depending on what shift I work that day), I clean the house, do the laundry, safety security and assist them with anything that they need.Every Tuesday I take one of the clients bowling. The clients in this house are higher functioning so they can do a lot of other things on their own.
 At the other house I work in the clients, who are husband and wife, are lower functioning and also physically disabled. They are both wheelchair bound. I do everything that I do at the other house as well as get them in and out of bed, give them showers, and assist them with toileting, pick out their clothing and dress them. Although this job requires a lot of physical work, I think the most important part of the job is bonding. It is important to bond with the clients. It makes them and myself feel good. I have established a great relationship with each of the 5 clients that I have.
 The company strives to keep clients as independent as they can be so they can feel more in control of their life. They are able to live on their own with the assistance of staff and it helps them feel great about having their Independence and not having to rely on their family members for everything. This job is very rewarding to me. I always have a good time talking and laughing with my clients while i am at work. It doesn't even feel like work to me because after you create a bond with them, they become more like family. The company has many outings and events for the clients throughout the year in which I attend with my clients. We recently attended a Christmas party and had a great time. I love bonding and spending time with my clients. When it comes down to it, all they want is it be listened to, included, and loved. I work to teach and help them with life skills but they also teach me a lot on a daily basis. 
 This is the most amazing job that I have ever had. I have never even kept a job as long as i have been with this company. Sometimes I feel more at home while I am at work than I do being at my actual home. The love that I give to my clients is the same love that they give back to me. I have one client who is non-verbal and yet I can tell just by looking at her gestures or expressions exactly what she wants and how she is feeling. Working with these people, your emotions will get involved. Mine definitely has. A lot of the clients are older and many have lots of health issues so we deal with clients being sick and also passing away. It never gets easier to deal with. 
 This job requires a lot of patience. Things do get hard and there are rough days but overall it is a great job and I love doing what I do. It is a very great experience and very rewarding.
Overall_Why: Yes, my current work is extremely meaningful. I help to improve the lives of adults that have mental, and some physical, disabilities. The goal of the company that I work for is to have the clients be as independent as possible and to improve and maintain their well being. They are placed in a caring environment where they are able to do things on their own and have staff in their homes twenty four hours a day to assist them with things they can not do for themselves and anything that they would need help with. Working with these adults is a very rewarding experience. I know that I get to help improve the lives of people. I get to offer them comfort, support, and a caring relationship. In return, in a different way, I get the same from them. This line of work has lots of value for both the clients and the staff. When I was first hired I was told about the health benefits, 401k plan, the job description and programs, but I was not told how much this job would change my life for the better. I have learned so much from working this job and I hold new and better values now. I have become more of a positive person because of this job. This was my first time working with the mentally disabled and it has come to be a very great, positive, and life changing experience. My work is extremely meaningful to me.

Work_Story:
I am one of three people working for the company from home. We sell products on eBay using drop shipping services. 
 
 My day to day tasks are keeping up with returns, refunds, shipping, tracking, product questions, and most importantly, providing superb customer service.
 
 I love my job because it allows me to work when I want to as long as I don't get too far behind. I can do this from the comfort of my recliner watching TV.
 
 I also maintain various spreadsheets designed to track returns, refunds, order issues, etc.
 
 I have learned that some people will ask very tedious questions before purchasing items. I would never dream of asking some of the questions people ask. It has caused my attention to detail to become better than before I worked as an online representative. 
 
 I love that I get to talk to people all over the United States of America and sometimes am able to provide much-needed items for people with health problems who can not get out and go shopping.
 
 We utilize Skype for communication between myself and the other two people who work for the company. We also use remote desktops to perform all of our work so that our personal laptops do not get turned into workstations. 
 
 Not only does using a remote system leave my computer free of work related content, the internet speeds of our remote systems are super fast compared to the minimal speeds available in my rural area. This aids in being more productive.
 
 The aforementioned spreadsheets are through Google Docs. Google Docs mirror the Microsoft Word and Excel as far as how they are used. Some of the same formulas from Excel also work in Google Docs. The best thing is that Google Docs is one hundred percent free.
 
 We just got done with the holiday rush. For a few weeks, I worked nearly 60 hours. I needed the extra time. Not only for making sure that each customer's needs were met, but also providing extra money for gifts for my children.
 
 I found this job using an application called Upwork. It is a job hub and you pitch yourself to various openings. I have also written some product reviews and blog posts for other clients. These tasks are generally short and pay very good if you have any talent in writing. 
 
 I write for my own blog on Wordpress also. This blog hasn't provided any income at all, but it is an avenue to vent and I hope one day it grows in size enough that I can make a little extra cash for medical bills.
 
 Until then, I will be one of the most important parts of a company based in {state}. Todays technology really does have a lot to offer for those that put forth the effort to explore. Who would have ever thought that a girl from rural {state} would one day be running a company in {state}? 
 
 I am trying the MTurk to fill in the gaps now. I was getting used to the 60 hour work week. It has slowed down since the holiday rush is over. 
 
 I believe I will work for this company for as long as it exists and my autoimmune diseased ridden body allows me to. The people I work with are amazing and make it so much easier to find my way.
Overall_Why:
Because we are an online retailer, some of the items mean the world to our customers. While others are just material things people want and that doesn't seem near as meaningful as other situations.
 
 We also sell pet supplies. I have developed good relationships with disabled people and their service dogs. If the normal suppliers don't have a special item in stock that a customer desires, I will search high and low to find the products for them. 
 
 Being able to do things like this makes my work feel extremely meaningful at times. As previously mentioned, I suffer from mixed connective tissue disease. My entire life I worked retail. My body will not allow me to do this any longer. I use all of my learned skills from brick and mortar stores and apply them to the world of online retail.
 
 My blog is mostly about living with mixed connective tissue disease along with other disorders. I have followers that I keep in touch with who feel touched by my words because it assures them they aren't alone. There are other people going through the same pain they are. We exchange helpful ideas as well. 
 
 I would have to say that overall, my job is extremely meaningful. It allows me to provide for my family which is the most meaningful thing in the world to me. In addition to providing for my family, I am able to make the money to pay medical bills and insurance premiums. 
 
 If you asked some customers they may disagree with the meaningfulness of my job, while others would say that I am a very meaningful person in their lives.

Work_Story:
It's pretty straightforward: I'm an entry-level wildlife rehabilitator. I think its unlikely that this will be my permanent job in the future, but for right now I'm very satisfied with it. I work in the Pacific Northwest, and answered an opportunity/call for people a while ago via my school. I started with an un-paid internship, given the fact that it's a non-profit and they'd be unlikely to be able to pay me until I was skilled. I then majored in animal biology and took this position after I graduated. I plan on going to graduate school in the future, but not at this point. 
 
 Our primary focus is to rehabilitate injured and diseased wildlife from the {state} region. This involves working with a team of volunteers including skilled and un-skilled ones. We prepare diets, diagnose injured and sick animals, and provide long-term care. We also run blood-work and other such technical stuff, and build enclosures/exhibits as necessary. We also liaison with the general public and the {company}, although it depends what types and kinds of animals we are currently working with. 
 
 We do not rehabilitate invasive species the majority of the time, but tend to defer to the state government regarding rules and regulations. European Starlings, for instance, are never accepted or rehabilitated. Animals like Eastern Cottontail rabbits and Eastern Grey Squirrels are, however. This is in contrast to animals in Oregon like the Eastern Grey Squirrel, which is actually illegal to rehabilitate. 
 
 I also work with a team of veterinarians, including some of the most skilled wildlife veterinarians in the country. We work together to answer questions the public has about how animals are being rehabilitated, particularly because the majority of our work that's being done is funded by private donations, rather than grant money from the government. Our facility tends to be maintained and built by volunteers primarily, but receives a good amount of care from work done by professional work crews when items are too complicated for unskilled labor. 
 
 A typical day in my work involves arriving at 7:30 AM and leaving around 6 PM, which adheres to the traditional schedule for people like nurses, who need to be able to work all-day with their patients rather than switching shifts. We administer medication in the morning and follow vets and vet-techs on rounds, then provide our opinions for rehabilitation and prep for releases as needed. We do another round of medication 8 hours later, and do a final check around sundown to ensure all animals are accounted for and progressing well. Ones that are primarily nocturnal also are fed around this time. Rehabilitation also includes making decisions about animals that will be released or ones that have to be humanely euthanized, which sounds heartbreaking but actually tends to be a form of release both for the patient and the rehabilitator. Rehabers tend to be able to recognize the signs of animals that are being kept alive more for the sake of the rehabbers than their own sakes, and because we don't have the resources to keep all of them indefinitely in captivity, it's a necessary step in the rehab process.
Overall_Why: Yes, obviously I do or I wouldn't do it the way I do now. It's not something I can see myself doing in many years because it's so emotionally and physically taxing, but for a position that puts me in the environmental and animal sciences this quickly after school, its more than worthwhile to me. I can't see myself doing it long-term, like I said, but that doesn't mean that it isn't meaningful. I'm appreciative in particular that we are staffed almost entirely by volunteers: I consider it something that's very encouraging about the populace, that so many people are willing to give their time for nothing to help us. Furthermore I know that we are doing a very important job by rehabbing in the first place, becaause you already have animals that are sick and suffering in the wild. Finally we provide an opportunity to educate the general population on the wilderness as a whole, and help them take seriously the consequences of anti-environmental policies and practices. It makes it easier for people and the general public to relate to the challenges of wildlife in the modern world, and provides education for people who might not see it otherwise. Either way, I'm appreciative of the opportunity to work with people who are interested in giving back to the world rather than furthering their own interests. In a way they are furthering their interests by helping, I suppose, but as long as it's a means to a good end, i am not bothered by it.

Work_Story: While in school I would never have dreamed I would be selling for a living. Mostly, because I didn't know any successful salespeople. My father was a programmer and most of my professional acquaintances were in the medical field or lower-middle class clerical jobs. It was ingrained in my mind that I needed a graduate degree in something to make six figures. When I graduated, my first job was a claims rep at a huge insurance company. I was working my ass off, making 30K. The "big swinging dicks" in my department were making about 80-90K. These were late 40's and 50's folks who reveled in office bureaucracy and enforced the ridiculous pecking order that dictated our pay regardless of contribution. At 21 or 22, most of my friends were in the same boat. Making about 25-35K in entry level jobs. At a house party, I met a dude who didn't go to college but was making 60K selling cars at a local dealership. I thought to myself, why the hell did I go to college for a degree when I could have made 60K without out (seemed like a ton of money to 22 year old me.) After prodding from friends who told me I had the personality for selling, I decided to tap into my network to see if I could find a decent sales role. "Having the personality for selling" to me at the time meant being of dubious character and somewhat of a loudmouth. That's what I thought of salespeople, which is from my experience public perception. Despite that stigma, I was tired of making crappy pay and working harder than anyone. Luckily, I was hired for my first B2B job making the same base pay I made as a claims rep. Imagine the ridicule I got from my co-workers at the insurance company when I told them I was going into sales. Year 1, I made 69K. year 2, I was over 100K. My friends were in disbelief as I left them in the dust 2 years out of college. Over the course of my time at that first B2B sales job, I saw a ton of folks prosper and drop off. It was clear many folks were there out of default, not because they desired the needed skills to succeed long term. Even as I entered the med device sales industry, many folks from my old B2B job struggled to make the same transition as I did. Not one made it from my office. Some gave up on sales altogether and went to law school. Now they are saddled with debt and can't find a job. Others made lateral transitions to other entry level B2B sales and make a mere 80-100K. They can't leave because they're too old to start something else and give up the mediocre pay they currently make. However, they aren't aggressive enough to break through and honestly don't seem to believe that the numbers I was throwing out would be achievable for them. Even in that med device role I kept dreaming bigger. What could I sell on a grander scale? I looked at the company issued ipads we had, or the new salesforce.com website we had to start logging shit into. Who sold our company these? I bet that guy makes more than me! From there, I pursued roles in tech and telemedicine. My base is now mid 100's with a low 200's OTE (which is actually pretty standard in context). Never in a million years would 22 year old me ever think that type of base pay was possible in sales. So, Sales is lucractive only if you are willing to learn and continuously progress. You have to like money. You have to value self improvement. It doesn't come from certifications and degrees. The path is not linear; many cannot handle that uncertainty. -Sales is a dead end career if you don't dream bigger and possess the resourcefulness to break into more technical roles and higher paying industries. Most don't even know these roles exist. - Many do not pursue it because they see salespeople as the stereotypical swindling used car salesman, regardless of what they sell. Even I thought this! It is pure ignorance and if you work to change that view through your actions with customers you can make a ton in this profession - Many do not pursue it because they simply don't believe the numbers are possible. I had to carry around my W2. Salespeople do lie about their income, or misrepresent it as consistent when it's not. That does not mean those numbers are not possible or being achieved by someone. The non-salespeople I tell can't believe I make a six figure base. It just doesn't calculate to my friends who are lawyers and engineers who make low 100's. However, it's true. Any enterprise tech position is likely going to pay a six figure base.
Overall_Why: My basic reasoning depends on my belief that finding genuine fulfillment through work is really unlikely. The vast majority of jobs are going to be largely mechanical, dull, and repetitive despite any surface appeal that they have. Because of this, I think the best thing to do is to just accept that you're job might not be meaningful. And that's fine. You're most likely going to work 8-10 hours 5 days a week. That's a lot of time, sure. That's 40-50 hours of work per week, then another 56 is lost to sleep. But what about the other 62-72 hours? That's where you live your life. That's where you pursue your passions and find meaning. I think a lot of people fall into the trap of looking for meaning in work and putting all of the value on what they do in those 40-50 work hours to the neglect of their free hours. People like this will see their time after work as nothing more than rest time. Their job becomes the most important thing in their life. On the other hand, if you accept that your job is just a job (something mildly unpleasant that you have to do to make a living), then you're going to put a lot more value on your free time. You won't waste evenings watching TV shows that you don't even like. Obviously, some jobs are worse than others and can be downright miserable. I'm not saying that job satisfaction is unimportant. But I think that most people my age expect too much out of their future job. I believe that it's best to aim for nothing more than a job that is tolerable and gives you a reasonable degree of financial freedom.

Work_Story:
I am a special education teacher in {state}. Specifically, I am a resource teacher at a high school. I have a caseload of students who are either in the tenth, eleventh, or twelfth grade. These students all have mild disabilities of some kind or another. I have quite a few on the Autism Spectrum, a few with emotional disabilities, many with learning disabilities, and many with other health impairment (typically ADHD or ADD). My students go to their general education classes for the majority of the day and then report to me for one resource period. During this resource period, we do a variety of things. Myself and my IA (instructional assistant) help the students with their homework and preparing for upcoming tests and quizzes so they can pass their classes and graduate on time with a high school diploma. We also provide remediation lessons on their area of weakness or in the subject in which they did not pass the required state exam necessary to graduate. Many of my students do not pass the math and english portions and therefore need the remediation in hopes they can pass before their senior year. Also, during resource, many students use extended time on tests and quizzes they were given in their classroom but they did not finish. Sometimes they have the accommodation of read aloud so we will read their test to them in hopes they can do the best possible job. Another huge aspect of my job is paperwork. Each student on my caseload has at least one IEP meeting per year, if not multiple meetings. I spend a lot of time preparing these documents and then preconferencing with parents to make sure everything looks ok prior to the conference. Then we will meet and discuss and edit the document to make sure it best suits their child and can help them be successful in their general education classes. Another part of my job that is relatively new to me is coteaching. I am now coteaching two sections of a US History class. There is a qualified history teacher in the room as well as myself. The cap is 22 kids. These are all kids with either IEPs or as identified as struggling in social studies. We are to provide many interventions and go over material slower than a standard US History class would. We also shorten tests, and accommodate things in many different ways. Students with behavioral issues are not supposed to be placed in these classrooms as they are really supposed to focus on students with academic needs, but we do have quite a few behavioral issues we have to address during this time as well. It certainly is a challenging, but fun part of my day! The most important thing about my job is being flexible. I never know when a kid will have a melt down or need me for whatever reason and my whole schedule can be thrown off.

Overall_Why:
I find my work very meaningful. I am helping kids with behavioral and academic challenges on a daily basis. Many of these kids see me as their only trusted adult and really trust me to help them get through high school as well as just their general teenage years. Furthermore, I feel as though I am a meaningful person from their parents perspective as well. Many of these parents rely on me to be their kids "person" on a day-to-day basis. It is amazing all of the inner-struggles kids are going through and I really feel as though I have an impact in helping them through. 
 
 I am not just sitting behind a desk looking at a computer all day, but rather I am interacting with our nations youth. I am not only teaching them math and english, but also social skills and more importantly, coping skills. Many of our students come to us with a very sincere lacking ability in these areas and need someone outside of their home to help. It is an honor to be able to have this time as my job to help these kids. I believe they truly value my help and opinions which makes it that much more meaningful. In terms of having a meaningful job, it probably can't get too much more meaningful to me than being a special education teacher. I feel rewarded each and every day by going to work and I am not sure everyone can say that!

Work_Story:
