I work a day shift schedule (Mon- Fri) at a warehouse. The General Warehouse Person is responsible for performing tasks involved in processing and shipping orders to the customer. Job Responsibilities (listed in order of importance and/or time spent) Responsibilities may include: receiving, scanning, picking, packing, auditing and shipping merchandise to the customer. Tracks all inbound and outbound items through radio frequency (RF) scanning to ensure accountability in inventory. Verifies orders and ensures entry into the inventory management system.Operates a clean and safe work environment, ensuring that all areas of the warehouse are well maintained, kept orderly and free of debris.Maintains quality standards set forth by warehouse management. Internal Basic Qualifications. You have to be willing to work a flexible shift. Willing and able to stand at least 8 hours with or without reasonable accommodation sometimes in the same place for over a long period of time. Willing and able to perform tasks which include pushing, pulling, grasping, and lifting with or without reasonable accommodation. Willing and able to carry up to 50 pounds with or without reasonable accommodation. and even carry items up and down staircases. Preferred Qualifications are at least a High School Diploma/GED and at least 2 years experience in a warehouse or distribution center. At least 6 months experience operating motorized material handling equipment. At least 6 months experience with radio frequency (RF) scanning. At least 6 months experience entering and retrieving data in an inventory management system. 
 Where I work is amazing how well the employee is treated well and respected. It is a great work environment. There are perks in addition to your salary. Such as health benefits, retirement accounts, discounts on company products as well as other companies products which is really sweet. My time spent working has been very rewarding. I was given great raises every few months when I earned it with my effort and my co-workers were very understanding with my life in that I could take off when I wanted and could work certain hours certain weeks, being a full-time student this was much appreciated. I loved my manager, he helped me learn and grow in the store and was sad when I moved away. When I worked at a different location near my University things could not have been different. I quit in a month when I found another job. That manager did not handle his employees the same as my hometown did. He had mostly students working so scheduling us all was a mess and he was not as flexible with everyone's hours, he depended on the part-timers to come in and hold the fort but then didn't try to allow time off for testing weeks. Working for big companies like this is probably all the same, it depends on the area/management crew. My company has very good benefits even for part-time employees and has 401K and stock options. The holidays were nice as the company catered food to all the employees and made sure each person was recognized. I think things like that matter a lot when chooses a company to work for.
Overall_Why: I find my work very meaningful. I am like the little ant working at making a the anthill one grain at a time. I know that it is not accomplished all in one day but there is progress with each grain. I don??t let myself get overwhelmed with all the work that has to be done but rather focus on just getting the task done and with each bit by the end of a 10 hour day a lot was completed. There is always more work needed to be done tomorrow but that is ok. That is the nature of having job and means I am in the right place. I would feel like I do not have meaning it I were home all day or retired. Sure It is fun for a little while but then you lose a connection with society. It is important to stay social and grounded working along with others to keep connected and keep a sense of meaning in my life. I could stay home and clean my house and do the laundry and watch tv. But then what? That shit is boring and depressing when you are alone. There is so much going on in the world and it is good to be in a job that makes a difference.

Work_Story:
Currently I am employed as Senior Software Developer for a multi-billion dollar corporation in the United States. I support existing software and hardware systems and business processes. Our systems are a combination of vendor and in-house developed products which reside on various platforms. No two days are the same and most of the time my planned activities for the day are sidetracked by production support, unplanned meetings and walk-ups (people who walk up to your cube and ask questions). The majority of the support tasks (other than unexpected problems) revolve around planned hardware/software upgrades, maintenance releases of software changes and business process cycle related support. Business process cycles involve the recurring processes that are executed by systems and users to support schedule generation, bidding and awarding and payroll. These cycles require support which may mean staying later to ensure everything works smoothly and correctly or it may mean fixing issues caused by a system or by a user.
 Most releases of in-house developed and supported software are medium to large in size and must be released in defined business down times which are generally early in the morning or on weekends. As a result I work a fair number of early mornings and Saturdays. We have many interconnected systems making any release quite involved from both a planning and execution perspective. Systems must be taken down and brought back up in a pre-defined order. In addition to meeting to plan our releases we also execute tests of our releases in test environments to try to find any missed dependencies or other problems that might arise. Even so, there are usually some problems on the production release day. We take those issues and update our template for implementations to try to avoid future occurrences.
 I spend time each day supporting users. Even users who have been in a particular business unit for 10 or more years forget how and why tasks are done. I quite often have to remind them why or show them how. I find there is not consistent communication between the users' supervisor and the users on issues relevant to executing the business processes. I keep track so I can advise them myself to avoid problems. So not only do I have to do my job I have to make up for he inadequate way that some supervisors do theirs. This not only saves the company from issues but also saves me from having the fix problems after the fact.
 Another part of my job is primary and secondary support whereby on a rotating weekly basis two of us take on the role of primary and secondary. The primary is the first person to be called when a system issue occurs. The secondary gets called if the primary doesn't answer the call. In the primary capacity I have frequently been woken up in the middle of the night to login and research a problem. I have also spent many hours on conference calls on weekends and in the middle of the night. While programming can very rewarding the reality of today's programmer does not meet the college hype.
Overall_Why: I do not find my current work very meaningful. There are many things that we have to do that are not logical. Much of it involves managers protecting their own interests by trying to look like they are doing their best for the company. As a result, the people at the bottom end up having to spend many wasted hours (often beyond a normal work day) doing things for the sake of appearances. We spend a lot of hours in meetings so managers can appear like they matter when in fact we get more work done and get it done more effectively and efficiently without them. Everyone loves the days when all the managers are out of the office. I think the company would be more profitable with few managers and by eliminating all of the wasted time on appearances and overkill on many tasks. Being a programmer is not very rewarding (other than financially). If you do something right it's just expected. If you do something wrong you get slammed for it. It's not the glamorous going to save the world pie in the sky fantasy promoted in colleges and by employers. A good manager leaves people alone to do their work. We don't have many of those. Mostly we have managers who are micro managers because they don't trust anyone to do their work. Moral goes down every year and every year when we fill out the employee survey nothing changes. The sad thing is it doesn't need to be that way. Managers choose to make it that way.

Work_Story:
I work for the Juvenile Court System. I am an Intake Court Counselor. In my job, I receive a new case for a juvenile. This happens when a juvenile is charged with committing a crime. Some of the cases I receive involve juvenile's that have never been arrested before, or maybe only have one or two previous charges. In those cases, I schedule an Intake conference to meet with the juvenile and guardian. When they arrive at the office they are asked to compete demographic papaperwork. Once their paperwork is complete, I take them back to my office. Once in my ofgice, I explain the process. I inform them that during this meeting, I will be going over their paperwork they just completed, then I would need them to complete three releases of information forms. The first one is to give me permission to speak to the juvenile. The second form gives me permission to contact the juvenile's school and request grades, attendance and discipline reports. The third form is a behavioral health release that gives me permission to discuss the case with any counseors, therapists or programs the juvenile is involved with. After the forms are signed, and the guardian receives a copy, I go over the juvenile's rights to be free from any kind of abuse. The juvenile and guardian sign that they have received this information. The next step is an interview with the juvenile and family. Questions are asked regarding the juvenile's strenghths, likes, dislikes, family relationships, family dynamic, family criminality, family involvement with Child Protective Services or Social Services, substance abuse, gang involvement, friendships, hospitalizations, diagnosis, medication, involvement with counseling and school behavior. Once the interview is complete, the juvenile takes a short assessment to determine if they require a complete psychological assessment. If it is determined that they require further assessment, a referral is made to a mental health provider. After that is all complete, a decision is made as to whether the juvenile can be placed on a diversion plan, where he or she is assigned some consequences and monitored until the consequences are completed, or sent to Court. If a juvenile is sent to Court, then his or her case is approved for Court and a Summons is created. Once a Court date is assigned, it is part of my job to request records from the school, any therapeutic programs the juvenile is involved in, and Child Protective Services, do a criminal background check on anyone residing in the home that is over sixteen years old and on any parent or guardian that may not live in the home, but that has regulag contact with the juvenile. All the information gathered during the interview, and from the records requested are then typed into a Court report that I must write and submit to my supervisor ten days prior to the juvenile's Court Hearing. Once the report is edited and returned to me, I must copy it and distribute it to the different parties involved in the juvenile's Hearing. 
 
 The process is similar for a juvenile that has a longer criminal history, the main difference is that juvenile's with a long history are not eligible for a Diversion plan, and must be approved for Court. The rest of the process is the same.
Overall_Why: I believe that my work is very meaningful. The overall purpose is to work with young criminal offenders to try to get them help. If a juvenile has a substance abuse problem, we will help them get treatment, if they have metal health issues, we will help the families adjust, and seek out the best course of treatment, be it weekly counseling, something more intensive, or in-patient. In my work I help a lot of families and juveniles. Most of the juvenile's I work with are not bad kids. Many of them are acting out, or participating in criminal behavior because they do not know any alternatives. It is either what they have learned from their familues, their friends or their communities. I help give these kids a chance to learn another way to cope and another way to live life. With some kids, their criminal behavior is the result of a drug or alcohol problem, or due to some kind of mental health problem. With those kids I help them get treatment for their drug use, and help them get into the appropriate setting for their mental health issues. I also help provide the family support in learning the best ways to deal with and address the problems that their kids are having. Unfortunately, in some cases, I deal with kids that will not change their criminal behavior. They are the ones that will be in and out of jail their whole lives.With those kids my focus has to change a little. It goes from working on rehabilitation of the juvenile to community protection.

Work_Story: I have went to school and graduated college in the field of education. I have started a new job working as a teacher aide. This is not the job that I originally wanted but I'm more qualified to be the main teacher but I needed more experience in this field and had to make a sacrifice. I do love my job as a teacher aide. It's not what I expected it would be. I get to interact with the children every day. In some ways I'm with the children more than the actual teacher. I get up early in the morning and try to get to the classroom pretty early to get started with the day. In the morning I go and collect any mail that we might have or other notices for the day. In the morning, I start getting the class ready. I may have to go make copies of papers or do other chores for the teacher. I normally take that time to organize the room and put seats where they may need to be. I like this quiet time in the morning it allows me to begin to get ready for my day and all that I will have to do. As I'm working in the classroom some children will start to stream in. Most come in and leave their backpacks and then they go to eat breakfast. I leave morning work on their desk so that they can sit quietly and work on that assignment or any others that they might have missed during the week. Sometimes I'll work one on one with a student if they need help in a certain area. Once class goes into session they do their morning calendar work which they really like. We work on reading and language arts. The students will sometimes work in groups or by themselves. They read silently or we do a group activity. They then work on science or social studies depending on the day. We try to do a lot of group work and work where the children are actively moving about and involved in the lesson. Later, they go have lunch. During lunch I'll make copies and set up for the afternoon session. After lunch they go to recess and then they go on to their specials. They may have music, pe, or art. It depends on the day of the week. After the children return to the classroom. We work on math and either science or social studies. During math we try to provide the children with manipulatives because it makes math easier for them to do. They seem to learn better when they are able to actually touch something and see it visually. We have a very full day and at the end of the day we start to clean up the classroom for the next day. I'll collect the work they did and assign any homework or other things they need to review. We like to have a sharing time at this point if there is still more time in the day. The kids like to share things about what they've done or fun things about their life.The kids are then dismissed for the day and sent to their buses or other means of transportation. After they are gone, I clean up the classroom and pull out any materials that will be needed for the next day. I sometimes grade assignments before leaving and do anything other tasks that will make the next day easier. I then turn off the lights and leave the classroom.
Overall_Why: I think my work is very meaningful because everyday you are presented with a new challenge to solve. There is always work to be done and you cal always improve on what you've done. I love that I'm challenged every day to be the very best me that I can be. I have to be on my A game everyday. If I'm not my children can suffer. I have to work hard in order to provide them with the best education that I can. The children really become attached to you and they tell you how much they appreciate your hard work. The children are kind and really want to work hard and do a good job. I'm impressed by their diligence and how they are always striving to achieve more. I feel really grateful to be able to teach them everyday. They rely on us to do our best. The students make me want to work hard because they work hard. Students are pushed very hard in school now more so than ever and they manage to never give up. The work I do every day is meaningful because it has a real impact on someone's life and their well being. The things you teach stay with the children. Kids are like sponges and they really soak up all that you teach them. You teach them so much more than math and science. They learn how to be respectful, kind, and good human beings. I love my job and the work that I'm doing on a daily basis. It has truly impacted me.

Work_Story: I am a fiber-artist/designer for an eco friendly, sustainable accessories line. I source organic materials from across the globe and create new items such as scarves, hats, belts and bags. The challenge of my job is to make certain the materials that I use are made using organic materials, such as cotton, silk, hemp, bamboo, etc. This may sound easy, however there are many production methods that are used to produce so called eco friendly fabrics that may be questionable. For example, when fabrics are dyed, the runoff/wastage from the production can causes harm to nearby rivers, streams, and lakes. There is no fix in place to clean up the dyes that runoff into these waters, so the after effects can be devastating to marine life and drinking water to people who live near these processing plants. Just because a fabric has been grown organically, the fabric may be ruined if it is dyed using chemically treated dyes. To prevent this, what I do is when I procure organic cotton fabric, I will dye it myself using organic plant and vegetable dyes, which is the most natural way to it, in my opinion. I also use fabrics, products, etc that are GOTS-certified or Oeko-Tex certified. These are certifications that covers every stage of production from the raw materials through the manufacturing which must be environmentally and socially responsible and provides consumers with a credible assurance that the product they are buying is both organic and thoughtfully and sustainably produced. In order to be certified, textiles must be made from at least 70% certified organic natural fibers. The final products may include, but are not limited to fiber products, yarns, fabrics, clothes and home textiles. A textile product carrying the GOTS label grade ??organic?? must contain a minimum of 95% certified organic fibers whereas a product with the label grade ??made with organic?? must contain a minimum of 70% certified organic fibers. At all stages of processing, organic fiber products must be separated from conventional fiber products and be free of a whole spectrum of chemicals such as chlorine bleach and known toxic substances such as heavy metals and azo dyes. In addition, manufacturers must have a credible environmental policy in place that covers such production factors as fair labor practices (including no child labor), water consumption, wastewater treatment and packaging that does not utilize PVC plastic. The Oeko-Tex??is a globally uniform testing certification system for textile raw materials, intermediate and end products at all stages of production. The tests for harmful substances include any substance that is prohibited or regulated by law such as carcinogenic dyestuffs, chemicals that are known to be harmful to health such as formaldehyde and heavy metals, substances such as pesticides, allergy-inducing dye-stuffs, etc. I also use organic fibers such as banana, coconut pineapple, bamboo and hemp fibers to create yarns for knitting, belts, hats, purses, etc. In addition, I also use fabrics created from recycled plastics mixed with cotton to create t-shirt weight materials to make scarves.
Overall_Why: Yes, I do. I am doing my part to save and protect our planet from harmful wastage. I am reducing waste from landfills, and not contributing to the harm of people and the planet. Our company produces and manufactures items/products that are simply good for people. We are trying to contribute the solution of the problem, not adding to it. I also find my work meaningful because I think about the future generation, and what kind of world they will inherit. It is up to all of us to help them keep our planet thriving, healthy and whole. This may sound corny, but it is up to each and every one of us to make some type of change for the better, big or small. This world need all the help it can get. The fashion industry is the 2nd largest polluters of the earth, all lot of people don't know that. I am proud of the company that I work for because we are doing everything in our power, using all of the natural resources at our disposal to manufacture products that are beautiful and causes no harm to people or our planet. I salute other truly eco-friendly companies out there, whether they are in fashion, food, education, etc that are making a difference and running their companies in a sustainable and conscious manner. We are making a difference one idea, initiative, product at a time. I wish that more companies come to realize that there are ways to make any company sustainable.

Work_Story:
My current occupation is in the HVAC, Heating Ventilation & Air Conditioning, industry. My specific role is in the residential sales department. I have been in this position at this company for just over 5 years. The industry as a whole has many different divisions, or specific areas that the work is performed. There??s commercial, residential and industrial aspects of the industry. Within those divisions falls different departments. Those departments are generally described as Sales, Service or Maintenance. Different technicians and employees specialize in different departments, of different divisions or different trades. 
 
 My current focus is in the sales departments, of the residential department of the air conditioning division. My main job involves focusing on replacement or upgrading of old inefficient air conditioning equipment in customers homes. My scheduled appointments occur on site at the customers home, and a typical visit involves taking measurements of the home, in both area, as well as insulation, windows, doors, unconditioned spaces, and more. After taking all my measurements I am able to calculate exactly what is required for that particular customers needs. Once the measurement portion is complete. I then will sit down with the customer and discuss any concerns the customer may have so I can help design the right setup for them. I then come up with several options based on what the customer is looking for and I present them with their options, and during this process describe the pros and cons of each option. 
 
 Prior to this, I worked in the service department of the residential division of air conditioning. This involved onsite visits to customers home who were having an issue with their existing system. I would perform diagnostics on their equipment to determine the problem and provide them with the solution. If the customer agrees I would then perform the work required to remedy their situation. 
 
 When I am not performing my main job, I work as what we call a lead generator. I do this when there are no current appointments for me to attend, in my downtime. I approach people either in person, I created letters and post cards to mail, I create email flyers to send out and I also make phone calls. Most of this is done through our existing customer databases, while a small portion involves ??cold calling??. My main focus when performing this task is on the existing customer database because those are what we call ??warm leads??, meaning there is a higher chance of scheduling an appointment with someone who is familiar with out services. Cold calling is always a last resort, mainly because people are not very receptive to that form of advertising and it mainly is what I would call a waste of time. 
 
 My work schedule usually consists of 50 to 60 hours a week. Which 75 percent of that being my main task of selling and the remaining 25 percent being lead generation. My pay is a small salary plus commission based, so selling is my top priority.
Overall_Why:
I do find my work meaningful because what I do actually helps people. By selling new air conditioning systems, I??m assisting home owners with decreasing their energy bills, saving them money, and also increasing the comfort inside their homes. Newer technology with air conditioners will greatly reduce the average home owners electricity consumption by up to 50 percent. And today, everyone is looking for ways to save their hard earned income. Another benefit of newer technology is designed around comfort. A newer system has technologies that help keep comes cooler during the summer hot months, and warmed during the colder winter months. Also, newer systems are a lot more efficient at reducing humidity levels inside homes, creating a ,ore comfortable environment. Other technologies are based around indoor air quality, meaning the air inside people??s homes is more pure. This greatly helps individuals who have asthma, allergies, or are more susceptible to illnesses. Indoor air quality products help kill and reduce mold, viruses, bacteria, mildew, odors, etc. 
 
 Taking all of this into account, my job is very meaningful, not only to me but to my customers, it??s one of the primary reasons I thoroughly enjoy what I do for a living. I make people??s lives better, I make them happier, and I create bonds with my customers, because they are so thankful for my help and my advice in solving their issues or concerns. I really think that very few careers have the same outcome or effect of being truly meaningful.

Work_Story:
Working for {company} consists of managing rental and vacation properties, dealing with customers, ensuring rental prices are consistent with local markets, and searching for further properties to purchase. This position is exceptionally independent which requires excellent time management. Responding to renter and property needs is the most challenging to part of the job. 
   The management of rental properties is the most cost-effective part of the job. Having renters that report issues promptly and are receptive to making the necessary repairs on a suggested schedule make this the most profitable part of the job. Renters who do not want to follow the suggested schedule or do not report issues promptly drive up the cost because of return visits and additional damage. The best cost-effective means is to accurately identify and repair problems with a property before they become larger in costly repairs or customer dissatisfaction.
 The management of vacation properties is one that requires some unusual hours at times. During the Spring and Fall seasons, customers return and leave their properties, and it is crucial that each property is ready for their owners to return to and leave for the season. When a property owner returns to their home, they want the home to be in good condition with air condition or heating at acceptable levels, the landscaping completed, and clean ready for them to return. When the properties are vacant, it is necessary to monitor them physically ensuring that there are no break-ins, vandalism, or natural disasters that would harm the property. 
 Long-term customers are the bread and butter of this business in both the rental and vacation property management parts of the business. Keeping customers happy is the best way to ensure repeat business with them and makes identifying and fixing problems much quicker and far easier in the long run. Sometimes this involves making concessions or taking firm stands to ensure the safety of the customer and the properties.
 Pricing the amount of rent in each area is essential because if the rental price is too high, no one will want to rent and if it is to low the company is losing money. There is a delicate balance that changes due to time, location, and property values. Finding the happy medium is as much as an art as it is a science. The process takes large amounts of time researching the market, finding prospective customers, and identifying historical pricing norms. 
 Researching available properties that are for sale, within the price range, and meets the requested criteria is an ever-evolving process. This ever-evolving process requires regular monitoring to ensure that properties are not missed and evaluated promptly. Once properties are found the method of assessing the property must start immediately safeguarding the possible loss of a great property that has the potential for great returns. Knowing the local marketplace is a great way to speed up this process and requires time and effort.
Overall_Why: I find my work meaningful because I am providing services to people for them to be happy with a place they can call home. I am also providing peace of mind to customers who are not in my area, so they do not have to worry about their property. I also ensure that my employer is spending only the necessary money to upkeep their properties in good working order and raise their profits which in turn I receive regular increases in my pay. I appreciate the independence that my employer provides me and would find it hard to do any other position that did not allow this amount of freedom. I also have the freedom to bring my environmental beliefs into this position. I can improve an issue with a more environmentally friendly product or process and can take actions like replacing incandescent light bulbs with LED ones to save on power requirements. When making improvements on properties, I can salvage items like kitchen cabinets, toilets, bathtubs, or carpet and give them to charities that resale these items to help out the disadvantaged.

Work_Story:
I am an elementary teacher. I work as a 4th grade full time in an elementary school about 4 miles from my house. I have been teaching for 25 years. I don??t like every day I teach, I don??t like every year that I have taught. I don??t like every kid that I taught. But I keep doing a good job with all the kids because of things that I have learned about teaching and being with kids. I try to have fun while learning, I have a sense of humor, I am nice, and I am strict. I prefer to teach the younger kids, because I have taught middle school and those kids have real serious issues.
 Teaching is a very compassionate profession. I try to show kids that you care about them, even if they make me angry and I don??t like the way they act. I try to show kids with the way I act that I care, and because of this students will be more open to understanding the world around them. I have to be very empathetic. Being able to put yourself in someone??s shoes and see things from their perspective is very important to understanding kids and what they need. I try to be a positive person, it??s not an easy. Being a positive teacher is even harder when we??re always met with problems and stupid paperwork in the modern classroom. Common core, new behavior plans, stupid mandates and rules that help the school board and not the kids make me so very angry and make me want to quit. But you can??t. Not just because of retirement or money. You have to stay positive when it??s tough and when things aren??t going your way. It can have such a real positive impact on the students and everyone around them when you have a lot to deal with and still keep on and still have a good outlook. Looking on the bright side always seems to help make things better.. Teachers have to look at ways that they can help not just the child, but their family too. Keeping up with problems that the kids have means also keeping up with the problems in the family. A great teacher shows kindness to kids, but those around her. Being a kind to kids and everyone else teacher makes students feel welcomed, cared for and loved.
 Of course some bad kids ruin a really good day. When kids misbehave in the class, and they have no reason to in my class, it creates real havoc. You have to turn in lesson plans in advance, and if the administration comes in and you are not doing what you are supposed to, you have to explain yourself. The day to day problems caused by discipline, or lack of it, can stop learning dead in its tracks. Kids can??t learn if there is disruption in the class. It doesn??t happen every day to me, but it happens enough. Usually it??s because of something that happened outside of the class that is brought inside- from PE, recess, home, changing classes, other teachers. It??s the one thing that really messes me up and makes me dislike my job. Probably because the office does not want to deal with serious problems. At our school, a kid brought a gun to school- 5th grader. He was let back in because my principal is ??compassionate??. Then when he came back, he brought a knife to school. Stuff like that makes me so mad. But it??s not the good kids fault. So I stay teaching. And try my best to educate, be compassionate, and be cheerful.
Overall_Why:
To know that you have influenced a child, to know that you have made a difference in their life is very important. I find sometimes that kids come to me without the trust in education that they need to go on. I have worked in enough poor schools to know that education is not that important to a lot of families. You have to make it important to the child. And I try to do that.
 I believe that children who have trouble learning can perform just as well provided they get clear teaching, catered to their learning ability, with direction and guidance. I try to make all my lessons meaningful for all students regardless of whether they are strong or weak in their learning. I am enthusiastic in my teaching as much possible and try to make my lessons interesting and clear. I can help a child to overcome problems by showing them that learning does not have to be a chore.
 I began my teaching career 25 years ago, I have realized the importance of providing personal relationships with kids. Not friendships, or fun relationships, but trust and honesty. When I was a kid, no one cared enough about me to even remember me long after they taught me. I make my kids feel important, so they grow up feeling important. I don??t think that any other profession can do as much. I don??t know if I succeeded much past the year I taught them, although they tell me that it did when they come back to visit. But I try.

Work_Story:
I work in an office setting for my full time job, outside of my home. In my department, we have 12 people. 
 In office, there is 4 departments in my area. 
 I purchase and procure airplane parts. It can be demand worth day to day stuff. The demand also increase, as we continue to purchase new and used airplanes. 
 Between the data entry, emails, and phone calls, it makes for a busy day. Daily I send emails and make phone calls requesting for quotes for parts that I am required to purchase. 
 Throughout the day we have to follow-up. It gets more busy with added projects come along, that we sometimes don't find out about until they are basically due. Then add on with problems, the day just gets more interesting. 
 I enjoy a challenge. I also enjoy my job. Of course, in every job setting there are going to be issues. Especially depending on how big it may be. There is about 100 in our area, but our building is big and has a couple thousand people, at least. So on that note, I do like my job, and most of the people I work with. However, there are office politics that I don't agree with. 
 Some of the things that make my daily job tough, is how they treat us, as far as the micromanaging goes. Which I sort of understand, though, because some people try to push the limits. Those people are the ones who ruin the good things for us. 
 I have worked for this company for 10 years. At the beginning, when I started, it seemed better, and they seemed to treat us better. Some of my co-workers, who have been with the company for 20 years, say it was even better then. It was a smaller. They seemed to actually care about their people. 
 The passed couple of years, my job has definitely gotten more demanding. The busier we get, the more I have to prioritize every task. The requirements seem to increase on going. With the demands going up, and the lack of people, it does make my job strenuous at times.
 They are not really flexible work much of the workplace. Unless you are in a salary position. Otherwise the hourly positions are required to put in their 40 hours a week. The only thing they are at all flexible with, is if you have time of accured and you use some of your time. No excuse is allowed unless you have a doctor's note and you are approved for FMLA. Then there will be a little flexibility. 
 The way I make it sound, is it is an awful place. It is actually doable. However, it's a good thing I have some great co-workers to work with, otherwise, I probably would not have lasted this long with the company. I do understand every company has their issues. All I ask is they respect their people and it will be more enjoyable of a workplace. 
 The pay is ok, at least it is more than minimum wage. However, the cost of living is crazy in the area my work is located, so it should be a more competitive pay. My work hours are great, and they don't change. So I do like the consistency there, and I know my requirements and expectations. I like that I am able to interact with many people throughout the day.
Overall_Why:
For the most part it's meaningful. My work expects things to be done. They also require things to be done. I think that helps with the drive to stay motivated to push daily. I know if I don't do my job, and buy the parts that are needed, the airplanes that require them at regular maintenance times, won't get back in the air. 
 So yes, my job is meaningful, because without the airplanes in the air, the company won't stay in business. 
 Sometimes it's nice to be recognized, however, I know at the end of the day it is my job, and things need to get done. The tasks need to be finished in a timely manner. 
 The requirements also help to push when things need to be pushed. When the demand is high, it seems to light a fire under everyone and we get moving just a little faster. 
 I also think that if my work wasn't as meaningful, I wouldn't have as much motivation to go to work everyday. Though the paycheck is nice, it would be hard to drag myself into the office every morning and last the full day. If there's no meaning, how dreadful would it be for the menoteny of the fait work stresses and there be no meaning.

Work_Story: My work is sometimes enjoyable and sometimes not. Sometimes my days are long and sometimes I in don't get out of bed until noon. My work is varied and unpredictable. I am self-employed and Is do various "gigs" or "odd jobs" for people from all over. A few of the things I do are: Laundry - washing, drying, ironing, folding, stain removal, patches, button replacement. House cleaning: windows, baseboards, ceiling fans, floors, dishes, etc... Computer work/Data entry - transcription, essays, resumes, freelance work, taxes, etc... Driving - delivery of "anything", rides to appts, run errands (grocery shop, prescription pick up, etc..), Yard work - mow lawns, trim trees, shape bushes, edge, weed removal, rake, remove debris, plant greenery and water and nurture, etc... Paint: indoor and outdoor painting, trim, minor repairs (small holes in drywall, baseboard replacement, etc). There really is no job that I won't do, as long as I am able to the job in a professional manner and in a reasonable amount of time with exceptional results, I am always going to accept. This is why my days are so varied and unpredictable, but I absolutely love it. I don't have to punch a time card or answer to a boss and I get to choose what I do or don't want to do and when and how I want to do it. I couldn't ask for a more awesome way to earn a living. I can stay home when my kids are sick, take an extended lunch and meet my husband at the local diner. I can stop halfway through the day to pick up my grandmother because she got lost at the grocery store, or I can work through the evening and leave when the job is complete. My ability to be so flexible is the reason I started doing this to begin with and it's the reason why I continue to do this and why I will probably always do this. My job is awesome. The money is great too. I have been paid $500 to write an essay that had to be turned in the next day for a college student. It took me maybe three or four hours to get it knocked out, which works out to over $125 an hour. Not bad wages at all I must say. Of course most of the jobs I do don't pay anywhere near that amount, I'd say, on average, I bring home about $15 to $20 an hour. I am more than able to meet my financial obligations and I do it on MY terms. There's also the tax perks. No, I'm not going to say that since I don't get a W-2, and there's no actual way to prove my income, I just don't pay taxes. That would be silly. I get so many tax breaks that I never end up owing anything anyway. In fact, I always get a pretty big check from IRS even though I don't pay one penny in. There is a write off I Some people have crappy nine to five jobs where some boss is hanging over there shoulder shouting out orders, dictating their every move, watching to make sure they aren't a minute late from break and that they don't leave a minute early for lunch. They make minimum wage and their families are left to deal with whatever comes up and they end up missing out on everything. They make little to no money and have no time to spend it on anything they would enjoy anyway. What an awful way to live. No, I think I'll stay right here, thank you.
Overall_Why: Yes. It supports my family and allows me the freedom to still be there for and with them. It pays very well. I don't have all of the troublesome problems that a mainstream job has. No supervisor, no time clock, no one making any stipulations about anything except for me. I have the ability to provide needed assistance to those who may be less fortunate at a low rate or even for free at times which makes me feel like I am doing something to help my community. I have time to be able to donate services to the needy. I gain more experience everyday and I will be able to retire at a age much younger than most are able to, if I should decide to do so. I am in a position to be able to help provide continuing education for my children if they choose to go to college. I am setting an example for my children. They are learning that they can do whatever they want to do and be whoever they want to be. If I can do it at my age being an unskilled, convicted felon with drug addiction issues; an older woman with no work history and no formal training, (nothing but a GED). If I can overcome those odds and be successful then the sky is the limit for my children who have none of those obstacles in their path. I am very proud of my work and find it very rewarding for the reasons I have stated above. Best reasons in the world. My children.

Work_Story:
I would like to think of my job as the ultimate combination between two industries: Customer Service and Sales. I sell houses for a living, which may seem like a walk in the park, but it is helping a customer or client make what is arguably the largest transactions of their lives. Every time they wake up, cook dinner, or play in their backyard, my presence and the relationship between myself and them will come to mind. It is my job to make the transaction for this large and important item as smooth as possible. I am there to be their motivator, mother, babysitter, and champion- you may be able to sell ice to an Eskimo, but it takes a different breed entirely to sell houses!
 A typical transaction starts when new customers either want me to sell their home, or they are interested in one of my properties. I love making a grand first impression; when you leave my office you will have your own specialized buyer or seller's folders. After I give them my presentation of good and services, we decide on the relationship between themselves and I. If they already have a real estate agent or they just do not want to work with someone closely, they become my customers and I give them face-value information when they inquire. If they want a true bond, we sign an agreement for them to be my clients and I go above and beyond for them. A client receives a Market Analysis on their home, professional advice, and clients also have the protection of 10 brokers behind them. Out of irony, customers do not sign a paper to stay safe, but end up losing out on exclusive information only real estate professionals could know. I take customers and clients to houses, but only my clients will know my exact and undisclosed opinion on everything!
 After I secure and assure my client, I find houses similar to what they are looking for an send them over. If they like it on paper, we go and check the house out in person. Finding the perfect house in the perfect location with the exact features they want with none of the extra money needed is really the easiest half of the home buying process. It seems a bit hectic, and it is when you travel from place to place with seemingly no results. But once you find the right home, securing it for your customer is a whole different ball game.
 If you are in a perfect world, you submit one offer, the other agent is a breeze and you settle right away. However, in the real world, that is not the case. You may have to deal with stubborn agents, sellers, or both; and after you settle upon a price, getting the house in an agreed upon condition is also imperative. There are inspections to be looked at, utilities to test, and a search must be done on the houses's title to make sure there are no outstanding liens on the house. Sometimes another party seems honest, but they are actually a scam or just ignorant to the home buying process, so you must always keep your clients' best intentions at hand. This is no candy bar you are selling, this is something you can not just give away! Being a real estate agent is very detailed work, but one of the most rewarding jobs out there.
Overall_Why:
Of course I find my work meaningful. When I had just began my working career as a cashier at a fast food restaurant, I believed my job mattered then as well. I enjoy helping people, and making them really smile. That makes a job worth it to me. I love real estate because not only do I get that satisfaction, I get it on a much grander scale because my customers see their house every day, and remember the great time we had securing their dream home. Their sink may remind them of a time we laughed about an inspection, or their backyard may remind them of the hurdles we jumped through to keep the outside furniture. I become a part of their family, truly, because I am with a person during their most fragile state of mind. 
 What I help them with is not merely a purchase, it is an investment that is more valuable than gold or silver. It is an investment your children grow up in, and one day will own to help take care of their own children. A car purchase is important but those happen frequently. Buying a house usually happens 3 or 4 times to a person or family at most- what you buy may be the thing you stick with until you are dead! It is not a matter to take lightly. If a home is not important to you, then I guess being in real estate would not be seen as rewarding or meaningful. But if you enjoy seeing the right house in your rearview mirror on your way to work, then I am sure this process is just as meaningful to you.

Work_Story: I work as a Tax Professional. I work full-time for a small company which I really enjoy. I work anywhere from 10 to 12 hours per day but the satisfaction of helping others is worth it. I started working this job in 2001. It was not supposed to be a job at all. I did my own and every since, other people wanted me to help them do theirs so I turned my hobby into a full-time job. I personally complete 100 returns a year. I have my own clientele. I prepare and assist in preparing simple to complex tax returns for individuals or small businesses. I interview clients to obtain additional information on taxable income and deductible expenses and allowances. I interview clients to gather financial information. I use all appropriate adjustments, deductions, and credits to keep clients' taxes to a minimum. I compute taxes owed or overpaid, using adding machines or personal computers, and complete entries on forms, following tax form instructions and tax tables. I furnish taxpayers with sufficient information and advice to ensure correct tax form completion. I check data input or verify totals on forms prepared by others to detect errors in arithmetic, data entry, or procedures. I consult tax law handbooks or bulletins to determine procedures for preparation of atypical returns. I calculate form preparation fees according to return complexity and processing time required. When the work is slow I get to answer questions and provide future tax planning to the client, more or less help them to develop financial plans for clients. I correspond with customers to answer questions or resolve complaints. I also review financial records such as income statements and documentation of expenditures to determine forms needed to prepare tax returns. I get to explain federal and state tax laws to individuals and companies. This is a major part of the job because there are so much hearsay and other stuff them people believe and think. When we explain regulations, policies, or procedures this is where it gets touchy. Most people can't stand to be told the truth about their financial situation. I am the one to do all the explaining and making logic of the unseen truths. People think that they can money alot of money but don't have to show the expense of what they did with the money. Just today I had a situation where the tiebreaker rule was used. The mother and the father have equal rights to the children but the mother wants to claim the children as dependents on her federal income taxes but the father thinks that he should since he has always claimed them on his federal return. I had to explain to both clients that it is their burden of proof to prove who is to claim the children as dependents on their return. My job is very exciting, I get to meet new and exciting people all the time. I love what I do and I enjoy working with people. I wouldn't change my job for anything. I come highly recommended and my clients feel very comfortable with me when disclosing their information. I am training my daughter these last few days to work with me. It is a big adjustment for her because she is in the medical field but I would love for her to learn to do what I do.
Overall_Why: I do find my work extremely meaningful because I get to help others and also do what I love to do. I am a mathematician at heart so any job that works with numbers is my ideal job. Combine my ideal job with the love of people and I am all in. My job isn't boring or stressful, it gives me the flexibility to move around and talk a lot. On a typical day, I meet about 10 new people and sometimes I even get to hold a few babies. I was always taught to lend a helping hand. I never meet a stranger and I will never leave anybody in need. The satisfaction of know that I am able to help another person on any given day is my motivation. My job had been extremely meaningful because it has helped to make me into a better person. I am selfless, humble and more driven. At one point in life, I stumbled and I didn't see any helping hands. But God brought me through and at that point, I turned my life around I knew what it felt like to not have help from people so I make it my life goal to help where ever it is needed and to be honest and truthful with every person that I come in contact with. This job had given me that ability to help and be open with a smile. I look forward to a new year and new tax season every year.

Work_Story:
I work at {company} which is run through the FCC, where I am a team lead supervisor and also a Captioning Agent. Our center is just one of 7 centers int the USA, and we are on the Up and Up of branching out and gaining even more customers to help! Here, we work on a daily basis to provide closed captions on thousands of phone calls for the hard of hearing. Our job is all done on the computer, and in real time, but we do not actually speak with our users. Rather, we listen to one half of the conversation and use and Automated Speech Recognition (ASR) software called Dragon to provide captions for the call. We do this by repeating verbatim what is said, and then adding in extras like proper punctuation, corrections, and parentheticals for laughing, coughing, sneezing, etc. It is our job to pay close attention to detail. It works just like how you do a talk-to-text on your cell phone. Sometimes it is challenging, typically at first, but as you work here longer it is a lot easier. That, however, is just the part of the job that I do. 
 As a supervisor, I have my own team of about 20 Captioning Agents that I work with on a day to day basis. Our teams change every month or so, so it is important to keep documentation of my progress with each CA in case they are moved to someone new. I work with them and help them through various tests, given to them on a bi-weekly basis, individual meetings, team meetings, daily exercises tailored to each one's needs, and sitting and observing them as they take calls. I have been a supervisor at my job for about 6 months now, and the rest of the time I moved around in the training department. As much as I love being a supervisor, I would love to go back to training as that seems to be where you get the most interaction with new employees. 
 As a supervisor, we see the same 20-30 people everyday, but we are also able to make friends among our supervisor group of about 35. As a Captioning Agent, they sometimes only really get to know about 3 or 4 really well. All of our time, aside from breaks or lunches, is spent in our own cubicles so it makes socializing and making friends here a bit more difficult. In fact, that is typically one of the largest reasons we have people quit at my job is due to a lack of social interaction. We try our best to make things fun at work: we bring in donuts, let people watch YouTube or Netflix in between calls (but no social media -- confidentiality reasons), the managers started a "jersey thursdays" a few football seasons ago where we can wear jerseys (of any sport) to work and dress down, and we even do spirit weeks. However, it's hard maintaining a good "work spirit" or moral in the company among our lower employees. They are usually college aged and work here for the flexible schedule, so we see a lot of come and go with our employees. The ones who do stay though are very spirited and fun to be around, we have had many who have stayed and worked with us in our center for our whole five years being open.
Overall_Why: I find it meaningful, though sometimes it is hard to see the "extremely" part of my job. It is meaningful in the aspect that I help 100s, maybe 1000s, of people every single day. Where it gets hard is sometimes people do not actually want to use the phone. You can tell when a person is using our service or just using the phone like normal, and it hurts my feelings a bit to know that I am trying my best and it isn't being noticed. However, we get compliments and statements from people once a week via email and that helps to make my job worth it. There are sometimes when you get to caption a call for someone is 100% deaf, and you have truly made a world of difference in their life. But still, like I mentioned, you don't get that satisfaction all of the time. I think that my job would be much more satisfying if I was able to see the fruits of my labor. This is something that my coworkers and I discuss all the time. It is hard to really feel like we are helping someone when we can't see them, at the end of the day each voice just kind of gets lost in the sound. You can't connect as well with a voice, no matter how much you try and tell yourself you are helping. Sometimes what makes a job or an experience most meaningful is to be able to actually see someone, give someone a hug, and tell them "you're welcome."

Work_Story: My full time job deals with sales of cutlery. The brand I work for chose a different way to get their products out there other than your retail and online stores. Most of the cutlery is design with restaurants and catering services in mind, so I'm on the look out for people who are interested in starting their own business as such. Given the serious wear this businesses have on the product, they will usually reach back to an associate for future restock purchase. I attend several business opening events a month that might be local, or up to an hour drive thus far. If the client makes a purchase, I like to check up with them every few months to get feedback. One of the things I like about this job is the schedule flexibility. About half the work can be done from home, which is mostly the ordering of products. The brand also focuses on getting young people to work for them, so I get to meet some of the newer and younger associates every once in a while. We are asked to come to a weekly meeting where the recruiter is set up, but we are only required to attend once a month. These meetings help coordinate sales efforts to see who is in contact with who and not step into others territory if possible. Some of the issues I've with this job is that it required quite a bit of an investment in clothing to look well in all social functions. Another challenge of my job is that many of the social functions don't consider disabilities all that much, so some can be harder to access. I've to admit that given this I'm usually remember by prospective customers and other people looking to network and coordinate sales efforts to make 'packages' of a variety of products. My company is pretty flexible as to how we handle orders and encourage creativity in sales. Another thing I found appealing is that even when you start they offer above minimum wage and a commission on top of it. That allows for us, sales people, to be less pushy because we have an assured salary. The company claims that they get more sales with this approach than just having the associates depend on their commission. A disadvantage of this job is also the driving, my vehicle had low mileage for its year and model, but I've been making up the difference quite a bit quicker than expected. Also, the company isn't responsible of anything related your vehicle, but obviously ask you to have insurance. I went into school for a business degree that was kind of general, but my interest has always been sales and marketing. I'm grateful to have found this company early on in my job search, but I would definitely be interested in exploring the brand marketing part of the company. It may take a few years more to be able to apply to such a position given how different the job descriptions are. The company has had a bit of a challenge with my disability, but they have made some sincere efforts to accommodate as much as possible, but nothing is ever fully covered or I get odd surprises from time to time when I am out in events most of the time, they can??t control this situations.
Overall_Why: I find my work meaningful as a step to finding the career I would like to have in the long term. I don't particularly feel indebted to the company because I keep seeing the constant turnover of people. Also, my generation doesn't seem to feel the same loyalty or commitment to a specific company as much as previous generations. I still feel more connected to my university than my current company. Soon after college, I spend a good deal of time cooped up and isolated because of health issues, so just being part of the workforce means a lot to me in a way to provide for myself. I would like to find some employment or company that is about giving back. This one does some charity, but it isn't actively involved on much unless the associates do it by themselves. I guess in a sense of contributing to society not as much because of their products. I do have to say that because the company has been around for some time I get some unexpected level of respect, especially by restaurants that have been around for a while. I find it interesting that the bigger purchase tend to call you rather than you finding them because a respected restaurant suggested the brand. I also find my work meaningful because it takes me out of my comfort zone of been shy. The challenge of getting yourself out there and start conversation is something that I've come to appreciate it usefulness if not actively like doing so.

Work_Story: I work for a corporate office, in the payroll department. My job is working on weekly payroll and employee maintenance of about four thousand United States union employees. I also handle the payment of the benefits to the unions for these employees. My team handles the payroll and union benefits of over 200 different unions across the United States. We also handle Canada union and non union mechanics as well as Puerto Rico. I only handle about 66 United States unions. My normal weekly busy days are Monday and Tuesday. Monday I do payroll exceptions, where I must adjust information the timesheet in order to pay the employee properly based on the terms .determined by the union. These exceptions include adjusting their taxing information if they are traveling out of state or their home state location, which may not be where they live this is where their main branch is located, if this home state is different than the one they worked in; they would need to have a temp tax code placed on their timesheet and then that will pull into the payroll software and they will be taxed for the correct state. There are state temp taxing as well as city temp taxing. Some states have special taxes for the different cities. The state of Ohio is one that each city has it's own tax code. Other exceptions related to the time sheet are On call pay, Sick pay, shift pay, bereavement, and others. These are all determined by each of the union requirements as well as any state laws. Another exception that is processed is for those mechanics that are working in different unions during this time. These employees will get the higher paying wage depending on their classification. On Tuesday the payroll continues and we process any adjustments needed for previous weeks whether it's payroll deduction adjustments or sometimes we have revised timesheets due to an employee putting in the wrong hours or not putting in an other union code or any number of issues. Once all adjustments and other changes are processed, payroll is transmitted to the payroll company that processes the payroll checks. On Wednesday the processed payroll is pulled in and information is analyzed. Once reports are processed then we process our weekly union reports. My team has six weekly unions and the rest of them are reported monthly. My other tasks are employee maintenance. This means I handle employees from New Hire to term, this includes new hires, promotions, any increase in pay for any reason, and the annual pay increase for the union. I also handle the benefits piece of this. I have to determine what benefit package they fit in to and set them up in our system with the correct package so the union is happy. My job also includes extensive analysis of union contracts, as well as the UA National Agreement. Included with this is customer service calls with employees and unions. Our busy season begins in May to September. There is an increase in employees beginning in May. Also beginning in May is the Across the Board (ATB) season, when unions do their annual increases. These ATB's mean a change in pay as well as a change in benefits. The summer time is also busy for step progressions which is the increase of Apprentice levels for those employees. These require pay adjustments as well as benefit package adjustments.
Overall_Why: Overall I really don't find my work extremely meaningful. I am torn by this question. I mean I feel it is meaningful to the US Mechanic who is being paid correctly and his union benefits are being reported correctly. But in the big scheme of things and how most likely in a few years my job duties will be shipped off to Mexico or somewhere else that has lower pay scale. I like to think my work matters, but I don't really feel very secure in my job. My job is so stressful, we are forever short handed and can not keep people in our department. I would like to think my work is meaningful, it is hard to go to work everyday thinking that I am wasting my time. We are apart of the bigger corporate payroll and while i feel working in payroll is indeed meaningful work, I don't always personally feel like my work matters in the big picture. Does it matter to the company? Well yes, paying employees is pretty important. So I try to look at it in that way, that I am managing to pay my people, and pay their benefits correctly and efficiently. So with that argument I will say that my work is meaningful. I think sometimes job security gets tangled up in "meaningful" and they shouldn't go hand in hand. They are separate issues. No job is secure and certainly if payroll can be done somewhere cheaper, the company will eventually move as much of the work to the cheaper place that they can.

Work_Story: I work as an administrative assistant for a small publishing company. I start my job by punching in my hours. Then I will go to my supervisor's office to see if there is any work for the day. Usually, she has several assignments for me. The first is usually to call our clients and confirm ads. The second is usually to fill out the paperwork for those ads. The paperwork includes writing down the size, copying the ad to a grid sheet, and then processing the payment for the ad. This entire process takes between 10-20 minutes per ad. Usually, I have about five or six initial ads to process. After I am done with the ads, usually I move on to the most pressing matter for the day. This could be to pack several marketing packages for our subscription clients, go to another department and help with some duty, or just sit and wait for calls. Most likely it will be the latter. I sit in the office and wait for calls. Depending on nthe calls, I either transfer them to another department or try to answer the callers question. We receive a lot of calls about the advertising process. I usually have a piece of paper nearby to address certain prices. However, by now, I know the majority of the major costs (how much it costs to advertse a half page, full page, and small ad). Usually, once I've answered all the advertising questions, they will ask me to allow them to place an ad. I inform them to mail, email, or fax over their ad. Most people fax it over immediately. Then I begin working on the new ad for the day. There are several other people who also share the office with me, these are people who sell ads and other administrative assistants. When another one comes in, I usually move to the billing office. This is where I sit behind a computer and begin meticulously entering in the information of our clients. I enter them in for hours, check to make sure I have entered everything correctly, then print it out for my supervisor to check over my work. When I'm not doing these main tasks, I do several small tasks. For example, sometime, the graphic design departmment has me look over some of the ads to check for any errors that they might have missed. Another tasks, is sometimes I have to go to other offices in the building to get things signed, payments, or just get change when we're out of it. Other times, random people will come to the office to ask questions for old issues of the newspaper that most of our ads run in. So I will have to dig through our archive section to find those papers, or I will have to put in a request for even older editions of the paper. It's a very tedious job, but I enjoy the company of my co-works and boss. I spend most of my time engaging in project after project but I do a little time to just sit there. Sometimes I am allowed to listen to some music as I process billing or wait for calls. My least favorite part of my job is confirming the ads. Most of the time this goes smoothly without any difficulty, but sometimes I will encounter a hostile person, who seems annoyed by my very existence. It's a very varied outcome, since people are hard to read and know how they'll react. My favorite part of my job is billing because it's the most isolated and 1 note. I can just sit there and do it without having to interact, be social, or think.
Overall_Why: I don't consider my work very meaningful. It definitely helps me learn new skills, like process biling, dealing with customer service, reacting to problems, etc. However, it doesn't add any true meaning to my life. It's not something I want to do long term. Right now, it's just a way to get a paycheck and survive. I don't feel like my work really helps anyone in the grand scheme of things. It helps people market their business and get their nname out there. However, it doesn't really help their business as much as it helps ours. These ads help spread a little bit of awarness of the brand, but it won't make or break their company. Right now, social media is the better way to build brand loyalty, but that's also hard to perfect, because it all depends on the company's message or brand becoming viral. Creating viral content is harder than people imagine. It also doesn't really offer much fullfillment for me because my long term goal is to write for a living. This job doesn't offer the right creative outlet to expand my personal and professional growth. I enjoy the people I work with, so it helps me gain stories and people to socialize with, but it doesn't do anything of real value to my life. I am greatful for any job because it helps me stay financially stable, which helps finance artistic pursuits, like attending museums, concerts, and book openings. However, I wish that my full-time job and my passion could match.

Work_Story:
I work as a professor at a university on the east coast. My duties are primarily to teach college classes, conduct and publish academic research, and complete department and university "service". The last of these duties means that I serve on committees for hiring and promotion and tenure of my colleagues, I help shape the curriculum of my school's programs, and I attend and participate in faculty meetings. I will focus my description on the first two duties, as those are the core of what I do on a day-to-day basis. 
 
 I teach two classes per semester. I teach at a PhD-granting institution and generally teach one undergraduate and one graduate class per semester. Teaching undergraduates is a diverse experience, as the level of the course and the number of students determines the type of work y job requires. I usually teach large lecture courses (100-25 students) on introductory topics. These classes involve a lot of lecture preparation. Even though I teach the same class regularly, I must adjust the content to keep up with current events. These large classes also involve managing graduate student teaching assistants, who are charged with evaluating graded assignments. In smaller undergraduate classes on more advanced topics, there is no such TA assistance and grading is more a part of my duties. Smaller classes often involve significant amounts of preparation to find and reach the level of the students. Sometimes, I find myself doing lots of one-on-one advising of students in smaller undergraduate classes on research projects or their career plans. I often write letters of recommendation for graduate admission, internships, or paid employment, as part of my duties with respect to undergraduate students. 
 
 Graduate classes involve far less lecture prep, but far more individual advising. The graduate seminars I teach generally have 8-10 students and individual class periods are entirely discussion-based and really pretty easy to get through in terms of prep. The time-consuming part of teaching grad students is in providing feedback and direction for their individual research endeavors. This is difficult insofar as it is not a structured task and varies depending on the student. Once a student has taken a number of my graduate classes, they may ask me to consult or formally advise their dissertation. This is very time intensive and recently has taken up to 10% of my working hours and more during certain times of the year. Part and parcel of such advising is helping students navigate the academic job market, including networking advice and writing letters of recommendation. 
 
 The most difficult (and rewarding) aspect of my job is conducting and publishing academic research. Academia is a "publish or perish" world and publishing is very difficult, even when you produce excellent and robust research. Most journals I submit to publish only 5-10% of their submissions. Part of this process is also reviewing for journals as part of the peer review system. This is not a timed component of my work, but it is a norm and reviewing is taking up more of my time now that I am becoming more seasoned in my position.
Overall_Why:
My work is extremely meaningful insofar as I get to teach young people how to think about the world. I teach social science, including statistics and research methods, so I feel I have a meaningful role in shaping how they see the world and evaluate truth in it. Sometimes particular aspects of teaching don't feel meaningful - the grading process is exceedingly boring and dealing with student complaints about grades is grating. 
 
 Publishing research once felt more meaningful to me than it once did. Over time, I have come to realize that my academic discipline is very fad-reliant and certain reviewers are partisans for certain types of research and they do not have open minds about different approaches. Methodological rigor is necessary for valid social scientific research, but its valorization drives the types of work that gets published in top journals and makes finding narrower and less relevant. This, although I strive for my research to be meaningful, it is often difficult to realize, given opportunities and constraints. 
 
 Service is very meaningful, but I often get frustrated by institutional/bureaucratic rules. I won't go into any ranty detail on this. Although advising graduate students is extremely difficult and consuming, it is very rewarding. Academia is a difficult industry to get a handle on, so I see quick and real benefits to socializing graduate students to disciplinary norms and seeing them succeed. Just this past year, I had one of my former PhD students secure a tenure track job -- this is my first "placement" and it is my most meaningful accomplishment to date.

Work_Story:
I am a special education paraprofessional at an elementary school in {city}, {state}. I have been working in this field for 3 years, but this is my first year at this school. I previously worked as a special education paraprofessional at a high school in {city}, {state}. In that position, I primarily worked in the high needs room.
  My job requires me to work one on one with a kindergarten student who is autistic and has behavioral issues. I am with him the entire day, and I help him work on goals that are in his IEP (Individualized Education Plan). I keep track of the data on his progress. For example, one of his goals is to recognize and name common objects such as crayons. He also has common kindergarten goals including learning letters and numbers. 
  It is a very challenging position, and it does not pay very much for the work it requires. It is very rewarding though seeing the progress that the students make. I have a Bachelors degree in Business, but I chose to work at a school so I would work the same hours that my daughter is in school. The job requires several different skills and personality traits.
  For most para positions, you have to take a para test or have 60 college credit hours. I felt it was even more important to have my degree when I worked at the high school, because I also helped with students with moderate disabilities in harder courses like algebra. It is very important to be a very patient and flexible person in this position. A lot of the students I have worked with have had very severe behaviors including urinating on the floor, attacking staff members including hitting and biting, and throwing objects including chairs at us. You have to make sure you can respond in a calm and appropriate way, and I think it is important to teach them how they should have responded and then not hold it against them in the future. I have seen some staff members who come to expect the worse from certain students, and they don't have a positive attitude when interacting with them which leads to the student not working or having more behaviors.
  It is also important to be flexible, because they will frequently change your schedule based on the changing needs of the students. In my current position, I was supposed to be a general classroom para who works with all of the students. They didn't anticipate that this student would have such severe behaviors and that he would need a one on one para. My job was changed to work solely with him, and they had us go to a special center for a month to work on his behaviors. You also have to balance being a team player with being able to make independent decisions.
  You usually have one to three other paras who are in the same classroom as you, and there is also the teacher who lets us know what to work on with each student. You have to work well with the co workers, and you have to follow what the teacher wants even if you don't agree with them. On the other hand, you have to make swift independent decisions when you are working with students on how to get them to complete tasks when they usually have learning and behavioral difficulties, and you have to learn how independently handle emergency behavioral issues when no one else is around in the class to help.
