  It is a challenging but exciting position. You get to work great hours where you get holidays and summers off. The downside is the pay is low, and you will work with challenging students where you risk getting injured.
Overall_Why: I find it very meaningful, because I can see that I am making a direct impact in the lives of the students I work with. Besides them improving from an educational standpoint, you see them grow and mature personally. I feel that a lot of special education students are feared and misjudged by people who do not understand them or how to interact with them. I love building relationships with them and letting them know that I care for them personally, and I take an interest in them. I know that we are helping to prepare them, so they can also succeed in the future when they are out of school. Each student has unique talents, and there are meaningful positions for them when they graduate. I love knowing I am helping them find and develop their talents, so they can have a happy future. We also focus on life skills, and one of my favorite things at the high school was the annual Thanksgiving dinner we had the students make. we had them research grocery ads to find the best deals, and then they helped make the entire meal in our classroom kitchen. They made invitations for the teachers at the school, and then they helped serve them the meal. It was a huge process, but the students felt such a sense of accomplishment and ownership in the process. It amazed the teachers who came that our students had completed the entire process. This is a very meaningful and rewarding job.

Work_Story: I am employed full-time as an Export Account representative for a leading 3PL(Third-Party Logistics Provider). More specifically, I am an Export Account representative for a freight forwarding company. I coordinate export shipments for a large chemical manufacturer, that ships chemicals around the world to keep our pools and water clean. I start my day be sorting through anywhere between twenty to fifty emails from the company that I coordinate the shipments for as well as from the ocean carriers with whom I create the export bookings. I print all the new orders and revised orders that come in and then create or update the files in our computer system as needed. My goal is to request bookings from the steamship lines two-weeks prior to the date they are ready to ship from the manufacturing plant. Prior to requesting a booking, I go into a system where all the rates are stored. I review the rates and unless there is a customer specified steamship line, I go with the east expensive one. Depending on the steamship line being used, I compile a booking request via email or the steamship line's website. As the chemicals that the manufacturer ships are often hazardous in nature, I have to identify the shipments as such when I request the booking from the steamship line, that I am sending the cargo with.To do this, I attach what is called a preliminary IMO document. IMO standards for International Maritime Organization and they set the regulations for the transport of hazardous materials on the ocean. If at any time the hazardous cargo being shipped changes, I have to send a revised preliminary document. Other orders do not contain hazardous chemicals, which are easier to process. When requesting the booking from the carrier, there are several factors that come in to play. I have to find out what the cutoff is(time the container has to be returned) either to an inland rail hub or water port and make sure there is enough time for the container to get there by that time. If on the steamship line's website, I select the vessel(ship) based on the cutoff that can be made, to the destination needed. I also provide the contract number in order for the booking to be rated properly by the steamship line. If it is a hazardous shipment, I attach the preliminary IMO document. If I am sending an email, I attach the preliminary document. After the booking is created by the steamship line, there is a period of about one to two days during which the steamship line review the preliminary IMO document for approval for transit. If there are any issues with the approval, they may request additional information, such as an SDS, which is a Safety Date Sheet. A Safety Data Sheet provides specifics to the handling of the hazardous chemical in the event of a spill; how it affects marine life and other animals exposed to it. I feel so bad for the rabbits whose skin is affected within just a few minutes time! Anyway, I have provide any necessary paperwork for approval of the shipment. Now I need to enter all the booking details into two different systems so that the warehouse can coordinate with a trucking company an appointment to load the container. Throughout the day, there is constant trouble-shooting and obtaining the documents needed to ship. After the hazardous shipment is approved, the trucking is arranged. Once the container is loaded, I have to obtain and send the Final IMO document, that is signed by the load facility.
Overall_Why: No, I do not find my work extremely meaningful. It is important to get goods to destinations throughout the world, however it is not appreciated. Most people do not even know or want to know how their clothes, food, electronics get to Walmart! It is only important to the companies selling and moving their product to other parts of the world. It is very stressful sometimes to make the cutoffs and deadlines set forth by the parties that are shipping and moving the cargo. Not to mention the parameters set by the company I work for. It is very important to be able to prioritize work to adhere to all the deadlines. Ultimately, even if the deadlines are met, there is very little gratitude expressed by those setting the deadlines. Luckily, I have an inherent drive to strive to make all the deadlines set forth in my day-to-day work. I find it important to keep the cargo moving as know it is needed at whatever destination it is bound for. Sometimes the computer systems crash, making it almost impossible to meet the multitude of deadlines, but somehow I find a way to persevere. Other times, God sends some help by minimizing trouble spots and allowing more productive work to be done. It is meaningful to me to meet the deadlines that have been set as I understand that if I don't make them, the circle of product movement will be disrupted. Product movement disruption caused a bouchon or bottleneck as they say in France!

Work_Story: I work in a County Jail as a Corrections officer. Typically as an officer I would oversee between twenty and fifty inmates on a housing unit. I am responsible for the safety of the inmates and so I am required to complete a full walk around of the housing unit within every half hour to make sure everybody is ok and are following the rules of the jail. One of the first things I do at the beginning of the day is attend our staff briefing where we are informed of any changes to the jail population, policies, rules, any housing unit moves, or anything important that we should know about before we go to our assigned unit. We then conduct our formal headcount where you count all of the inmates and make sure everybody is there on your unit. After headcount is cleared, depending on what unit we are on, if the inmates are allowed out, we unlock the doors. We use direct supervision at our jail so we mingle amongst the inmates as they are free to sit out in the dayroom and play cards or watch TV. They also have the ability to use the multipurpose room for law library or even go out to the recreation yard. Usually the next thing that happens is our medical pass. The nurse comes on the unit to pass out meds so we have to lock everybody in before hand and let them out one at a time to get their meds. It is my job to make sure the inmates do not cheek their medication and actually take it. Shortly after med pass the trays usually roll for dinner, this occurs around 4:30. We usually have a few block workers that help pass trays out and it is my job to make sure everything that is supposed to be on the tray is on there and that nobody tries to take any food off of the tray in order to get more or take more than one tray. After dinner we lock everybody in so that the workers can clean the tables and floor and so I am able to count all the trays, sporks and cups to make sure everything that came in is going back out. Then after the floors are dry the inmates are allowed back out. For the next couple of hours until about 9:00 PM, besides having a twenty minute lunch break, they inmates do their thing and I hang out with them doing my thing. Some of the time I would sit at my desk and watch TV with them if there was a good movie on or a football game, but most of the time I spend answering any questions or complaints that they have, making sure they follow the rules like not being too loud, not arguing or fighting, not hanging out on the top tier, not going any cell but their own. Our jail has a lot of petty rules so there is a lot to look for throughout the day but it helps keep some of the bigger problems to a minimum. There is then another med pass then around 9:00PM, so again everybody is locked in until complete and then left back out until 10:00PM when everybody is locked back down for the night except for the block cleaners. The only thing left is to lock the cleaners in before 10:45PM before shift change at 11:00PM. Overall I guess the job could be worse, some days are alright and some are pretty horrible. It really depends on who and how many inmates your have on a unit and how bad the call offs are. There is always a lot of overtime and we are constantly getting stuck for the next shift due to the call offs. If it wasn??t for a pretty decent pay and benefits though I definitely wouldn??t still be there.
Overall_Why: No not at all, I am the type of person that likes to work with my hands, I like to work hard and help people out but that is not a good thing for working at a jail. I don't really like to be the boss and tell people what to do but thats basically what a corrections officer does. I like to go home at the end of the day physically tired and worn out from a hard days work, not just mentally drained at the end of the day like I am now. At the end of every day also, you have nothing to show for your work, there is never any finished product or anything, there is nothing to show for your work. This job is all mental and very seldom physical. I would rather be out digging a ditch or some kind of physical labor job where I have a job to do and everything is black and white. In corrections everything seems to be gray, there is never an exact way or policy to do something, everything seems to be up in the air. I also found out a long time ago while I was still a rookie that the harder you worked and the more you tried to do, the more you got in trouble for, so working hard doesn't exactly work out in a jail, the lazier you are basically the better corrections officer you make.

Work_Story: Currently, I am affiliated with my local branch of {company}. I am presently serving my apprenticeship to receive my journeyman status in my union. I have been working as an iron worker for roughly six months at present. Our local is considered to be a mixed local, meaning we cover all aspects of ironwork whereas some locals stick strictly to one part of the trade. Even with that being the case, not everyone in my local works all aspects. Some deal strictly with reinforcing structures through the use of rebar. This can include forming rebar mats, caissons, forming up walls and a number of other aspects. We also have structural iron workers, who come in after concrete is poured to begin setting up cranes and unloading fabricated pieces from trucks to be laid on dunnage. Once that is done the pieces need to be separated using shake out hooks, be labeled clearly and have their centers of mass marked as to be able to lift them safely with the crane. All rigging used for the crane has to be rated appropriately for all lifts to reduce risk of serious injury. Once the pieces are in place they are fastened temporarily by connectors, who place temporary bolts in each piece to prevent the material from rolling or falling. After pieces are in place the building must be made plumb, typically using what are referred to as chokers, plumb cable and turnbuckles. Someone comes around to bolt up the connections securely, adding bolts where they are missing and replacing the temporary bolts that were placed by the connectors. The bolts are typically tension control bolts, referred to as TC bolts. The bolts must be of the appropriate length, so that when they are tightened the spline on the end of the bolt is capable of snapping off once appropriate tension is reached. Once the bolts are installed and hand tightened appropriately someone comes around with a TC gun that grabs the bolts by the spline preventing the bolt from spinning as the nut is being tightened. Once that is accomplished the process moves on to laying down the metal decking, which can either have concrete poured over it or another material. The metal decking is supported by the steel beams and all ends must rest on a beam, or be properly supported otherwise. The deck is cut to fit around vertical columns using a torch and finally fastened down using powder actuated guns that shoot fasteners into the material. That covers a general sense of a structural job, there are also what are considered to be miscellaneous contracts which can range from stair cases to just about anything ornamental or non structural in nature. Currently the specific job I am at involves sheeting, which is somewhat comparable to the general concept of residential house siding. Essentially metal panels, either pre-insulated or not, are fastened to the exterior of the building. They can vary greatly in size and weight and are generally fabricated off site. Occasionally, it is necessary to field cut the panels when called for depending on blueprint specifications. The outer edges of windows and doors need to be flashed with various metal in order to prevent leakage and everything is sealed with synthetic butyl. I chose to focus mostly on structural, as that is what i have the most experience in at the present time in my early career.
Overall_Why: I do find my work meaningful, at least on a personal level. Ironwork is considered to be an unskilled trade technically, but it takes a great amount of care, patience and planning to perform tasks in a safe manner. I enjoy the tangibility of the work, being able to see what I have accomplished in a day, week or month. To be able to see and touch it, to drive by a year from now and be able to say I contributed in building that. Obviously it is an acquired taste, most people would not appreciate the dangers. Whether that be the heights, the heavy personal lifting, the ability for things to go wrong during a crane pick, or just general job safety. There is a lot that goes into it in order to make sure everyone leaves as they came even with current safety regulations. To be honest I find the dangers associated with the job exciting. We risk ourselves to build bridges, hospitals and just about anything you can think of on a commercial building scale. Without what we do, construction methods would be much more costly in terms of materials needed for completing these large buildings. I think the ability to provide the local, state and national communities with the necessary facilities, in addition to maintaining current ones is very valuable. While I work primarily in my home state given another two year period, I could choose to travel the country building different facilities as journeyman status allows.

Work_Story:
I work as an Eligibility Worker for a county in {state}. It is a Social Service position. I found this work by searching the internet extensively. I found the work and in order to apply i had to take a test. The test was an accuracy test to see if I could handle the work. I passed the test on my first try. I was offered employment the same week that I passed the test. I was working a menial labor job at the time of hire and I was very happy to get away from it. 
 
 The work involves helping people get their information entered into a state qualification system for benefits. The benefits are for basic needs such as food and healthcare. The people are considered clients and treated professionally. Regardless of how they act or look it is my job to set all judgement aside and help them with their needs.
 
  The clients bring me their information on income, housing, and needs. The clients submit an application, I interview them to check the accuracy of the information of what they attest on the applications. I usually have to request proof of their situation such as billing statements, pay check stubs, bank statements, utility bills, proof of assets, and proof of identity. I ask them for as much information as allowed by guidelines. 
 
 People bring me the proof of their needs and I go to work submitting the information into a database. I save copies off all proofs for auditing needs. The copies are scanned into a document database. The database can be accessed by the workers in my office. Some of the items can get filed incorrectly if the person working the scanner and software gets confused. 
 
 We all work together and research accuracy to comply with policies. My co-workers are very good people that work well together. Lately I have had extra duties added to my regular work. The duties include answering intake calls, sorting mail, and preparing application packets for clients to take for application of programs. I really do not like the added duties, I enjoy doing my regular work. I despise sorting mail because it requires knowing where things go and there are no guidelines to sorting. I am expected to know where things go even when I have no idea. 
 
 When I first started I worked forty hours per week and I was paid $17.87 per hour. Now I work 35 hours per week and I am paid $21.36 per hour. The wages are very good for the region that I live in. Most of my clients make $10.00 per hour or less. So I am very grateful for my employment. 
 
 The programs are for SNAP (Food Benefits), Healthcare, and Cash Assistance. Basic needs programs such as housing etc. I refer them to other agencies for help such as HUD or energy Assistance. It is not uncommon to have someone come in to my office with no assets, no income, and no place to stay. My work is to get them going in a positive direction. 
 
 The clients can have physical and/ or mental health conditions. They may be certified disabled or elderly. I help people survive while they are in times of crisis. Some of my clients are just not in physical or emotional shape to handle employment. Some of the clients have issues managing money. Some of the clients are homeless and some are just having problems based on low income and lack of resources. 
 
 I can become stressed when the clients do not follow through with requests or fail to inform me of a situation that I could have prevented from escalating. Many times I have had clients wait until they are evicted or lost a basic utility only to come in and seek help that isn't available because they used up funding that was available only once per month or once per year.
 
 Communication is vital to the work that I perform. Many people have barriers to communication such a speech, language, or emotional issues. I have seen clients that can only do one task at a time and asking more than one question can make them break down to a fight or flight response. I have sen clients that break all contact with my agency because they got what they wanted and take on a job. Fraud rarely happens because once the client is on the system and the employer makes a tax contribution from what was withheld from wages the state systems interface and produce tasks for me to follow up on the client. 
 
 People are very delicate when it comes to asking for help. There are social stigmas that go with asking for help in the region of the United States that I work. Confidentiality is a major part of the work. I can not discuss case work when I meet the clients in the community off duty. Fortunately I work a different county from where I reside.I leave work every day not personally knowing any of the clients outside of work and that makes me happy. The drive home listening to the radio relaxes me. I get a half hour of peace from all of life's troubles. Its the best feeling in the world.
 
 My work can be very stressful one day and very satisfying the next day. Helping people and seeing them light up with joy is the best part of my job.I guide people towards jobs or training. I help them negotiate with debt collectors, I help them find the programs to fit their needs. I make mistakes sometimes when entering data or delay processing because I am missing information. I am human and make mistakes occasionally and the clients can get very upset with me. I always admit my mistakes and do my best to remedy them.
 
 Time limits for applying for programs and performing updates timely are vital to the end result for the client. I have had clients miss deadlines and lose benefits because they did not follow through. I have had clients get delayed benefits because I missed what I was looking for. Categorizing proof of income and expenses is rather simple. But the other items I may have to tend to can be confusing at times. Medical opinions and disclosures that are handwritten can be very difficult to decipher. 
 
 The information that I have access to is very personal. I have to change passwords on my work computer regularly. I keep my computer locked down when I am not in the vicinity of it. The office is secure, but we can have maintenance personnel come through. We sometimes have clients in the office area when space to do an interview is limited. Clients are sometimes an open book to personal information and some are very particular about it. Its not uncommon to get proofs from a client that have been blacked out.
 
  I have had a lot of training to do my work. I am constantly sent to training and webinars. I am sent hundreds of miles to stay in a hotel for a week away from family and friends. I enjoy the getaways sometimes, other times I see them as a major disruption. Especially as of late as I have family with health and wellness issues. 
 
 The duties of my work can change considerably based on policy changes. Our regulations and policies change as legislation and laws are changed. Federal legislation plays a major role in State legislation guidelines for the programs and clients I help. 
 
 I am very lucky to have the position that I do. It has public employees retirement and a great salary. Health Insurance is considerably better than any work I have ever had. I am making the most money I have ever made in my entire life. The rewards of my work outweigh the perils. People are good and I enjoy helping guide them to the programs that help them to live life.
Overall_Why:
I find my work to be almost extremely meaningful. The reason for this rating is because I have basic policies and paperwork that I have to decipher that are ever-changing. Helping people is a very meaningful part of my work. Seeing people that are suffering because they have no means to survive and then I step in to help with a positive outcome is worth more than anything to me. 
 
 Making people cheer up from a gloom state is a very good feeling. This type of work is what that is all about. Seeing children taken care of because I did stepped up and did something to help. Seeing disabled people treated fair and in a dignified manner is rewarding as well. There is so much bad in this world and to see your work produce good is the best.
 
 Not everyone sees my work as a good work. There are those that feel workers like myself are bureaucrats. I don't see it that way because in the course of my duties I find myself with empathy for my clients. I understand what suffering is like and not fitting in to society. I have felt that this work is the most meaningful I have done in the course of my entire life. 
 
 At the end of the day I can look in the mirror. I know I did good for people and that is what meaningful work is. Meaningful work isn't about money or power, it's doing what's right.

Work_Story:
In my twenty years of being in the workforce, my current position as a travel consultant for corporate travelers has to be the most challenging one of them all. My main duties are to provide domestic and international assistance to corporate travelers using a variety of interactions including but not limited to chat, email, inbound and outbound phone calls. These duties require the ability to multitask on multiple system platforms including client specific platforms. One of the program used which is standard across the travel industry is Sabre. Sabre is a global distribution system which is used for travel agents to book air, hotel, car rental, cruises as well as train tickets. The training was quite extensive as it is done through a mainframe system that uses scripts and commands. Although we do have the ability to use a web-based reservation tool, most of the times I'm left with no other option but to resort to using Sabre to finish. The training for just the domestic travel was about 3 months and on-going training to handle international and Canada travel takes about a year just to grasp the basics. 
 
 It can be very difficult providing excellent customer service when you are under time constraints as well as deal with demanding corporate clients who somehow feel empowered by the positions that they hold. It is a very important part of my job to make sure that the traveler gets to and from their destination to handle their business meetings and negotiations. Most of the times things go as planned. However, there are many encounters that are beyond our control that can make it frustrating for myself and the client.
 
 I am also responsible for working with other vendors such as airlines and hotel to ensure that the reservations are secure or find alternatives when flights are canceled or delayed or if the hotel is oversold. I find the most difficult calls are when a corporate traveler is stranded at the airport or at the hotel who is denying their reservation and the city is sold out. It can be very hard to find suitable alternatives that will coincide with their business needs. 
 
 The one thing that makes this all bearable is that I am able to work from home which helps maintain a calm and peaceful atmosphere which makes it easier to handle challenging calls. I really love the position and the pay is also a nice boost to the spirits. However, it can be difficult to meet such high expectation of the client especially when it comes to the average handle time to take the calls. The set goals expected to meet can be unrealistic. For instance, I am expected to handle the calls within 1020 seconds or less which is roughly 17 minutes which given the nature of the calls can be unrealistic especially if a travel is a multi-destination international or if it requires assistance from the vendor. There have been calls that have been more than 2 hours and sometimes this is just the hold time waiting to be assisted in handling the call. Nevertheless, this is one goal that I am determined to meet.
Overall_Why: I do not find it extremely meaningful because it is not an industry that I am passionate about. I accepted the position because of the company that I work for which has proven to be a reliable source of employment for me for the past 10 years. The most meaningful aspect of my job would be the customer service aspect. I am very passionate about providing excellent customer service no matter what industry I am working in or the type of customers I serve. This position specifically caters to major corporations and their executives who expect White-Glove treatment. This is no issue for me because I believe that type of service should be given to all customers no matter what position you hold. While it may not be extremely meaningful because I do desire to fulfill my purpose which I do not believe it is a travel agent, I find it to be most meaningful given the work/life flexibility and pay that I am blessed to be able to work at home and be present for my family. I would only be able to describe it as extremely meaningful if it fully satisfied my purpose in life. Also, I have had to encounter the stress that comes with the challenges this position has which have no immediate resolution. I have to be very careful to not take on the work stress personally which may change the atmosphere in my home. This can be quite tricky when you work at home.

Work_Story:
I work in a large phone center as an 'insurance processor' which is really a customer service representative, but we don't call our department that because there's a separate (and more highly trained) customer service department. The department I work for, answers calls for a large nationwide disability insurance company. Mostly what I do is help clients start new claims, or add them to our system, and occasionally help them change some minor thing in their claim, but more complicated stuff related to claims is out of my league. In addition to that, I will initiate faxes or emails to get claim forms to the clients, or their doctors, or bosses, or whoever needs them.
 
 Each day I have to get up at about 6 am, because my job is pretty far away, all the way by the airport, and I don't have a car and have to take the bus, two buses actually, which take forever. I can't use my bike to speed things up either because sometimes there's no room on the bike rack and I'd risk losing it or missing the bus. Anyway, I get out to the bus stop early, catch a bus down town, and then transfer to a bus to the airport, the whole thing takes about two hours, and it's a tremendous waste of time, since I can't do anything productive on the bus, I can't read or else I get car sick. I hate it, and I hate it even more coming back.
 
 Despite my unhappiness with the commute, I've worked at the same place for almost 6 years, mostly because I can't find anything better. I was awkward on the phones at first, I'm not really naturally suited to this type of job, but at some point I put two and two together and started making my calls much shorter so that I'd get a better 'handle time', and as it turned out, if I hadn't done that, I'd probably have gotten laid off in the first few months working there. My manager took me aside and told me to keep doing what I was doing, and I did, and things got smoother and smoother, and I started getting paid a bonus of a dollar or two a month based on my performance. 
 
 However, over a year ago that manager got replaced with a girl who I used to work with, who got promoted, and she nit picks my calls for quality, and my performance reviews say my performance is 'unsatisfactory' so I don't get a bonus anymore, and the only way to change that is to keep every unnecessary line of our 'script' intact in my calls, which the clients hate and makes them very slow and awkward, and then I still don't get a bonus because now I'm slow. Despite my 'bad' evaluations, I've never been disciplined or threatened with dismissal, because the higher up bosses know I'm actually a competent employee, and they don't want to lose me. However, they don't value me enough to see I get paid a bonus that I'm missing out on because my supervisor is a bitch with a grudge against me. She's more important than I am, so I'm kind of stuck with things as they stand.
 
 Other than her, I get along okay with most of the people at work. I don't really like them or have much in common with them, we don't socialize, but we are friendly enough on the job.
Overall_Why:
It's only meaningful in the sense that it keeps a roof over my head and enables me to pay bills and buy other things that I need to survive. I don't like the way the company does business, we lie to clients all the time, and I don't think that the way they treat employees accords with my ideas about fairness and justice. And, even though I hate the way they treat clients, I don't really relate to the clients either. Most of them have very good jobs and decent lives that I can only envy, after all, their bosses care enough about them to give them disability insurance, though if they knew what the company we represent actually *does* for their workers, they might think twice about that. 
 
 It's not something I ever considered doing with my life, it's not something I'm good at. I don't like talking to people all the time, basically, and I'm not a good liar. I also hate sitting at a desk all day in front of a computer, I'd prefer a job where I could get up and move a bit more, even teaching was better. I don't have that much social contact there, but I'm around people all the time, and they're people I don't like. On top of that, it doesn't really do a good job of paying the bills, I'm not getting ahead. If I have to have a job that I don't like, I'd prefer it at least pay pretty well.
 
 It's meaningless work, the disability insurance company doesn't really do anything, they are parasites. And we are parasites, on a parasite. This is probably true of most jobs in the US, though :(

Work_Story:
I provide training, information, and assistance to tutors to meet the requirement for basic level tutor certification, prepare hiring packets for qualified tutors and peer mentors for quantitative, writing, and supplemental instruction, serve as tutor administrator for our early alert system, hire and manage tutor center monitors, serve as timesheet approver for student workers, train all student and non-student employees to navigate the early alert system through the submission of session reports, scheduling appointments, tracking, and monitoring student visits. I also assist in marketing and advertising tutoring events to the campus community as well as plan and coordinate end of year recognition service for tutors and student workers.
 I have taken on an added responsibility of facilitating a first year seminar course entitled, educational equity. This course is designed to build social justice awareness for first year students. In this role, I work with the students to build critical thinking skills, writing skills, research skills, and time management skills. The students will create a signature project around educational equity. My responsibility is to assist with creating the environment from which they will create the project. I begin by brainstorming solutions to the education equity problem in this country. I have designed a syllabus for the students to access in the learning management system. The syllabus includes readings, activities, and assessments for the students for the entire semester. This class meets every week for one hour and 15 minutes. We discuss the assignments due for each week. We also discuss any barriers that occur in which the student finds difficult to complete the assignments. I also take roll and update the LMS for each day??s activities. 
 I also work with students who need assistance with finding resources in the area. I assist with providing laptops, computers, copying, and scanning. 
 Additionally, I advise students in the application process for both admissions to the university and obtaining their Veterans Administration education benefits, monitor the veteran students progression toward academic goals and degree requirements, maintained contact with veteran students to ensure continuity of coursework and successful transition from military service to collegiate academics, serve as campus advisor for Military Veteran Student Organization, served as ex-officio officer of Military Support Advisory Committee, assist students with peer mentoring groups, coordinate activities with academic advisors to ensure veteran students are on track for timely graduation, assist with the evaluation and review of academic transcripts, developed workshops, seminars, and other activities designed to engage students and faculty of special needs requirements for military and veteran students. I also train staff on current and changing requirements for military and veteran students. I provide scholarship information and resources to assist students with college expenses, participate in campus orientations and open house events designed for military and veteran students as well as nontraditional adult students, maintain a working knowledge of post 911-/GI Bill and other veterans benefits/regulations to assist students with questions or concerns. In this roll I also provide assistance in off-campus events such as participating in education fairs and information sessions.
Overall_Why:
My work is extremely meaningful because I am adding to the value of the workforce in this community and the country. I am honored to be able to influence the minds of every student who enters to learn and departs to serve. I am a respected staff member in all areas of academic life. I believe in and practice innovative and engaging methods of developing students?? intellectual and professional skills. I practice team-based learning in all aspects of my work. In the area of student advising, I am one who is considered both highly knowledgeable and conscientious and to whom the hard cases are referred. I am honest, student-focused, and require students to demonstrate professionalism, integrity, good work ethics and responsibility for their actions at all times. 
 In my quest to find a personal teaching philosophy that I could use to inspire and engage traditional and non traditional learners as well as encourage their active participation in the learning process, several characteristics came to mind. First of all, I believe that good teaching practices transcend delivery systems. Secondly, a good instructor will have traits of dependability, clarity of policy, preparedness, subject matter expertise, a positive attitude, and most of all concern for the learner??s welfare. Simply reading text on a screen can hardly be considered active learning nor does it provide opportunities to practice or share the new knowledge with peers. 
 I take pride in the service I provide to my students, the faculty, the administrators, and the constituencies involved with the university.

Work_Story: My work is a full time job. I am team lead in my office. The work for me starts early in the morning by eight to night eight. There are files which I have to go through such that there are no errors. The error might occur in many ways like in the code or the system and even sometimes in the network. The thing which I can solve is the code or the system, but the network problem is handled by the network team. The work process starts like we have to come up with new ideas of the daily needs of the people. Discuss it in a group of people, crunch some numbers, looking all the algorithms, the flow chart , the designs and the basic problems which may occur. I have to go through all the possibilities of problems that can occur in developing a website or an app. I have to think along with my colleagues in what possible way does an user think to access this product. So, in developing a product I have to come across the design, the graphics, the style and segmentation of how the product should look like and mainly its background access. I prepare a slide show on these possible effects of the products towards the people and present it to the chief head of the company, if the chief agrees the work starts. After all the possible check outs I grantee my colleagues that the product can be developed. So I split up my team in four groups like one for the design, other for coding , one for graphics and the last for networking. Overall_Why: Yes of course I find my work to be extremely meaningful. The work I do in the office is meaningful because I am trying to develop technology. Work_Story: I am a medical assistant at at medium sized orthopedic treatment and surgery center. I assist the doctors and different types of techs that work there with all types of tasks. I guess you could say I am a Jack of All Trades. My job duties vary on a day to day basis depending on who I work with and what department I am working in. Sometimes I sit in the front of the building to answer phone calls and direct patients when they come in the door. I answer their questions and tell them where they need to go, who they need to speak to, or assist them in any way I can by getting wheelchairs or by helping them navigate to where they need to be in the building. When I work for one of the surgeons, I usually assist them in dealing with the post op or after care part of their care. I can check a patient in, take them back to the exam room, take their vitals, and talk to them about what they are experiencing and document those symptoms in the chart before the doctor even gets into the room. If needed, I draw up any injections they might need and write out any prescriptions they will need so the doctor only needs to sign them. When the patient is ready to leave, I give them all their needed paperwork and instructions and schedule any follow up visits they might need. Sometimes there are minor surgical procedures performed in the exam rooms as well as a variety of diagnostic tests. I assist the doctor and nurses with anything they need during these procedures. Anything form fetching and holding any type of tools or equipment that might be needed, to even just holding a light steady to assist the doctor in seeing the proper area being worked on. I can remove stitches and staples as part of the post op experience as well. Sometimes I assist the specialist that makes casts and durable medical equipment. This includes casting, air boots, special surgical shoes and all other types of supplies used to treat orthopedic issues. I have even assisted this individual with diabetic wound care which is done right in the exam room under sterile procedures. We ensure the areas are properly debrided and cleaned before wrapping up the area and instructing the patient on how to take care of this at home. Patients do not always listen or do what they are supposed to which can be very frustrating in this field. We also have an outpatient surgery center, but I don't do much over there except occasionally fetch different types of tools needed for some of the in office surgeries. There is also a physical therapy department where I work, but I don't typically do anything over there except occasionally escort a patient to that area for an appointment or show them where it is for any scheduling they might need. That area is typically staffed with Therapists and therapy assistants. These are just some of the things I do. My job varies greatly day to day depending on what area I am working in, and I really like that about my job. Once a week we have meetings where everyone in the department you are working in have a sit down and talk about any tasks, patients, areas that need improving and any expectations presented to us for that week. Once a month there is a company wide staff meeting that I usually don't have to attend. My direct supervisor usually attends that and reports back to the staff what was transcribed in the meeting.
Overall_Why: Yes, I find my job incredibly meaningful. Healthcare is very important and I feel a real sense of satisfaction helping people feel better or get things that are causing them pain to resolve. There are frustrations to every job, like non compliant patients, but when you get to help someone get rid of pain or issues they are experiencing, it can be very satisfying and meaningful. People need to get around and function and I feel a deep sense of satisfaction at helping people do this. Pain can be debilitating and helping people at least get on the road to being pain free or at least reducing their pain brings warm feelings to me. I have to deal with some jerks from time to time, but if they get the help they need, it is still a very satisfying feeling. Some people are very much appreciative of the relief we can provide, while others don't seem to get enough relief due to the fact that they are non-compliant. Non-compliant means that they just flat out do not do the things they are told to do. When getting someone to see that their level of pain relief can be reduced if they just do the simple things the doctor says (PT stretches, taking medication properly, not over using medication, and such), that is a very satisfying feeling to watch the light bulb go off in their head and realize they finally get it. That in itself makes my work very meaningful.

Work_Story: According to Biscom, over 25 percent of employees steal proprietary data when departing a company or organization. To that end, my experience shows that departing employees have a sense of ownership over the data that they copy. Intellectual property commonly stolen includes customer lists, source code, strategy documents and other trade secrets. The information is often used against the organization when the former employee goes to work for a competitor or decides to start a new company. As a Cyber Security Analyst and Computer Forensics Officer, its my job to mitigate these risks by ensuring the integrity of all data, intellectual property, and trade secrets. The first step in a theft of data investigation is to forensically preserve the data on the employee's devices (phone, tablet, computer, etc.). I will then create chain of custody documentation, photograph the hardware, and verify the integrity of the preserved data, among other things. These steps ensure that the electronic evidence will be admissible in court. Once the data is preserved, the next step in the investigation is to perform an analysis to identify software and artifacts that may be indicative of data theft. These areas on a typical Windows installation include, USB activity, file versioning, cloud account activity, personal storage or email account activity, browsing history, and print spool history. If this was done on a *Nix based machine the procedure can change, however, the core ideas remain the same. This is a time sensitive process and needs to be accomplished on strict deadlines. Unfortunately for me, that means spending many extra hours at the office for a few days to complete my investigation. Due to the low occurrence of breaches on a per company basis, most don't hire full-time Computer Forensics Officers or experts; instead many look to third parties to provide their expertise. This of course means, my job can be quite dynamic and span a wide variety of sectors. Beyond data forensics, my days are typically spent doing assessments on security and data policies for various clients. This includes penetration testing, reviewing employee behavior via fake spam emails, leaving random fake "infected" USB flash drives around, or mailing fake espionage offers to C-level employees. Essentially, we're trying to test the actors and the companies polices to ensure their trade secrets are safe. Sometimes this will mean doing physical testing or hardware, such as cameras, thermal detectors, safety equipment like smoke detectors, or even sprinkler systems. I'm trying to find unconventional ways inside a companies network, while also testing to make sure their infrastructure is secure. After all testing has ceased we will generate a report with our findings. This usually includes video evidence, system logs, various system scans, and my recommendations. Afterwards, I'm requested to implement new policies if any system checks failed, and this usually means presenting to management or a board. These audits can last a fews days up to 6 months, so again, it's a very dynamic position.
Overall_Why: I'm going to do something a bit different here; I'm going to talk about my most rewarding job first. My first year in this job was fairly hectic. I was quite new and still rough around the edges. After working for 9 months I received a job to test a small power co-op. This co-op was owned by ~5,000 citizens of a small town about an hour away. They were upset with their current electricity company switching back to coal fire plants, that they decided to start their own co-op. I believe it was a mixture of solar and natural gas, and was much cheaper to run that coal, lower their cost significantly. I was invited to do a security audit and found many holes in their policies and practices. Upon inspecting their physical infrastructure, I noticed several potential issues, including some questionable networking appliances. I began outlining a plan of action to get the small co-op up to compliance standards and to ensure complete integrity of their data and physical network. I worked many hours off the clock and made some life long friends out of the ordeal. In the end, I moved to that community because of the work I had done. I felt so invested in their goal of a sustainable power company that I changed my living situation. Yes, I think the work I do is meaningful. This was the first job to show me how my work can make an impact on people's lives.

Work_Story: MY work is very important to the community in which I work in. I help individuals and families with financial means to stop their utilities from being disconnected or being evicted from their homes. I also help individuals provide food for their families. A person will come in to my office and we will review their current incomes along with their bills and we work on ways to cut things out of their lives that they can live without. Reviewing these things can shed light on what they need to do to make it possible to keep their utilities on or keep their family in their home. This also brings to light if their current income can financially sustain them in what needs to be paid to keep their four walls protected. Listing out the funds they have available and then listing their expenses going out really sheds a light on what sacrifices they will have to make to keep things working in their lives. Most importantly bring awareness to the cuts that can be made even the miniscule cuts can help them make it through the month to pay their bills. The important things are brought to the persons ability to understand what has to be done to solve their crisis. I spend the time with the client so that they can feel good about making little changes taking baby steps to financial security. I also work with the client on how the help that they receive is a stepping stone to a more important goal that they need to reach on a bi-weekly basis. The review of their expenses is eye opening to most of the individuals that I help. I'm able to help them with utilities and rent which in turn allows them to make better choices than they have made in the past. The assistance that I provide makes a person feel good and that there is hope that they can take care of their families as needed. I spend anywhere from 30 minutes to and hour focusing on them being able to reach their goals. A person needs a hand up to open their eyes that their goals are possible to reach. The ability to have positive hope in their minds is key to being successful. The amount of time that is truly focused on their income and expense is so beneficial in the long run. They see that the smallest changes can make a difference and makes them feel that they can make ends meet. The financial assistance that I provide also make me feel that I am truly able to make a difference in our community. Every small change is a success in feeling better about themselves and also gives them hope of a better future. Having a minimum wage job is not a curse when they look at all the positive changes that can be made. Success is only a little step away and the financial assistance that I provide for them helps them make their goal possible. If they need a little help with their utility bills that can make the difference I can make it possible so that they can use their income for other things to get caught up and make sure that they can have a positive outcome for months to come. They will return bi-weekly to follow up on how things are going and they will continue with this for 12 months. In 12 months I have had a success rate of 97%.
Overall_Why: My work is extremely meaningful and important to our community. The work I do and the services that I provide for individuals not only help them in the moment but helps them in the future choices that they make. Making positive choices is extremely important and meaningful for each individual that I help. Giving them the hope that their is a better situation for them in the future is extremely meaningful to me and make me feel that I have accomplished a great service. A service that I feel is extremely important in my career and in my life. The accomplishments that these individuals make within a twelve month period is the most satisfying feeling that anyone could have. The thought that I actually helped these people make important, positive and help changes just makes the community a better place to live. When a person feels better about themselves that are encouraged to make better choices so they can stop living paycheck to paycheck. This to me is such a gratifying feeling and makes me feel that I have truly done a good job. The work I do makes the person feel that their changes are positive and it gives them better self esteem about themselves that they are helping their families strive for a better life. The goals that these people make is such a positive goal in each persons life. My heart is full of joy when I see them make the positive changes and make their goals happen.

Work_Story: I work as a Mechanical Engineer at a manufacturing plant in rural {state}, about 40 miles East of {city}. My degree is in Electro Mechanical Engineering Technology, which I acquired from Penn State University in 2013. This is my third full time job in my professional career, and I have been in my current position for 2 years and 9 months. I wear many hats in my every day work. One of which is that of the Maintenance Supervisor. I am the leader of a team of 4 maintenance technicians. Their names are {name1}, electrical technician, {name2}, mechanical technician, {name3}, drill technician, and {name4}, electrical technician. I work for a company called Specialty Bar Products, and we are owned by Nelson Fastener Systems, who is owned by Doncasters, a company based in the UK but financed by Saudi investors. I work with a variety of different machines, such as computer numeric controlled (CNC) lathes and mills, deep hole drilling machines, surface grinders, long bar and pin grinders, broaching machines, cutoff machines and saws, reaming machines, button rifling machines, forging machines, induction hardening systems, heat treating furnaces, induction heating furnaces, various cooling systems, magnetic particle inspection machines and other optical comparators. Many of the machines are over 25 years old so much of my teams time is spent putting out fires so to say. I have created a preventive maintenance system at our plant and implemented it a couple years ago, with the help of the IT department. A system for tracking work orders and PM schedules was developed, and it has been running smoothly ever since. I also wear the hat of Project Manager, CNC programmer, PLC programmer, Mechanical Engineer (creating fabrication drawings and assemblies in 2d and 3d computer aided design software), and overall problem solver. I make about $71,250 per year in base salary plus quarterly bonuses (usually about $200 to $400 each) and health and dental insurance. I also have a 401K with company matching my contribution up to 3.5%. My health insurance is garbage, as I have a $3500 yearly individual deductible. The company tries to offset this by giving us a Health Savings Account with $750 per year to go toward medical expenses. My boss is the Director of Operations, and he reports to the head honcho at Nelson Fastener Systems. I do not think I have ever met that guy in my tenure so it would be safe to say he is not too involved in our day to day business. Other responsibilities at my current role include creating Capital Expense Appropriation Requests, formal documentation that justifies spending company money on a capital project, and ordering replacement parts for machines. I have done many different tasks such as electrical drawings and design, cooling system design and piping drawings and design. Some of the new equipment that I purchased and installed was a free standing 5-ton overhead crane system, an automatic bar cutoff machine, a dual spindle automatic button rifling machine (for rifling the barrel of sporting arms).
Overall_Why: In some respects, I would say that my current job has meaningful work behind it. I do keep machines and people running to create a product that is sold to big customers. We help many different companies in the gun, oil and gas, aerospace, and transportation industries achieve their goals by provide quality products. My work is meaningful because I support about 50 other people in completing their work by setting up work procedures and providing technical support and problem solving ability. My work is also meaningful because every day I gain more experience in different areas of the plant. Also, in some respects my job is not meaningful, because I do not really have a passion for the mindless tasks that sometimes consume my day to day schedule. I would prefer to be challenged so that my mind stays sharp and I am constantly learning new things, and if that is not happening I get extremely bored with myself. My work is meaningful because I keep machines running, and I manage projects that cost a lot of money. I have been in charge of a 2 million dollar request for construction proposals, a $500k roofing project, and pretty much single handedly moved an entire plant with 60 machines about 200 miles down the road to another facility and resumed production. I have a lot of pride in the effort that I put forward, and that is meaningful to my self esteem and resume to be honest. My job provides a source of income for my family as well.

Work_Story:
I work full-time as an Android software developer as part of a team with two other developers and a manager. It's a larger company with lots of other teams like marketing, HR, research, etc. After I get to work around 9am, my team starts by having a short stand-up meeting where we discuss what we accomplish the day before and what we will be doing that day. Then we all get to work fixing bugs, developing new parts to our application, or having meetings with contract developers from other parts of the world.
 
 I enjoy my job very much. While every day has a new set of problems, it is rewarding to be able to fix something and know that I am contributing to a much larger company goal. Somedays I might try out many different ideas on how to solve a problem and not find a solution. But like Thomas Edison, I don't see these as failures, but as figuring out ways that don't work.
 
 While most of the work is about problem solving, there are also tasks that allow you to use your artistic side. You get decide what a program or app is going to look like. It is rewarding to come up with an elegant and easy to use user interface. It is also especially rewarding to get to see end users appreciating the program that you developed. It is also fun to find ways that you can make others job easier.
 
 Probably the worst part about my job is that I am sitting for almost an entire eight hour period. But most days the time goes by fast. Some times, a co-worker or two and I will go for a walk in the adjacent warehouse. Or we might take a break and play a game of ping-pong. But both of those happen less often then I would like. One person in my office has a convertible desk that can be raised and used standing up. I asked around and found out that he got his doctor to recommend it. I'm going to try to do the same thing because I think I am getting more and more out of shape as a result of sitting so much each day.
 
 One nice thing about being a software developer is that it is a skill that can be used to solve lots of different types of problems. Over the years, I've created two side businesses that bring in additional income. Having these other businesses allows me to not worry as much as my co-workers about job security since I know I'd have money coming in even if the company I work for had to let people go. But even if they did, there are usually lots of other companies needing to hire software developers, especially if you are open to relocating.
 
 Even though I work in a room with no windows with other people who would prefer to keep the lights off and work in darkness, at the end of the day I am able to look back and see what I have accomplished. And when I go home, I am able to not worry about anything that is happening at work. When I wake up the next day, I am looking forward to putting in another day of work.
 
 One of the hardest parts about being a software developer is that you are continually needing to learn new skills. Since technology keeps advancing, new programming languages are developed, and new types of hardware need software written for them, you'll need to keep up with the times. Luckily, with the internet, it is usually easy to find answers by searching and finding other people that are dealing with the same problem.
Overall_Why: While I enjoy what I do very much, I have often wanted to be a teacher instead. When I was in high school, my older sister who was a teacher talked me out of going in to that career. A large part was that she felt that since I was smart and enjoyed computers that I should go into computer programming. So that's what I did. It has been great in that it pays very well. But when I read about teachers of the year and the happiness that they get out of helping so many students I sometimes feel like maybe I'm missing out. As a computer programmer, it is rare to see the people using the products that you've developed. Although you might write an application that thousands of people may use you usually don't hear back from them. I think as a teacher I would appreciate getting to see the difference that I could make it students lives. I am hoping that some day soon, I could retire from my main job and teach computer classes at a local community college or high school. But in addition to teaching the skills needed for being a computer programmer, I'd be able to share my life experiences and help students find their path to having a career that they find meaningful. In watching my own kids grow up, I know that many students are having a hard time deciding on what career they want to go into. But there are so many opportunities available today that I would like to be able to encourage students to not be scared of trying something new.

Work_Story:
I work as an office manager for a small, 6 person, medical practice. The medical practice has a variety of provider services ranging from psychology to nutrition. Each provider has there own room in which they rent out from the two owners of the building. The owners get a percentage of what they make or they can choose to pay a set fee. It all depends on the amount of money they make and how comfortable they feel about either option. They can also choose to share a room with another provider in order to split the costs of renting the room.
 In my daily routine, I get to work at 8:00 AM and start cleaning and organizing the office area and waiting room. I restock all products in the coffee bar and check the bathrooms for supplies and cleanliness. The first clients usually come in around 8:30 to 9:00 AM. They come and check-in with me and fill out any paperwork that is needed. I then let the client's provider that they are here so they can start the appointment. Between clients walking in and getting them checked in, I answer the phone to make new appoints, call insurance companies to ensure that they are paying the proper amounts and clients are covered. I take copays from customers whose insurance does not cover the full amount, or any of the amount. We allow payment plans for clients who cannot get help by insurance or cannot pay out of pocket right away. 
 The medical practice is a Christian based organization. Many of the providers have a large focus on faith and use it to help their clients. This is why we work well with all people in need. 
 I am given an hour and a half for lunch. Many times lunch is catered by organizations wanting the providers to use their products or services. Other times I leave and grab lunch with my other coworkers. 
 When returning from work I continue my daily tasks. Constantly checking on the waiting area and coffee bar. We have a large amount of kids that come in so it can get messy during the day. Actually this practice has a great focus on the family. 
 Towards the end of the day I work on human resource and payroll tasks. I conduct all the interviewing and hiring processes of our other workers. I ensure that all payroll is up to date and correct. I am continually checking for any updates to information for workers. I also handle complaints made by workers and clients. 
 After checking out the last client, I start to clean up, once again. Wiping down the coffee bar and cleaning up the waiting area. I file all paperwork into the correct cabinets and restock my office area. Finally, I call all clients that have appointments the next day to remind them what time and who they are with. Sometimes providers have late clients but my day ends at 6:00 PM, and they handle all tasks on their own after that time. I shut down and lock the computer. Count and deposit any cash from copays, every week. I say goodbye to the remaining coworkers who are still there and clock out until the next day. If I am the last one out, I turn off the lights and set the alarm.
Overall_Why:
Yes, I believe my work is meaningful. I make sure that the medical practice runs smoothly and that all clients feel welcome at our office. It can be nerve wracking to go to a "doctors" office, therefore making clients feel warm and welcomed is a top priority. It makes the office a positive environment which can easily be turned negative if people are not feeling like they want to be there. 
 Also, I make sure that important paper work is done correctly so that patients are not having to pay money if they are not supposed to. Calling insurance companies to ensure everything is being processed correctly and all information is given to them so that things function efficiently. I help manage events in the community and work with schools to help sponsor local athletes and teams. This is just another way to get this medical practice out there for people to see where we are and who we are. I hope that this brings in more clients who need help, because we can help them. Even the little things that I do are meaningful to my work, making sure that the coffee bar area is clean and restocked so clients can come and grab some warm coffee or water. Cleaning the waiting area throughout the day to keep it clean and organized so clients know that we are a clean and organized business. Also restocking the bathroom areas because the last thing you want is a dirty bathroom and no paper towels.

Work_Story:
I work for a local goverment Parks, Recreation and Tourism department. I have worked for them on and off for almost 8 years now, but have worked full time for two and a half years at this point. My current job description is Account Technician III - I am technically under Fiance, though that is not really all I do. While I only hae one "boss", the accountant, I also work closely with/for the Chief park Ranger and the Parks Manage. I mostly work out of one office, it is where campground reservations, boat rentals, snack sales and information are found. I have a variety of tasks when I am working in that office. I do regularly have part time staff to supervise and let run the front counter, however, they often need assistance when I am around with answering questions and fixing problems. I also spend a lot of my time keeping them busy, especially in the winter when we fare not busy with customers. With them I keep the store stocked with fishing supplies, snacks and rinks, and keep it looking tidy. In my own little office I am who reconciles money, runs reports, and does a bit of budgeting and analysis. I work closely with the Parks Manager in researching and processing purchases for all parks in our area. That includes contacting bids, dealing with contrats and coding purchases correctly. I also do a lot of contacting or patrons for him dealing with complaints and refunds. Working with the Chief Ranger I also help in obtaining purchases for the rangers, and we deal with day to day operations within the main park and all other parks. I help manage and keep the rangers on track when the Chief is not around. Together we mange and hire all of the part time staff that we use. We make decisions about the campground and store operations in different types of weather and emergency situations. I also help assist with park programming, summer camp and vounteer projects which go on year round in our parks. When I travel to the admin office to help my boss, I do clerical tasks like input orders and receipt that we have received them. I also will help file and organize documents and archive them when we no longer need them in our office. I've also been learning some other interesting account things, like more about the financial program we use and the chain of command above us for purchases, especially expensive ones. There are also other odd tasks I may do for special events, like working booths, checking tickets and handing out snacks. Every year I help with a Halloween event, and I've done everything including acting in it. On any given day I might need to go move a snake, clean a paddleboat, fix a bicycle, or help a person find their dog. I've assisted in rescuing an owl, I've helped plant a butterfly garden There is a huge amount of variety in my job, and that is a reason why I love it.

Overall_Why: I find it fairly meaningful. I get to help the community with their recreation needs. I also get to help the environment a bit and keep a piece of nature in our community. Our park offers things like an urban trout stocking program so more people can get out and fish. That is a who draw to our senior citizens a lot of the time, and we have a place they can come and socialize with other fishermen or even just us on a daily basis. That is pretty fulfilling. However, our budget is very tight, and parks and recreation always gets the short end of the stick. I don't feel like I can help citizens and parks as much as I want to, because there is red tape and roadblocks everywhere I turn. I get very frustrated and often want to give up and maybe go to private industry, so I could get more done and feel like I've done something more days of the week. Working with bureaucrats can be the least meaningful thing you can do, however, working for public parks is very different than any sort of public sector job. That is the difficult thing - however, local government parks are the least funded of all the types of parks jobs you can have. So they are needed, beautiful hidden gem that someone needs to work at and take care of. I don't mind it too much even with all the frustrations and annoyances of the governemtn.

Work_Story:
MY work is great, someday it can be beyond stressful and other days it seems more joyus and worth while. You are helping people. You are basically in control of them and what may or may not happen to them. You have to be stealthy and quick. You have to stay on your toes and be ready for things to happen at ny given moment. Today might be a slow day ot it might be super hectic one. You will never know. It like you wake up in the morning and you put on your scrubs and go to the clinic and wait to see what happens next. Its fun to meet new people and hear them talk. Some days its more sad talk than good. However, every day is different. Some days you don't deal with as many patients. There ar the days of paper work and filing and all the computer task of inputting all of the information into the system, sometimes it can be faitly quick and others it takes way longer because you're so backed up. paper work is stressful, the phones will be ringing off the hook and you have to put your customer service voice on and act like you have any real idea about what they need. sometimes you actually do though. Then you have to deal with injections and you really don't know ethically if this is actually good stuff, so you inject these babies and on the inside you are praying that those parents do not come back and complain that you hurt their child. that they are sick or in some kind of pain from the injection you did. but you have to remember that these kids have parents and that this was in fact the parents choice, not yours. this is just your job. Work_Story: I work as a federal bank regulator. I perform onsite regulatory risk management examinations of financial institutions including operational and financial analysis, loan and investment credit quality analysis, internal controls and external audit procedure validation, information technology assessment, bank secrecy act compliance, and other various analyses as it pertains to the business model of the bank. We evaluate banks on a 1 to 5 scale, 1 being the best and 5 being the worst, on six operational components: Capital Adequacy, Asset Quality, Management Ability, Earnings Performance, Liquidity Levels, and Sensitivity to Market Risk. Capital Adequacy refers to whether capital levels are sufficient according to regulatory minimums to support the operations of the bank; Asset Quality refers to the level of financial risk associated with a bank's loan portfolio, typically their largest asset category, Management Ability evaluates senior bank management and the Board if Directors for competency and adherence to safe and sound operation of the bank, Earnings Performance is concerned with evaluating whether the level of net income is sufficient to support the operations of the bank and to augment capital levels, Liquidity Levels refers to ensuring that the bank has access to a sufficient amount liquid assets on favorable terms to fund withdrawals, and Sensitivity to Market Risk evaluates how changes in the economic environment and market conditions can affect the bank's asset or liability portfolio. We ensure compliance with Generally Accepted Accounting Procedures (GAAP), federal banking regulations, state banking regulations, and interagency statements of policy which are "guidelines" formed and agreed upon by the three primary federal regulators: the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, the Federal Reserve, and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency. During our examinations we perform a review of a sample of loans from the bank's loan portfolio to determine the credit quality of the banks assets and to evaluate market conditions in the local economy. We issue a confidential regulatory examination report with our examination findings and recommendations. This is only shared between federal and state bank regulators and the Board of Directors of the bank. Examination ratings are used to support regulation of certain bank practices, pricing of FDIC insurance premiums, and as a monitoring tool of the safety and soundness of the banking industry as a whole. When we are not physically onsite at a bank, we are usually preparing for an examination which consists of reviewing previous examination reports and correspondence with the bank, analyzing recent financial data provided through Federal Financial Examinations Counsel filings, preparing the scope documents for our examination, and planning our time and assignments for the team based on experience and training needs. Maintaining a cohesive team environment is essential for my work as well as maintaining a positive rapport with senior bank management and bank directors. My territory is based largely in the southern region of the United States and on a day to day basis, I am usually on the road. Travel time comprises approximately 20 percent of my time "on the clock".
